Action: write concrete signatures with arg/return types for all 5 functions; specify the disposal lifecycle (when does an adapter shut down, who calls it, what happens to in-flight effects).
close_reason: Adapter API contract now has concrete signatures. Section restructured with: (1) make-state-container — explicit type sketch, host realisations; (2) read-container / replace-container! split out (read pure; replace is the only mutation primitive); (3) subscribe-container with on-change callback signature, unsubscribe-fn signature, fallback rule when adapter doesn't support; (4) make-derived-value with compute-fn signature, replace-container! errors with :rf.error/derived-container-replaced; (5) render with mount-point/opts/unmount-fn signatures; (6) render-to-string with opts; (7) register-context-provider with fallback-rule for hosts without context; (8) NEW dispose-adapter! disposal lifecycle (cancel subscriptions, release resources, discard caches, single-use). Added error categories :rf.error/derived-container-replaced and :rf.error/adapter-disposed to API.md.

title: :platforms #{:client} default silently breaks SSR-shared fx
description: HIGH (backwards-compat). 011-SSR:135 declares :platforms defaults to #{:client} when absent. v1 apps register fx without :platforms. Any fx that runs on JVM headless tests or shared CLJC code would now be silently skipped (with :rf.fx/skipped-on-platform trace, not error). Not covered in MIGRATION.md. Action: either (a) change default to #{:server :client} (break-but-explicit; new fx must declare :platforms #{:client} to opt in to client-only); (b) add migration rule that explicitly lists every fx without :platforms as a Type-B human-review (review whether it should be #{:client} or #{:server :client} or #{:client :server}). Lean: (a) — safer default for migrating apps; new code follows the pattern's discipline of explicit :platforms anyway. Update 011 + MIGRATION.
close_reason: Default :platforms changed from #{:client} to #{:server :client} (universal). Matches v1's effective behaviour — fx ran wherever dispatched. Migrating apps don't get silent skipping. New fx that genuinely cannot run server-side (browser-DOM, localStorage, requireJS-window) declare :platforms #{:client} explicitly. Updated 011-SSR §:platforms metadata in two places (overview + S-3 detailed design). MIGRATION.md 'What stays the same' lists reg-fx/reg-cofx without :platforms as preserved-and-universal.

title: Kind-vocabulary inconsistency: :event vs :event-db/:event-fx vs :machine across 001/002/005
description: HIGH. (a) 001-Registration:114 query API table lists kinds as :event, :sub, :fx, :cofx, :view, :frame, :route, :machine-action, :machine-guard, :app-schema. (b) 002-Frames:784 uses :event-db, :event-fx, :event-ctx, :sub, :sub-raw, :fx, :cofx, :view, :interceptor. (c) 005-StateMachines:230 uses (rf/handlers :machine), but 001 omits :machine entirely. AI implementer cannot tell what kinds the registrar accepts. Action: pick canonical vocabulary (lean: 001's flat :event/:sub/:fx; introduce :machine as a kind, not just :machine-action/:machine-guard) and propagate everywhere.
close_reason: Kind-vocabulary canonicalised. 001 §The query API now has a canonical-kind table: :event (covers all 3 reg-event-*; sub-kind on metadata as :event/kind ∈ {:db :fx :ctx}), :sub, :sub-raw, :fx, :cofx, :view, :frame, :interceptor, :route, :machine, :machine-action, :machine-guard, :app-schema. 002 §Public registrar query API now points at 001 for the canonical list rather than enumerating its own (different) version. AI implementer has one definitive source.

title: EffectMap closure consistency: examples violate the closed contract; M-7/O-7/'stays-the-same' say different things
description: HIGH. Spec-Schemas:111 declares :rf/effect-map :closed with only :db and :fx, but: (a) 002-Frames:401-405 worked example returns {:http {...}} top-level; (b) 005-StateMachines:147 machine actions example returns {:dispatch ... :http ...}; (c) MIGRATION M-7 (line 220-280) says all top-level non-:db keys move to :fx and supersedes O-7, BUT MIGRATION 'What stays the same' (line 495) says :dispatch-n is deprecated-but-works, AND O-7 (line 401) describes :dispatch-n as preserved. AI agent migrating cannot tell whether :dispatch-n is hard-break or kept. Also: closed-by-default conflicts with open-shapes-everywhere principle (Best-Practice review). Also: M-7 says 'any user-registered fx that was previously called as a top-level key' but no mechanical pattern for discovering them. Action: (1) fix the 002 and 005 examples; (2) reconcile M-7 vs O-7 vs stays-the-same — make :dispatch-n a hard-break with O-7 absorbed into M-7; (3) document the migration agent's discovery procedure (sweep reg-fx ids); (4) reconsider whether EffectMap should be open with documented known keys (consistent with everywhere-else) — proposal: keep closed, since this is a contract between handler and runtime, but add this rationale in Spec-Schemas.
close_reason: EffectMap closure made consistent. (1) Fixed worked example in 002 §Async effects (top-level :http now inside :fx). (2) Fixed worked example in 005 §Actions (top-level :dispatch / :http now inside :fx; merge-strategy prose updated to match — fx vectors concatenate). (3) Reconciled :dispatch-n: M-7 now the canonical rewrite rule; O-7 marked absorbed-into-M-7 with a pointer; What-stays-the-same updated to say only :db and :fx are top-level effect-map keys. (4) M-7 now includes a mechanical discovery procedure: sweep reg-fx ids first, then rewrite per-handler; unknown top-level keys flag for human review. (5) Intro 'covers five cases' updated to 'six cases'.

title: Run-to-completion drain (M-3) is Type B but classified Type A — silent breakage risk
description: HIGH. C1 in 000-Vision says: a breaking change is acceptable if Detectable + Mechanically-rewritable + Behaviour-preserving. M-3 (MIGRATION:87-126) admits the rewrite 'may need behavioural review, not a mechanical rewrite' and depends on intent — that's Type B. But MIGRATION:281 lists M-3 alongside fully-mechanical rules in the 'sweep is complete' check. Per C1: 'Side-effects can be silently reordered with observable consequences' is a design failure. Action: explicitly tag M-3 as Type B (flag-for-human-review). Update MIGRATION's sweep-complete check at line 281 to acknowledge that M-3 hits require human review, even after detection.
close_reason: M-3 explicitly marked Type B at the sweep-complete summary in MIGRATION. Rule list now distinguishes Type A (M-1, M-4, M-5, M-6, M-7) — agent rewrites mechanically — from Type B (M-3) — agent flags + asks before rewriting. Per C1 a breaking change with silent reordering is a design failure; M-3's classification now matches that contract. Migration-complete gate now requires both Type-A auto-rewrites pass AND Type-B human-approval recorded.

title: 011-SSR is too incomplete for v1-required
description: Multiple core protocol decisions still open as 'Lean:' answers in §Open questions: S-2 hydration-mismatch contract, S-3 server-side effect handling beyond the basic platforms gate, S-4 JVM-runnable view rendering details, S-5 routing-and-SSR specifics, S-6 auth/session at the SSR layer, S-7 hydration of non-state runtime artefacts. The status banner still says 'Skeleton. v1-required. Full design to follow.' Resolve each open question with a concrete decision or bump 011 from v1-required if the design isn't ready.
close_reason: 011-SSR open questions S-2..S-7 all resolved with concrete decisions. S-2 hydration mismatch: structured trace event + warn-and-replace default, strict-mode opt-in. S-3 server fx: :platforms metadata + skipped-on-platform trace, default :client-only. S-4 JVM rendering: pure hiccup → string, no JVM React, no server-side lifecycle. S-5 routing: :rf/server-init dispatches :route/handle-url-change with request URL; same handler universal. S-6 auth: cofx injection from request session at :rf/server-init time; no SSR-specific surface. S-7 non-state hydration: v1 ships only :app-db hydration; sub-warmups etc. post-v1. Status banner moved from 'Skeleton' to 'Drafting'.

title: Migrate reorient.md content into permanent docs (so reorient can be removed)
description:
Goal: be able to delete reorient.md without losing content. The following substantive content currently lives ONLY in reorient.md and would need to move to permanent docs first:

1. **Identity primitive — required properties + per-host realisations** (reorient §The identity primitive lines 118-144). The 7 properties (stable, namespaceable, value-equal, cheap, serialisable, human-readable, reflective) and the per-host table (CLJS keywords, TS branded strings, Python Id-class, Kotlin sealed-classes, Rust newtype). Target: 000-Vision §The pattern, or new Spec 001 once drafted.

2. **What the pattern should NOT over-commit to** (reorient §What the pattern should NOT over-commit to lines 146-159). Full bullet list (macros, Vars, Reagent return types, hiccup-only, CLJS-only, React context, goog-define, Malli). Target: 000-Vision §The pattern.

3. **Concrete CLJS choices table** (reorient §Concrete CLJS choices lines 175-199). 17-row table mapping pattern primitive → CLJS reference choice. Target: 000-Vision §Reference implementation.

4. **AI-first principles in practice** (reorient §What 'AI-first' should mean in practice lines 201-295). 11 sub-principles. The 5-layer dampening essay ('A simple dynamic model — the layered constraint') and the data-is-code-VM essay are substantive content that's referenced from AI-Audit.md and the human guide but only fully written in reorient. Target: a new Principles.md or fold into AI-Audit.md.

5. **What we retain from re-frame** list (reorient §What we retain from re-frame lines 363-377). Captures simple-dynamic-model, data-oriented-design, event-driven logic, effect maps, derived reads, single-app ergonomics, upgradeability. Target: 000-Vision.

6. **Working design implications** list (reorient §Working design implications lines 393-408). 12 design hints. Some already in Spec docs; others (definitions-serialisable, host-adapters, contracts-additive) belong in 000.

7. **Tensions and resolutions** (reorient §Tensions and resolutions lines 335-361). Some tensions resolved and captured in Spec docs (override seam, naming, frame vocab); the backwards-compat-vs-AI-first stance is in memory but not yet in any Spec doc as text. Target: 000-Vision §C1 expanded.

8. **Rationale table** (reorient §Rationale lines 51-65) pointing at on-dynamics, data-oriented-design, etc. Target: README's reading-order already covers these but the full table doesn't live anywhere else.

Once these all have permanent homes, reorient.md can be deleted (or kept as a 'historical orientation note' with a top banner pointing at the new locations).
close_reason:
reorient.md content fully migrated and the file deleted. New permanent homes:
- Identity primitive properties + per-host realisations → 000-Vision §The identity primitive (with anchor #the-identity-primitive--required-properties)
- What the pattern should NOT over-commit to → 000-Vision §What the pattern does NOT over-commit to
- Concrete CLJS choices table → 000-Vision §The reference implementation (full table)
- AI-first practical principles incl. 5-layer dampening + data-is-code-VM essays → new Principles.md
- What we retain from re-frame → 000-Vision §What re-frame2 retains from re-frame
- Working design implications → 000-Vision §Working design implications
- Tensions and resolutions → 000-Vision §Tensions and resolutions
- Rationale table → README §Rationale (also referenced from 000)
- Construction prompts as a deliverable → Principles.md §Construction prompts as a deliverable

Cross-references repointed via sed: [reorient.md §X](reorient.md...) → appropriate new home; banner phrases like 'Reframed per reorient.md' → 'Per the reorientation' (history-preserving). Examples (routing/core.cljs, ssr/core.cljc) updated. Guide had no reorient refs. Final grep confirms zero residual reorient.md references in docs/, examples/, or README.

title: Documentation voice: human is marketing/philosophy, AI is dry/precise
description:
Per Mike's directive on the two-track docs (rf2-doc-split):

HUMAN VERSION — marketing-oriented:
- Explains the philosophy and the reasons behind design choices
- Communicates the benefits (why this pattern, what it gives you)
- Includes enough technical detail to be useful, but not exhaustive
- Inherits re-frame's existing voice — opinionated, conversational, sometimes philosophical
- Read by humans evaluating, adopting, or building with re-frame2

AI VERSION — dry and precise:
- No narrative, no rationale, no marketing
- Just contracts, signatures, shapes, semantics, fixtures, prompts
- Optimised for AI consumption: read-and-act
- Aligns with the existing Construction-Prompts / Spec-Schemas / MIGRATION / conformance artefacts which are already in this register

The two are not redundant — they cover the same design but at different abstraction levels and for different consumers. A human reads the human track to understand WHY; an AI reads the AI track to know WHAT and HOW PRECISELY.
close_reason: Voice direction captured in memory rf2-doc-voice and now demonstrated across 8 chapters in docs/guide/. Three voice registers used: argumentative/philosophical (01, 08, with Perlis/Dijkstra epigraphs); code-led narrative (02, 03, 04, 05, 06); migration narrative (07). Voice inherits re-frame's existing tone — opinionated, conversational, sometimes rhetorical. The AI track (docs/specification/) remains in the dry/precise register it was already in, so the contrast is clear.

title: Design two-track doc split — for-humans / for-AIs
description: Per Mike's directive: re-frame2 docs should be split into two parallel tracks. Human track: tutorials, conceptual guides, design rationale, narrative — written in re-frame's existing voice. AI track: precise contracts, construction prompts, schemas, conformance corpus, migration rules — already largely exists as separate artefacts (Construction-Prompts, Spec-Schemas, MIGRATION, conformance/). Several load-bearing questions before restructuring (see comments).
notes: Five chapters + README done. ~1280 lines: 01 (why, marketing), 02 (counter walkthrough), 03 (events/effects), 04 (views/frames), 05 (state machines), 08 (dynamic-model essay). Three voice registers on display. Remaining: 06 (server side), 07 (from re-frame v1). Stopping here for Mike to react before grinding through the last two.
close_reason: Human guide first pass complete. docs/guide/ has README plus 7 chapters (~1820 lines): 01 (why, marketing argument), 02 (counter walkthrough, code-led), 03 (events/effects, side-effects-as-data), 04 (views/frames, multi-frame), 05 (state machines, xstate adoption), 06 (server side, SSR/hydration), 07 (from re-frame v1, migration narrative), 08 (the dynamic-model essay, Dijkstra/Perlis epigraphs). Three voice registers: argumentative (01, 08), code-led narrative (02, 03, 04, 05, 06), migration-narrative (07). Two-track structure now demonstrated end-to-end: docs/guide/ for humans (this), docs/specification/ for AIs (existing). Voice consistent with re-frame's existing on-dynamics.md / data-oriented-design.md tone — opinionated, conversational, philosophical. Awaiting Mike's react before any deeper revisions.

title: Corpus-wide register-error-emit-listener! API on rf2-hqbeh's error_emit substrate (production error observability)
description:
Sibling to rf2-rirbq. Extends rf2-hqbeh's already-shipped error_emit substrate (PR #716) with a corpus-wide listener registry so off-box shippers (Datadog/Sentry/Honeycomb) can observe every framework error, not just per-frame :on-error policy fires.

## Context

- rf2-hqbeh PR #716 shipped `implementation/core/src/re_frame/error_emit.cljc` as an always-on production-survivable substrate. It currently fans out to per-frame :on-error policy fns (in-app RECOVERY — catch, retry, mark frame broken).
- rf2-rirbq is filed for the parallel event_emit substrate (production observability of events).
- This bead: extend error_emit with a parallel corpus-wide listener API for production observability of errors (no overlap with the :on-error per-frame policy concern).

## The asymmetry resolves cleanly

| Substrate | Per-frame policy | Corpus-wide listener |
|---|---|---|
| error_emit | :on-error reg-frame slot (recovery) — SHIPPED rf2-hqbeh | register-error-emit-listener! (observability) — THIS BEAD |
| event_emit | n/a (events aren't 'handled' per frame) | register-event-emit-listener! — rf2-rirbq |

Errors have two legitimate consumers (in-app recovery + off-box observability). Events have one (off-box observability). Listener APIs are symmetric in shape; registration concerns are separate.

## What ships

- Public API in implementation/core/src/re_frame/core.cljc:
  - (rf/register-error-emit-listener! id (fn [error-record] ...))
  - (rf/unregister-error-emit-listener! id)
- Listener-registry atom in error_emit.cljc; dispatch-on-error! fans out to BOTH the per-frame :on-error policy AND the listener registry (independent paths, both wrapped in try/catch so one bad listener can't break the other)
- Tight error-record shape: {:error <kw eg :rf.error/handler-exception> :event <vec> :event-id <kw> :frame <kw> :time <inst> :exception <ex-data> :elapsed-ms <int>}
- Listeners run AFTER rf/elide-wire-value is applied — same off-box-strict defaults as event_emit
- Always-on, survives :advanced + goog.DEBUG=false
- Zero cost when no listener is registered (registry-empty check is a single deref + branch)

## Mike's three locks (same as rf2-rirbq)

1. PRODUCTION-ONLY for off-box shippers. Dev gets Causa/pair2/story.
2. PRODUCTION HAS NO TRACE. This substrate is parallel to trace, not a fallback from it.
3. ONLY errors logged through this — not warnings, not info. Warnings/info would ride event_emit's :outcome :error variant if motivated; this listener fires on actual :rf.error/* events.

## Surfaces

- impl: implementation/core/src/re_frame/error_emit.cljc (add listener registry + dispatch fn)
- impl: implementation/core/src/re_frame/core.cljc (re-export the public API)
- tests: extend implementation/core/test/re_frame/on_error_test.cljc (dev-side JVM) + on_error_elision_prod_test.cljs (prod-elision suite, mirror rf2-hqbeh's pattern)
- spec: 009-Instrumentation.md §What IS available in production (#3, parallel to :on-error policy and event_emit)
- api: spec/API.md row
- guide: ch.22 Datadog rewrite (will be done in rf2-rirbq's ch.22 sweep — register BOTH listeners and route both through one Datadog forwarder)

## Dependencies

- rf2-rirbq should land first (defines the listener-API SHAPE convention that this mirror)
- Or this lands in parallel with rf2-rirbq if scope permits — they touch different code paths in router.cljc + late-bind directory; should be disjoint enough

## Pre-alpha

No back-compat. Mirror rf2-rirbq's elegance exactly: opt-in listener, zero cost when unused, tight record shape, off-box-strict defaults. Masterpiece-grade.

Source: Mike, 2026-05-13 22:05 AUSEST — 'BTW, errors should also be logged'.
notes: Same goog.DEBUG-at-registration framing as rf2-rirbq (see its notes 22:08 AUSEST). Document the AND-gated registration pattern in spec/009 + ch.22 alongside the event-emit example.

title: Always-on event-emit substrate for production observability (Datadog/Sentry/Honeycomb)
description:
The current ch.22 'Trace forwarding to Datadog' recipe registers `register-trace-cb!`, but the trace bus is dev-only (dead-code-eliminated in :advanced + goog.DEBUG=false). The chapter buries this caveat at §Per-environment gating and offers two unsatisfying escape hatches: (a) ship goog.DEBUG=true in prod (bundle + hot-path cost), (b) fall back to User Timing (coarser, no sensitive/large elision).

Mike's framing (2026-05-13 21:50 AUSEST): 'In production there is no trace. I would have thought it would have to be a production-time intercept which records events taking into account large blobs and secrets before logging. Only the events need to be logged, not all the rest of the trace information.'

## The right shape

Mirror rf2-hqbeh's :on-error always-on substrate:
- New `implementation/core/src/re_frame/event_emit.cljc` (always-on, NOT gated on `interop/debug-enabled?`)
- Public API: `(rf/register-event-emit-listener! id (fn [event-record] ...))` and `(rf/unregister-event-emit-listener! id)`
- Router fans out: `router.cljc` invokes both error_emit/dispatch-on-error! (rf2-hqbeh) AND the new event-emit/dispatch-on-event! independently
- Each listener receives an `event-record` map with a TIGHT shape: `{:event <vector> :event-id <kw> :frame <kw> :time <inst> :outcome :ok|:error :elapsed-ms <int>}` — NOT the full trace event
- Listeners MUST run elision themselves via `rf/elide-wire-value event-record opts` before shipping off-box; framework guarantees the helper survives production-elision
- Survives :advanced + goog.DEBUG=false
- Spec home: spec/009 §What IS available in production (#2, alongside :on-error)
- Off-box defaults: include-large? false, include-sensitive? false (drop)

## Surfaces

- impl: implementation/core/src/re_frame/event_emit.cljc (new), router.cljc (fan-out hook), late-bind directory.cljc
- tests: dev-side JVM tests + prod-elision suite test (mirror rf2-hqbeh's on_error_elision_prod_test.cljs shape)
- spec: 009-Instrumentation.md §What IS available in production
- guide: ch.22 REWRITE — drop register-trace-cb! recipe; replace with register-event-emit-listener!; the §Per-environment gating section collapses to one paragraph (no goog.DEBUG flip needed); the elision flow stays (rf/elide-wire-value still applies); Datadog payload mapping stays
- api: spec/API.md row

## Out of scope (file separate beads if motivated)

- Subscription-emit listener for sub-recompute observability (different value/cost tradeoff)
- Effect-emit listener for fx-execution observability (likely overkill)
- Backwards-compat retain of register-trace-cb! for dev — keep dev trace bus as-is; this is a new orthogonal substrate

## Why it matters

- Production observability is a first-class use case; the framework should not need a footnote workaround
- Composes naturally with rf2-hqbeh's :on-error (both always-on, both production-survivable, parallel listener APIs)
- :sensitive? / :large? elision composition is exactly the value-add re-frame2 wants to offer over hand-rolled JS observability

Source: Mike, 2026-05-13 21:55 AUSEST.
notes:
Mike clarification 2026-05-13 22:08 AUSEST — goog.DEBUG matters at REGISTRATION (not substrate). Substrate is always-on (no goog.DEBUG check inside event_emit.cljc). Listener REGISTRATION site uses goog.DEBUG as a belt-and-braces gate alongside the user's explicit config flag:

  (when (and (= "production" (:env config))
             (not ^boolean re-frame.interop/debug-enabled?)
             (:api-key config))
    (rf/register-event-emit-listener! ...))

Catches the 'accidentally deployed a dev bundle with prod config' bug class. Document this pattern in the chapter rewrite + spec/009 §Production observability example block.

title: Pre-existing CI failure cluster on main: pair2-mcp + CLJS browser-test + Examples + MCP conformance pair2-mcp
description:
Since approximately the PR #708 (cursor pagination) / #710 (sensitive? predicate) wave, 4 CI jobs have been consistently RED on main and every subsequent PR:

- Node tools/pair2-mcp (shadow-cljs :server-test)
- MCP conformance tools/pair2-mcp (rf2-cum40)
- CLJS (shadow-cljs :browser-test, headless Chromium)
- Examples (browser, headless Chromium, rf2-lyj0)

Latest main commit (28533651) still shows all 4 failing. JVM schemas was fixed by rf2-pcasr PR #711 (no longer in the list). This is a separate cluster.

Likely culprits:
- PR #708 (rf2-kbqq3 cursor pagination) — introduced new pair2-mcp surface that other tests don't expect
- PR #710 (rf2-sqxjn sensitive?) — explicitly NOT touched pair2-mcp's private sensitive-event? per agent report, but might have surfaced an inter-artefact require issue elsewhere
- The CLJS browser-test + Examples flakiness could be Playwright/headless Chromium flake; need to check whether failures are deterministic across re-runs

Investigation:
1. gh run view <latest run> --log-failed | tail -200 — extract the actual assertion failures + error messages.
2. git bisect across PRs #708/#710 if non-obvious.
3. Once root cause identified, fix or revert.

Tracking via this bead so map.md reflects 'main is RED' clearly. Source: PR-merger cron, 2026-05-13 ~20:52 AUSEST.
notes: Update: keeping bead OPEN because Examples (causa.spec) is still RED after this PR — the Trace panel step (line 153) fails due to rf2-iw5ym's sub change. Filed rf2-e9s81 for the trace-panel architectural fix; close THIS bead when both PR #728 lands AND rf2-e9s81 closes.

title: day8/de-dupe: triage + likely-close issues #1 and #2 (both 2015)
description:
Two issues open on day8/de-dupe since 2015. Both probably stale; agent should read each, judge whether still relevant, and close with a thoughtful comment that points to current state.

Issues to triage:
- #1 'Dependency [de-dupe "0.2.2"] not found' (2015-08-04) — user couldn't pull from Maven or Clojars. Likely resolved by the upcoming 0.3.0 Clojars publish (rf2-rd1at + rf2-ucbuz lines). Close with a comment pointing at the new 0.3.0 install snippet.
- #2 'Provide helpful examples showing compression' (2015-08-04) — user tried small fixtures and saw output LARGER than input. The point of de-dupe is structural-sharing preservation, NOT raw byte compression. The README already addresses this in 'When does de-dupe help?' section (or should). Read the current README; if the question is already answered, close with a link to that section. If not, the agent SHOULD add a short 'When this library helps' paragraph to the README that explicitly states 'small inputs grow; the win is on large duplicated structures, particularly for preserving identical? across the wire'.

Workflow:
1. cd C:\Users\miket\code\de-dupe && checkout cljc-port (PR #3 branch — has the modern README).
2. Read README + each issue carefully.
3. For #1: close with comment 'Resolved by 0.3.0 Clojars publish — install with [day8/de-dupe "0.3.0"]'.
4. For #2: if README already has a 'when does this help' section, close with comment pointing there. If not, propose a small README addition and commit on the cljc-port branch.
5. Use gh issue close --comment '<text>' for both.

Source: Mike, 2026-05-13 ~20:43 AUSEST — 'get a background issue to look at the two issues for that repo and (probably close them)'.
notes: Issue #1 closed (https://github.com/day8/de-dupe/issues/1): explained 0.2.2 was never published to Clojars, pointed at upcoming 0.3.0 release on PR #3 with [day8/de-dupe "0.3.0"] install snippet. Issue #2 closed (https://github.com/day8/de-dupe/issues/2): README didn't have a 'when this helps' section, added one on cljc-port branch (commit 1ffdc87, 14-line section before Usage) covering small-input-grows / large-sharing-shrinks tradeoff with three worked examples (tiny no-sharing input, vector of 100 copies of same map, real pair2-mcp ~89% number) plus rule of thumb. README-only commit; did not touch deps.edn/project.clj/build.clj (left to rf2-ucbuz's work-in-progress unstaged changes). Push successful: e7a6e42..1ffdc87 cljc-port.
close_reason: Closed

title: day8/de-dupe: swap from leiningen to tools.build + slipset/deps-deploy
description:
Modernise the build toolchain on day8/de-dupe — drop project.clj + lein, adopt clj -T:build with tools.build + slipset/deps-deploy.

Current state on branch cljc-port (commit e7a6e42):
- project.clj — artefact day8/de-dupe 0.3.0, :deploy-repositories pointing at Clojars (env-var creds), :scope provided on Clojure deps
- deps.edn — has :compile-test / :test / :compile-bench aliases
- .github/workflows/test.yml — runs 'clojure -M:test'
- .github/workflows/release.yml — uses 'lein deploy clojars' on v* tags
- README.md — Clojars badge + install snippets (Leiningen + deps.edn)

Target state:

title: day8/de-dupe: verify test coverage is comprehensive + actually exercised in CI
description:
Cross-repo verification on day8/de-dupe (just landed .cljc port via PR #3 from rf2-nw6sj).

Two questions to answer:
1. Are the tests GOOD? Coverage of: round-trip identity, structural sharing preservation (identical? on shared substructures), all collection types (vec/map/set/list/lazy-seq/record), depth-bounded recursion limits, edge cases (nil, empty, single-element, cycles if supported), platform parity (CLJS and JVM produce same dedup behaviour).
2. Are tests EXERCISED in CI? The workflow runs 'clojure -M:test' which invokes test-runner.clj. Verify that runner ACTUALLY runs both targets (CLJS via Node + JVM) and reports assertion counts in the CI log, not just exit-0 from compilation. Add explicit assertion-count gate if needed (e.g., 'fail if total assertions < N' to catch silent test-loading failures).

Source: Mike, 2026-05-13 ~20:28 AUSEST. Follow-on to rf2-nw6sj port + my CI workflow modernisation (SHA-pinning, setup-node, deps cache).

If gaps found: extend test coverage, push to cljc-port branch (PR #3 still open). If green: leave a comment on PR #3 documenting what was verified.
notes: Audit complete. Coverage was decent (9 tests / 43 assertions) but had real gaps: nil, empties, single-elem, sets, sorted variants, deep/wide, quoted forms, platform-parity shape. Added all of these — suite now 16 tests / 77 assertions on both CLJS and JVM legs. Bigger find: test-runner.clj had a silent-failure hole — if either platform's tests failed to load, the runner happily exited 0. Added a per-leg minimum-assertion-count gate (floor 60); verified it trips with exit 2 when forced. Commit 7d5a7a4 pushed to day8/de-dupe cljc-port branch; CI run 25793542162 green and shows '→ CLJS/JVM: 16 tests / 77 assertions ✓ All test legs passed assertion-floor gate.' in log. PR #3 comment posted: https://github.com/day8/de-dupe/pull/3#issuecomment-4439965843. Cycles documented as unsupported (persistent colls cannot express them; walk would not terminate). No follow-on beads — PR #3 is good to merge.

title: [story] Chrome-level test widget + sidebar status dots (Vitest-reporter parity)
description:
## SB9 parity (rf2-v05qb)

Storybook 9's headline 9.0 feature: `@storybook/addon-vitest` ships a test widget at the bottom of the sidebar (total / passed / failed counts) plus per-story status dots in the sidebar rows + a debug panel that scrolls to the failing story on click. Story's :test mode tab (rf2-qmjo, shipped) runs the variant's :play + :rf.assert/* and renders an in-canvas aggregated pass/fail summary **for the currently-selected variant only** — there is no chrome-level aggregation, no per-row status dot.

## Proposed shape

Add two surfaces, both reading from the same data source (per-variant `run-variant` :assertions records):

1. **Chrome-level test widget.** A strip at the bottom of the sidebar (or a panel below the existing tag-filter row) showing 'N total · ✓ M passed · ✗ K failed' across all variants tagged :test. A 'Run all' button drives `run-variant` over each :test-tagged variant; results stream back as they complete. The widget mirrors tools/story/spec/009-Test-Mode.md's per-variant pane shape, scaled up.
2. **Sidebar status dots.** Each variant row in the sidebar shows a small colour glyph (pass=green / fail=red / unrun=grey / running=spinner) read from the chrome-level run's result map keyed by variant-id.

Scope: explicit run-all-tests only, **no watch mode** (the v2 deferral per 009-Test-Mode.md §'Out of scope at v1' — needs the registration-diff signal Story doesn't have yet). Watch mode is filed separately.

## Where this lives

- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/sidebar.cljs (status dots + the widget strip)
- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/state.cljc (run-all-results slot, keyed by variant-id)
- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/test_mode.cljs (factor out the per-variant aggregate-summary into a shared helper the widget reuses)
- tools/story/spec/009-Test-Mode.md (extend §'Foundational status' with the chrome-level widget contract)
- tools/story/spec/005-SOTA-Features.md (add to v1.1 or v1 ship list)

## Why P2

Vitest-reporter is one of the four SB9 deltas explicitly named in rf2-v05qb's description ('Vitest reporter — partial'). The runtime is there; the missing piece is purely UI aggregation. Decomposes cleanly from rf2-qmjo (the per-variant pane).

## Source

ai/findings/story-storybook9-parity-20260513.md §2.4
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/713
close_reason: Closed

title: [story] Save current canvas state as a new variant (SB9 story-from-UI parity)
description:
## SB9 parity (rf2-v05qb)

Storybook 9 lets users tweak controls on an existing story, click 'Save', and the change writes back to the source file as a new exported story (or modification). This is SB9's killer adoption hook for non-coder contributors.

Story's recorder (rf2-5fc15) handles the *interaction* recording case but not the *args-tweak* case — to capture a tweaked-controls variant the user must copy-paste args or drive the change through the agent.

## Proposed shape

A 'Save variant' button on the controls panel that:
1. Reads the current effective args (per tools/story/spec/002-Runtime.md §Args resolution) for the selected variant + active modes.
2. Opens the same save-as-variant modal the recorder already uses.
3. Emits a (reg-variant ...) form pinned to the current selection, optionally with :extends pointing at the source variant.

## Where this lives

- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/controls.cljs (button + save-args helper)
- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/recorder.cljs (reuse the modal)
- tools/story/spec/005-SOTA-Features.md (new subsection)
- adjacent: story-mcp save-variant-from-args tool (separate P3 if scope warrants)

## Why P2

Story-from-UI is one of the SB9 features explicitly named in rf2-v05qb's description ('Story generation from UI — missing'). The implementation is small (re-uses recorder modal + the existing get-effective-args lookup) but the user-facing impact is large — it's the affordance new contributors find first.

## Source

ai/findings/story-storybook9-parity-20260513.md §2.2

title: spec: introduce 'managed external effect' as the unifying conceptual frame across 014/005/011/Pattern-WebSocket
description:
Mike-coined phrase 'managed external effect' captures the architectural shape shared across:

- :rf.http/managed (Spec 014) — HTTP requests
- :rf.ws/* (Pattern-WebSocket) — WebSockets
- :invoke / :invoke-all (Spec 005) — state-machine actor spawn
- :rf.server/* (Spec 011) — SSR per-request lifecycle
- :rf.flow/* (Spec 013) — managed derived computation (the internal-effect cousin)
- Future: managed timers, managed background jobs

The shared shape:
1. Effect-as-data (not callbacks)
2. Framework-owned execution lifecycle
3. Structured failure taxonomy under :rf.<surface>/*
4. Observable via the trace bus
5. :sensitive? + :large? composition (privacy + elision)
6. Built-in retry / abort / teardown semantics
7. In-flight registry
8. Per-frame interceptors / scoping

Today this shape exists in each spec independently. Mike's 'managed external effect' phrase names it.

Output: introduce the concept in one of these homes (pick best):
(a) spec/000-Vision.md — as a load-bearing architectural concept up front
(b) spec/Principles.md — as one of the 9 AI-first principles or as an extension
(c) NEW spec/Managed-Effects.md — dedicated doc enumerating the shape + each surface's instantiation

Cross-reference from each downstream spec (014, 005, 011, 013, Pattern-WebSocket).

Could be the conceptual umbrella that future surfaces (timers, jobs, etc.) inherit by name.

Scope: read each of the 5 specs first, then propose where the phrase lives + write the introduction. ~80-150 LoC of normative text. Cross-link sweep across the downstream specs.

Source: Mike, 2026-05-13 ~19:45 AUSEST: 'I like that term managed external effect'.
notes: PR #703 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/703. NEW spec/Managed-Effects.md (Type: Reference, 119 LoC) — names 8 properties (effect-as-data, framework-owned lifecycle, :rf.<surface>/* failure taxonomy, trace-bus observability, :sensitive?+:large? composition, retry/abort/teardown, in-flight registry, per-frame scoping). 5 downstream cross-links (014, 005, 011, 013, Pattern-WebSocket) as MUST-reference lines near top of each doc. 9 MUSTs total. mkdocs.yml + spec/README.md updated. Disjoint from rf2-4pcx8 (their 011 edit is line ~316; mine is line 4).

title: guide: write 'Privacy + Size Elision' chapter — sensitive/large flags end-to-end from app-writer perspective
description:
Per Mike: 'we need to document rf2-2p8wr and others in a guide page'. rf2-2p8wr (URL query-string sensitive) is one piece of a larger story that ISNT YET COVERED in /docs/guide/ from the app-writer perspective.

Recent landings that need a unified user-facing chapter:
- rf2-isdwf — runtime :sensitive? stamping from registration meta
- rf2-bma05 — HTTP header + body redaction (12-name denylist + handler-meta-driven)
- rf2-kj51z — schema-validation error redaction
- rf2-hmmx7 + rf2-v9tw2 — unified rf/elide-wire-value walker
- rf2-nwv63 — schema :large? per-slot metadata
- rf2-urjnc — wire elision into pair2-mcp snapshot + get-path
- rf2-2p8wr — HTTP URL query-string redaction (in flight as of filing)
- rf2-c1l4d — schema :sensitive? walker (in flight)

The Datadog guide page (rf2-tgt6n, docs/guide/22-trace-to-datadog.md) covers ELISION CONSUMPTION from the trace-listener angle. There is no guide chapter that explains the WRITER-side picture:

1. How to mark events sensitive (^{:sensitive? true} on reg-event-*)
2. How to use with-redacted interceptor for per-event payload scrubbing
3. How to declare paths large — schema :large? AND rf/declare-large-path! fx AND the runtime threshold-bytes auto-detect
4. What ends up redacted in HTTP traces — headers (denylist) + bodies (handler-meta) + URL query-string (rf2-2p8wr denylist + handler-meta)
5. What schema-driven privacy looks like (rf2-c1l4d when it lands) — :sensitive? per-slot in :rf/app-schema-meta
6. How the consumer-side flags interact (include-sensitive? / include-large? per consumer)
7. The composition rule — sensitive wins on both-flagged

Output: ONE new guide chapter — suggested name docs/guide/23-privacy-and-elision.md (chapter 23 after Stories ch.21 + Datadog ch.22). 200-350 LoC.

Structure:
- Why the framework cares about privacy
- The 2-axis design: sensitive (drop) vs large (elide-with-fetch)
- Three declaration sites: registration meta, schema meta, fx
- The unified walker
- HTTP coverage (headers + bodies + query-string)
- Schema-validation errors
- Consumer-side flags
- Worked example: a real app component with sensitive credentials + a large blob

Cross-references: spec/009 §Privacy + §Size elision, spec/010, spec/014, spec/API.md rf/elide-wire-value, guide/22-trace-to-datadog.md.

Tests: mkdocs build --strict passes + scripts/check_doc_slugs.py clean.

Source: Mike-directed audit need.

title: End-to-end MCP client integration test — drive each server via a real MCP-client harness
description:
From the MCP-testing audit. All current tests are server-side: unit, stdio-roundtrip (server-driven), live-nrepl (server-driven). No test drives an MCP server FROM the perspective of a real MCP-aware client (Claude Code, Continue, etc.).

Build a test harness that uses an embedded MCP client library (e.g. @modelcontextprotocol/sdk on Node, or an equivalent JVM client) to:
1. Spawn each MCP server
2. Initialize the client connection
3. Walk one canonical workflow per server:
   - pair2-mcp: dispatch event → watch-epochs → snapshot
   - story-mcp: register-variant → run-variant → read-failures
   - causa-mcp: (spec-only today — skip with TODO until impl lands)
4. Assert end-to-end success

This catches protocol-mismatch bugs that server-side tests can't.

Where it lives: maybe a new tools/mcp-conformance/ artefact with the test harness, run from CI alongside existing test suites.

Source: MCP-testing audit.
notes: PR #702 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/702. New artefact tools/mcp-conformance/ drives pair2-mcp + story-mcp via @modelcontextprotocol/sdk Client. Local runs GREEN. CI: three new mcp-conformance-{pair2,story,causa} jobs in test.yml. Bead remains OPEN pending merge.
close_reason: Closed

title: Cross-MCP wire-vocabulary conformance test — :rf.mcp/* shapes must match across pair2-mcp/story-mcp/causa-mcp
description:
From the MCP-testing audit. The 3 MCP servers share reserved cross-server vocabulary per rf2-lwgg8 + rf2-hmmx7 + rf2-obpa9:

- {:rf.mcp/overflow {...}} — when wire-cap fires
- {:rf.mcp/summary {...}} — tree-summary mode
- {:rf.mcp/dedup-table <cache>} — structural dedup
- {:rf.mcp/redacted ...} — sensitive elision
- {:rf.size/large-elided {... :handle [:rf.elision/at <path>]}} — size elision

No test currently asserts these shapes are CONSISTENT across the 3 servers. If pair2-mcp emits :rf.mcp/overflow with {:cap-tokens N} and story-mcp emits {:cap_tokens N}, agents would have to special-case per server.

Build a conformance test (Clojure, JVM-runnable):
1. Feed each MCP server a synthetic input that exercises one of the 5 wire shapes
2. Assert the response marker conforms to a single canonical Malli schema
3. Run for all 3 servers (causa-mcp impl is empty today — skip with a TODO until rf2-NEW filed for causa-mcp impl)

Where it lives: maybe spec/conformance/ as a cross-server harness, OR a new top-level integration test artefact.

Source: MCP-testing audit.
notes:
PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/707

Conformance set (6 markers, 140 assertions, 6 tests):
- :rf.mcp/overflow      (pair2-mcp + causa-mcp fixtures, both conform)
- :rf.mcp/summary       (6 fixtures: map/vector/set/seq/scalar/clamped + causa-mcp variant)
- :rf.mcp/dedup-table   (pair2-mcp + causa-mcp fixtures)
- :rf.mcp/diff-from     (pair2-mcp only; vocabulary reserved cross-server)
- :rf.size/large-elided (pair2-mcp; causa-mcp drift caught — rf2-04ozp)
- :rf.elision/at        (covered transitively by :rf.size/large-elided handle slot)

Divergence found: causa-mcp's Principles.md uses ad-hoc :elided shape
instead of canonical :rf.size/large-elided. Filed rf2-04ozp.

story-mcp tripwire: asserts story-mcp does NOT contain any of the 6
literal markers; will fire when story-mcp adopts one.
close_reason: Closed

title: HTTP URL query-string :sensitive? — :rf.warning/decode-defaulted URL slot leaks auth params
description:
Follow-on from rf2-bma05 (PR #675). The HTTP :sensitive? coverage covers headers + bodies but DOES NOT yet redact query-string auth params in URLs.

Today: a request to https://api.example.com/users?api_key=SECRET&page=2 would land in trace events with the full URL string visible. The header denylist catches Authorization-header auth flows but query-string-auth is a parallel pattern in some APIs (especially older REST APIs + webhooks).

Fix: thread sensitivity through implementation/http/src/re_frame/http_encoding.cljc decode-response-body OR through a URL-stamping site in http_managed/normalise-args. Probably the simpler path: at trace-emit time, if the event is :sensitive?, redact the query-string portion of the URL with :rf/redacted (preserving the path so the operator can still see which endpoint was called).

Optional: an app-extensible denylist of query-param names (api_key, token, auth, etc.) parallel to the header denylist (rf.http/declare-sensitive-query-param! ?).

Source: rf2-bma05 PR #675 cross-cutting follow-on observation.
notes: PR #691 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/691
close_reason: Closed

title: Schema-driven :sensitive? per-slot key — plug into the elision walker alongside :large?
description:
Follow-on from rf2-nwv63 (PR #669). The Malli schema walker now recognises :large? as a per-slot metadata key and populates the elision registry. :sensitive? is already reserved as a per-slot key in Spec-Schemas, but today the canonical declaration site for :sensitive? is registration-meta (per Spec 009 Privacy) — the walker doesn't read :sensitive? from schema yet.

This bead: extend the walker (rf2-nwv63's schema walker) to also recognise :sensitive? as a per-slot key + write entries into a sibling registry slot. Same shape, same conflict-resolution rule, same idempotency. Enables schema-driven privacy declarations parallel to schema-driven size declarations.

Scope: implementation/schemas/src/re_frame/schemas.cljc + spec/010-Schemas.md §Per-slot metadata vocabulary section. Test parity with the :large? coverage (rf2-nwv63's schemas_elision_test.clj).

Source: rf2-nwv63 PR #669 cross-cutting observation. Walker is the natural plug-point; symmetry is now concrete since the walker exists.
close_reason: Closed

title: Publish day8/reagent-slim to Maven + rename adapter ns at publication

Today the in-tree path is suffixed: re-frame.adapter.reagent-slim (so the bridge and rewrite coexist under shadow-cljs).

Scope at publication PR:
1. Rename implementation/adapters/reagent-slim/src/re_frame/adapter/reagent_slim.cljs → reagent.cljs (ns becomes re-frame.adapter.reagent).
2. Coordinate with bridge artefact (day8/re-frame2-reagent) — when both ship, downstream apps pick exactly one.
3. Add release-workflow deploy-reagent-slim job per IMPL-SPEC §1.7 (mirrors deploy-reagent in .github/workflows/release.yml lines 294-357).
4. Smoke against a downstream :mvn/version coord.

Unblocks rf2-wl5pa Causa migration when Causa moves off in-tree :local/root to the published artefact. Note Causa can begin migration TODAY against :local/root "../../implementation/adapters/reagent-slim" — Maven release is the user-facing milestone, not the in-tree gate.

title: guide: write 'Trace to Datadog via interceptor' worked-example page — honour :sensitive? + :large? elision
description:
Write a new guide page demonstrating how to forward re-frame2 trace events to Datadog (events only) using a trace listener, with proper elision-flag honouring.

Scope: docs/guide/ — new page. Suggested filename: docs/guide/22-trace-to-datadog.md or docs/guide/recipes/trace-to-datadog.md.

What the page should cover:

1. The mechanism: register a trace listener via register-trace-cb! (per Spec 009 §User-side listener registration). That is the canonical sink for off-box forwarding.

2. Filter shape: events only. Use the filter vocabulary from Spec 009 §Filter vocabulary — op-types is #{:event} — to drop subs/fx/cofx/etc.

3. Privacy + size elision (CRITICAL): BEFORE sending to Datadog, run each captured trace event through rf/elide-wire-value (the unified walker from rf2-hmmx7, see spec/API.md). This:
   - Drops sensitive-flagged events entirely (per the §Privacy contract)
   - Replaces large values with rf.size/large-elided markers (per §Size elision)
   - Composes correctly when both flags apply (sensitive wins)

4. Datadog API details:
   - HTTP POST to https://api.datadoghq.com/api/v1/events (or v2)
   - Bearer / DD-API-KEY header
   - Event shape: title, text, alert_type, tags, ...
   - Mapping: trace event to Datadog event shape

5. Operational concerns (worked-example callouts):
   - Do not block the dispatch — listener fires sync but POST should be async
   - Backpressure: buffer/drop policy if Datadog is slow
   - Rate-limit Datadog API
   - Failure handling: log the Datadog send failure as its own trace event (carefully — do not loop)
   - Per-environment gate (production-only? a config knob)

6. A complete worked code snippet showing all of the above stitched together — registration, listener, elision, async send, error handling. ~30-50 lines of Clojure(Script).

7. Cross-references: Spec 009 §User-side listener registration, §Privacy, §Size elision; spec/API.md rf/elide-wire-value; spec/014-HTTPRequests.md for the canonical HTTP request shape if you use Managed HTTP to send (recommended — gets retry + redact + privacy for free).

8. A sanity caveat: this is a worked example, not a normative spec. Other observability platforms (Honeycomb, Sentry, Mezmo, etc.) follow the same pattern — Datadog is just the concrete example.

Tests / verification: mkdocs builds clean + python scripts/check_doc_slugs.py passes.

Output: one new guide page (or section). Cross-link from docs/guide/README.md if there is an index entry.

Why this matters: a real shipped app needs observability. The framework trace bus is the right surface for this, but USERS need to know how to plumb it AND how to do so without leaking sensitive data. This page is the canonical recipe.
close_reason: Closed

title: Migrate tools/causa to target reagent-slim once rf2-6hyy ships
description:
Per architectural discussion: Causa should target reagent-slim once the rewrite is operational. Reasoning:

1. Causa is already bundle-isolated (rf2-51x5) — production builds must NOT carry Causa code. So Causa can carry its own substrate without leaking to user-app production bundles.
2. Decouples Causa from user-app's adapter choice — a UIx-only or Helix-only user shouldn't have to pull stock Reagent to mount Causa.
3. Smaller dev bundle — faster hot-reload (the loop where Causa overhead is felt most acutely).
4. Reagent-slim is shaped for re-frame2's 'views are pure render fns over subs' model — exactly Causa's load profile.
5. Trust signal — the framework's own devtools running on the framework's rewrite.

**Sequence**:
- (a) rf2-fn5rk lands — stock-Reagent React-18 fix unblocks Causa today
- (b) rf2-tba5e smoke-test — confirm Reagent-slim is operational
- (c) rf2-6hyy Stage 4 impl ships
- THIS BEAD: migrate tools/causa to target reagent-slim
- (e) Causa tests + Playwright spec all green on the slim adapter

**Migration scope**:
- tools/causa/deps.edn — switch dep from re-frame.adapter.reagent → re-frame2-reagent-slim (or whatever the artefact name lands as)
- tools/causa/src/day8/re_frame2_causa/preload.cljs (and mount.cljs, panels/*.cljs) — switch reagent.* requires to reagent2.*
- Verify all 13 Phase 5+ panels render correctly on slim
- Verify Playwright spec (rf2-s2bhn) passes on slim
- Update tools/causa/spec/* if any normative pin to stock-Reagent exists

**Blocked on**: rf2-6hyy (Stage 4 reagent-slim impl). Likely also rf2-tba5e for operational confirmation.

Source: architectural discussion 2026-05-13 ~18:30 AUSEST — Mike confirmed Causa-on-slim is the masterpiece move.
notes: PR #674 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/674. Migrated tools/causa/deps.edn from stock reagent/reagent 2.0.1 to day8/reagent-slim :local/root. Zero ns rewrites (Causa has no direct reagent.* requires). mount.cljs intentionally unchanged — substrate-adapter/render is the right contract per Spec 006. Causa Playwright spec already unskipped on main (rf2-fn5rk landed); this PR keeps it green. Tests: 487 Causa JVM + 1688 CLJS + bundle-isolation + 26 examples Playwright incl. causa — all PASS.
close_reason: Closed

- API surface: CONFORMS to Spec 006 (9-key contract + :kind :reagent-slim, all 9 late-bind hooks routed through substrate-adapter/route-hook!)

Operational gaps blocking rf2-wl5pa Causa migration:
