title: Investigation: should :sensitive? use the same fetch-handle technique as :large? elision?
description:
**Mike's architectural-revisit question** triggered by reading the sensitive elision + large elision findings docs together.

**Current state** (per rf2-hmmx7 spec lock-in, PR #664, on main):
- **Same walker** () — yes, unified
- **Same registry shape** — yes
- **Same per-tool config surface** — yes
- **DIFFERENT marker shape on the wire**:
  - Sensitive: {:rf.mcp/redacted ...} — DROP AND FORGET, no fetch handle
  - Large: {:rf.size/large-elided {... :handle [:rf.elision/at <path>]}} — FETCHABLE via get-path

**The question Mike is asking**: should sensitive ALSO carry a fetch handle, so a consumer with override permission can opt-in retrieve the sensitive value at need, parallel to how large values work? OR should sensitive remain drop-and-forget?

**Arguments FOR full unification (sensitive gets a fetch handle too)**:
- One mental model, one wire shape, one technique. The masterpiece-grade move.
- Operator with override permission can opt-in to see the value via the handle — parallel to how large values are retrievable
- Reduces the marker zoo (currently 2 markers; could be 1)
- Audit trail: the trace event SHOWS that a sensitive value was elided + how to fetch it (if authorised), rather than silently disappearing

**Arguments AGAINST (the rf2-ok47g recommendation that rf2-hmmx7 locked in)**:
- Privacy semantics inherently differ from size semantics. A 'sensitive value' should NOT be readable from a handle that lives in the trace stream — privacy regs, audit posture, defense-in-depth
- Drop-and-forget IS the security default — explicit re-fetch path implies the data is still somewhere accessible from the trace
- The handle leaks :path — and for sensitive data, even the path can be sensitive (knowing 'user X's password is at [:users :X :password]' is a leak even without the value)
- The opt-in for sensitive is ALREADY :include-sensitive? true at the consumer — adding a second opt-in axis (fetch handle) is redundant and goes against 'don't put it on the wire'

**Required output**: ai/findings/sensitive-vs-large-technique-<timestamp>.md covering:
- Side-by-side comparison of the two techniques (drop-and-forget vs fetch-handle)
- Privacy + security threat model: what does the fetch handle enable that opt-in alone doesn't?
- The audit-trail argument: does sensitive elision NEED to leave a trace at all (yes — for the suppressed-count indicator)? Does it need to be RETRIEVABLE? (Probably no.)
- The path-leakage angle: how much can a privacy-aware adversary infer from JUST seeing {:rf.mcp/redacted :path [...]} ?
- The opt-in symmetry argument: if sensitive HAD a fetch handle, would the :include-sensitive? config knob become unnecessary? Or would they layer?
- Recommendation: confirm the rf2-hmmx7 lock-in (separate markers) OR propose a revisit
- If revisit: what would the unified marker shape look like, and what beads would need amendment (rf2-hmmx7's spec sections, rf2-v9tw2 walker impl in flight)?

**Read first**: ai/findings/sensitive-elision-audit-20260513-1748.md (rf2-ok47g) + ai/findings/declare-large-path-20260513-1748.md (rf2-vnmt6). Both are sibling investigations whose recommendations converged on 'separate flags, shared mechanism' — this bead asks: should they also converge on 'same marker shape'?

**Timing note**: rf2-v9tw2 walker impl is in flight as of this bead's filing. If this investigation recommends revisiting the marker shape, the walker impl should pause or accommodate the revisit. Coordinate via bead notes.
close_reason: Findings doc: ai/findings/sensitive-vs-large-technique-20260513-1823.md. Recommendation: (a) keep rf2-hmmx7's separate-markers lock-in. Trust-boundary semantics genuinely diverge — sensitive needs drop-and-forget defence-in-depth (no path/handle on wire); large needs fetchable marker. Handle is redundant for sensitive (the survived-event vs vanished-event filter has no useful intermediate state where a handle helps). No amendment beads filed; rf2-v9tw2 walker impl does NOT need to pause — current cascade is correct.

title: story-mcp: integrate de-dupe at runtime boundary (rf2-obpa9 parity)
description:
Follow-on from rf2-obpa9 (PR #662). pair2-mcp now integrates day8/de-dupe at the wire boundary with ~89.5% reduction on typical 10-epoch workloads. story-mcp has analogous wire surfaces (epoch records via variant playback, trace events via the recorder loop) that could benefit from the same treatment.

Scope: tools/story-mcp/src/ + tools/story-mcp/spec/Principles.md (the latter currently doesn't have a structural-dedup mechanism — add one aligned to pair2-mcp's wording from rf2-obpa9).

Likely surfaces to dedup: variant snapshot returns, trace-emission in recorder loop, any other large-payload tool returns.

Wire vocabulary: same :rf.mcp/dedup-table reserved key (cross-MCP per causa-mcp mechanism #5 from rf2-lwgg8).

Dep: requires the same day8/de-dupe dep that pair2-mcp now carries (or its .cljc port from rf2-nw6sj if JVM reach is needed — story-mcp may have JVM surfaces).

Source: rf2-obpa9 PR #662 cross-MCP follow-on observation.
notes: Blocked on rf2-nw6sj (day8/de-dupe .cljc port). story-mcp is JVM (.cljc → .clj canonical deploy via /tools/story-mcp/deps.edn :main re-frame.story-mcp.server); day8/de-dupe pinned at SHA a2be701d is CLJS-only — uses js/Map. in count-cacheable-elements / create-cache-internal. Verified the latest master is the same SHA — no JVM reach yet. Branch worktree did pre-flight (git pull, bd show, surface read of tools/pair2-mcp/src/re_frame_pair2_mcp/tools.cljs + tools/story-mcp/src/re_frame/story_mcp/tools.cljc + Principles.md across all three MCPs) and prepared the design, but stopped before deps.edn change because there is nothing the integration can usefully do until de-dupe runs on JVM. rf2-nw6sj is the prerequisite — landing that (or its proxy: an in-tree port under tools/story-mcp/src/de_dupe/core.clj) unblocks this work. The wire vocabulary (:rf.mcp/dedup-table), opt-out arg (:dedup), and Principles wording are ready to apply once dedup runs.

title: SSR: per-request frame teardown audit + memory-leak load test
description:
Per ssr-audit-20260513-1757 findings (rf2-ul137) gap #6. Spec 011's flow diagram (§84–114) ends with 'destroy-frame' but neither spec nor impl pins what happens at teardown to:

1. Pending :dispatch-later calls (Spec 005 says :after is no-op under SSR, but what about non-machine :dispatch-later?).
2. The trace ring buffer (does it accumulate per-request? Per-frame?).
3. Error-trace pending buffer (the pending-error-traces atom in ssr.cljc is keyed by frame-id — does destroy-frame clear it?).
4. add-watch registrations on the frame's atom (the ssr-subscribe-container in ssr.cljc:404 adds watches with (gensym "rf-ssr-sub-") — never explicitly removed).
5. on-dispose callbacks (interop.clj has an on-dispose-callbacks atom keyed by ratom — does destroy-frame fire callbacks?).
6. Scheduled timers (interop.clj uses ScheduledExecutorService — pending timers survive frame destruction).

## Reproduction
A long-running server process serving 1000 SSR requests/sec — does memory grow? Unknown. No load test.

## Recommendation
Two-part:

A) **Audit pass**. Walk every per-request allocation site and document:
   - What allocates.
   - What teardown clears it.
   - What remains after destroy-frame.

B) **Load test**. Add a JVM-side load harness:
   - Spin up an SSR loop that makes 10,000 requests synthetically.
   - Snapshot heap before and after.
   - Assert frame-count, watch-count, trace-buffer-size, pending-error-trace-keys all return to baseline.
   - Run as part of CI optionally; gate as :extra-test alias.

## Surfaces touched
- implementation/ssr/test/re_frame/ssr_leak_test.clj (new)
- implementation/core/src/re_frame/frame.cljc (destroy-frame may need additions)
- implementation/ssr/src/re_frame/ssr.cljc (pending-error-traces cleanup hook)
- spec/011-SSR.md (per-request teardown contract addition — currently absent)

## Acceptance
- Audit doc lists every per-request allocation + its teardown.
- Load test runs 10K SSR requests; heap returns to baseline (within tolerance).
- destroy-frame clears pending-error-traces entry + removes the gensym'd watch + fires on-dispose callbacks.

## Severity rationale
A slow leak under prod load is the kind of bug that ships SSR-broken. Better to find it before users do.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/712 — Found and fixed two leaks in re-frame.ssr's defonce side-channel atoms (pending-error-traces, request-slots). Both released via new :ssr/on-frame-destroyed late-bind hook wired into frame/destroy-frame!. Load test (2000 SSR requests, ^:slow): 0.6 MB heap delta, 313 bytes/req retained, ~9K req/s, side-channel atoms return to baseline. Verified the test catches the leak by temporarily disabling the hook (failed with 2100 leaked request-slots + 50 leaked pending-error-traces). Spec 011 gets a new §Per-request frame teardown contract subsection.
close_reason: Closed

title: SSR: spec the form-action POST handling pattern — SOTA parity with Next/Remix actions
description:
Per ssr-audit-20260513-1757 findings (rf2-ul137) gap #7. Both Remix (actions exports) and Next.js (Server Actions) make form POST handling a first-class SSR concern with progressive enhancement (forms work without JS, server validates, re-renders with form-state or redirects).

re-frame2's Pattern-Forms.md (per Spec 000-Vision §483) covers client-side forms; no spec addresses the 'form posts to /article/new → server dispatches :create-article → response is either :rf.server/redirect to /articles/123 OR the same form re-rendered with validation errors' pattern.

The pieces exist (events, :rf.server/redirect, error projector, dispatch, form schemas). What's missing is the normative pattern doc + the form-POST → dispatch wiring.

## Sketches

Spec-side: spec/Pattern-SSR-Forms.md (or extend Pattern-Forms.md with an SSR section).
- 'A form's action attribute points to an event. The host adapter parses POST bodies, dispatches the named event with the form-data as args.'
- 'Validation errors land in app-db's form slice; the server re-renders with the same form state, no redirect.'
- 'Success cases dispatch :rf.server/redirect; the host adapter emits a 302 Location response with no body.'
- 'Progressive enhancement: forms work without JS because the server response is a full HTML page; client-side hydration adds re-validation + optimistic UI on top.'

Host-adapter-side (depends on rf2-NEW-1):
- POST body → application/x-www-form-urlencoded or multipart/form-data → Clojure map.
- :form-action route metadata → 'on POST to this route, dispatch :event-id with body as the arg.'

## Surfaces touched
- spec/Pattern-SSR-Forms.md (new) OR spec/Pattern-Forms.md (extension)
- implementation/ssr-ring/ (POST body parsing + form-action dispatch)
- spec/conformance/fixtures/ssr-form-action.edn (new)
- spec/conformance/fixtures/ssr-form-validation-error.edn (new)

## Dependencies
- rf2-NEW-1 (host adapter) — POST body parsing lives there.
- Coordinates with Spec 010 Schemas (form validation) and Spec 014 HTTP requests (for context).

## Acceptance
- A form with action='/articles' method='POST' submits to the host adapter; the framework dispatches :articles/create with the form data.
- On success, :rf.server/redirect causes a 302 response.
- On validation failure, the same form re-renders with errors from app-db.
notes: PR #685 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/685
close_reason: Closed

title: SSR: ship reg-head / render-head / active-head — head/meta contract sits in spec, no impl
description:
Per ssr-audit-20260513-1757 findings (rf2-ul137) gap #5. Spec 011 §425–519 documents the head/meta contract in normative-looking prose with a deferral banner at §427 pointing to rf2-gr0n. Impl is absent. The :ssr/head-emits conformance fixture is aspirational. SEO meta tags must be hand-rolled per-app today — examples/reagent/ssr/core.cljc just emits <title>SSR demo</title> in the HTML shell.

## What the spec promises
- reg-head registers a :head registry kind, a fn (db, route) → head-model.
- Routes name which head to use via :head route metadata.
- render-head computes the head model for a frame.
- active-head sugar — looks up the active route's :head, calls render-head.
- The head model is data: {:title :meta :link :script :json-ld :html-attrs :body-attrs}.
- Runtime emits canonical-ordered tags from the model.
- Default head fallback when no route declares :head.
- Mismatch detection: head-vs-body piggy-backs on the unified :rf/render-hash via :failing-id discriminator.

## What ships today
- The :failing-id :rf.ssr/head-mismatch discriminator (verify-hydration! supports it, tested in ssr_end_to_end_test.clj ssr-head-hash-mismatch).
- Nothing else.

## Recommendation
Land impl in re-frame.ssr (or a sub-ns re-frame.ssr.head). Surfaces:
- reg-head — registers under :head kind.
- render-head — computes the head-model map.
- active-head — sugar.
- Default head fallback fn.
- SSR emitter integration: when render-to-string is called and a frame's active route declares :head, the emitter wraps body output with <head>...</head> from the model.

## Dependencies
- Routing (Spec 012) already integrates — head is a route metadata key.

## Surfaces touched
- spec/011-SSR.md §Head/meta contract (remove deferral banner; promote to live).
- spec/API.md (head registry kind, three new public fns).
- implementation/ssr/src/re_frame/ssr.cljc (or new sub-ns).
- spec/conformance/fixtures/ssr-head-emits.edn (mark as live — runs).
- spec/conformance/fixtures/ssr-head-hydration.edn (mark as live).

## Acceptance
- (rf/reg-head :head/article (fn [db route] {:title (str ...) :meta [...]})) registers.
- Routes with :head :head/article emit the model's tags in canonical order in <head>.
- Default head fallback fires when no :head declared.
- Head-mismatch trace (already wired) covers the round-trip.

## Severity rationale (P2 not P1)
Hand-rolled head emission works today (each app writes its own <head> shell). But spec promises a surface that's not there — and SEO is a real concern for any production SSR app.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/724
close_reason: Closed

title: SSR: streaming primitive — TTFB optimisation / SOTA parity with Next/Remix defer
description:
Per ssr-audit-20260513-1757 findings (rf2-ul137) gap #4. Spec 011 §735–751 explicitly defers streaming SSR as out-of-initial-scope, naming the implementation sketch but not committing to a primitive. Next.js (Suspense + loading.js), Remix (defer), Solid (Suspense), Astro (server islands) all ship some form of progressive HTML chunking — re-frame2 doesn't.

Real-world TTFB is the main win: render the shell + above-fold content fast (under 200ms), stream in the slow-loading sections as their data resolves. Without it, an article-page that fetches comments serially blocks the entire HTML response on the slowest fetch.

## Spec 011's sketch (§741–746)
1. Render the shell (everything that doesn't need data) via render-to-string and flush.
2. For each suspense-boundary subtree, register a 'pending' placeholder; dispatch the data-loading event; render the placeholder HTML and flush.
3. As each pending event resolves (drain settles for that piece), render the resolved subtree and stream its HTML.
4. Once all pending events settle, ship the final :rf/hydration-payload.

## Implementation costs (per spec)
- Chunked HTTP response handling (Transfer-Encoding: chunked).
- Framework integration with the host's streaming response API.
- Ordering of payload chunks vs body chunks.

## Surfaces that would change
- spec/011-SSR.md §Streaming SSR (move from open to specced).
- implementation/ssr/src/re_frame/ssr.cljc (or new ns re-frame.ssr.streaming) — partial-render emitter.
- implementation/ssr-ring/ (host adapter — chunked response wiring).
- spec/conformance/fixtures/ssr-streaming.edn (new fixture).
- 1 worked example.

## Dependencies
- rf2-NEW-1 (host adapter) — chunked response wiring lives there.
- Probably wants a new primitive: a 'suspense-boundary' marker in hiccup the emitter recognises and treats as a deferred subtree.

## Acceptance
- An :on-create event spawns 3 fetch actors; the emitter flushes the shell immediately, placeholder DIVs for each subtree, then streams each subtree as its actor resolves.
- HTML output is chunked; browser displays shell within 200ms of request.
- Hydration payload arrives last; client hydrates against the now-complete server state.
- Conformance fixture pins the chunk ordering + final hash equality.

## Notes
- This is the spec-level commitment Spec 011 §739 says 'an implementation that wants streaming can layer it on the existing emitter without framework changes' — but we want a NORMATIVE pattern, not a per-app DIY.

title: Causa suppressed-sensitive-count: make reactive container so REDACTED indicator updates immediately
description:
Follow-on from rf2-s2bhn PR #660. The :rf.causa/suppressed-sensitive-count sub thunks a plain atom (config/suppressed-count) which is outside the reactive sub-graph. Effect: the bottom-rail [● REDACTED N] indicator only re-renders when SOMETHING ELSE in the sub-graph recomputes (e.g. next dispatch). Bumping the counter doesn't immediately drive a re-render.

The Playwright spec had to force a host dispatch to drive the indicator's re-render in scenario 6 — workable but indicates a reactivity gap.

Fix: replace plain-atom with a reactive container (likely r/atom or registered as a re-frame sub-graph root) so the indicator becomes a first-class observable. The current pattern works because suppression and dispatches typically coincide, but it's a latent bug if suppression happens out-of-band.

Scope: tools/causa/src/day8/re_frame2_causa/config.cljc — change the suppressed-count atom to a reactive form + update the sub registration in registry.cljs to subscribe properly.

Tests: extend rf2-3lh6h shell tests (already in main) to confirm bump → immediate indicator re-render without a separate dispatch.

Source: rf2-s2bhn PR #660 follow-on observation.
notes: PR #681 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/681
close_reason: Closed

title: Extract shared retro protocol leaf for cross-skill retrospect-style workflows
description:
Source: rf2-zf7zd investigation (ai/findings/improver-architecture-20260513-1752.md §The unified-improver-surface proposal).

## The unified surface

Three skills currently or potentially do "structured retro on a body of work":

1. `re-frame-pair-improver2` (today; soon-to-be-renamed re-frame-pair-retro2 per rf2-75z9d) — retro on pair2 session
2. `re-frame2-improver` (proposed in rf2-sqw80) — retro on re-frame2 code
3. `re-frame-story-improver` (future; only if rf2-1v7tu chooses the standalone-skill path) — retro on story authoring

All three share:

- Diagnosis-first workflow (reconstruct → timeline → friction → classify → improvements → prioritise)
- Evidence-based findings (cite concrete moments)
- Layer-routed fixes (friction in tool X → bead against X; framework friction → upstream bead)
- Opt-in bead drafting (never autonomous)
- Compact output shape (Goal / Observed / Causes / Improvements / Bolder / Bead candidates / Other)
- Pattern-matching against a domain catalogue
- Redaction discipline before filing

## Proposed extraction

Lift the workflow + output-shape + filing-discipline portions of `skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/SKILL.md` into a **shared cross-skill leaf**:

- Location: `skills/_shared/retro-protocol.md` (or equivalent)
- Scope (~250 lines): the 6-step diagnosis workflow; the evidence-vs-vibes discipline; the layer-routing rules; the opt-in bead protocol; the compact output sections; the redaction rules; the bolder-ideas-labelled discipline

Each retro skill then provides only its domain-specific material:

- domain catalogue (`known-frictions.md` for pair2; `anti-patterns.md` for re-frame2; `story-frictions.md` for story)
- domain-specific analysis lenses (today's `analysis-lenses.md` becomes a per-skill addendum on top of the shared protocol)
- domain-specific bead templates / target-repo routing

## Benefits

- Shared discipline across all retro skills
- New retro skills inherit the workflow; only the catalogue needs authoring
- Improvements to retro protocol (e.g. new prioritisation heuristic) propagate to all consumers
- Reduces per-skill SKILL.md size by ~80-100 lines

## Costs

- Cross-skill file references (skills don't currently share reference files; verify the agent host supports cross-directory `Read` for skill references)
- Loading the shared protocol adds context to every retro skill session

## Sequencing

Should land alongside or shortly after rf2-sqw80 (the re-frame2-improver skill) — easier to extract from one canonical reference (re-frame-pair-improver2) while building the second consumer, rather than refactoring after three independent copies drift.

## Cross-refs

- ai/findings/improver-architecture-20260513-1752.md §The unified-improver-surface proposal
- skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/SKILL.md (source of extraction)
- skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/spec/design.md L5-L8 (the locked decisions become the protocol's locks)
notes: PR #700 — shared leaf at skills/shared/retro-protocol.md; both SKILL.md loading-maps updated; left OPEN per dispatch instructions.
close_reason: Closed

title: Rename re-frame-pair-improver2 → re-frame-pair-retro2; soften activation precondition
description:
Source: rf2-zf7zd investigation (ai/findings/improver-architecture-20260513-1752.md §Angle 1 recommendation).

## Why rename

The skill's actual job is **retrospection** on a finished pair2 session. The word "improver" suggests the skill *makes* improvements (active) but the locked decision L2 explicitly forbids autonomous filing — the skill drafts bead text only on request. Renaming to `re-frame-pair-retro2` surfaces the discipline into the trigger fingerprint.

## Trigger-fingerprint benefit

Currently the skill risks misfiring when the user says generic phrases like "can you improve my code?" (which is an authoring concern, not a pair-tool retro concern). A name with "retro" in it makes the activation criterion clearer to Claude's routing layer: this is a *retrospective on a recent session*, not an active improver.

## Disambiguate from the proposed re-frame2-improver

If rf2-sqw80 is approved, having `re-frame-pair-improver2` + `re-frame2-improver` as siblings creates routing ambiguity ("which improver?"). `re-frame-pair-retro2` + `re-frame2-improver` are mutually distinct — one is post-session retrospection on a pair tool, the other is on-demand code-pattern critique on framework usage.

## Soften activation precondition

Currently the frontmatter precondition demands a concrete pair2 session in conversation. The SKILL.md body already mentions recap support (L60) — "ask for a recap or permission to use a longer conversation as input". Lift this acceptance into the frontmatter so user-supplied recaps activate the skill cleanly:

> Vocabulary matches alone do not justify activation — a real `re-frame-pair2` session must have occurred in this conversation **or be summarised by the user as a recap**.

## Scope of change

- Rename directory: `skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/` → `skills/re-frame-pair-retro2/`
- Update frontmatter `name:` field
- Update `description:` to use "retrospect" / "retrospection" instead of "improver"
- Update `skills/README.md` routing table + picking-the-right-one section
- Update all internal cross-refs (other skills referencing "improver2")
- Update npm package name (`package.json`)
- Update plugin metadata (`.claude-plugin/plugin.json`)
- Update spec/ docs in-place (no rename of internal terms beyond name field)

## Risk

Pre-alpha rename; no external consumers to break. The npm package can be republished under the new name.

## Cross-refs

- ai/findings/improver-architecture-20260513-1752.md §Angle 1
- skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/SKILL.md (current frontmatter)
- skills/README.md §Picking the right one + §Skill routing — single source
notes: PR #690 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/690
close_reason: Closed

title: [pair2-mcp] Wire :rf.size/large-elided into snapshot + get-path tools
description:
**Source**: ai/findings/declare-large-path-20260513-1748.md rec 4 (rf2-vnmt6).

Plug the elision walker (rf2-v9tw2) into the pair2-mcp wire path.

**Sites to wire**:

1. `tools/pair2-mcp/src/re_frame_pair2_mcp/tools.cljs` — the `snapshot` tool's `:app-db` slice path (after slice-app-db-in-snapshot, before the cap check) and the `get-path` tool's value path (after get-in, before pr-str).

2. New per-tool arg `:elision-policy {:include-large? false :include-sensitive? false :include-digests? false :max-bytes 16384}` with the defaults shown. Surfaces on the tool descriptor so agents can opt in.

3. Marker handle round-trip: confirm that passing `{:handle [:rf.elision/at <path>]}` from a marker back through `get-path` fetches the elided value. Document the handle vocabulary.

4. **Composition tests**:
   - Large + path-slice: drilling into a path that contains an elided child returns the marker for that child only; the rest of the tree fits in the response.
   - Large + diff-encode (rf2-1wdzp): a patch's :assoc slot pointing at a large value substitutes the marker (with :as-of-epoch qualifier on the handle).
   - Large + sensitive: drop (no marker emitted, :sensitive? true rides).
   - Large + cap (rf2-rvyzy): elision happens first; if the post-elision response still exceeds cap, the overflow marker fires.

5. **Spec update**: new §"Size-elision wire markers" in `tools/pair2-mcp/spec/Principles.md` parallel to the existing §"Diff-encoded :db-after on epoch slices" — same shape: motivation, marker shape, modes, cross-MCP vocabulary note pointing at `:rf.size/*`.

**Tests**: `tools/pair2-mcp/test/re_frame_pair2_mcp/elision_test.cljs` covering each composition above.

**Depends on**: rf2-v9tw2 (the walker itself).

**Hot zone**: Principles.md (sequential, never parallel).

**Acceptance**:
- snapshot + get-path consume the walker.
- New :elision-policy arg documented and surfaces in tools/list.
- All composition tests green.
- pair2-mcp Principles.md §Size-elision wire markers landed.
close_reason: Closed

title: [implementation] :rf.elision/* fx + rf/elide-wire-value walker + schema-driven boot population
description:
**Source**: ai/findings/declare-large-path-20260513-1748.md rec 3 (rf2-vnmt6).

Build the framework runtime layer for size-elision in implementation/core/.

**New namespace**: `implementation/core/src/re_frame/elision.cljc` (or similar) — keeps the elision concerns out of core.cljc.

**Surface**:

1. **Two fx symmetric with :rf.machine/spawn/destroy**:
   - `:rf.elision/declare-large {:path [...] :hint "..."}` — adds an entry to `[:rf/elision :declarations]` on the frame's app-db.
   - `:rf.elision/clear {:path [...]}` — removes it.

2. **Top-level convenience functions** (REPL/boot wiring):
   - `(rf/declare-large-path! path opts)` and `(rf/clear-large-path! path)` — dispatch synthetic events that invoke the fx. Pattern: same as how `rf/spawn-machine` wraps :rf.machine/spawn.

3. **The unified elision walker**: `(rf/elide-wire-value v {:keys [path frame include-sensitive? include-large? include-digests? max-bytes] :as policy})`. Returns either `v` or a value with `{:rf.size/large-elided {...}}` markers substituted at large subtrees. Predicates short-circuit (don't descend into elided subtrees).

4. **Schema-driven boot population**: at app start (or on `(rf/reg-app-schema ...)`), walk the registered app-schema and populate `[:rf/elision :declarations]` with `{:source :schema}` entries for every slot whose schema-metadata carries `:large? true`.

5. **Runtime auto-detect** (the option (d) entry): when the walker sees a value whose serialised size exceeds `max-bytes` AND there's no declaration for the path, add an entry to `[:rf/elision :runtime-flagged]` and emit one-shot `:rf.warning/runtime-large-elision` per path. Idempotent.

**Tests** (`test/re_frame/elision_test.cljc`):
- Restore-epoch correctness: declaring a large path at epoch 7, time-travelling back, sees the declaration.
- Atomic fx: a cascade that declares + writes + checks sees the declaration on the post-cascade db.
- Schema-driven population: registering an app-schema with two :large? true slots populates two declarations.
- Walker short-circuits: a 5MB nested map elides at the top, doesn't recurse.
- Sensitive + large composition: drop (no marker emitted), :sensitive? true rides.

**Depends on**: rf2-hmmx7 (spec contract), rf2-nwv63 (schema key).

**Hot zone awareness**: new namespace, not editing core.cljc — safe to parallel with other implementation work.

**Acceptance**:
- All five surface items shipped.
- Test suite green.
- Round-trips through restore-epoch verified.
notes: PR #668 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/668
close_reason: Closed

title: [spec/005 + spec/013] Machine and flow trace events MUST specify :sensitive? inheritance
description:
Audit rf2-ok47g §Completeness matrix found that Spec 005 (state machines) and Spec 013 (flows) have zero :sensitive? mention. Machine snapshots (:rf.machine/transition events) carry the post-transition snapshot which can include user-derived data; flow :output values can carry derived sensitive data.

The inheritance rule already exists in spec/009 line 1141: every trace event emitted within a handler scope picks up the in-scope handler's :sensitive? flag. Spec 005 and Spec 013 just need to cross-reference this and confirm machine-transition / flow-eval events ride the inheritance.

Required:
- spec/005-StateMachines.md adds a §Privacy subsection (or one-liner) noting :rf.machine/* events inherit :sensitive? from the surrounding handler scope per spec/009.
- spec/013-Flows.md adds the same note for :rf.flow/* events.
- Confirm the runtime implementation (once G1 lands) covers these emit sites.

Source: ai/findings/sensitive-elision-audit-20260513-1748.md §G8

title: [runtime] Expose public sensitive? predicate to deduplicate the consumer-side helper
description:
Audit rf2-ok47g §Elegance assessment found that all five consumers (Causa, Story, pair2 preload, pair2-mcp, story-mcp) reimplement the same predicate as a private helper:

  (and (map? ev) (true? (:sensitive? ev)))

Five copies of the same five-token predicate. The framework should publish 're-frame.trace/sensitive?' (or similar location on public API) so consumers compose against one line:

  (when-not (rf/sensitive? ev) ...)

Tiny, mechanical, removes drift risk if the spec ever extends the predicate (e.g. recognising a future :rf.privacy/* axis).

Source: ai/findings/sensitive-elision-audit-20260513-1748.md §G5
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/710
close_reason: Closed

title: [spec/Conventions] Document config-knob naming for sensitive-events suppression (show-sensitive vs include-sensitive)
description:
Audit rf2-ok47g §Elegance assessment found that the four consumers of the privacy gate use two different naming axes. On-box devtools UI consumers (Causa, Story) use ':trace/show-sensitive?' semantics ('the panel is for me, do I want to look'). Off-box LLM-egress consumers (pair2 preload, pair2-mcp, story-mcp, causa-mcp) use ':include-sensitive?' semantics ('do I cross the trust boundary'). The defaults are aligned (both default to suppress) but the verbs are not. Defensible but unspec'd — the sixth consumer will guess.

Required:
- One paragraph in spec/Conventions.md naming the convention: on-box panels use 'show-sensitive?' (UI verb, namespaced under :trace/); off-box wire egress uses 'include-sensitive?' (wire verb, bare).
- Cross-reference from spec/009-Instrumentation.md §Privacy.

Source: ai/findings/sensitive-elision-audit-20260513-1748.md §G6

title: Investigation: re-frame2 SSR implementation vs SOTA — gaps + pragmatic fills
description:
Audit the current SSR implementation in implementation/ssr/ against the normative spec/011-SSR.md AND against the SOTA in JS-land (React 18+, Next.js, Remix, Astro). Identify pragmatic gaps.

**Audit dimensions**:

1. **Spec-vs-impl alignment for SSR**: parallel to rf2-j41ya (Causa impl-vs-spec audit). Walk spec/011-SSR.md's normative claims and check the impl honours each. Surfaces:
   - Pure hiccup → HTML emitter (JVM-runnable)
   - Per-request frame lifecycle (frames created/destroyed per HTTP request)
   - Hydration-mismatch detection with structured error projection
   - :rf/hydrate event semantics
   - Cofx :platforms metadata gating (rf2-gey3d)
   - Effect handling on the server side (Spec 011 §634-642)
   - Streaming?
   - Suspense / progressive rendering?
   - Server-component-like patterns?

2. **SOTA comparison**:
   - **Next.js**: App Router, RSC (React Server Components), streaming SSR, partial hydration, Suspense boundaries, edge runtime. Which of these are re-frame2-shaped vs out-of-scope by design?
   - **Remix**: nested routes, server loaders/actions, defer for streaming, optimistic UI. Re-frame2 has frame-aware routing (Spec 012) — how does it compare?
   - **Astro**: islands architecture, partial hydration, content-collections, MDX. Re-frame2 isn't a static site framework but might learn from the islands approach for incremental hydration.
   - **Solid/Qwik**: resumability, fine-grained reactivity. Different model entirely.
   - The fundamental question: re-frame2 SSR is hiccup → HTML on the JVM, then hand to React for hydration. Where does that model SHINE (no React-on-server, JVM tooling, predictable trace bus) and where is it FUNDAMENTALLY weaker than React-on-server approaches?

3. **Pragmatic gaps** — what would actually trip a user trying to ship a re-frame2 SSR app today?
   - Async data loading per request (typical 'server fetch on render' pattern)
   - Per-request authentication context propagation
   - Streaming output (Transfer-Encoding: chunked) for fast TTFB
   - SEO concerns (meta tags, og: tags, dynamic per-route)
   - Sitemap generation
   - 404 / 500 / redirect handling on the JVM
   - File upload handling
   - Session management
   - CDN/edge-runtime compatibility
   - Image optimization
   - Per-request frame teardown (does it leak?)
   - Cookies / headers in the request → app-db cofx
   - Form action POST handling

4. **The mental-model question**: spec/011 says SSR 'doesn't require a different mental model'. Is that actually true given the gaps above? What concessions/extensions are needed to make the claim true under real workloads?

**Output**: ai/findings/ssr-audit-<timestamp>.md with:
- Spec-vs-impl alignment matrix (with verdicts)
- SOTA comparison table per feature
- Pragmatic gap list (numbered, prioritised)
- Recommendations: which gaps are P1 (blocks shipping), P2 (quality), P3 (polish)
- File beads per actionable gap

**Surfaces to read**: spec/011-SSR.md, implementation/ssr/ source + tests, examples/* for any SSR-style example, spec/000-Vision.md §Host-profile matrix for the server-side declarations, Spec 014 §HTTP requests for client-side composition. Read Next.js / Remix / Astro websites for SOTA reference if needed via WebFetch.
close_reason: Investigation complete. Findings doc at ai/findings/ssr-audit-20260513-1757.md. Filed 8 follow-up beads: rf2-ny6v7 (P1 host adapter), rf2-e825b (P1 :rf.server/request cofx), rf2-1xw9g (P1 parallel-loader pattern), rf2-olb64 (P2 streaming), rf2-4dra9 (P2 reg-head impl), rf2-zbvyd (P2 form-action), rf2-fcj33 (P2 teardown audit), rf2-69ad2 (P3 schema-digest/version fxs). Headline: SSR runtime mechanics solid + one-shottable, but the host-integration layer (Ring adapter + request cofx) is the gap that turns 'we have SSR' into 'shippable SSR'. Mental-model claim is 80% true.

title: Investigation: should re-frame-pair-improver merge into re-frame-pair? Should re-frame2 itself have an improver feature?
description:
**Mike's design question**: re-examine the improver-skill architecture.

**Three angles to investigate**:

1. **Merge improver into pair?** Currently re-frame-pair-improver2 is a sibling skill to re-frame-pair2 (separate SKILL.md, separate references/, separate routing). The meta-skill watches pair2 sessions and surfaces improvements to the pair2 tool itself.
   - Should this be subsumed into re-frame-pair2 directly? (One skill, two modes — pair-program mode + critique-the-tool mode.)
   - Tradeoffs: discoverability + reduced routing burden vs blurred boundary + larger skill payload + risk of confusing Claude's routing layer.
   - Recent precedent: the re-frame-pair-story-skill investigation (rf2-ncp5n) chose HYBRID — extend existing skills rather than spin new ones. Same logic might apply here in reverse: collapse instead of spawn.

2. **Should re-frame2 itself include an improver feature?** The base re-frame2 skill currently documents the framework. What if it ALSO observed how users code against the framework and suggested framework-level improvements?
   - Example: user writes 5 reg-event-fx with the same retry-pattern → improver suggests 'consider Managed HTTP'. User has no schemas anywhere → improver suggests Spec 010 adoption. User has manual loading-state flags → improver suggests Nine States pattern.
   - This is 'framework dogfooding via the skill that documents the framework' — the framework learns from how it's used.
   - Tradeoffs: scope creep, when-do-I-teach-vs-suggest tension, signal-to-noise (how loud should it be?).

3. **Trigger mechanism**: how do improver moments fire during a user session?
   - **End-of-session retrospective** (manual: 'review this session')
   - **Anti-pattern detection** (auto: skill notices a known smell, surfaces it inline)
   - **Periodic check-ins** ('you've been at this 30 min; want a review?')
   - **On error events** (post-mortem trigger after a stack trace or red CI)
   - **Mike asks** ('any improvements?' — explicit pull)
   - **Background passive** (skill remembers but only surfaces on demand)
   - Each has tradeoffs: interruption cost, false-positive rate, agent-context budget, user trust.

**Output**: ai/findings/improver-architecture-<timestamp>.md covering:
- Current state mapping (re-frame-pair-improver2 today + scope)
- Angle 1 analysis: merge improver into pair? Recommendation + tradeoffs.
- Angle 2 analysis: re-frame2 base improver feature? Recommendation + scope proposal.
- Angle 3 analysis: trigger mechanisms × surface (pair, re-frame2, story, etc). Recommend a small set.
- The unified 'observability + self-critique' surface that might emerge (sibling to rf2-vnmt6/rf2-ok47g elision-surface thinking, but for improvers rather than elision)
- File beads per actionable recommendation

**Surfaces to read**: skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/ (current improver), skills/re-frame-pair2/ (pair), skills/re-frame2/ (base framework skill), skills/re-frame2-setup/ (greenfield scope contrast), ai/findings/skills-mcp-interaction-20260513-1519.md (rf2-fvy7o audit of the skill matrix), ai/findings/re-frame-pair-story-skill-investigation-2026-05-13.md (rf2-ncp5n — the precedent decision).
close_reason: Investigation complete. Findings: ai/findings/improver-architecture-20260513-1752.md. Top recommendations per angle: (Angle 1) keep improver2 separate from pair2, rename to re-frame-pair-retro2; (Angle 2) yes — file a sibling re-frame2-improver skill with 6-pattern catalogue, narrow on-demand trigger only; (Angle 3) three canonical triggers — explicit pull, end-of-session-on-request, on-error (pair2 only). Reject inline interruption + periodic check-ins + auto-end-session detection. Unified-improver-surface: extract shared retro-protocol leaf across all retro-style skills. Cross-cutting designs inventory meta-bead filed to capture this + sibling investigations' wire-elision convergence. Beads filed: rf2-sqw80 (P1 decision), rf2-75z9d (P2 rename), rf2-dhe9v (P2 retro protocol), rf2-bquci (P3 catalogue), rf2-i7bvy (P3 meta inventory).

title: Investigation: audit how :sensitive? elides from trace — is the mechanism complete and elegant?
description:
**Mike's review request**: capture the current :sensitive? mechanism's design in a single findings doc, audit for completeness/elegance, and surface any gaps.

**Current state** (per rf2-a32kd implementation work this session):

**Declaration layer** (where the app says 'this is sensitive'):
- Registration-time metadata: ^{:sensitive? true} (reg-event-db :auth/login [...]) — entire registration is sensitive
- Per-event via with-redacted interceptor — payload-level redaction during dispatch
- Spec home: spec/009-Instrumentation.md §Privacy / sensitive data in traces (line 1149+)

**Wire layer** (how trace carries it):
- Trace events carry :sensitive? true at top level when emitted from a sensitive registration

**Suppression layer** (consumers gating it):
- Causa devtools panel (rf2-azls9, PR #636) — collect-trace! gate
- pair2 preload streaming (rf2-3cted, PR #631) — streaming-drop? predicate
- Story (rf2-bclgj, PR #632) — 4 listener-site gates + per-variant counter
- All 3 MCP servers (rf2-zq0n1, PR #628)
- Each has named config knob: include-sensitive? boolean

**Audit dimensions**:

1. **Completeness**: are there trace surfaces that DON'T currently honor :sensitive?
   - Epoch-level (rf2-re2s3 already filed for this)
   - Schema validation errors (do they expose values?)
   - HTTP request/response logging (Spec 014)
   - State machine snapshots
   - Flow trace events
   - Subscription cache state

2. **Elegance**: is the surface uniform across consumers?
   - Config knob naming: include-sensitive? vs show-sensitive? — are they aligned across all 4 consumers?
   - Wire shape uniformity: does every consumer see the same :sensitive? field shape?
   - The REDACTED indicator (rf2-azls9) — should every consumer have a parallel 'X suppressed' breadcrumb, or only some?

3. **Declaration ergonomics**:
   - Is the registration-metadata pattern (^{:sensitive? true}) discoverable?
   - Is with-redacted documented well enough for the per-payload case?
   - Could a schema-driven approach (:sensitive? in :rf/schema) be cleaner?
   - What about per-path declarations like the parallel rf2-vnmt6 large-path proposal?

4. **Composition with related mechanisms**:
   - :sensitive? + diff-encoded epochs (rf2-1wdzp) — does the diff layer respect the flag?
   - :sensitive? + path slicing (rf2-tygdv) — does get-path block sensitive paths?
   - :sensitive? + dedup (rf2-obpa9) — dedup table shouldn't leak the sensitive value via a reference

5. **The :sensitive? + :large? composition** (rf2-vnmt6 sibling): a value can be both sensitive AND large. Handling differs (redact vs elide-with-fetch). Should there be a unified declaration surface that says 'don't put this on the wire'?

**Output**: write findings doc ai/findings/sensitive-elision-audit-<timestamp>.md covering:
- Surface inventory (declaration + wire + suppression layers)
- Completeness matrix per trace surface
- Elegance gaps (naming, shape, indicator uniformity)
- Composition tests (with diff-encode, path slicing, dedup)
- Comparison to rf2-vnmt6 large-path mechanism — should they share a surface?
- File beads per actionable recommendation

**Surfaces to read**: spec/009-Instrumentation.md §Privacy, with-redacted interceptor in implementation/, the 4 :sensitive? PRs (#628 #631 #632 #636), tools/pair2-mcp/spec/Principles.md, tools/causa/spec/Principles.md, tools/story/src/.../config.cljc, skills/re-frame-pair2/preload/.../runtime.cljs configure-privacy!.
close_reason: Audit complete. Findings: ai/findings/sensitive-elision-audit-20260513-1748.md. Verdict: spec-layer mechanism is elegant and complete; implementation-layer has critical gap (runtime stamping not implemented — every consumer's filter is currently a no-op). Filed 9 follow-on beads: rf2-isdwf (P1 runtime stamping), rf2-kj51z (P1 schema-validation errors), rf2-bma05 (P1 HTTP), rf2-psrcx (P2 config-knob naming), rf2-sqxjn (P2 public predicate), rf2-ta2eh (P2 machines+flows), rf2-ltjz5 (P3 direct-read posture), rf2-st9aw (P3 with-redacted path forms), rf2-4onzx (P3 schema-driven declaration), rf2-2499j (P3 indicator vocab). Extended rf2-re2s3 (P3 -> P2 with epoch propagation rule) and rf2-obpa9 (filter-first ordering rule). Position on rf2-vnmt6 unified-elision-surface question: separate flags, shared mechanism — drop-vs-elide-with-fetch semantics genuinely diverge, but declaration ergonomics + wire shape + counter pattern should align.

title: Investigation: declare-large-path mechanism — flag big values in app-db; consumers get placeholder + on-demand slice
description:
**Mike's idea** from review of ai/findings/wire-protocol-bigapp-20260513-1541.md.

**Premise**: app-db sometimes holds large data — a binary blob downloaded for upload preview, a base64-encoded PDF, a multi-megabyte JSON payload from an external API. These don't NEED to appear in trace by default — but they DO bloat the wire (the same 290× problem rf2-jlq5j surfaced, just localized to a single value rather than the whole db).

**Proposed shape**: similar to :sensitive? privacy posture (rf2-a32kd):
- Application nominates 'large' paths/values via some declaration mechanism
- The trace bus + MCP wire layer detects the flag and substitutes a placeholder
- Consumers (Causa, MCP, skills) see something like {:rf.size/large-elided {:path [:user :uploads :pdf] :bytes ~5000000 :type :blob :digest 'sha256:...'} } in place of the value
- Per-consumer config: 'show full' opt-in, or 'fetch specific slice' on demand (via get-path or equivalent)

**Open design questions** (this bead is to investigate, not implement):

1. **How does the application nominate 'large' sections?**
   - (a) Registration-time metadata: ^{:rf/large-path? [:user :uploads]} (reg-event-db ...) — declares paths a handler writes that may be large
   - (b) Per-value wrapping: ^:rf/large metadata on the value at write time (event handlers tag values they produce)
   - (c) Schema-driven: Malli :rf/schema declares :large? true on a sub-tree
   - (d) Runtime size detector: trace bus measures (pr-str) length of each writeen value, auto-flags over N bytes (cheap heuristic, no app-side declaration needed)
   - (e) Hybrid: runtime auto-flag + app-side overrides
   - Each has tradeoffs — ergonomics, false-positives, schema-coupling

2. **Where does the metadata live?**
   - Per-path: a registry of {path -> {:large? true :hint ...}} maintained by the app via a fx
   - In app-db itself: {:rf.size/declarations {[:user :uploads] {:large? true}}} — self-describing
   - Per-event: in the trace event's :tags slot
   - In the framework's app-db meta (separate from data)

3. **What's the placeholder shape on the wire?**
   - Match :rf.mcp/* vocabulary: {:rf.size/large-elided {:path :bytes :type :digest}}
   - Should it carry a cursor/handle for fetching the actual value?

4. **Composition with diff-encode (rf2-1wdzp) + path slicing (rf2-tygdv)?**
   - rf2-tygdv path slicing already lets agents drill into a subtree; but the default summary shape doesn't currently distinguish 'small value not shown' from 'large value elided'
   - rf2-1wdzp diff-encoding produces patches; if a patch points at a large value, same elision should apply

5. **Sensitive (rf2-a32kd) vs Large**: these are orthogonal flags but compositional — a value can be both. The handling differs: sensitive = redact + suppress by default; large = elide + on-demand-fetch.

**Output**: write findings doc ai/findings/declare-large-path-<timestamp>.md covering:
- Survey of the 5 design options (a-e)
- Recommendation with rationale
- Wire-shape proposal (likely :rf.size/* keyword family — confirm cross-MCP coordination)
- Metadata-home recommendation
- Composition with :sensitive? + diff-encode + path slicing
- File beads per actionable recommendation

**Surfaces to read**: rf2-jlq5j findings doc, rf2-a32kd implementation (for the :sensitive? template), tools/pair2-mcp/spec/Principles.md (for cross-MCP vocabulary), spec/009-Instrumentation.md §Privacy section.
close_reason: Investigation complete. Findings at ai/findings/declare-large-path-20260513-1748.md. 6 follow-on beads filed: rf2-hmmx7 (P1 spec :rf/elision + :rf.size/large-elided wire marker), rf2-nwv63 (P1 :large? schema-meta key), rf2-v9tw2 (P2 fx + walker + schema-driven boot in implementation/core), rf2-urjnc (P2 wire into pair2-mcp snapshot+get-path), rf2-knshj (P3 causa-mcp 6th wire mechanism), rf2-123y5 (P3 runtime-auto-detect warning + skill docs). Top recommendation: hybrid (d)+(c)+(a) nomination with per-path registry in app-db at :rf/elision; wire marker :rf.size/large-elided with :handle field that round-trips through existing get-path tool. The walker is the unification point with the sibling rf2-ok47g :sensitive? work — both predicates over the same wire-boundary pipeline.

title: Cross-tree leak: Edit/Write tool intermittently writes to main checkout instead of agent worktree
description:
Recurring infrastructure pattern observed all session. When agents run with isolation: worktree (via the Agent tool's worktree mode), their Edit and Write tool calls SOMETIMES write to the main repo checkout (C:/Users/miket/code/re-frame2) instead of (or in addition to) the agent's isolated worktree (.claude/worktrees/agent-<id>).

Symptoms observed multiple times this session:
- After dispatch, git status on main shows modified files that the agent intended for its worktree only
- The agent's worktree DOES have its work too — the leak is duplicate, not replacement
- Several agents detected the leak themselves and reverted main (e.g. rf2-bba4s, rf2-3pnob, rf2-t84ye, rf2-iz6si all explicitly mentioned this in their reports)
- One agent (rf2-ccnp) switched the main checkouts BRANCH to its worktree branch via path-resolution confusion — required manual git checkout main recovery
- Another agent (rf2-queq0) reported the Edit/Write tools silently NO-OPing on Windows worktrees: the tool reported success, Read showed the edit, but git status and on-disk inspection showed no actual change. Worked around with PowerShell System.IO.File WriteAllText for direct filesystem writes.

Likely root cause (best guess from agent reports): the Edit/Write tool path resolution doesnt reliably scope to the worktree CWD when invoked via background Agent tool with isolation: worktree. The Windows-specific NO-OP symptom suggests path handling specifically with backslashes or drive-letter prefixes.

Workarounds in use:
- Agents detect the leak themselves and revert main, re-apply in worktree
- Some agents fall back to PowerShell for direct filesystem writes
- The mayor periodically reverts leaks on main before committing

Investigation needed:
- Is this a Claude Code harness bug? An OS-specific path-resolution issue? A subtlety of git worktree on Windows?
- Are mac/linux dispatches affected, or just Windows?
- The Edit tools old_string-validation should fail if the file content differs from the worktrees copy — that it succeeds AND writes to main suggests the tool is reading and writing different files in those cases

Severity: medium-low operational. Doesnt lose work (worktree has the canonical copy; main is duplicate). Costs cleanup time + risks accidental commit-on-main of partial agent work.

Mitigation ideas (catalogue, not all in scope):
- Pre-commit hook on main that warns if changes look like agent-worktree leaks
- Loop-prompt that periodically reverts mains working tree to HEAD when dirty with files matching agent worktree branches
- Agent prompts could explicitly run pwd before every Edit to verify worktree CWD
- File a claude-code-guide issue if root-caused to harness

Source: observed continuously across the 2026-05-13 session, ~10 agents reporting the pattern.

title: test(causa): add perf-budget tests — assert spec/007-UX-IA §Performance budget invariants
description:
Per rf2-otcbz findings doc, recommendation #7.

`tools/causa/spec/007-UX-IA.md` §Performance budget (L602) declares: *'opening Causa must not change observable INP on a relevant module.'* The spec lists:

  - Rendering caps at <200 rows per panel.
  - Causality graph caps at the last 200 dispatches.
  - O(visible rows) badge derivation (spec/012-Subscriptions.md L362).
  - Chain walk O(depth), capped at 8 layers.

**No test enforces any of these budgets.** The repo's general `npm run test:perf-bundle` probes the framework's perf-API bundle presence (`performance.mark` / `performance.measure` strings), not Causa render budgets.

Add `tools/causa/test/day8/re_frame2_causa/perf_budget_cljs_test.cljc` that seeds Causa's buffer to 1000 events (the trace-bus depth) and asserts:

  - `causality_graph_helpers/project-cascades-to-graph` completes in O(n) under stress.
  - The performance panel's `tier-counts` / `over-budget-count` aggregation handles 200 dispatch records without quadratic blowup.
  - `:rf.causa/event-detail` composite returns within a documented millisecond budget.
  - Subscription badge derivation is O(visible rows), not O(total subs).

JVM-runnable so the algebra runs under the JVM target (the `.cljc` shape mirrors `causality_graph_cljs_test.cljc`).

Source: rf2-otcbz findings doc at `ai/findings/causa-test-coverage-20260513-1706.md`, recommendation #7.
notes: PR #656 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/656
close_reason: Closed

title: test(causa): add mount.cljs tests — open/close/toggle/teardown state machine + missing-adapter gate
description:
Per rf2-otcbz findings doc, recommendation #6.

`tools/causa/src/day8/re_frame2_causa/mount.cljs` (123 LoC) has six untested public fns: `mounted?`, `visible?`, `open!`, `close!`, `toggle!`, `teardown!`.

A noteworthy failure mode is silent: `open!` is gated on `(substrate-adapter/current-adapter)` — if the adapter is unset, the call silently returns nil and the next Ctrl+Shift+C does nothing. No test exercises this.

Add `tools/causa/test/day8/re_frame2_causa/mount_cljs_test.cljs` covering the state machine against a stub adapter:

  - Before any call: `mounted?` false, `visible?` false.
  - First `open!`: creates a `<div id="rf-causa-root">` under `document.body`, invokes adapter render, sets visible.
  - Second `open!`: no re-render; just flips display back to block.
  - `close!` after open: container display = none; mount-state retained.
  - `toggle!` round-trip: open → close → open.
  - `teardown!`: unmount fn invoked, DOM node removed, singleton cleared.
  - `open!` with no installed substrate adapter: returns nil, no DOM node created, no state mutation.

Source: rf2-otcbz findings doc at `ai/findings/causa-test-coverage-20260513-1706.md`, recommendation #6.
notes: PR #663 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/663
close_reason: Closed

title: test(causa): add keybinding.cljs tests — key predicates + idempotency sentinel
description:
Per rf2-otcbz findings doc, recommendation #5.

`tools/causa/src/day8/re_frame2_causa/keybinding.cljs` (115 LoC) has untested logic:

  - `causa-toggle-key?` (Ctrl+Shift+C) and `copilot-toggle-key?` (Ctrl+Shift+/) — pure functions over synthetic event maps. Trivially testable; both untested.
  - The `attached?` defonce sentinel — the contract preventing double-attach across shadow-cljs `:after-load` reloads. A known footgun (the comment in attach! calls it out) that should carry a regression test.

Add `tools/causa/test/day8/re_frame2_causa/keybinding_cljs_test.cljs` covering:

  - `causa-toggle-key?` true for {ctrl, shift, key=C / c / KeyC}; false for missing modifier, extra modifier (meta, alt), or wrong key.
  - `copilot-toggle-key?` true for {ctrl, shift, key=/ / ? / Slash}; false otherwise.
  - `attach!` is idempotent — calling twice attaches one listener (assert via the `attached?*` introspection helper).
  - `detach!` round-trips (sentinel flips back to false, re-attach is possible).

Source: rf2-otcbz findings doc at `ai/findings/causa-test-coverage-20260513-1706.md`, recommendation #5.
notes: PR #659 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/659 (14 deftests / 44 assertions; suite 1626→1640, 4478→4522)
close_reason: Closed

title: test(causa): add Playwright spec for shell mount + Ctrl+Shift+C toggle + panel handoff
description:
Per rf2-otcbz findings doc, recommendation #4.

Causa has **zero browser-test or Playwright spec coverage** despite being a UI tool. `tools/causa/src/day8/re_frame2_causa/preload.cljs` is wired into the `:examples/counter` build's `:devtools/:preloads` block — but no automated test asserts that:

  - The shell mounts when Ctrl+Shift+C is pressed.
  - The `[data-testid="rf-causa-shell"]` element appears in the DOM.
  - Clicking each sidebar item updates the canvas to that panel's view.
  - A second Ctrl+Shift+C hides the shell (CSS-only toggle per spec/007-UX-IA.md).

This is the only path that proves the full mount → keybinding → substrate-adapter render → shell hiccup tree → panel routing pipeline works end-to-end. Node-test alone cannot exercise it (no DOM, no substrate render).

Add `examples/reagent/counter/causa.spec.cjs` driving:

  - Open `/counter/`, press `Ctrl+Shift+C`, assert `[data-testid="rf-causa-shell"]` exists.
  - Click each `[data-testid^="rf-causa-sidebar-item-"]`, assert the canvas content updates.
  - Press `Ctrl+Shift+C` again, assert the shell's container is `display: none` (per mount.cljs).

Mirror the existing `counter.spec.cjs` Playwright setup at the same path. The bundle-isolation contract (tools/README.md) is not violated — the preload is already in the counter example's :devtools block, and Playwright specs run against the dev build.

Source: rf2-otcbz findings doc at `ai/findings/causa-test-coverage-20260513-1706.md`, recommendation #4.
notes: PR #660 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/660. Spec landed under examples/reagent/counter/causa.spec.cjs with all six scenarios (mount + toggle + panel handoff + toggle-off + live trace bus + REDACTED indicator). Shipped with skip: awaiting rf2-fn5rk (filed P1 — Reagent adapter render slot calls rdc/render with raw DOM element instead of a Root from create-root; Causa's mount/open! throws root.render is not a function). Drop the skip: field as part of rf2-fn5rk's PR.
close_reason: Closed

title: Causa spec: [● REDACTED N] redaction indicator UI is unspec'd
description: Investigation rf2-j41ya found the bottom-rail [● REDACTED N] indicator (tools/causa/src/day8/re_frame2_causa/shell.cljs L318–351 + the per-frame counter in config.cljc L123–174) has no spec home. It's load-bearing — without it, sensitive cascades vanish silently — but no spec text says where it renders, what its tooltip is, or that it exists at all. title: Causa spec: MCP-server panel is fully impl-spec'd with three admitted spec-deficient decisions
description: Investigation rf2-j41ya found the Causa-side MCP-server panel (tools/causa/src/day8/re_frame2_causa/panels/mcp_server.cljs 485 LOC + mcp_server_helpers.cljc 394 LOC) has no spec home. tools/causa/spec/010-MCP-Server.md describes the *server* (the causa-mcp jar) but not the in-Causa panel surface. The impl carries three explicit 'INFERENTIAL DECISIONS' (rf2-81qjj — spec-deficient bead): (a) sidebar y/n → impl picks yes; (b) origin colour for :causa-mcp → impl picks #06B6D4 cyan-500; (c) bidirectional Causa→agent surface → impl picks out-of-scope. Plus the proposed cross-panel :rf.causa/mcp-origin-filter-enabled? dimming is impl-only. Recommendation: extend 010-MCP-Server.md with §Panel-side consumer, OR add tools/causa/spec/014-MCP-Panel.md. Lock all three inferential decisions and the cross-panel dim toggle. Findings: ai/findings/causa-impl-spec-gap-20260513-1705.md Gap #4.

title: Causa spec: hydration-debugger bisector path walker is impl-only
description: Investigation rf2-j41ya found the parallel-tree-walk bisector (tools/causa/src/day8/re_frame2_causa/panels/hydration_debugger_helpers.cljc L257–301 — first-divergence-path) is not derivable from the current spec. tools/causa/spec/006-Hydration-Debugger.md §Render-tree hash bisector describes the principle (1 paragraph + visual chips) but not the walking algorithm: descend matching children; record [path-as-vector-of-int-indices] to the first divergent slot; bail at differing tag; handle child-count mismatch. The impl uses pr-str as a content-equality stand-in (L228–247) for the hash; spec assumes a real FNV-1a hash from Spec 011. Recommendation: 006-Hydration-Debugger.md should grow an §Algorithm section pinning the walker semantics + the hash-stand-in fallback. Findings: ai/findings/causa-impl-spec-gap-20260513-1705.md Gap #3 (hydration slice).
notes: PR #676 — Algorithm section added to tools/causa/spec/006-Hydration-Debugger.md (+252 LoC, 11 MUSTs). Section pins: input model, parallel-pre-order bisector walk with pseudo-code, hiccup tree representation + attrs-skipped invariant, content-equality stand-in for FNV-1a hash, runtime :first-diff-path override priority, hiccup-is-a-tree cycle handling, five-kind divergence classification, chip-list derivation, source-coord resolution per Lock #11, performance bound, rendering contract. Parallel to rf2-b1jgy + rf2-9sdgc; matches their voice.

title: Investigation: Causa impl vs spec — is the spec complete enough?
description:
Audit tools/causa/src against tools/causa/spec to identify spec gaps. Specifically:

**Question being asked**: Does the Causa spec describe everything the impl does — to the level where a sufficiently capable AI could one-shot the Causa impl from the spec alone? Per the project's foundational thesis (README: 'the spec is the artefact; the code is downstream'), the impl should be a *consequence* of the spec, not the source of truth. Any feature in the impl that isn't normatively in the spec is a gap.

**Surfaces to map**:

1. **Panel-by-panel impl-to-spec mapping**: For each of the 13 panels under tools/causa/src/day8/re_frame2_causa/panels/:
   - Does the spec name this panel?
   - Does the spec describe its purpose, contract, data sources (which trace events / which subscriptions consumed), and rendering behaviour?
   - Are the panel's events under :rf.causa/* declared in the spec? (Spec 009 instrumentation, or causa-specific spec docs?)
   - Edge cases — empty, error, sensitive-redacted — are these handled in the spec?

2. **Cross-cutting impl features to check against spec**:
   - The trace-bus collector (collect-trace! gate; :sensitive? suppression added under rf2-azls9)
   - The Causa registry conventions (:rf.causa/* prefix per Spec Conventions)
   - The editor-scheme contract for click-to-source (just gained :windsurf + :zed via rf2-queq0)
   - The Causa shell.cljs sidebar/canvas wiring
   - The causality engine — its algorithm, the graph derivation rules
   - The privacy posture integration (suppress-sensitive flag + :rf.causa/suppressed-sensitive-count indicator)
   - The MCP server panel (causa-mcp is spec-only currently; the panel-side consumer hookup)
   - The AI co-pilot rail (Phase 5 panel)
   - The schema-violation timeline
   - The hydration debugger

3. **What spec docs exist today**: tools/causa/spec/ — list everything; map each spec doc to the impl areas it covers; flag impl areas with NO corresponding spec.

4. **The 'one-shot test'**: If you only had access to tools/causa/spec/ and the wider re-frame2 spec, could you reconstruct what tools/causa/src/ does? If not, what's missing?

**Output**: ai/findings/causa-impl-spec-gap-<timestamp>.md with:
  - Impl-feature inventory (what tools/causa/src/ actually does)
  - Spec-coverage matrix (each impl feature → covering spec section, or 'GAP')
  - Prioritised list of spec-completion recommendations
  - File a bead per actionable gap

Sibling investigation rf2-otcbz (test-coverage audit) is running in parallel and is **read-only just like this one**. Stay disjoint: read tools/causa/spec/ and tools/causa/src/ for prose comparison; don't touch test infrastructure (that's rf2-otcbz).
close_reason: Investigation complete. Findings at ai/findings/causa-impl-spec-gap-20260513-1705.md. Verdict: PARTIALLY one-shottable from spec. Spec corpus is structurally strong on per-panel surfaces and direction-set locks; weak on (1) load-bearing infrastructure (trace-bus, registry, epoch-pump, mount lifecycle); (2) algorithmic content (causality BFS layout, sub chain-walk, hydration bisector); (3) shipped impl features with no normative home (configure!, REDACTED indicator, MCP-panel inferential decisions). 11 follow-on beads filed: rf2-lc3hy P1 (trace-bus), rf2-cnw9q P1 (registry catalogue), rf2-b1jgy P2 (causality algorithm), rf2-9sdgc P2 (subs chain-walk), rf2-l0zu2 P2 (hydration bisector), rf2-vfyng P2 (MCP panel), rf2-imw8w P2 (configure! spec), rf2-a6buk P2 (REDACTED indicator), rf2-yp92j P3 (epoch-pump), rf2-9kkrm P3 (mount lifecycle), rf2-qictc P3 (reserved keys), rf2-x2d7c P3 (schema flash-cue), rf2-ovu4i P3 (co-pilot streaming).

title: Investigation: Causa test coverage — is it sufficient?
description:
Audit the test coverage of tools/causa/ across its 13 first-class panels + supporting infrastructure. Specifically:

**Quantitative**: How many test files? How many deftests? How many assertions? Lines-of-code ratio test:src? Compare to a baseline (e.g. implementation/core/ for ratio reference).

**Per-panel coverage**: For each of the 13 panels (event-detail, time-travel, app-DB diff, causality, subscriptions, fx, trace, machines, flows, routes, performance, issues, schemas, hydration, mcp-server, copilot), does it have:
  - a *_helpers_cljs_test.cljc for the pure-data side?
  - a *_view_cljs_test.cljs for the view-tree side?
  - End-to-end trace-bus → panel-render coverage?
  - Edge case coverage (empty state, error state, large-data state, sensitive-event redaction)?

**Cross-cutting coverage**:
  - shell.cljs (panel wiring, sidebar, canvas routing)
  - registry.cljs (subs + events under :rf.causa/*)
  - trace_bus.cljc (the collector after rf2-azls9 sensitive-suppress)
  - config.cljc (editor schemes, suppress-sensitive flag)
  - causality engine (the graph derivation)
  - source-coord-stamping + click-to-source

**Gaps to look for**:
  - Panels with helpers tests but no view tests (or vice versa)
  - Pure logic tested but integration not (does trace-bus → causality → panel render actually flow?)
  - Untested error paths (panel renders nothing when subscriptions are nil, etc)
  - Browser-level tests vs JVM-level — is the balance right?
  - Performance/perf-budget tests for panels that handle large epoch counts

**Output**: Write findings to ai/findings/causa-test-coverage-<timestamp>.md with:
  - Per-panel coverage matrix
  - Cross-cutting coverage summary
  - Concrete gaps with priorities
  - Recommendations → bead each one

**Surfaces to read**: tools/causa/src/, tools/causa/test/, tools/causa/deps.edn (:test alias to confirm test entry points), tools/causa/spec/ if it has a testing principle, plus the CI workflow's Causa test job for ground truth on what runs in CI.
close_reason: Closed

title: pair2-mcp Principles: lift path slicing + dedup + :max-tokens + overflow marker from causa-mcp spec
description:
Follow-on from rf2-lwgg8 (PR #642). The causa-mcp Principles.md now declares 5 wire-protocol mechanisms with MUST wording:

1. Token budget cap (5K default + :max-tokens override + {:rf.mcp/overflow ...} marker)
2. Path slicing (:path EDN arg, tree-summary default)
3. Cursor pagination (:cursor + :limit + :next-cursor + :remaining)
4. Lazy summary (:mode :summary default + {:rf.mcp/summary ...} shape)
5. Structural dedup (day8/de-dupe substitution table + {:rf.mcp/dedup-table ... :items [{:rf.mcp/ref N} ...]} wire shape)

pair2-mcp's Principles.md §'Tight token budget per response' (per rf2-rvyzy PR #641) covers mechanism 1 but is SILENT on:
- (b) path slicing (mechanism 2)
- (e) structural dedup (mechanism 5)
- (a) :max-tokens per-call override (covered in rf2-rvyzy)
- (d) {:rf.mcp/overflow ...} marker shape (covered in rf2-rvyzy)

Alignment task: lift mechanisms 2 + 5 from causa-mcp's wording back into pair2-mcp's Principles.md. Establishes :rf.mcp/* as cross-server reserved vocabulary rather than causa-mcp-private. Should land AFTER the impl beads for those mechanisms ship (rf2-tygdv for path slicing, rf2-obpa9 for dedup) so the spec wording matches the implementation reality.

Source: rf2-lwgg8 PR #642 'Cross-MCP divergence worth an alignment bead'.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/721

title: [pair2-mcp] Per-MCP-session app-db caching keyed on root hash
description:
Source: rf2-jlq5j findings doc (ai/findings/wire-protocol-bigapp-20260513-1541.md), recommendation #9

Common pair2-mcp workflow: agent inspects state, dispatches one thing, inspects again. Second inspect is byte-identical to first when nothing else mutated. Today the wire pays full app-db cost twice.

## Scope

MCP server keeps per-session map `{<frame-id> {<app-db-hash> <last-sent-at>}}`. On each read of any tool that returns app-db (`snapshot`, future `get-path`):

1. Compute hash of result before serialising.
2. If hash matches last sent for this frame-id: return `{:ok? true :unchanged-since <last-sent-at> :hash <h>}` instead of full payload.
3. Else: serialise normally, record hash + timestamp.

Invalidation is implicit: any cascade producing a new app-db identity produces a new hash, busts the cache.

## Subtleties

- Agent has to understand the unchanged response. Document in spec/003 and skill ref.
- Path-sliced reads (rf2-tygdv) need their own subhash — cache per (frame-id, path-vec, hash).
- Cache state is per-session (per persistent socket); cleared on reconnect. Doesn't leak across MCP processes.

## Acceptance

- Cache key strategy documented.
- Smoke test: two snapshot calls on idle app return :unchanged-since on second.
- Smoke test: dispatch between calls busts cache.
- Spec/003-Tool-Catalogue documents the response shape.
- skill re-frame-pair2 ref documents agent-side handling.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/732
close_reason: Closed

title: [de-dupe] Port day8/de-dupe to .cljc for JVM reach (story-mcp adoption)
description:
Source: rf2-jlq5j findings doc (ai/findings/wire-protocol-bigapp-20260513-1541.md), recommendation #8

day8/de-dupe is CLJS-only (`src/de_dupe/core.cljs`). story-mcp is JVM Clojure (`tools/story-mcp/src/re_frame/story_mcp/tools.cljc`). For story-mcp to benefit from structural dedup on its `:app-db` / `:rendered-hiccup` payloads, de-dupe needs JVM reach.

## Scope

~150-line port. Substitute `js/Map` for `java.util.HashMap`. Move `src/de_dupe/core.cljs` to `src/de_dupe/core.cljc` with reader conditionals. Algorithm is platform-agnostic; the hash bucket store is the only platform-specific bit.

This is upstream work in day8/de-dupe, not in re-frame2. Bead owner: Mike Thompson (maintainer of de-dupe per the April 2026 commits).

## Cost

- Single PR to day8/de-dupe; ~150-line change.
- Bump de-dupe version from 0.2.2 to 0.3.0 (CLJS+CLJ now supported).
- Add JVM smoke tests in de-dupe's `test/` covering the same fixtures as CLJS.

## Unblocks

- rf2-obpa9 (de-dupe integration in pair2-mcp) is CLJS-only and already moves without this.
- Future story-mcp size work depends on this.

## Acceptance

- de-dupe 0.3.0 ships with CLJS+JVM support.
- Both implementations produce byte-identical compressed forms for the same input.
- Round-trip property test on both platforms.
notes:
Cross-repo PR opened: https://github.com/day8/de-dupe/pull/3 — ports day8/de-dupe to .cljc (CLJS + JVM). All 9 tests / 43 assertions pass on both platforms via clojure -M:test. Version bumped 0.2.2 -> 0.3.0. Algorithm unchanged; only the mutable hash-bucket store is platform-specific (js/Map on CLJS, java.util.HashMap on JVM, isolated behind bucket-get / bucket-set! / new-bucket-store helpers). cache-id-counter moved from def+set! (CLJS-only) to atom for JVM compat.

re-frame2-side change: deferred. tools/pair2-mcp/deps.edn pins day8/de-dupe via :git/sha a2be701 (pre-port commit). Once PR #3 merges, that sha needs bumping to the merge commit (or to a release tag if cut). Suggest folding the bump into rf2-90eft's first commit since story-mcp is the new consumer driving this — its deps.edn will need day8/de-dupe added anyway, pointing at the new sha. No re-frame2 PR opened in this bead because bumping before the upstream merge would point at a branch ref that disappears post-rebase.

Unblocks rf2-90eft: story-mcp can now :require de-dupe.core on JVM once PR #3 lands. Tools/pair2-mcp on CLJS already works (and continues to work — .cljc compiles to CLJS fine).
close_reason: Closed

title: [pair2-mcp] Size-aware overflow on subscribe (replace event-count :max-buffered with byte+event budget)
description:
Source: rf2-jlq5j findings doc (ai/findings/wire-protocol-bigapp-20260513-1541.md), recommendation #7

`tools.cljs:557 subscribe-tool` defaults `:max-buffered 500` — an event COUNT. Five overflowed 1KB events and five overflowed 5MB events look identical. The agent draining a sub to "catch up" pulls arbitrarily large payloads with no transport bound.

## Scope

Replace `:max-buffered N` with a budget pair: `:max-buffered-events N :max-buffered-bytes M`. On overflow, the runtime drops the OLDEST events (FIFO) and reports both `:dropped-events N :dropped-bytes M` in the next notification batch's `:data` slot.

Server-side measurement uses cheap byte-counting (`pr-str` length, counted ad-hoc). For epoch topics this becomes meaningful only after rf2-1wdzp + rf2-obpa9 land (diff encoding + dedupe shrink the worst case).

## Acceptance

- Subscribe tool accepts both budgets.
- Runtime queue enforces both.
- Notification :data slot carries both drop counts.
- Spec/003-Tool-Catalogue documents the byte budget contract.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/717
close_reason: Closed

title: [pair2-mcp] Cursor pagination on trace-window and watch-epochs
description:
Source: rf2-jlq5j findings doc (ai/findings/wire-protocol-bigapp-20260513-1541.md), recommendation #6

`tools.cljs:277 trace-window-tool` and `tools.cljs:304 watch-epochs-tool` return unbounded vectors. With default epoch-history depth 50 and 2x-app-db per record, both can blow the cap on a single call.

## Scope

Both tools accept `:limit` (int) and return `{:items [...] :next-cursor "<opaque-str>" :has-more? boolean :estimated-remaining int}`. Default `:limit` sized to fit the 5K-token cap (after rf2-1wdzp diff encoding lands, ~50 epochs; without, ~10 epochs).

Cursor is opaque on the wire — server-side it's a `since-id` that the runtime already supports (`runtime.cljs:380 epochs-since`). No new runtime infrastructure.

## Acceptance

- Both tools accept `:limit` + `:cursor`.
- Default limit fits the 5K cap.
- Smoke test: 50-epoch ring; pagination over 5 batches returns 50 distinct records in order.
- Spec/003-Tool-Catalogue documents pagination contract.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/708
close_reason: Closed

title: [pair2-mcp] Lazy-summary mode as default for snapshot discovery workflow
description:
Source: rf2-jlq5j findings doc (ai/findings/wire-protocol-bigapp-20260513-1541.md), recommendation #5

`tools/pair2-mcp/spec/Principles.md:156` prescribes a `:mode` arg with `:count` / `:sample` / `:full` for rich payload ops. Today snapshot only has `:full` (implicit). The discovery workflow ("I don't know where to look") fetches everything, blows the cap.

## Scope

Add `:mode :sample` as the snapshot tool's default. `:sample` returns:

- For maps: `{:keys [...] :sizes {:k 1234 :k 56000} :types {:k :map :k :vector}}` (top 2-3 levels deep, bounded).
- For vectors/lists: `{:count N :sample (take 5 ...) :truncated? true}`.
- For scalars: as-is.

Agent drills in with `:mode :full` + `:path` (per rf2-tygdv).

Make sure the summary walker bounds its own cost — easy mistake to walk the whole tree counting sizes. Cap walk depth (e.g. 3 levels) and sample lists (e.g. 5 items).

## Acceptance

- snapshot default mode returns shape descriptor < 500 tokens for any app-db.
- `:mode :full` preserves today's behaviour.
- Summary walker has a depth cap and a per-collection sample cap; both configurable.
- Smoke test: 5MB app-db snapshot in `:sample` mode < 2KB.
notes: PR #697 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/697
close_reason: Closed

title: [pair2-mcp] Integrate day8/de-dupe at runtime boundary for :epochs slice and subscribe streaming
description:
Source: rf2-jlq5j findings doc (ai/findings/wire-protocol-bigapp-20260513-1541.md), recommendation #4

day8/de-dupe (CLJS, alive, last commit 2026-04-29 by mike.thompson) is well-suited to re-frame's structural-sharing patterns. Applied pre-`pr-str` inside the browser runtime where structural identity is intact (`identical?` path), it collapses repeated subtrees across the epoch ring and across subscribe batches.

## Scope

Two integration sites:

1. `skills/re-frame-pair2/preload/re_frame_pair2/runtime.cljs:1236` (`snapshot-frame-slice :epochs`) — wrap output in `de-dupe/de-dupe`. Resulting wire payload is `{::dedupe/cache {...} ::dedupe/form <pointer-tree>}`.
2. `runtime.cljs/drain-subscription!` for `:topic :epoch` — same wrap per batch.

Add a tiny EDN tagged-literal decoder on the consumer side (the agent host or a wrapper around `tools.cljs/ok-text`) so the model sees the expanded form. Alternative: leave the deduped form on the wire and require agent-host opt-in to call `(expand cache)` themselves — less surprising for ad-hoc reads, more work for routine consumers.

## Complement to rf2-1wdzp (diff encoding)

Diff encoding (rf2-1wdzp) is the FORMAT change; structural dedup is the STORAGE LAYER underneath. Together: ~99% of epoch wire bytes go away.

## Cost

- Adds `[day8/de-dupe "0.2.2"]` to the preload's deps (~10KB compiled).
- One round-trip-test fixture per integration site.
- Documentation of the wire shape change.

## Acceptance

- snapshot :epochs wire payload through dedupe; expand round-trips byte-equal.
- subscribe :topic :epoch batches deduped; cross-batch sharing exploited within a single subscription's lifetime (server-side cache held for the sub's duration; emits new cache entries incrementally).
- Performance budget test: 50-epoch snapshot of 1MB app-db < 200KB wire (currently can be 100MB).
notes: PR #662 https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/662 — feat(pair2-mcp): integrate day8/de-dupe at runtime boundary. 151/151 tests green, 89.5% reduction on the 10-epoch / 256-key shared :db-before scenario (683KB→72KB). Wire shape {:rf.mcp/dedup-table <cache-map>}; per-call table reset; opt-out via dedup=false. Surfaces: trace-window, watch-epochs, snapshot per-frame :epochs, subscribe per-tick :events.
close_reason: Closed
