title: machines.cljc drain-raises uses runtime resolve — silently no-ops in CLJS
description:
## Problem

`implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines.cljc:1045` inside `drain-raises`:

```clojure
(let [step-result ((resolve (quote machine-transition)) machine snap args)]
  ...)
```

(actual source uses syntax-quote: `` `machine-transition ``)

This is the exact anti-pattern called out in `re-frame.late-bind`'s docstring: CLJS has no runtime `resolve`, so this call silently no-ops (or throws "not a function") in production CLJS. The JVM build works because JVM `clojure.core/resolve` is a runtime fn.

## Impact

The machine `:raise` reserved fx-id (Spec 005 §`:raise`) is the documented pre-commit dispatch primitive. Any handler `:action` that returns `{:fx [[:raise ...]]}` should recursively re-enter `machine-transition` via `drain-raises`. On CLJS, every nested `:raise` silently drops.

## Fix scope

One-line. Replace the `((resolve ...) ...)` call with a direct `(machine-transition ...)` call. The forward `(declare machine-transition machine-transition-single)` at line 1286 already makes the symbol resolve at compile time on both JVM and CLJS.

## Validation

Add a conformance fixture (if not already present) that exercises a 2-deep `:raise` chain on CLJS. Today's test suite passes on JVM by virtue of `resolve` being a runtime fn there.

Found by 2026-05-12 correctness review: ai/findings/correctness-review-2026-05-12.md §P2-1.
close_reason:
Fixed in PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/336.

`drain-raises` now calls `machine-transition` directly, backed by an
explicit `(declare machine-transition)` forward reference just above the
`drain-raises` body. The `((resolve `machine-transition) ...)` call is
gone, removing the anti-pattern surface flagged by the correctness review.

Regression coverage: `machine-raise-chain-cljs` (1-deep AND 2-deep raise
chains) in implementation/machines/test/re_frame/machines_cljs_test.cljs.

Note: investigation revealed the bead's "silently no-ops on CLJS" claim
is overstated for this specific call site. Because the call uses a
syntax-quoted (namespace-qualified) symbol, CLJS's `cljs.core/resolve`
macro emits a working Var-construction, so the `:raise` chain DID work
on CLJS pre-fix (existing JVM smoke_test coverage transparently
extends). The fix is still a correctness improvement — eliminates the
misleading anti-pattern, drops a per-raise Var allocation on CLJS, and
the new test pins explicit CLJS regression coverage so future
refactors can't silently break the path.

title: flows.cljc: remove dead memoised topo-sort + no-op invalidate-topo!
description:
implementation/flows/src/re_frame/flows.cljc lines 97-104:

  (def ^:private topo-sort* (memoize topo-sort))   ;; never called anywhere
  (defn- invalidate-topo! []                        ;; no-op
    ;; Cheap: rebuild memoise key by re-deref. Rather than tracking memo
    ;; cache, we rely on shape: callers call ordered-flows which dereferences
    ;; @flows; if @flows changes since last memoise call, the memoise misses.
    ;; ...
    nil)

The comment misleads readers: there is no `ordered-flows` fn, and `run-flows!` calls `topo-sort` (not `topo-sort*`) directly. The two call sites at lines 155 and 201 invoke `invalidate-topo!` for nothing.

ACTION: delete `topo-sort*`, delete `invalidate-topo!`, delete the two call sites. OR wire `topo-sort*` into `run-flows!` and make `invalidate-topo!` real (with an explicit cache-invalidation hook off the `flows` atom watch). Likely the first — `topo-sort` is cheap; the memo is a premature optimisation that never landed.

Discovered during clarity review 2026-05-12; see ai/findings/clarity-review-2026-05-12.md §F1.
close_reason: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/335 - vestigial path. Deleted dead memoised topo-sort*, no-op invalidate-topo!, and the two callers in reg-flow/clear-flow. Replaced with a short comment explaining why the memo is gone. All tests pass (26 flows JVM, 529 CLJS, elision clean).

title: Chapter 02 first code block omits init! and dispatch-sync; teaches a non-runnable shape
description:
docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md lines 19-55 present a 40-line code block introduced as 'the whole thing' / 'That's everything'. The block omits (rf/init! reagent/adapter) and (rf/dispatch-sync [:counter/initialise]) — both of which are needed for a runnable counter unless :on-create is used (which the block does use, so dispatch-sync is in fact unnecessary, but init! is essential). The chapter takes init! back in a buried blockquote at line 185 ('A real app's run does two more things before rdc/render'). A reader who copy-pastes the first block does not have a running app.

Fix: Either (a) commit to the canonical mount shape upfront and walk it (with init! and the adapter wiring explained inline), or (b) stop calling the stripped-down block 'the whole thing'. Option (a) is preferable — chapter 02 should teach the canonical shape, not a teaser shape.

This is the only P2 finding from the tutorial-clarity review (most are P3 polish); P2 because the chapter literally teaches code that does not run.
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F1.\nSee ai/findings/tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md for context.
close_reason: Fixed in PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/333 — first code block in docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md now includes the [re-frame.adapter.reagent :as reagent-adapter] require and (rf/init! reagent-adapter/adapter) in run, matching examples/reagent/counter/core.cljs. The buried 'Minimum-viable mount' blockquote was removed; its content is folded into the body of '## The mount'. dispatch-sync is intentionally not used here because :on-create already seeds app-db at frame-creation time (with a note clarifying dispatch-sync is the alternative at mount time). mkdocs build --strict warning count identical to origin/main (186/186 — all pre-existing spec/* cross-link warnings). No anchors broken; no other chapters touched.

title: [bug] Story shell: clicking a variant in the sidebar without a workspace selection blanks the page
description:
RESHAPED 2026-05-12 (Mike): a screenshot of #/stories with :Workspace.counter/auto-grid selected reveals the real bug. The variants in the workspace render as EMPTY CARDS — the frame headers are present ('variant frame: :story.counter/clicked-three-times' etc.) but NO COUNTER UI is rendered inside any of the four variant cards. Trace panel shows 'no cascades captured yet — dispatch an event to see the six dominos'. So no event has fired and the variant body isn't producing output.

Earlier framing ('clicking a variant without a workspace goes blank') was a downstream symptom: the variant alone produces nothing → page blanks. Inside a workspace you at least see the empty container scaffolding. Same root cause.

Diagnose: investigate why the variant render fn isn't producing output inside its variant frame. Candidates:
1. The variant body never runs (fn-form not invoked; macro expanded but registration not wiring the body to the frame)
2. The body runs but its dispatched event (e.g. :counter/initialise) is routed to the wrong frame and the sub returns nil
3. The body runs but mounts into the wrong DOM node
4. The body runs but the view fn returns nil (no app-db state to drive the render)
5. Per-variant frame isolation is interfering — the variant has its own frame but the sub queries app-db on a frame that's never been initialised

Check examples/reagent/counter_with_stories/stories.cljs (the reg-variant calls — what do they bind to? loaders? args?) + the variant runtime in tools/story/src/re_frame/story/runtime.cljc + the workspace renderer in tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/workspace.cljc.

Repro: stage examples/reagent/counter_with_stories/, compile + serve on 8030, navigate to #/stories, click :Workspace.counter/auto-grid. Variants should show counter UI; bug observed: they're empty.

Fix should produce: visible counter rendered inside each variant card, distinct for each (empty shows 0 or initial state; loaded shows non-zero; clicked-three-times shows 3; save-stubbed shows the stubbed save UI).
close_reason: Closed

title: [bug] *current-frame* binding lost across setTimeout / async callbacks; dispatch routes to default frame
description:
Discovered during rf2-yf97 (websocket example) authoring. rf/dispatch inside an action body picks up *current-frame* via dynamic binding — but setTimeout-deferred (and presumably js/Promise.then, requestAnimationFrame, etc.) callbacks escape the binding, so the dispatch routes to :rf/default frame instead of the multi-frame the action body was scoped to.

The workaround the example used: actions return :fx [[:dispatch ...]] instead of calling rf/dispatch directly. The fx-dispatcher runs synchronously within the action's frame context.

Direction:
1. Write a failing test: dispatch from inside (js/setTimeout #(rf/dispatch ...) 50) inside an action body of a multi-frame machine. Assert the event lands in the correct frame, not :rf/default.
2. If the test fails, fix one of:
   - Pin the current frame to the closure: rf/dispatch's call site captures (current-frame) and the deferred callback uses (with-frame frame-id ...) explicitly.
   - Or document the gotcha + the :fx-based workaround as the canonical pattern.

This is a footgun for any app using setTimeout / async APIs with multi-frame architecture. Pattern-WebSocket (rf2-yf97), Pattern-LongRunningWork (rf2-o9fg) both touch this surface; either could be hitting it silently.

Scope: implementation/core/src/re_frame/{frame,core}.cljc — verify dispatch's *current-frame* capture; possibly implementation/adapters/reagent/test/ or implementation/core/test/ for the regression test.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #332 (Outcome A). process-event! now binds frame/*current-frame* to the envelope's :frame for the duration of the handler chain. Synchronous (rf/dispatch ...) from inside a handler body now routes to the handler's frame, not :rf/default. Async callbacks (setTimeout, Promise.then, RAF) still need explicit-capture affordances — :fx [[:dispatch ...]] (canonical), :dispatch-later, or (rf/bound-dispatcher). Spec 002 §Dispatches issued from inside a handler body documents the contract. Regression coverage: re-frame.dispatch-frame-capture-cljs-test + smoke_test.clj. rf2-yf97's :fx workaround still works (unchanged code path).

title: [bug] Machine initial-cascade :entry actions don't fire on spawn (use :on :rf.machine/spawned)
description:
Discovered during rf2-yf97 (websocket example) authoring. When a machine spawns, its initial state's :entry actions don't actually fire in the cascade. Workaround the example used: add :on :rf.machine/spawned :target ... :action ... — the http-managed wrapper uses the same workaround.

If this is intentional, the spec needs to call it out clearly (Pattern docs assume :entry fires on initial state). If unintentional, the runtime should fire initial :entry actions during the spawn cascade just like any other state-entry.

Scope: implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines.cljc (the spawn handler) + a test in implementation/machines/test/ to lock the contract. May also need a spec/005-StateMachines.md update depending on which way this lands.

Direction: verify the current behaviour with a small test (declare a machine with an :entry on the initial state; spawn it; observe whether :entry fires). If yes → close as no-bug. If no → fix the spawn handler to fire initial :entry, and update Pattern docs/examples that have been working around it.

Surfaced from: rf2-yf97 (websocket example). The example's connection.cljs and the http-managed wrapper both use the :rf.machine/spawned workaround.
close_reason: Closed

title: [bug] Flake: re-frame.frame-lifecycle-test/destroy-frame-discards-pending-events (timing race)
description:
implementation/core/test/re_frame/frame_lifecycle_test.clj:99 (`destroy-frame-discards-pending-events`) is a recurring flake: expected 2 events in queue, occasionally gets 1. Async dispatch + drain-tick timing race. Has flaked on at least 3 PRs in this session (#305, #312, #322) — all admin-merged after re-run because every other check is green and the failing PR doesn't touch frame-lifecycle code.

Earlier observation (from rf2-er0t agent): "reproduced 0/5 times when isolated. Pre-existing, unrelated to [my] change."

Root cause direction: the test asserts ordering against an async drain. Either (a) the test needs a deterministic synchronisation point (await drain explicitly before assertion), (b) the production code needs a quiescence signal the test can lock against, or (c) the test should use synchronous dispatch.

Action: stabilise the test so it doesn't flake. Until then, the loop's "re-run on single failure" heuristic absorbs the cost — but with 3+ failures per N-PR cycle, that's a real tax.

Scope: implementation/core/test/re_frame/frame_lifecycle_test.clj (and possibly implementation/core/src/re_frame/frame.cljc if a quiescence signal is added).
close_reason: Closed

title: slim re-render reactive
notes: Surfaced during rf2-s36l implementation: after fixing the IDisposable seam (rf2-s36l) and the as-element conversion in render() (rf2-08t0), counter-slim-and-fast initial render works but reactive re-renders don't fire. Click '+' fails to update the displayed count from 5 to 6. Suspect: the make-render-method's Reaction never gets _handle-change-driven re-runs even though it sits in *ratom-context* and the subscribe Reaction's watching graph is wired through deref-capture. Investigation hooks: instrument _handle-change / rea-enqueue / queue-render! to verify the chain on dispatch. The counter mounts cleanly and bundle-comparison/elision/cljs/browser/boundary-prod tests stay green; this is the runtime reactive-update gap.
close_reason: Closed

title: Slim adapter: wrap-render returns raw hiccup instead of React element
description: After rf2-s36l (interop late-binding) lands the slim adapter's runtime smoke can clear the IDisposable seam but trips on a separate bug in the slim's render path. reagent2.impl.component/wrap-render returns the user fn's hiccup output directly (a CLJS vector); stock Reagent's reagent.impl.component/wrap-render calls (p/as-element compiler res) to convert hiccup → React element before returning to React's render() method. When the slim's render method returns a CLJS vector, React fails with 'Objects are not valid as a React child (found: object with keys {afn, meta, ...})' because the vector's head is a MetaFn-wrapped reg-view fn and React tries to treat it as a child node. Symptom: counter-slim-and-fast Playwright smoke fails at mount even after rf2-s36l. Fix: wrap-render in implementation/adapters/reagent-slim/src/reagent2/impl/component.cljs (around lines 233-272) must invoke reagent2.impl.template/as-element on its hiccup result before returning — matching stock Reagent's reagent.impl.component:wrap-render shape (lines 93-96 in the upstream extract). Form-2's cached inner-fn path needs the same as-element wrap on its return. Verification: re-enable the smoke when this is fixed (the rf2-s36l fix already unsipped the spec.cjs file — if this bead lands after rf2-s36l, the smoke should turn green).
close_reason: Fixed inline by rf2-s36l/PR #326 — wrap-render now converts hiccup → React element via reagent2.impl.template/as-element. Two test updates included in #326.

title: Suggestion: add fixture-app and prompt-regression validation for re-frame-pair2
description:
## Problem

`re-frame-pair2` is a runtime-mutating skill, but its most important workflows are still not forward-tested against a real app or against representative prompts.

This is explicitly called out in the skill's own status and testing docs, which means the current validation envelope is known to be incomplete.

## Evidence from the current repo

- `skills/re-frame-pair2/README.md` lines 19-25 describe the skill as pre-alpha and say a fixture app and end-to-end exercise are still missing.
- `skills/re-frame-pair2/STATUS.md` lines 19-23 say the fixture app does not exist and nothing has been exercised against a running re-frame2 + shadow-cljs build yet.
- `skills/re-frame-pair2/docs/TESTING.md` defines three still-missing surfaces:
  - `tests/shim/`
  - `tests/e2e/`
  - `tests/prompts/`

## Suggested change

Create a validation track that covers the real failure modes of a runtime pairing skill:

1. Stand up a minimal fixture app (Reagent + shadow-cljs + Tool-Pair surfaces on).
2. Add shell-shim integration tests for the documented scripts.
3. Add browser-level E2E checks for:
   - connect/discover
   - dispatch + trace collection
   - hot-reload probe gating
   - DOM source-coord workflows
   - epoch restore and failure modes
4. Add prompt-regression coverage for representative conversations so recipe drift is caught when SKILL.md changes.

## Why this matters

This skill asks an agent to mutate runtime state, time-travel through epochs, and trust probe-based reload confirmation. Those are exactly the cases where forward-testing matters more than prose correctness.

## Expected impact

- stronger trust in the runtime workflows
- earlier detection of drift between SKILL recipes and actual scripts
- safer iteration on reload gating and time-travel behavior
- a clearer path out of pre-alpha
close_reason: PRs #511 #513 #512 merged

title: Suggestion: tighten re-frame-pair2 trigger metadata around live-runtime use
description:
## Problem

`skills/re-frame-pair2/SKILL.md` uses its frontmatter description as a broad keyword bucket: `re-frame2`, `app-db`, `dispatch`, `subscribe`, `reg-event`, `reg-sub`, `reg-fx`, `reg-machine`, `frame`, `epoch`, `interceptor`, `re-com`, `shadow-cljs`.

That metadata is the trigger surface. It reads as if the skill should activate for many ordinary source-code or spec questions that do **not** require a live runtime.

## Evidence from the current repo

- `skills/re-frame-pair2/SKILL.md` lines 3-13 describe the skill correctly at first, then broaden into a long list of generic terms.
- The body immediately makes the precondition stricter: the app is live, running behind `shadow-cljs watch`, and the goal is to operate on the runtime, not just inspect source.

## Suggested change

Rewrite the frontmatter description to optimize for routing accuracy instead of keyword breadth.

Candidate direction:

- state the positive trigger clearly:
  - use when the user is working against a **running** re-frame2 app
  - use when the task needs **live runtime introspection** / dispatch / tracing / hot-swap / epoch restore
- add a negative boundary:
  - do not use for static spec reading, architecture questions, or ordinary source-only edits when no runtime is involved
- move the long vocabulary list out of frontmatter and into README or a reference file if it is still useful as documentation

## Why this matters

A false-positive activation on a large runtime skill is expensive: it loads a long SKILL, biases the agent toward runtime operations that may not be possible, and buries the simpler path for ordinary code-reading tasks.

## Expected impact

- fewer false activations
- better separation between static-code help and live-runtime help
- lower context cost on routine re-frame2 questions
- clearer mental model of what re-frame-pair2 is actually for
close_reason: PRs merged in batch landing wave 2026-05-13

title: Suggestion: split re-frame-pair2 SKILL.md into a lean core plus references
description:
## Problem

`skills/re-frame-pair2/SKILL.md` is currently 409 lines and carries nearly the entire operational corpus inline: the op catalogue, hot-reload protocol, restore failure table, recipe catalogue, error handling, and the 'dropped from v1' appendix.

By comparison, `skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/` already follows a more progressive-disclosure shape: a compact SKILL plus targeted `references/*.md` files.

## Evidence from the current repo

- `skills/re-frame-pair2/SKILL.md` lines 80-398 hold detailed tables and long-form procedures.
- `skills/re-frame-pair2/` already has supporting docs (`docs/initial-spec.md`, `docs/capabilities.md`, `docs/TESTING.md`), but SKILL still duplicates a large amount of that content instead of pointing to thinner agent-facing references.
- `skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/SKILL.md` explicitly loads `references/analysis-lenses.md`, `references/known-frictions.md`, and `references/issue-template.md` only when needed.

## Suggested change

Refactor `re-frame-pair2` toward the same progressive-disclosure model:

- keep only the highest-signal trigger-time instructions in `SKILL.md`:
  - live-runtime framing
  - REPL-vs-source-edit distinction
  - connect-first guard rail
  - frame-selection safety
  - a short list of core workflows
- move bulk material into agent-facing `references/` files such as:
  - `references/operations.md`
  - `references/recipes.md`
  - `references/errors.md`
  - `references/time-travel.md`
- have `SKILL.md` say explicitly when to read each reference
- treat `docs/` as design/human material and `references/` as agent-consumable operating material

## Why this matters

This is a runtime-heavy skill. Every false activation or routine activation currently drags a large body of text into context, which makes the highest-value guard rails easier to miss and raises the token cost of using the skill.

## Expected impact

- better context economy
- clearer guard rails at trigger time
- easier maintenance when ops or recipes change
- closer alignment with the repo's cleaner improver-skill shape
close_reason: Done. PR opened: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/311

title: Create SKILL-REDIRECT.md at repo root (canonical pointer table for AI skills)
description:
Add a standalone file SKILL-REDIRECT.md at the re-frame2 repo root — a flat list of name→URL pairs that the rf2-qumf skill (and other skills like rf2-glc8 setup, re-frame-pair2) instruct AI agents to consult for deep-dive content.

Why a separate file (not a README section): smaller load (AI reads only what it needs), obvious discovery (filename names exactly what it is), reusable by every skill in the repo, single source of truth for pointers across skills.

Initial entries (verify URLs at filing time):
- Definitive API reference → https://day8.github.io/re-frame2/api/ (or actual)
- Pattern docs (in full) → https://day8.github.io/re-frame2/spec/Pattern-*/
- Migration from re-frame v1 → https://day8.github.io/re-frame2/spec/MIGRATION/
- EP design rationale → https://day8.github.io/re-frame2/spec/000-Vision/
- Construction prompts (AI-shaped templates) → https://day8.github.io/re-frame2/spec/Construction-Prompts/
- Examples directory → https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/tree/main/examples/reagent
- Stories authoring → wherever Story's spec lands
- Pair2 skill (live-inspect) → wherever published

Format: flat bullet list with '→' separator. No nesting. Easy for an AI to scan; easy for a human to read.

Header preamble: a one-paragraph 'this file exists so AI skills can redirect deep-dive lookups without hardcoding URLs' — sets expectations for anyone discovering it via repo browsing.

Gates: rf2-qumf skill authoring beads depend on this file existing — leaves reference it by name. Land this before any authoring bead off rf2-qumf is dispatched.

Discovered from rf2-qumf design work (findings doc: ai/findings/re-frame2-skill-design.md, 'Deep-dive content lives behind a single redirect' banner). Mike's design call 2026-05-12.
close_reason: Landed via PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/308 — SKILL-REDIRECT.md at repo root with the canonical pointer table for AI skills.

title: New skill: re-frame2-setup — greenfield project bootstrap
description:
Companion skill to rf2-qumf (the re-frame2 code-writing skill). Handles greenfield project bootstrap — the things rf2-qumf deliberately excludes because deps and setup drift over time.

Scope:
- Add re-frame2 to deps.edn / package.json (current versions discovered at skill-load time, not hardcoded)
- shadow-cljs.edn build configuration (basic single-page-app build; reagent adapter)
- npm install + dependency versions
- Initial namespace structure (entry ns, core, frames root)
- Minimal mounted counter-like first-app so the user sees something work
- Pointer to rf2-qumf for "now write code"

Audience: people starting a NEW re-frame2 project. Once setup is done, they switch to rf2-qumf.

Same conventions as rf2-qumf:
- Audience: app authors, not implementors
- Verified against current implementation/, not spec
- Consult github.com/anthropics/skills/skill-creator/ first
- Context-economy discipline (small, distillable)
- Assume training knowledge; don't teach what deps.edn / npm / shadow-cljs are

Out of scope:
- Teaching re-frame2 concepts (that's rf2-qumf)
- v1-to-v2 migration (separate concern)

Discovered while locking Q3 of rf2-qumf — Mike (2026-05-12) ruled setup out of rf2-qumf because deps change over time and would stale the skill; separate skill is the right home.
close_reason: Done — PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/310. New skill at skills/re-frame2-setup/: SKILL.md (172 lines) + 4 reference leaves (~115 lines avg) + README, LICENSE, package.json, .claude-plugin/plugin.json. Greenfield seven-step path: VERSION lookup, deps.edn (core + reagent adapter only), package.json, shadow-cljs.edn, entry ns with (rf/init! adapter), first counter. Versions discovered, never hardcoded. Defers re-frame2 API to rf2-eipb skill, migration to SKILL-REDIRECT.md, live inspection to re-frame-pair2.

title: Extend realworld LoC comparison with robustness / correctness / testability analysis
description:
Extend the existing findings doc at ai/findings/realworld-loc-comparison.md (rf2-a8aa4459 research, completed 2026-05-12) with a qualitative analysis on three axes the LoC-only comparison hides:

1. **Managed HTTP robustness** — re-frame2's realworld uses :rf.http/managed (retries, cancellation cascade on parent unmount, terminal-state semantics, request/reply correlation). Audit how the other implementations handle HTTP — most likely ad-hoc fetch / axios / xhr with manual error handling. Does the LoC delta look different once you account for the failure semantics each implementation actually delivers?

2. **State machine correctness** — re-frame2 uses formal state machines (reg-machine, regions, tags, parallel regions) with declarative transitions and terminal states. Audit the other implementations' state-management code: Redux reducer fragments, Vue refs/stores, Angular services, Svelte stores, Elm models. Where are the state transitions encoded? Are they enumerable, auditable, exhaustively-testable? Or scattered across components as ad-hoc mutations?

3. **Testability** — re-frame2's realworld ships ~555 LoC of tests covering machine snapshots, tag queries, transition assertions. Audit the other implementations' tests qualitatively (not just LoC):
   - Are they unit tests, integration tests, or end-to-end?
   - Do they exercise state-transition correctness, or only output rendering?
   - Can a reader of the test suite enumerate the app's behaviour?
   - What's NOT tested (and silently relied-upon)?

Frame the analysis as: **is re-frame2's realworld provably more correct than the alternatives?** Be honest about where the answer is yes (state machines as formal artefacts), where it's nuanced (testability depends on what you measure), and where it's marginal or wrong (e.g. if a typed peer has compile-time guarantees re-frame2 doesn't).

Output: append a new section to ai/findings/realworld-loc-comparison.md titled "Beyond LoC — robustness, correctness, testability" with sub-sections per axis. Keep the existing LoC table and analysis intact.

Do NOT commit. ai/findings/ is local-only.

This is research follow-up — output is the extended findings doc + any new open questions for Mike at the bottom.
close_reason: Findings doc extended (Beyond LoC section, §6 in ai/findings/realworld-loc-comparison.md)

title: New skill: re-frame2 — modular AI guidance for writing re-frame2 apps
description:
Create a skill at /skills/re-frame2/ (location TBD — Mike's guess; verify in research) that provides everything an AI needs to write re-frame2 applications.

Requirements:
- Modular structure — each module loadable independently so context isn't wasted
- Coverage:
  - Basics (frames, events, subs, fx, cofx, schemas, the event-state cycle)
  - Stories (the testing/playground primitive)
  - State machines (reg-machine, regions, tags, parallel regions, invoke, terminal states)
  - Patterns:
    - Nine States of UI (parallel regions + tags worked example)
    - WebSockets (Pattern-WebSocket — connection machine, request/reply correlation)
    - Managed HTTP (:rf.http/managed as fx and as invokable machine)
    - Boot (Pattern-Boot — entry-action consolidated boot)
    - Forms (Pattern-Forms — slice vs form-region)
    - RemoteData (Pattern-RemoteData — slice vs data-region)
- Audience: AI agents writing re-frame2 code. The skill must give them enough to one-shot non-trivial apps.

This bead anchors the work. Step 1 is the findings doc (ai/findings/re-frame2-skill-design.md) that surveys the existing skill convention in this repo, audits what spec/ docs already cover, proposes the module structure + load order, and lists open questions for Mike before any skill files are written.
notes:
Audience locked: CLJS APPLICATION AUTHORS, NOT framework implementors.

Goal locked: help an AI write working re-frame2 code with minimum context. Four pillars: correctness, idiomaticness (impl-grounded), context economy, assume training knowledge (teach only re-frame2-specific bindings).

Authoring prerequisite: consult github.com/anthropics/skills/skill-creator/.

Source priority: implementation is ground truth, not spec.

Deep-dive redirects: standalone SKILL-REDIRECT.md at repo root (rf2-12gf gate). No URL hardcoding in skill.

Locked design answers (see findings doc top banners + v2 §7b):
- Single skill, CLJS only
- 150-line default per leaf / 250 ceiling
- Patterns: BOTH inline mini + link to examples
- Patterns without examples: synthesise mini + follow-on beads (3 new example beads: websocket, boot, long-running-work)
- Eval harness blocks v1.0
- Distribution: both npm + plugin
- Decision trees: separate files
- Principles: link only
- Write against current main
- Q3 (setup): OUT — separate skill rf2-glc8 (re-frame2-setup)
- Q5 (v1 migration): no module; one-line redirect via SKILL-REDIRECT.md
- Q14 (verify output): LOCKED NO — skill does NOT teach verification. No verify.md module, no hard rule. Running tests is the user's job, not the AI's. Consistent with Pillar 4 (training knowledge).

Still need Mike's call:
- Q15 (skill name): 're-frame2' is project-name, not gerund form (Anthropic best-practice prefers gerund). v2 recommends keeping 're-frame2' (description does heavy lifting, naming pattern already established by rf2-glc8 re-frame2-setup). Mike to confirm or pick alternative.

Authoring bead roster (per v2 §9, minus the verify module): 10 mandatory + 1 deferred (Story spinout) + 3 new example apps = 14 beads to file once Q15 is locked.

Findings docs: ai/findings/re-frame2-skill-design.md (v1, deprecated), ai/findings/re-frame2-skill-design-v2.md (current).
close_reason: Skill ecosystem shipped: skills/re-frame2/ (scaffolding #309 + 12 leaf PRs #312-#321, evals #324, integration #325), SKILL-REDIRECT.md (#308), companion re-frame2-setup skill (#310), 3 new pattern example apps (#322, #323, #328), pair2 SKILL.md split (#311), XState tip (84ee83c). All design locks from ai/findings/re-frame2-skill-design-v2.md realised. Closed by Mike 2026-05-12.

title: Slim adapter runtime gap: re-frame.interop hardcodes stock reagent.ratom for IDisposable / make-reaction / etc.
description:
Surfaced by rf2-5lbx (counter-slim-and-fast Playwright smoke). With the slim adapter installed via (rf/init! reagent-slim-adapter/adapter), the first subscribe call fails with 'No protocol method IDisposable.add-on-dispose! defined for type reagent2.ratom/Reaction'. Stack trace: re_frame.interop/add_on_dispose_BANG_ -> reagent.ratom/add_on_dispose_BANG_ -> stock Reagent's IDisposable protocol dispatch -> the slim's Reaction does NOT implement stock's IDisposable (it implements reagent2.ratom/IDisposable).

Root cause: implementation/core/src/re_frame/interop.cljs:9-11 statically requires reagent.core / reagent.ratom and binds every reaction-related fn to stock Reagent's namespace (lines 19, 24, 29, 34, 37, 40, 44). This is the seam IMPL-SPEC §2.1 docstring of reagent_slim.cljs flags as the Stage 4-E follow-up: 'views.cljs needs adapter-agnostic dispatch through a late-bind hook' — the same shape applies to interop.cljs.

Scope:
1. Move re-frame.interop's ratom/reaction/disposable surfaces to late-bind hooks installed by the active adapter (analogous to :adapter/current-frame, :adapter/current-component).
2. Decide: does the slim adapter own its own ratom impl ns + late-bind set, or does interop dispatch through a single :adapter/ratom hook table?
3. Either way: the seam keeps the existing per-call signatures so consumers (re-frame.subs, re-frame.views, anywhere that does (interop/add-on-dispose! r f)) need no change.

Verification: re-enable the runtime smoke in examples/reagent/counter_slim_and_fast/counter_slim_and_fast.spec.cjs (drop the 'skip' field). The bundle-comparison contract (cljs-bundle-comparison CI job, rf2-5lbx) already enforces that the slim displaces stock Reagent in the :advanced bundle — that contract stays untouched.

Bundle-size note (rf2-5lbx finding for R-007): once interop stops statically requiring reagent.core/reagent.ratom, the slim build's stock-Reagent dep can be made conditional. Today, the in-tree shadow-cljs build pulls both adapter trees + stock Reagent into every example's classpath, so the gzipped counter sizes are essentially identical (slim ~93 KB gz vs stock ~93 KB gz). The 'slim wins ~25 KB gz' framing from Stage 2 §3.5 holds ONLY for downstream Maven consumers that depend on reagent-slim alone; until interop's adapter-agnostic dispatch lands, the framing cannot be measured from inside the monorepo.
close_reason: Fixed in PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/326. Moved re-frame.interop's seven CLJS reactive surfaces (ratom, ratom?, make-reaction, add-on-dispose!, dispose!, reactive?, after-render) to per-fn late-bind hooks (Option B; consistent with the existing :adapter/current-frame / :adapter/current-component precedent). Classic Reagent, slim, UIx, and Helix all install their hook set, routed through (substrate-adapter/current-adapter) per rf2-0d35. The IDisposable protocol-dispatch error at first subscribe under the slim adapter is gone. Two downstream slim-adapter bugs surfaced during verification: rf2-08t0 (slim wrap-render returns raw hiccup — fixed inline via cycle-break setter so render() returns React elements) and rf2-u5p5 (Reaction → React forceUpdate watch chain not firing — still open). counter-slim-and-fast smoke remains skip-parked behind the rf2-u5p5 pointer; bundle-comparison contract (rf2-51x5) unaffected. All test suites pass: test:cljs (506), test:browser (503), test:browser-schemas-boundary-prod (5), test:elision, test:bundle-comparison, test:examples, JVM per-artefact tests.

title: Remove findings/sci-macro-compatibility.md from repo (shouldn't be checked in)
description:
findings/sci-macro-compatibility.md landed in PR #303 (rf2-97zo). It shouldn't be checked in — findings/ is meant to be local-only working substrate, not committed history. The agent committed it because findings/svelte-style-playground-feasibility.md was already there as precedent, but the precedent is wrong.

Action:
1. `git rm findings/sci-macro-compatibility.md` and commit/PR the removal.
2. Verify `findings/` is in `.gitignore` (or `.git/info/exclude` locally — but the project-wide `.gitignore` should cover it so future agents don't repeat the mistake). If not in `.gitignore`, add it.
3. Audit other files currently checked in under `findings/` — list them in the PR body so Mike can decide which (if any) should also be removed. Do NOT delete them in this PR; just enumerate.

PR title: `chore: remove findings/sci-macro-compatibility.md (should not be tracked)`.
close_reason: commit b3e1db6

title: Extract inline tests from /examples source files into separate test files
description:
nine_states/core.cljs has ~64 lines of (deftest ...) and (is (= ...)) test code inline in the source file (lines 691-755). Tests should live in a separate test file, not co-mingled with the example's source — readers reaching for the example shouldn't have to scroll past test code, and the test code itself is harder to navigate when it shares a file with hundreds of lines of pedagogical narrative + implementation.

Realworld already does it the right way: a sibling `test/realworld/` directory with one `*_test.cljs` per resource.

Scope:
1. Extract nine_states inline tests into `examples/reagent/nine_states/test/nine_states/core_test.cljs` (or similar) — mirror the realworld pattern.
2. Audit ALL other examples for the same pattern. Files to check: examples/reagent/{counter, login, todomvc, managed_http_counter, state_machine_walkthrough, routing, ssr, counter_with_stories, 7Guis/*}/core.cljs (and any other .cljs sources), plus examples/uix/* and examples/helix/* counter/login bundles. Look for `deftest`, `is`, `testing`, or `t/is` invocations in non-test files.
3. For each example found with inline tests, move them out into a proper test file under that example's directory (create the test/ subdirectory if needed). Wire the new test namespace into whatever test runner the example uses (shadow-cljs build, the spec.cjs harness, or the examples test entry point).
4. Verify: `npm run test:cljs` still passes and all previously asserted behaviour is still covered.

Out of scope: changing what the tests assert. Just relocate.
close_reason:
PR opened: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/301

Extracted nine_states inline tests (109 LoC) into examples/reagent/nine_states/test/nine_states/core_test.cljs (ns nine-states.core-test), mirroring the realworld layout. Source file is now test-free (793 -> 684 lines). Aggregator at implementation/adapters/reagent/test/re_frame/nine_states_cljs_test.cljs now wraps each fixture in its own testing block under one deftest.

Audit confirmed nine_states/core.cljs was the only example source file with inline test code. All other examples either had no inline tests or already used properly-named *_test.cljs files.

Test counts: 496 tests / 1205 assertions / 0 failures (previously 1206; the 1 lost assertion was the redundant outer (is (= :ok (run-all-tests))) wrapper, now subsumed by per-state testing contexts).

Verified: npm run test:cljs, npm run test:elision, shadow-cljs compile examples/nine-states.

title: Strip bead-ID references from /examples and /docs/guide
description:
Reader-facing material (examples + guide) should not mention internal bead IDs (rf2-XXXX) — these are project-management artefacts, not pedagogy. Grep finds 68 files in examples/ and 7 files in docs/guide/ that currently contain rf2-XXXX references. For each occurrence, decide: (a) drop the parenthetical/footnote entirely, (b) rephrase to keep the substantive content but remove the bead ID, or (c) preserve only when the bead ID acts as a stable historical anchor that has educational value. Default to (a) — most refs are 'rf2-XXXX' parenthetical attributions from when content was first authored and add no value for readers. Also check examples/scratch/malli_bundle_test/README.md ('Mike's call per the rf2-qnxf bead') and similar prose mentions of 'bead' as a word.

Scope: examples/**, docs/guide/**. Do NOT touch spec/ (those refs are intentional and part of normative spec history), /findings (working notes), /tools (internal), or CHANGELOG/CLAUDE.md (project metadata).

Verify after: re-grep both directories for `rf2-[a-z0-9]` and the word `bead` (case-insensitive) — should return zero results in scope.
close_reason: PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/300 — stripped ~120 bead-ID occurrences from /examples and /docs/guide across 73 files. 4 rule-3 preservations remain (all spec-URL fragment anchors); zero bead-word matches in scope. Example bundles compile clean; npm run test:cljs is 496/1206 green.

title: Implementation coherence audit: sweep implementation/ core + per-feature extras for drift
description: Parallel audit to rf2-eetv (spec drift) — sweep the implementation side. Background agent should: (1) read git log of substantive landings since ~10 days ago; (2) for each affected implementation surface, verify the impl matches the spec it claims to implement; (3) per-feature artefacts to audit: implementation/core (registry/drain/fx/dispatch/subs/frame-provider/trace), implementation/machines (where most recent feature work landed — tags + parallel-regions + fn-form :data + managed-HTTP-as-child-machine), implementation/http (managed HTTP cancellation + wrapper integration), implementation/flows, implementation/routing, implementation/ssr, implementation/schemas, implementation/epoch, implementation/adapters/* (reagent / uix / helix / reagent-slim); (4) audit categories: missing exports, stale docstrings, leftover scaffolding from prior architectural shapes, capability-matrix claims that don't match real behaviour, elision sentinels that don't elide cleanly, test coverage gaps; (5) small fixes inline via PR; (6) bigger drift filed as follow-up beads. Output: audit report at findings/implementation-coherence-audit.md + drift fixes + follow-up beads. Match rf2-eetv quality bar.
notes:
PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/296

Audit summary:
- Landings audited (sampled from git log --since='10 days ago' --no-merges, ~95 commits): Stage 1 tags (8f72c3f), Stage 2 parallel-regions (c039b7d), fn-form :data (9b339e6), managed-HTTP-as-child-machine (4e7602f), rename sweep day8/re-frame-2-* -> day8/re-frame2-* (d0a4b06), rf2-wvkn cancellation cascade (077c872), Stage-4 reagent-slim (763d907 + Stage 4-B/C/D/E/F), frame-context corruption error (ea3e81d), set-schema-validator! seam (06700e1), validate-at-boundary interceptor (f21e8b0), SSR head-hydration reconcile (a35a709), adapter routing through installed adapter (b70e3ea), and refactor sweeps (60dfec6 / bca7778 / 1915ff9 / 95b446d / 79d67cb / 3f05671).

Inline fix:
- implementation/adapters/reagent-slim/IMPL-SPEC.md — 30 surgical edits (28 reagent-slim-and-fast refs + §2.1 adapter-file path).

Follow-up beads (P3, discovered-from:rf2-5nwb):
- rf2-er0t — invoke-all conformance fixtures carry stale :id key
- rf2-84e9 — validate-at-boundary lacks CLJS prod-mode coverage
- rf2-5lbx — Stage-4 reagent-slim shipped without counter-slim-and-fast comparison build

Test deltas: no regressions. All JVM (8 artefacts), CLJS (496/1206), elision probe (35 sentinels), bundle isolation (20 sentinels), and mkdocs --strict remain green.

Coherence verdict: post-recent-landings, the implementation is in a coherent state. Recent feature work shipped with matching tests, spec-referencing docstrings, gated emit sites, and clean per-artefact isolation. The three follow-up beads are housekeeping (spec-side fixture cleanup; one cross-substrate test gap; one missing comparison build) rather than meaningful spec/impl divergence.
close_reason: Closed

title: Spec coherence audit: sweep spec/ for drift after recent feature landings
description: A lot has landed in the past ~10 days (Nine States tags + parallel regions, Pattern-WebSocket rewrite, Pattern-NineStates rewrite + managed-HTTP integration, Story 8-stage epic, tools/ convention, day8/re-frame2-* rename sweep, v0.0.1.alpha sweep, machines fn-form :data, :rf.http/managed as child machine, Pattern-Boot :entry fix, etc.). Background agent should: (1) read git log --since='10 days ago' --no-merges to enumerate substantive landings; (2) for each landing, identify which spec/* files were affected OR should have been affected; (3) audit each spec doc for drift relative to what actually shipped; (4) small fixes (cross-link updates, missing cross-references, line-count refresh, stale phrasing) applied inline; (5) bigger drift filed as follow-up beads; (6) capability-matrix audit — every shipped feature should have its capability row claimed. Output: audit report + drift fixes via PR + follow-up beads. Match the rf2-buor / rf2-90d5 quality bar.
notes:
PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/291

Summary: Audited the last ~10 days of substantive landings (Nine States Stages 1/2/3, Pattern-NineStates rewrite, Pattern-WebSocket rewrite, Pattern-Boot :entry fix, managed-HTTP as child machine, :invoke-all spawn-and-join, :invoke cancellation cascade, :system-id named addressing, fn-form :data materialisation, day8/re-frame2-* rename sweep, v0.0.1.alpha sweep, tools/ convention, Story 8-stage epic) against the spec/ tree. Most of the substantive landings had already self-propagated through 005-StateMachines, Spec-Schemas, and the second M-39..M-46 MIGRATION block — but Vision (000), Implementor-Checklist, conformance/README, AI-Audit, CP-5-MachineGuide, Construction-Prompts, and Ownership had stale claims about parallel regions being out of scope and the v1 reference's CLJS-claimed capability list. Seven inline edits brought those into alignment. The biggest piece of drift surfaced — duplicate M-39/M-41/M-42 numbering in MIGRATION.md between the user-side rules section and the additive pre-release notes section — is large enough to file as its own bead rather than fix in-line.

Follow-up beads filed:
- rf2-478m (P3) — MIGRATION.md duplicate M-39/M-41/M-42 numbering between user-side and pre-release additive sections

mkdocs build --strict: zero WARNING/ERROR (183 pre-existing INFO-level slug-normalisation anchor warnings untouched, out of scope).
close_reason: Closed

title: Nine States feature work — Stage 3: rewrite Pattern-NineStates + example
description: Per findings/nine-states-parallel-machines-design.md §10 Stage 3. Documentation/example-only work; depends on Stages 1 + 2 landing. No new implementation work. Parent: rf2-8qz1; blocked-by rf2-ee0d + rf2-l67o.

Pattern-NineStates.md rewritten with the canonical rules collapsed 7 → 4; the boolean-discriminator-sub variant lives in an Appendix (Pre-parallel-regions variant — deprecated but supported). examples/reagent/nine_states/core.cljs rewritten around one parallel machine with three regions (:data / :form / :mode), :fsm/tags on every state, a render-priority vector + :ui/render selector sub, and a root view whose only branch is a case over the resolved render keyword. title: [bug] Pattern-WebSocket worked example is internally inconsistent and incomplete
description:
The worked example in `spec/Pattern-WebSocket.md` is not just rough around the edges; it is internally inconsistent enough that readers cannot safely treat it as a canonical reference example.

Main problems found:
- The socket actor is invoked in `:connecting`, but the prose says leaving `:connecting` destroys it. The machine then enters `:authenticating` and `:connected`, both of which still dispatch through `:socket-id`. As written, the actor lifetime and the happy path disagree.
- `:ws/connect` only records `:url` / `:auth-token` in `:disconnected`. The `:reconnecting` and `:failed` handlers reset retry state but do not refresh connection opts, while the prose later says token refresh can be followed by `[:ws/connection [:ws/connect]]`.
- The pattern text claims exponential backoff and connection-epoch stale suppression, but the worked machine only shows a literal `1000` ms `:after` and no explicit epoch / stale-event handling path.
- `:in-flight` request/reply correlation is described later in the prose, but the actual `:ws/received` action in the worked example only forwards pushed messages and never performs correlation lookup / dispatch / cleanup.
- `:connected` declares nested `:idle / :sending / :receiving` states, but nothing in the example transitions between them, so the hierarchy reads as decorative rather than explanatory.

Actionable direction:
- Rewrite the example so it is either a genuinely coherent worked machine or explicitly label it as a partial sketch.
- Make actor lifetime, reconnect/token-refresh flow, backoff, stale suppression, and request/reply correlation all line up in the same example.
- Either use the `:connected` sub-states for something real or remove them from the example.
notes:
PR opened: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/282

Rewrote spec/Pattern-WebSocket.md's worked example. Five inconsistencies fixed:

1. Actor lifetime — :invoke moved to a hierarchical :active parent state; actor now spans :connecting/:authenticating/:connected.
2. Reconnect refreshes opts — :invoke's :data fn re-reads from parent's :data on every :active entry; explicit :ws/refresh-token slot at parent level.
3. Exponential backoff + stale-suppression — fn-form :after delay (min (* base-ms (Math/pow 2 retries)) max-backoff-ms); connection-epoch via socket-id + :current-socket? guard.
4. Request/reply correlation fully worked through — :register-request action, :in-flight map, :dispatch-later timeout, :ws/received branches on :request-id.
5. Decorative :connected sub-states removed.

Plus: guide ch.05's Pattern-WebSocket subsection updated to match; two new anti-patterns added; :ws/error lifted to :active for uniform leaf inheritance; vector :entry / :action forms (Spec 005 invalid) consolidated into named compound actions.

LoC delta 314 → 401 (+27%, under +30% cap). mkdocs --strict clean.

Suggestion bead filed during rewrite: rf2-8pls (Pattern-Boot has the same :entry [vector] violation Spec 005 forbids).
close_reason: Closed by PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/282.

title: Research: Causa UX/UI design (follow-up to rf2-buor)
description: Mike (2026-05-11): the high-level design doc (rf2-buor / findings/re-frame-10x-v2-design.md) sets WHAT Causa is and WHICH features ship. Now we need a careful UX/UI pass — the actual interface design: how panels are laid out, how users navigate, how the causality graph reads at a glance, how the AI co-pilot panel feels, the visual language (typography, density, colour, spacing), the interaction patterns (hover/click/drag/keyboard), the empty states, the 'first 30 seconds with Causa' onboarding experience. Output: findings/causa-ux-design.md (local-only). Human-facing surface: human ergonomics is the dominant constraint. References: findings/re-frame-10x-v2-design.md (the WHAT), spec/Tool-Pair.md (what's queryable), spec/009-Instrumentation.md (the trace bus surface). Should resolve §10.10 (is the causality graph the right hero feature?) with a concrete design proposal.
notes: **#13 Voice STT LOCKED 2026-05-12 (Mike): NO VOICE AT v1.0.** Keyboard only. Web Speech API quality is patchy; cloud STT adds cost+latency; v1.0 scope is already large. Pure additive feature — can ship in v1.1+ if users demand. No mic permission prompts in v1.0 onboarding (privacy-default-friendly).
close_reason: Research phase complete — findings doc moved to tools/10x/spec/findings/causa-ux-design.md as permanent committed record (via rf2-1lls PR #431). UX/UI design decisions decomposed into tools/10x/spec/007-UX-IA.md + DESIGN-RATIONALE.md. All §10 UX questions locked 2026-05-12.

title: Implement re-frame2-template — scaffolding tool for new re-frame2 apps (under tools/)
description: Equivalent of v1's day8/re-frame-template (https://github.com/day8/re-frame-template). Generates a fresh re-frame2 application skeleton. Substrate choice (Reagent / UIx / Helix), modern CLJS workflow (shadow-cljs), basic reg-event / reg-sub / view example, optional stories scaffold (once tools/story stabilises), deps.edn wired against day8/re-frame2-* coords. Lands at tools/template/ per the rf2-e6g9 /tools convention. Could be a clj-new template, a deps-new template, or a small CLI — pick at IMPL time. The template is the front door for new users — important enough to ship alongside the first Clojars artefact (v0.0.1.alpha). Mike (2026-05-11).
notes: Implemented in PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/280. Picked Option A (clj-new template) per the bead lean. Three substrate variants (Reagent / UIx / Helix). JVM test exercises all three end-to-end; clojure -P deps-parse step skips gracefully until alpha publish lands on Clojars.

title: re-frame2-story Stage 3 — Runtime (frame, args, decorators, loaders)
description: Stage 3 of rf2-u6fb. Implement the runtime per IMPL-SPEC §5 and §10 (Stage 3). Scope: per-variant frame allocation; effective-args resolution (precedence: global < story < mode < variant < cell — IMPL-SPEC §5.2); decorator composition order (IMPL-SPEC §5.3); the four-phase loader lifecycle with :loaders-complete-when predicate evaluation (IMPL-SPEC §5.4); error-projection into :assertions (IMPL-SPEC §5.5); snapshot-identity computation (IMPL-SPEC §5.6) including the (variant × mode × substrate) cell composition Stage 3 must pick. Files: tools/story/src/tools/story/runtime/{frame,args,decorators,loaders,play,snapshot-id,source-coord}.cljs and additions to re-frame.story for run-variant / reset-variant / watch-variant / variants-with-tags / variant->edn / snapshot-identity. Stage 3 also picks the async-return shape for run-variant (Promise vs manifold.deferred) — IMPL-SPEC §13.2 #1. Blocked by rf2-32dk.
close_reason:
Landed in PR #276 (https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/276). Stage 3 runtime ships:

- run-variant / reset-variant / watch-variant / snapshot-identity / destroy-variant! / configure! on re-frame.story
- Async return shape (IMPL-SPEC §13.2 #1) locked: JS Promise (CLJS) / CompletableFuture (JVM), abstracted in re-frame.story.async
- Per-variant frame allocation via existing rf/reg-frame (:preset :story; :fx-overrides for fx-override decorators)
- Args precedence: global < story < mode < variant < cell-overrides with deep-merge
- Decorator composition: three kinds classified + ordered (story before variant; hiccup outermost-first)
- Loader lifecycle as a re-frame.machines machine at id :rf.story.lifecycle/machine; states :pre-mount → :mounting → :loading → :ready (with :error branch)
- Snapshot-identity content hash (8-char hex, canonical-key :rf/snapshot-canonical-v1) stable across JVM/CLJS
- Error projection into [:rf.story/assertions] via trace listener on :rf.error/handler-exception

Tests: 61 JVM (139 assertions) + 5 CLJS smokes (in the full 428-test suite). Elision contract preserved; mkdocs clean.

Stage 3 leaves unresolved:
- Full :loaders-complete-when semantics (registered-event id + vector-of-events forms) — routed through Stage 5's assertion runtime hook
- Render phase (Stage 4)
- Play sequence + :rf.assert/* (Stage 5)

title: re-frame2-story Stage 1 — IMPL-SPEC + architectural decisions
description: First stage of rf2-u6fb. Produce tools/story/IMPL-SPEC.md crystallising: (a) the 7 §6 decisions from rf2-m6tu (resolved by Mike-delegation 2026-05-11: separate MCP jar; substrate-failures-inline with :substrates opt-out; record-not-throw assertions; :loaders-complete-when predicate; workspace local-storage + transit-save; registration dev-only DCE; re-frame-10x embed), (b) v1/v1.1/v2 tiering, (c) substrate choice for Story's own UI shell (lean: Reagent), (d) namespace + file layout, (e) sub-stage bead breakdown. Touches: tools/story/ (new dir), tools/story/IMPL-SPEC.md (new), tools/story/README.md (new overview), tools/story/deps.edn (skeleton). Optionally file stage-2 through stage-8 beads. References spec/007-Stories.md, findings/re-frame-2-story-feature-set.md, findings/re-frame-2-story-sota-refinement.md.
