title: [epic] Implement re-frame2-story: SOTA component playground for re-frame2
description: Parent epic for re-frame2-story implementation. Lands at tools/story/. Goal: state-of-the-art Storybook-class component playground that honours re-frame2's spec (007-Stories.md) + Phase 1 research (rf2-m6tu) + Phase 2 SOTA refinement (rf2-94b0). Mike (2026-05-11) delegated architectural decisions. Eight stages: (1) IMPL-SPEC + decisions, (2) Core authoring surface, (3) Runtime (frame/args/decorators/loaders), (4) Render shell, (5) Assertions + play, (6) SOTA features (perf ribbon, layout-debug, a11y, design-tokens, QR, multi-substrate, 10x embed), (7) tools/story-mcp/ separate jar, (8) Examples + guide integration. Each stage gets its own bead. Don't ship until excellent.

title: Rename artefact coords from day8/re-frame-2-* to day8/re-frame2-* (drop the dash)
description: Drop the dash in artefact coord names: day8/re-frame-2-* -> day8/re-frame2-*. Aligns coord names with the project's prose convention (README and docs say 're-frame2' as a unified brand, not 're-frame 2' as a version-qualified lib). Pre-alpha so no Clojars publish has happened — rename is free externally. Touches: implementation/*/deps.edn, top-level shadow-cljs.edn / deps.edn coordinators, spec/Conventions.md (naming section), spec/API.md, spec/Spec-Schemas.md, spec/000-Vision.md, docs/release-process.md, docs/guide/, CHANGELOG.md, .github/workflows/*, verify-version-lockstep.sh, examples/*/deps.edn, README.md Project layout, tools/README.md (just landed; coord pattern). Code namespaces (re-frame.core etc.) DO NOT contain '2' and need no change. Don't touch .beads/issues.jsonl. The phrase 're-frame v2' in version-qualifier prose is distinct from the artefact name and stays. Confirmed by Mike 2026-05-11.
notes:
PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/273 (drop-dash-rf2-pcy1).

Renamed 85 files, 573 insertions / 573 deletions. Coord prefix day8/re-frame-2-* -> day8/re-frame2-* applied to: core (day8/re-frame2), three adapters (reagent, uix, helix), seven per-feature artefacts (schemas, machines, routing, flows, http, ssr, epoch), five tools coords (story, story-mcp, machines-viz, machines-viz-mcp, 10x), plus generic prose references. After sweep, only remaining 're-frame-2' matches are in .beads/issues.jsonl (deliberately preserved). Verifications: clojure -P clean; JVM core tests 183/824 0 failures; elision PASS; mkdocs build (incl. --strict) clean — INFO message count unchanged before/after.

title: Suggestion: guide needs a short 'Where next?' exit chapter or close-out section
description:
When the reader finishes the guide today, they reach the end of chapter 12 (Routing) — which terminates with a 'Next: Spec 012, chapter 07' pointer. There's no overall 'now what?' moment. The reader has built the mental model; the guide should hand them off explicitly to:

- the examples directory (counter, login, routing, ssr, managed-http-counter, realworld scaffold)
- spec/Runtime-Architecture.md and spec/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md (the system-level bridge — guide/README.md already names them, but the chapters don't reach them naturally)
- spec/API.md for daily reference
- spec/Pattern-*.md for shape-matched feature recipes
- the migration agent if coming from re-frame v1

Recommendation: either add a short '13 — Where next?' chapter, or expand the close-out paragraph in ch.09 (the natural end of the philosophical thread). Currently the exit story is buried in guide/README.md, which the reader has likely left far behind by ch.12.
notes:
Closed by PR #264 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/264

Added chapter 13 'Where to go next' as a one-screen exit pointer.
Option A chosen (new chapter) over Option B (section in ch.12) — twelve
chapters and the explicit core/deep-dive structure made a dedicated
wrap-up the cleaner read. mkdocs build clean. README.md, ch.12 'Next',
and mkdocs.yml nav updated to reach the new chapter.

title: Suggestion: chapter 12 (Routing) should move into the core path or earn its place at chapter 8
description:
Chapter 12 (Routing) is currently filed as an optional deep dive, but routing is almost universally required for SPAs. The chapter is also 547 lines — the longest in the guide. Two problems compound: a programmer reading the core path 01-07 doesn't see routing introduced, and the deep-dive position pairs an essential surface with optional appendices like ch.08 (re-frame v1 migration) and ch.09 (philosophy).

Recommendation: either (a) promote routing to chapter 8 of the core path with a shorter narrative, deferring the route ranking algorithm + nav-token contract + can-leave protocol details to Spec 012, OR (b) split into a short 'Routing basics' core chapter and a longer 'Routing reference' deep dive. The current 547-line chapter blends both audiences.
notes: Closed by PR #265 https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/265 — picked option B (reposition in place). Added explicit basics/reference split with a divider header at the midpoint of ch.12. No file split, no renumbering, no cross-link churn. The worked-example threads basics AND reference together, so a clean cut would have created awkward forward-references. Net change +8 lines (divider header + index paragraph + two-half-structure pointer in intro). mkdocs build clean.

title: Suggestion: converge guide chapters on the counter as the worked-example thread
description:
Chapters 02 (counter), 03 (login/auth+cart), 04 (counter+split-counter), 05 (login flow), 06 (article + counter + realworld), 07 (auth+routing+server), 12 (realworld). Each chapter uses a different toy example. The counter introduced in ch.02 doesn't survive past ch.04; ch.03 introduces login, ch.05 reuses login but differently, ch.06 introduces article+managed-http-counter+realworld.

Recommendation: keep the counter as the running thread through ch.02-04 (introduce events, then effects-as-data, then frames) and bring it back in ch.06 via managed-http-counter (already exists in examples/). Use login only for state-machines chapter (its native shape) and realworld for chapter 12 routing. This means rewriting ch.03's worked example from login to a counter-with-localStorage or counter-with-fetch shape.
notes: Landed in PR #267 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/267. Converged ch.02-04 + ch.06 onto the counter; login confined to ch.05. Word-count deltas minimal (+21/+24/+31 across three chapters). mkdocs build clean.

title: Suggestion: refine guide chapter 11 to be the third-pillar tooling story for new readers
description:
Chapter 11 (Devtools and pair tools) is currently shaped as a contract reference — listing every API, every trace-event op-type, every failure mode. That's right for someone implementing a tool, but wrong for someone reading the guide cold. The README's third pillar (Tooling is first-class) deserves a narrative chapter that earns the reader's interest before drowning them in op-types.

Recommendation: split into two chapters, or rewrite ch.11 with the contract reference relegated to spec/Tool-Pair.md and a shorter narrative chapter focused on what the reader gets for free (click-to-source, scrub epochs, AI pair-tools attaching), with the contract reference moved out. The current chapter reads as Spec 009/Tool-Pair narrative-ified rather than as guide voice.
notes:
Discovered during guide audit 2026-05-11. The README pitches three pillars; the guide delivers strongly on pillars 1 (spec-first surfaces) and 2 (views-derivative), but pillar 3 (tooling) is currently a wall-of-contract that newcomers won't read.
Shipped in PR #266 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/266. Rewrote ch.11 top-down: opens with the third-pillar tooling pitch (trace bus + epochs + source-coord stamping as the substrate); walks the four tools by name (re-frame-pair2, re-frame-2-story, re-frame-10x v2, machine visualisers); states the peer-listener punchline; shows a concrete 20-line debug-panel example. Original contract material (destroyed-frame tables, failure-mode enumerations, callback signatures) preserved as clearly-marked Reference sections at the bottom — nothing deleted. Renamed chapter to 'Tooling' in mkdocs.yml + guide README. Updated ch.04 cross-link anchor. mkdocs build clean. Word count 3317 -> 4430.
close_reason: Shipped in PR #266

title: IMPL-SPEC §11.1 over-specifies coerce-context-value deletion — breaks classic Reagent path
description:
Stage 4-F's bead description (rf2-6hyy item #2) and IMPL-SPEC §11.1 instruct deleting re-frame.adapter.context/coerce-context-value on the rationale that Stage 4-D's narrowed convert-prop-value no longer over-stringifies keyword Provider values. But:

- The classic Reagent bridge (day8/re-frame-2-reagent, at implementation/adapters/reagent/) still uses stock reagent.impl.template/convert-prop-value, which DOES stringify keyword props. The bridge is meant to coexist with the slim adapter (per IMPL-SPEC §11.4 / §13).
- BOTH adapters register the SAME re-frame.views/current-frame as :adapter/current-frame (reagent.cljs:147 and reagent_slim.cljs:174). The fn is shared.
- Under the bridge: (.-context cmp) returns a stringified keyword. The shared current-frame impl needs coerce-context-value (string -> keyword) for the React-context tier to function.
- Under the slim adapter: (.-context cmp) returns the keyword directly; coerce-context-value is a no-op on keywords (returns them as-is). The shared impl works for slim with or without the coerce call — but for classic it is load-bearing.

Therefore coerce-context-value is NOT vestigial — it is still load-bearing for the classic adapter path. IMPL-SPEC §11.1's proposed (when (keyword? v) v) fast-path would break rf2-22ds Scenario 4-7 under the classic adapter (subscribe / dispatch under [rf/frame-provider {:frame :tenant}] would silently route to :rf/default again).

PROPOSED RESOLUTIONS (pick one):

(A) Delete coerce-context-value but split the shared current-frame impl into per-adapter impls. Slim adapter registers a keyword-only impl; classic adapter keeps the coerce-tolerant impl. Tradeoff: two impls, two namespaces, slight duplication; per-adapter publishing of :adapter/current-frame.

(B) Keep coerce-context-value as defensive coercion shared between both adapters. Cost: ~10 lines of code; not actually vestigial. Update IMPL-SPEC §11.1 to call out that the function survives the slim rewrite because the classic adapter still needs it; only the *forced-defensive comments* (IMPL-SPEC §11.2) can be cleaned up. Stage 4-F still cleans up the comments around it.

(C) Defer the deletion to a future stage that retires the classic bridge entirely (post-1.0; per IMPL-SPEC §11.4 / §13).

Stage 4-F encountered this when reading scenario-3-context-corrupted-emits-structured-error (implementation/adapters/reagent/test/re_frame/frame_provider_context_cljs_test.cljs:210) plus scenarios 4-7 which mount via [rf/frame-provider {:frame :tenant}] under stock Reagent and assert subscribe/dispatch route correctly to the named frame.

Filed per the file-spec-beads-for-implementation-findings directive — Stage 4-F is paused awaiting direction on (A)/(B)/(C). The throw-on-call shims (item #1 in rf2-6hyy Stage 4-F) are unaffected and can proceed; only the coerce-context-value deletion (item #2) is blocked.
notes:
Discovered during Stage 4-F (rf2-6hyy) implementation. Worktree: re-frame2-stage-4f branch reagent-slim-stage-4f-shims.

Concrete failing scenarios under (when (keyword? v) v) simplification:
- rf2-22ds Scenario 4 (subscribe routes via React context, classic adapter) — would silently route to :rf/default.
- rf2-22ds Scenario 5 (dispatch routes via React context, classic adapter) — same.
- rf2-22ds Scenario 6 (StrictMode) — same.
- rf2-22ds Scenario 7 (concurrent renders) — same.
- M-41 plain-fn warning detection — would stop firing on plain fns under [:> Provider {:value :tenant}] interop under classic.

IMPL-SPEC sections affected: §11.1 (proposes deletion), §11.2 (comments reference rf2-d4sf stringification rationale), §13.1 (migration path).

Spec files referencing coerce-context-value:
- spec/006-ReactiveSubstrate.md:740
- spec/009-Instrumentation.md:690 (omitted ref)
- spec/002-Frames.md:501 (omitted ref)

No code edits made in this worktree yet — investigation only.
close_reason: Closed

title: Adapter current-component late-bind hook (slim adapter drop-in prerequisite)
description:
Surfaced by Stage 4-D agent (PR #246). The slim adapter (reagent-slim) is structurally complete but not yet usable via (rf/init! reagent-slim/adapter) because re-frame.views/current-frame reads stock Reagent's current-component directly.

## The problem

views.cljs's resolver reads stock-Reagent's current-component as one of its resolution paths. Slim adapter exposes its own current-component (reagent2.core/current-component) but views.cljs doesn't know to consult it.

## The fix

Add a new late-bind hook key in re-frame.views/current-frame so the resolver flow becomes:
  dynamic var → adapter.current-component (late-bind) → nil

Slim adapter sets the hook to reagent2.core/current-component.
Bridge (reagent-classic) sets the hook to reagent.core/current-component.

## Touches

- implementation/core/src/re_frame/views.cljs — replace direct Reagent call with late-bind lookup
- implementation/core/src/re_frame/late_bind.cljc — register the new hook key
- implementation/adapters/reagent/src/re_frame/adapter/reagent.cljs — install hook on init
- implementation/adapters/reagent-slim/src/re_frame/adapter/reagent_slim.cljs — install hook on init
- Tests covering both adapters' current-component resolution

## Acceptance

After this lands:
- (rf/init! reagent-slim/adapter) becomes drop-in functional
- (rf/init! reagent/adapter) (existing classic bridge) still works via the same seam
- Tests pass on both adapters

## P2 — gates Stage 4-G drop-in story
notes:
PR #249: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/249

Implementation summary:
- Added :adapter/current-component late-bind hook key
- views.cljs drops static :require [reagent.core]; private current-component helper does the hook lookup
- Three call sites refactored: current-frame, reagent-component-token, maybe-warn-plain-fn-under-non-default-frame!
- Classic bridge wires hook to reagent.core/current-component
- Slim adapter wires hook to reagent2.core/current-component
- Tests cover both adapters + no-adapter / nil-hook fall-through
- All 346 CLJS + 183 JVM + 187 browser tests pass; 4 elision schema sentinels still ABSENT under :advanced + goog.DEBUG=false
close_reason: Closed

title: Spec-Schemas.md fixture/calls schema is too narrow — types only :machine-transition, but corpus uses 5 other :call ops
description:
Surfaced during PR #244 (rf2-275x + rf2-6ejx).

Evidence:
- spec/Spec-Schemas.md:1831-1840 types :fixture/calls as a vector of records each with :call = :machine-transition (literal).
- The live corpus uses six :call operators: :machine-transition, :match-url, :route-url, :round-trip, :assert-rank-greater, :render-to-string.
- The fixture-runner at implementation/core/test/re_frame/conformance_test.clj:619-714 dispatches on all six.
- :expected-fx-emitted is also declared in :fixture/expect (Spec-Schemas.md:1850) but is unused in the live corpus (24 occurrences of :effects-routed; 0 of :expected-fx-emitted). Schema should drop or document the deprecation.

Why it matters:
- Implementors who lint their fixture files against the published schema will reject every routing, SSR, and routing-rank fixture in the live corpus.
- Spec-Schemas.md is the canonical schema reference; drift between schema and corpus undermines the conformance contract.

Suggested fix:
- Widen the :call enum (or use a multimethod-style dispatched schema keyed on :call) so all six operators validate.
- Either restore :expected-fx-emitted to active use or remove it from :fixture/expect.

Labels: documentation, spec-review
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Mike Thompson
comments.text:
Resolved by PR #245: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/245

- Fix 1: widened :fixture/calls to a Malli :multi dispatched on :call, with one per-op record schema for each of the six operators (:machine-transition, :match-url, :route-url, :round-trip, :assert-rank-greater, :render-to-string). Per-op shapes match the fixture-runner's case dispatch.
- Fix 2: removed :expected-fx-emitted from :fixture/expect — corpus + runner + conformance README all use :effects-routed exclusively.

title: Fix mkdocs Build site failure — pygments AttributeError
description:
Discovered while verifying rf2-xmmh (the upload-artifact transitive SHA-pin fix). Once the org-policy block was removed (PR #232 / branch fix-docs-ci-rf2-xmmh), the next workflow step `Build site` (`mkdocs build`) failed with:

```
File "/opt/hostedtoolcache/Python/3.12.13/x64/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pymdownx/highlight.py", line 402, in highlight
  formatter = html_formatter(
File "/opt/hostedtoolcache/Python/3.12.13/x64/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pygments/formatters/html.py", line 434, in __init__
  self.filename = html.escape(self._decodeifneeded(options.get('filename', '')))
File "/opt/hostedtoolcache/Python/3.12.13/x64/lib/python3.12/html/__init__.py", line 19, in escape
  s = s.replace("&", "&") # Must be done first!
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'replace'
```

Likely a code fence in the docs is passing a `filename=` option whose value resolves to `None`, or pymdownx-highlight + pygments versions are incompatible. Reproducible run: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/actions/runs/25634461356

Fix paths to investigate:
1. Pin a known-good pygments version in `requirements.txt`.
2. Find the offending fenced code block (search for code fences with empty `title=` / `filename=` attrs in spec/ or docs/) and fix the source.
3. Bump pymdownx to a version that handles `None` defensively.

Workflow only triggers on push-to-main / workflow_dispatch, so the failure is invisible on PRs. Worth adding a `pull_request:` trigger for changes under `docs/`, `spec/`, or `mkdocs.yml` so we don't regress silently again.

This bead is the follow-up to rf2-xmmh — out of scope for that fix per Mike's explicit "Don't change mkdocs.yml or anything about the docs build itself" constraint.
notes:
Fix in https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/235 (branch fix-mkdocs-build-rf2-4jaz).

Root cause confirmed: pygments 2.20.0 (released 2026-03-29) added an html.escape() call around the HtmlFormatter 'filename' option (pygments/formatters/html.py L434). pymdown-extensions 10.11.2 always passes filename=title in highlight.py, where 'title' defaults to None for fenced code blocks without an explicit title — so html.escape(None) crashes the build.

Path chosen: Path 1 (pin pygments). Pinned pygments==2.19.2 in requirements.txt. 2.19.2 still uses bare _decodeifneeded(...) which tolerates None. No source markdown changes were necessary — the title=None comes from pymdownx, not from our content.

Verified locally: 'pip install -r requirements.txt && rm -rf docs/spec && cp -r spec docs/spec && mkdocs build' completes with exit 0 and produces site/guide/05-state-machines/index.html (the page that was failing).

Verified on CI: workflow_dispatch run https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/actions/runs/25634649649 — 'MkDocs build' job and 'Build site' step both green. The 'Deploy to GitHub Pages' job fails downstream with HttpError 404 ('Ensure GitHub Pages has been enabled') — this is unrelated to rf2-4jaz; the workflow comment block already documents that Pages must be configured in repo Settings → Pages → Source = 'GitHub Actions'. Out of scope here.

Lingering concern (not blocking this fix): the bead's bonus suggestion to add a pull_request: trigger on changes under docs/, spec/, mkdocs.yml is still valid — without it, regressions in the build will remain invisible on PRs. Worth its own bead. NOT done here because the constraints for this bead said 'DON'T touch .github/workflows/docs.yml — that's settled by rf2-xmmh'.
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix docs workflow — actions/upload-artifact@v4 not allowed (transitive)
description:
Every push to main since session start has failed the docs workflow. Error: 'The action actions/upload-artifact@v4 is not allowed in day8/re-frame2 because all actions must be pinned to a full-length commit SHA.'

The .github/workflows/docs.yml file uses actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3 (SHA-pinned) and actions/deploy-pages@v4 (SHA-pinned). Both pinned correctly. The failure is a TRANSITIVE call: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3 internally calls actions/upload-artifact@v4 which is not in the org's SHA-pinned allowlist.

## Fix paths

1. **Pin transitive dependency** by switching to a SHA-pinned fork or a custom upload step that bypasses upload-artifact.
2. **Use a different artifact upload mechanism** — direct HTTP upload via the GitHub API (would lose some convenience of the official action).
3. **Update the upload-pages-artifact version** to a release that uses a SHA-pinned upload-artifact@v4 (if such a version exists). Worth checking actions/upload-pages-artifact's release history.
4. **Bypass via custom workflow** that does the page-build + page-deploy steps without the artifact intermediary. Less standard.

Path 3 is the cleanest if a recent enough release exists. Path 1 is the fallback.

## Out of scope

- Don't update the allowlist (org admin only; Mike's call).
- Don't change anything about MkDocs build itself; the failure is in the upload step.

## Reporting

PR with the fix; verify docs workflow goes green on the PR's CI run before merge.
notes:
PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/232 (branch fix-docs-ci-rf2-xmmh)

Path chosen: **Path 2** — bumped `actions/upload-pages-artifact` from v3 (SHA `56afc609…`) to v5.0.0 (SHA `fc324d35…`). v5 transitively pins `actions/upload-artifact` to a SHA (`bbbca2dd…` / v7.0.0), satisfying the org policy. `actions/deploy-pages@v4.0.5` has no transitive `uses:` and stays on its existing SHA.

Verification (workflow_dispatch run https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/actions/runs/25634461356 against the fix branch):
- "Set up job" — SUCCESS (org-policy gate now passes; the rf2-xmmh failure mode is gone)
- "Checkout", "Set up Python", "Install MkDocs + plugins", "Stage spec/" — all SUCCESS
- "Build site" — FAILURE (pygments `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'replace'`)

The remaining failure is in `mkdocs build` itself, which is **out of scope** for rf2-xmmh per Mike's explicit "Don't change mkdocs.yml or anything about the docs build itself" constraint. Filed as **rf2-4jaz** (linked discovered-from rf2-xmmh).

Caveat: the docs workflow only triggers on push-to-main / workflow_dispatch (not pull_request), so the verification was a manual `gh workflow run docs.yml --ref fix-docs-ci-rf2-xmmh` rather than the PR's own CI. The PR's `tests` workflow ran and is unrelated.
close_reason: Closed

title: Implement boundary-validation interceptor (:spec/validate-at-boundary) wired to set-schema-validator! seam
description:
Per Spec 010 §Production builds (lines 130-148) and the rf2-qnxf Malli bundle-cost audit (findings/malli-bundle-cost-audit.md §5): the boundary-validation interceptor is documented in Spec 010 but not yet implemented (zero matches for 'validate-at-boundary' / 'spec/strict' / 'boundary-validation' across implementation/ as of 2026-05-11).

## Why

Spec 010 §Production builds calls the boundary-validation interceptor 'the only legitimate prod-validation surface' (rf2-9qsk's resolution). Without it implemented, re-frame2 apps have NO production validation at all — schema validation is dev-only and elides under :advanced.

## Scope

- Implement a `:spec/validate-at-boundary` interceptor (or with whatever final name Mike picks per Spec 010 §Boundary-validation interceptor naming open question).
- Re-uses the handler's existing `:spec` metadata — does NOT introduce a parallel schema (per Spec 010 L143-144).
- In dev: no-op (step-1 validation already runs).
- In production: runs the same `:spec` check inline regardless of the global elision flag.
- Routes through the validator-fn registered via rf2-froe's `set-schema-validator!` seam — works under Malli or any substituted validator.
- Failures emit `:rf.error/schema-validation-failure :where :event` (same path as dev-mode step-1 failures).

## Out of scope

- The set-schema-validator! seam itself (rf2-froe).
- The interceptor name decision (open question in Spec 010 §Boundary-validation interceptor naming).

## Dependencies

- rf2-froe (set-schema-validator!) — ideally lands first so the boundary interceptor uses the seam, not a hard-coded malli.core import.

## References

- Spec 010 §Production builds (lines 130-151)
- Spec 010 §Boundary-validation interceptor naming (open question)
- rf2-9qsk (closed — strict-elision ratification that removed the re-enable-validation-in-prod escape hatches)
- findings/malli-bundle-cost-audit.md §5
close_reason: Closed

title: Implement set-schema-validator! per Spec 010 §Non-Malli validators
description:
Per the rf2-qnxf Malli bundle-cost audit (findings/malli-bundle-cost-audit.md §3.7 / §4): implement Spec 010's documented `(rf/set-schema-validator! f)` extension point. Today re-frame.schemas hard-codes `(resolve 'malli.core/validate)` and `(resolve 'malli.core/explain)`; the spec calls for a single registration that swaps in any `(fn [schema value] -> result)` validator.

## Why

The audit measured a ~24 KB gzipped Malli surface for typical re-frame2 apps (scenario C: schemas-required + reg-app-schema + :spec on every reg-* = 121.5 KB vs scenario A baseline 91.7 KB = +29.8 KB; the malli.core delta is 121.5 - 97.2 = ~24 KB). That cost is bounded by the user's `(:require [malli.core])` keeping the ns reachable. Implementing the documented seam lets opt-out users avoid the cost; default users keep Malli with no behaviour change.

## Scope

- Add a `validator-fn` atom (per-frame? or process-wide? — spec says 'one validator fn per frame') to re-frame.schemas.
- Public API: `(rf/set-schema-validator! f)` (forwards through late-bind so re-frame.core can re-export).
- Default validator: today's malli-validate* / malli-explain* pair behind a single fn that returns nil-on-conform or {:explanation ...} on fail per Spec 010's documented shape.
- Update re-frame.schemas/validate-app-db! / validate-event! / validate-sub-return! / validate-cofx! to call `@validator-fn` rather than malli-validate*/malli-explain* directly.
- Demote metosin/malli to an optional dep of the schemas artefact's deps.edn (mirror how re-frame v1 handles re-com — declared but not transitively pulled).
- Document the seam in Spec 010 §Why Malli with a no-op-validator example.
- Tests: register a no-op validator; assert validate-* fns route through it.

## Out of scope

- Boundary-validation interceptor implementation (separate bead — see follow-ups in findings/malli-bundle-cost-audit.md §5).
- `re-frame.epoch` and `re-frame.http-managed` Malli routing (separate beads).

## Validation

- Bundle-cost regression: schema-using app with `(rf/set-schema-validator! (fn [_ _] nil))` registered before any reg-app-schema call should ship without malli.core in the bundle. Measure via the harness at examples/scratch/malli_bundle_test/.

## References

- Spec 010 §Non-Malli validators — the validator-fn extension point (lines 255-280)
- Spec 010 §Resolved decisions — Non-Malli library support (lines 322-324)
- findings/malli-bundle-cost-audit.md §3.7 / §4 / §5
- Surface inventory: findings/malli-bundle-cost-audit.md §2 / Appendix B

title: coerce-context-value drops keyword namespace via Reagent prop conversion
description:
Reagent's reagent.impl.template/convert-prop-value calls (name kw) on named prop values. For a namespaced keyword like :foo/bar, (name :foo/bar) returns "bar" — the namespace is dropped before React sees the prop. The shared re-frame.adapter.context/coerce-context-value keywordises the read-side string back to a keyword but cannot reconstruct the lost namespace, so a [:> Provider {:value :foo/bar}] reaches the read side as :bar.

Discovered while writing the rf2-22ds frame-provider runtime tests: every nested-frame scenario with a namespaced frame-id (e.g. :rf.22ds-1/inner) failed because the round-trip stripped the namespace and the resulting unprefixed keyword pointed at no registered frame. Tests work around it by using unnamespaced frame-ids — but real apps targeting multi-frame architectures (Spec 002) will hit this when they pick namespaced frame-ids for collision safety.

Repro: render

  [:> (.-Provider adapter-context/frame-context) {:value :foo/bar}
    [some-reg-view'd-component]]

then inside the component check (rf/current-frame) — observed :bar, expected :foo/bar.

Possible fixes:
- coerce-context-value rounds back through (keyword (or-namespace-cache string)) — needs the namespace to be carried alongside the prop
- emit a known-shape struct or fully-qualified string ('foo/bar') as the Provider :value so (keyword s) round-trips
- bypass Reagent's prop-conversion via React.createElement directly (the adapter-context/provider-element fn already does this)

Filed against rf2-22ds (frame-provider context coverage). Documents the gap; not fixed in this PR per the bead's 'don't fix bugs revealed by tests' rule.
close_reason: Closed

title: Investigate Malli's bundle-size cost; identify DCE strategies (or alternatives)
description:
Mike's concern 2026-05-11: re-frame2 mandates Malli (per Spec 010 schemas). Malli is substantial code, and it's very difficult to DCE via Closure compiler — meaning every re-frame2 app pays the Malli bundle cost even when:
- C-000.35 strict elision means schema validation MUST NOT alter production state (validation calls eliminated).
- The boundary-validation interceptor is the only legitimate prod-validation surface.

If validation is gone in production but Malli's CODE remains in the bundle, that's a wasted dependency cost.

This tension affects:
- The 'slim' claim of reagent-slim — bundle reductions there are partially offset if Malli stays large.
- Any re-frame2 app's bundle size.
- Adoption framing — schemas-mandatory + 50-100KB-bundle-cost is a real ask.

## Scope

### Phase A — Measurement

1. Build a minimal re-frame2 app on reagent-classic + Malli schemas + advanced compilation.
2. Build the same app WITHOUT Malli (stub the schema fns).
3. Measure bundle delta (gzipped):
   - With full Malli surface used (registry, validate, explain, coerce).
   - With minimal Malli surface (just registry + validate).
   - With Malli imported but no validation calls reachable.
4. Inspect the post-Closure bundle to identify which Malli code persists vs eliminates.

### Phase B — Surface analysis

What Malli features does re-frame2 actually use today? Audit:
-   — validates event vector
-   — validates sub return
-   — validates fx args
-   — validates cofx data
-  (path-based) — validates app-db slices
- Boundary-validation interceptor — the legit prod path

Each of these uses some Malli function. Inventory the actual function set used. Probably much smaller than Malli's full surface.

### Phase C — DCE strategies (research)

Investigate which of these pay off:
1. **Build-time-only Malli**: schemas defined as Clojure data (no Malli runtime); a dev-time test pipeline validates against Malli; production bundle has zero Malli code.
2. **Pin to malli-core only** (Malli's minimal subset, if such a thing exists).
3. **Split production paths**:  namespace shadow that emits no-op functions in production, replacing dev's Malli calls. Closure DCE eliminates the dev-Malli-calling code path entirely.
4. **Goog-define gating**: every Malli call site guarded by  or  constant. Closure DCE eliminates the  branch.
5. **Lazy-load Malli for the dev-tools tier only** (10x, devtools UI). Dev users pay the cost; prod doesn't.

### Phase D — Recommendation

Given the measurements + surface analysis + DCE strategy options, recommend:
- Status quo (accept Malli cost as the validation-mandate price)
- Apply strategy N (specific DCE technique)
- Make Malli optional + support alternative schema libs (clojure.spec, plumatic.schema, etc.)
- Build re-frame2's own minimal schema layer

## Output

 with:
- Measured bundle deltas (KB gzipped, before/after several scenarios)
- Surface inventory (Malli fns actually used)
- DCE strategy matrix (cost / risk / coverage)
- Recommendation + rationale
- Follow-up beads for any chosen strategy

## Why P2

- Affects the slim claim of reagent-slim materially.
- Pre-1.0 — we can still change schema-lib mandate if needed.
- Bundle size is one of re-frame2's competitive axes.

## Out of scope

- **Don't change Spec 010** until investigation lands.
- **Don't change Malli mandate** until Mike picks a strategy.
- **Don't write code** beyond a minimal measurement harness.
- Audit + recommendation only.

## Dispatchability

Direction-set; suitable for a research-heavy background agent. Touches:
- A minimal measurement harness (probably a throwaway example app).
-  (output).
- bd beads for any chosen strategy as follow-ups.

No spec changes; no implementation changes.
close_reason: Closed

title: Spec 005: adopt XState 'after' semantics — state-level timed transitions (multi-stage, dynamic delays, guards, no-invoke)
description:
Mike's directive 2026-05-11: adopt full XState 'after' semantics in Spec 005. Four expressivity gaps to close as one unified surface:

1. Multi-stage 'after' — multiple timed transitions per state (e.g. warn at 5s, fail at 30s).
2. Dynamic delays — delay can resolve via subscription, context, or function lookup (XState calls this 'delays config').
3. Guards on timeout transitions — :cond or :when slot on the after transition target.
4. State-level pure timed transitions — 'after' without any :invoke ('splash screen' pattern).

Target shape (rough; subject to spec design):

  {:after {5000  {:target :warn   :actions [:log-slow]}
           30000 {:target :offline :cond :no-partial-progress?}
           [:sub :configurable-timeout] :alt-path}}

Relationship to existing beads:
- **rf2-1lop (PR #194 shipped)**: simpler :timeout-ms on :invoke. Decision needed: deprecate (replace with after) or keep as sugar that compiles to state-level after. My lean: keep as sugar — clean for the canonical 'invoke times out' case; advanced users reach for state-level after.
- **rf2-gn80 (XState parity umbrella, P3)**: this bead is one chunk of that broader catch-up. May want to upgrade rf2-gn80 priority once this lands, or leave the rest as v1.1.
- **rf2-wvkn (cancellation contract, OPEN)**: state-level after fires the same cancellation cascade as parent-destroys-child. Composes.

Phases (separable):
- **Phase A — Spec amendment**. Spec 005 gets state-level :after primitive. MIGRATION.md entry. Possibly new Conventions cell. Spec hot-zone — sequence carefully.
- **Phase B — Implementation**. re-frame.machine namespace gains the after timer/cancellation machinery. Tests covering all four expressivity dimensions + interaction with :invoke + cancellation cascade.
- **Phase C — Migration tooling**. Optional: codemod for :timeout-ms-on-invoke → state-level after if rf2-1lop syntax deprecates.

Pre/post v1.0: default lean is spec-pre-v1.0 (lock the canonical surface), impl can ship v1.0 OR v1.1. Mike to confirm timing.

Open design questions for the spec:
1. Syntax: XState's after-as-map-of-ms-to-target, or a more Clojure-idiomatic shape?
2. Dynamic delay resolution: subscription? context-fn? both?
3. Guard syntax: :cond / :when / both — match existing Spec 005 transition guards.
4. Interaction with :invoke: does state-level after override an :invoke's :timeout-ms (deprecated path) or are they independent?
5. Does rf2-1lop's :timeout-ms-on-invoke deprecate, or stay as sugar that desugars to state-level after?

Output: spec amendment + migration entry. Implementation spec + bead later.
notes:
Phase A spec amendment shipped as PR #223 (https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/223). Status: PR open; awaiting review. Phase B (impl) and Phase C (migration tooling) remain on this bead — do not close yet.

Decisions captured in the spec:
- Q1 syntax: flat map of {<delay> <transition>} where transition matches the canonical :rf/transition shape with :guard/:target/:action/:meta slots.
- Q2 dynamic delay: three forms — pos-int? literal, subscription vector ([:sub-id & args]; re-resolves on subscription change with restart-from-now), and (fn [snapshot] ms) for local-data-derived delays.
- Q3 guard: :guard slot on the transition map; reuses machine-local :guards map machinery unchanged.
- Q5 :invoke :timeout-ms DROPPED in favour of state-level :after — one canonical primitive, not two. MIGRATION M-41 rewritten as the dropped-slot record with mechanical lift-into-:after recipe. Retired alongside: :rf.machine.invoke/timed-out trace event, three :rf.error/machine-invoke-timeout-* categories, :actor/timeout capability axis (folded into :fsm/delayed-after).
- Q6 pre/post v1.0: pre-1.0 spec lock; this amendment is canonical Spec 005.
- Q7 multi-stage with :guard: false guard at expiry suppresses the transition; sibling :after timers continue running unchanged (timer is fired-and-discarded, not re-scheduled).

Cross-refs in spec:
- rf2-wvkn — :after firing triggers the same cancellation cascade as parent-destroys-child; composes with the in-flight :rf.http/managed abort contract once rf2-wvkn lands.
- rf2-1lop — its underlying infrastructure (timer cancellation cascade, on-timeout transition firing) is reusable for the :after primitive; only the :invoke surface itself retires.

Trace events:
- :rf.machine.timer/scheduled extended with :delay-source <:literal | :sub | :fn> tag.
- New: :rf.machine.timer/cancelled-on-resolution for subscription-driven re-resolution.
- Existing :rf.machine.timer/fired carries :fired? <bool> for guard-suppressed expiries.
- 009 trace taxonomy unchanged; no new category needed.

Open questions for Mike (none blocking PR review):
- The Phase B implementation will need to decide whether the per-machine :rf/after-epoch counter sub-counters per :after entry, or stays single. Spec lets implementations choose; rf2-3y3y Phase B picks.
- Conformance fixtures named in the v1 ship-list and capability matrix (after-no-invoke-splash, after-sub-vector-delay-restarts-on-change, after-multi-stage-with-guard, after-races-with-on-event) need to be authored as part of Phase B.
Phase B implementation PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/253. State-level :after primitive with :literal/:sub/:fn delay forms, multi-stage + guard suppression, no-invoke splash, :invoke-bearing-state cascade. :timeout-ms on :invoke / :invoke-all dropped (Type-A pre-1.0; registration throws :rf.error/invoke-timeout-ms-removed pointing at MIGRATION §M-41). All JVM machines tests (49) + core tests (183) + http tests (30) + CLJS node tests (342) green; elision still green (4 schema sentinels still ABSENT).
close_reason: Closed

description:

The 7-key cap: `:component-did-mount, :component-will-unmount, :component-did-update, :reagent-render, :display-name, :get-snapshot-before-update, :component-did-catch`. Out-of-cap keys throw `:rf.error/create-class-key-unsupported` at registration time (R-002 mitigation).

rf2-gigc closed: error-boundary tests cover render-time error caught, commit-time error caught (via direct-routing — the integration with React's commit phase is a real-DOM contract for the browser-test target in 4-D), nested-boundary isolation (inner catches; outer does not fire), rethrow-bubbles (cDC user-throw escapes the plumbing untouched), and the auto-installed `getDerivedStateFromError` shape (default flips a `cljsHasError` flag; only installed when `:component-did-catch` is supplied — no silent boundary semantics).

The runtime `wrap-render` is the load-bearing correctness path; the compile-time tag is an additive perf hint that lets `wrap-render` skip the classification cond on the hot path. Plain `(reg-view* :id (fn ...))` callers (no macro) get correctness via the runtime cond.

Source-coord stamping (per IMPL-SPEC §5.4): the renderer-side stamp continues to live in `re-frame.views/reg-view*` and runs over the user's render output. The Form-2 wrapper in views.cljs:284-287 still walks the inner fn's output. The component wrap-render ensures Form-2's inner fn is invoked with current args so the stamping wrapper sees fresh hiccup on each render. Source-coord-tagged-render integration tests exercise the fast-path through wrap-render with `^{:reagent2/form ...}` meta on the render fn.

Implementation note (no spec gap): IMPL-SPEC §5.1 sketches `(out (get-argv c))` for Form-2; the rewrite slices via `(rest argv)` so the user fn receives the user-args tail without the head — same shape the renderer uses for Form-1's `(apply render-fn args)`. Stock Reagent's contract for `:reagent-render` does NOT pass `this`; user code reads `this` via `current-component`.

  - reagent2.dom.client/render — wire to as-element; the existing throw-shim becomes a real implementation.
  - reagent2.dom.client/hydrate-root — same.
  - Wire deref-capture inside the class's render method so dependency tracking works (reactive subscription via `reagent2.ratom`'s notify-deref-watcher!).

HTML5 void-tag handling (br/img/input/etc emit no children). Source-coord stamping seam via *source-coord* dynamic var.

On dep change the Reaction's auto-run callback enqueues batching/queue-render! (no synchronous recompute; React drives the next render). componentWillUnmount always installed (independently of whether the user spec carries one) so the per-instance render Reaction is dispose!'d on unmount, releasing watch-graph references.

The Stage 4-B throw-shim tests rewritten to assert the symbol is bound + not the old throw.

For mixed-mode environments the slim's Provider components would not be visible to that reader. This is a known Stage 4-E follow-up: views.cljs needs adapter-agnostic dispatch through a late-bind hook (:adapter/current-component) before the slim adapter is fully drop-in. Today it is structurally complete + callable from tests, but mounting through (rf/init! adapter) is not yet a drop-in replacement for the bridge.

Implementation note (no spec gap; flagging): the spec at §7.3 describes parse-tag as caching by tag-name string. The implementation does that, but the deftype HiccupTag's class field had to be renamed to className to dodge CLJS's munging — the JS-reserved word `class` becomes `class$` on instance fields in compiled output, while (.-class obj) reads from `.class` and silently returns undefined. Worth a sentence in the IMPL-SPEC §7.3 if anyone's doing follow-up reading; it's a CLJS-implementation detail, not a contract change.

The hook indirection mirrors :adapter/current-frame (already in use by the bridge); the resolver flow is then: dynamic var -> adapter.current-component -> nil. Slim's adapter sets the hook to reagent2.core/current-component; bridge's sets it to reagent.core/current-component. Once landed, (rf/init! reagent-slim/adapter) is drop-in.

WHAT IS STILL OUTSTANDING from Stage 4-F's bead description: item #2, the coerce-context-value deletion (IMPL-SPEC §11.1). FILED AS rf2-4g71 — spec gap. The deletion would break the classic Reagent bridge (day8/re-frame-2-reagent) at scenarios 4-7 of rf2-22ds: stock Reagent's convert-prop-value still stringifies keyword props on Provider :value, and both adapters share re-frame.views/current-frame via the :adapter/current-frame late-bind hook, so the coerce step is load-bearing for the bridge. IMPL-SPEC §11.1's proposed (when (keyword? v) v) fast-path would silently route subscribe / dispatch under [rf/frame-provider {:frame :tenant}] to :rf/default under the classic adapter.

Three options surfaced in rf2-4g71:
(A) Per-adapter :adapter/current-frame impls (slim keyword-only; classic keeps coerce-tolerant). Slight duplication, fully resolves the conflict.
(B) Keep coerce-context-value as load-bearing defensive coercion shared between both adapters; update IMPL-SPEC §11.1 to call out that the fn survives the slim rewrite. Lowest-cost; ~10 lines preserved. Only the defensive comments (§11.2) get cleaned.
(C) Defer deletion to a future stage that retires the classic bridge (post-1.0; IMPL-SPEC §11.4 / §13).

Stage 4-G dispatch should pick one of (A)/(B)/(C) before proceeding.

=== Stage 4-G dispatch (handoff) ===

Per the original Stage 4 plan + IMPL-SPEC §13: examples + final MIGRATION polish. Two streams:

1. Per IMPL-SPEC §12.2: build examples/counter-slim-and-fast (or whatever the chosen name is now that the artefact is day8/reagent-slim). Counter logic identical to examples/counter; mounted via reagent2.dom.client/{create-root, render}. Bundle-isolation grep: assert NO reagent.impl.* (stock Reagent internals) appear, AND NO react-dom/server appears (render-to-static-markup is pure-CLJS per §1.8 + §8). Playwright spec asserts identical user-visible behaviour to examples/counter (Reagent-classic baseline).

2. Per IMPL-SPEC §13: final MIGRATION.md polish. Confirm the M-XX numbering in the shim migration-message bodies (currently linking to MIGRATION.md#legacy-mount-path + #dom-node-removal) is consistent with the rest of the doc. Confirm the URL https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/blob/main/MIGRATION.md is correct at branch-cut (spec/MIGRATION.md is the source; the URL should point at the rendered page if one exists, or stay on the source if not — Stage 4-G confirms).

3. Resolve rf2-4g71 (the coerce-context-value question) and either (a) land the deletion per the chosen path, or (b) update IMPL-SPEC §11.1 to reflect the load-bearing reality and clean the surrounding comments only.

4. Stage 4-G is the LAST sub-stage. After it lands, the rewrite is feature-complete and Stage 4 (rf2-6hyy) can close — the artefact ships at the next release.

title: re-frame-reagent: backward-compat Reagent rewrite optimised for re-frame2 (parent epic)
description:
Mike (2026-05-10): build a new substrate adapter — name TBD, candidates re-frame-reagent / re-frame-2-reagent-fast / reagent-rewrite — that is fully API-compatible with Reagent but rewritten for two objectives:

1. Bundle-size efficiency.
2. Runtime performance.

Plus alignment with re-frame2's ethos: production-elision strict, frame-aware, source-coord native, trace native.

Treated as a separate substrate adapter alongside the existing day8/re-frame-2-reagent — users opt in by choosing the artefact. The current Reagent adapter stays available for users who want to stay with stock Reagent.

This is implementation work, NOT spec work. The spec doesn't change.

Four stages:
- Stage 1 — API surface analysis (back-compat)
- Stage 2 — Efficiency analysis
- Stage 3 — Detailed implementation spec
- Stage 4 — Implementation

Each blocked-by the previous. Sequential.
notes: RENAMED 2026-05-11: artefact is now `day8/reagent-slim` (was reagent-slim-and-fast). 'Fast' claim dropped per Stage 2 honest assessment — production runtime wins are marginal; the load-bearing claim is bundle reduction (slim). All sub-beads inherit the rename.

title: re-frame-pair2: snapshot mega-op cuts round-trip count for investigate-X workflows
description:
Many investigate-X tasks today are 5-10 ops chained — each a round-trip plus Claude-think latency. Per-op speed is one bottleneck; round-trip count is another.

Add a coarser 'snapshot' mega-op that returns frames + sub-cache + machines + epoch-history + recent-traces in one round-trip. Claude reasons about state from one big map rather than 5 sequential reads.

## Scope

- New op: '(snapshot {:frames :all :include [:app-db :sub-cache :machines :epochs :traces]})'
- Returns: { :<frame-id> { :app-db ... :sub-cache ... :machines [...] :epochs [...] :traces [...] }, ... }
- Composable: pass smaller :include if you only want subsets.
- Deprecate granular reads as PRIMARY surface (keep them as fine-grain alternatives).

## Disposition

P2 — composes with rf2-5b8e (MCP transport) and rf2-???-preload. Blocked-by rf2-5b8e since the bigger payload benefits more from a persistent nREPL connection than a fresh-bash-shim per op.

title: re-frame-pair2: auto-inject runtime via shadow-cljs :preloads (no per-session inject step)
description:
Currently re-frame-pair2.runtime is injected via cljs-eval on first connect each session. Each connection pays the inject cost (~hundreds of ms — cljs-eval is slow to compile + load runtime helpers).

Move the runtime into a shadow-cljs :preload entry (or equivalent build-time hook). When the consumer's app boots, the runtime is already there. Pair2's first-connect step verifies + skips inject.

## Scope

- Add re-frame.pair.runtime to a shadow-cljs preload. Document for consumers how to opt in (':preloads [re-frame.pair.runtime]' or similar).
- pair2's connect-flow detects the preload presence (check for runtime sentinel) and skips inject.
- Fall-back path: if preload not configured, current cljs-eval inject still works (back-compat).

## Disposition

P2 — composes with rf2-5b8e (MCP server) for compounding speed wins. Blocked-by rf2-5b8e (the MCP server is the connection-holder; this optimisation only compounds once direct nREPL is in place).
close_reason: merged in 2026-05-13 batch landing wave

title: re-frame-pair2 MCP server: native nREPL transport (structural shift)
description:
Replace the bash-shim → babashka → nREPL chain with a native MCP server speaking nREPL directly. Claude calls MCP tools; the server holds the nREPL connection per session.

Current chain pays per-op: bash startup (~50ms Win, less elsewhere) + babashka startup (~50-100ms) + nREPL connect (cold ~200-500ms) + form ser/eval/response + bash exit. Direct MCP-nREPL: ~10-50ms per op.

## Scope

- Build a Node-based or babashka-based MCP server that exposes pair2's op surface (eval-cljs, app-db/snapshot, app-db/get, app-db/reset, registrar/list, registrar/describe, subs/cache, subs/sample, machines/*, pair-dispatch, watch-*, etc.).
- Server holds a persistent nREPL connection across the session.
- Sentinel-based reconnect on app reload (per existing pattern).
- Deprecate the bash shims in favour of MCP tool calls; keep the shims for back-compat if appropriate.
- Update SKILL.md to teach Claude the MCP tool calls.
- Document the install + connect flow.

## Implementation language

Node + nrepl-client npm package OR babashka + bencode. Pick whichever has cleaner MCP server tooling. Probably Node — MCP SDK is JS-flavoured.

## Bottlenecks expected to dissolve

- First-op latency drops from cold-bash-babashka-nREPL (~700ms) to single nREPL message (~10-50ms).
- Subsequent ops in the same session are persistent-connection-fast (~5-20ms).
- Eliminates Windows bash quirks.

## Bottlenecks NOT addressed by this bead alone

- Runtime auto-injection (rf2-???-pair2-preloads).
- Round-trip count per task (rf2-???-pair2-snapshot-mega-op).
- Polling vs streaming (rf2-???-pair2-streaming-subs).
These are the 3 sub-beads filed alongside.

## Disposition

P2 — pre-1.0 candidate since pair2's iteration speed gates productive use. Direction set.
notes:
**Implementation-language pick locked 2026-05-12 (Mike walkthrough)**:

**Primary**: ClojureScript + shadow-cljs → Node.
- Mike's team is already running shadow-cljs daily for re-frame2; one more `:node-library` / `:node-script` build target is trivial.
- End users install **Node only** (compiled output is plain JS).
- Developers stay in Clojure — write the MCP server in `.cljs`, compile via shadow-cljs.
- npm MCP SDK reachable via js-interop / cljs-bean.
- `nrepl-client` npm package is one `:npm-deps` line.
- Compiled binary ships as a normal npm package.

**Fallback**: TypeScript on Node — if CLJS+shadow interop with the MCP SDK turns sour during the pilot.

**Skip**: nbb (extra install for marginal upside when shadow is already in place), pure babashka (no mature MCP SDK), JVM Clojure (startup + no SDK), foreign languages (Rust/Go/Python — no team affinity).

**Pilot-first directive (do BEFORE committing to the full port)**:
1. Write a hello-world MCP tool in `.cljs`, shadow-cljs `:node-script` or `:node-library` target.
2. Verify the npm MCP SDK (`@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`) interops cleanly — register one tool, exchange one stdio JSON-RPC message.
3. Verify the `nrepl-client` npm package interops cleanly — open a persistent nREPL connection, send one eval, get the reply.
4. If both work: commit to CLJS+shadow. Port pair2's op surface in passes.
5. If either is rough: fall back to TypeScript on Node, isolate op-definitions in a module that could be ported back to CLJS later.

**Pilot expected to take 1-2 hours.** Decision-blocking — don't dispatch the full port until the pilot is green.

Skill update + bash-shim deprecation flow downstream of the pilot.
close_reason: Closed

title: Make re-frame-pair2 faster to iterate with (parent)
description: Mike (2026-05-10): pair2's iteration speed is gated by transport overhead + non-structural inefficiencies. Four sub-beads compose: (1) MCP server speaking nREPL natively (structural shift, prerequisite for the others); (2) auto-injected runtime via shadow-cljs :preloads (no per-session inject cost); (3) coarser snapshot mega-op (cuts round-trip count); (4) streaming subscription channel for trace + epoch events. Each is independently valuable; (1) unblocks (2)-(4).

title: Conformance README could either point to the published JSON corpus or soften the claim
description:
Suggestion to consider:

`spec/conformance/README.md` tells non-EDN implementors that a JSON-translated conformance corpus is also published, but the spec tree does not say where that artefact lives or how it is generated.

Evidence:
- `spec/conformance/README.md:28` says: "A JSON-translated corpus is also published for hosts where EDN is too friction; the JSON form is mechanically derived from the EDN source so the two never drift."
- There is no adjacent path, download location, generation command, or publication note in `spec/conformance/README.md`.
- The `spec/conformance/` directory only contains `README.md` and `fixtures/`, so a reader staying inside `/spec` has no way to find the promised JSON form.

Why it matters:
For non-Clojure implementors, this reads as a completeness promise the corpus does not cash out locally. The gap is not the absence of JSON itself; the gap is that the spec does not tell the implementor where the canonical JSON artefact is or how to reproduce it.

Suggested improvement:
- If the JSON corpus exists, add its location and the generation/publication contract.
- If it is planned rather than shipped, reword the sentence to say so explicitly.
- Prefer a single source of truth: either a checked-in/generated path or a documented release artefact, not an implied external somewhere.
notes: Closed via PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/244 — JSON corpus claim softened to 'may be published in the future' since no scripts, artefacts, or pointers exist in the spec tree today. EDN files in fixtures/ are noted as the canonical source.
close_reason: Closed

title: Conformance README could explicitly document the two fixture modes now in the corpus
description:
Suggestion to consider:

`spec/conformance/README.md` still introduces fixtures as if the corpus has one primary shape: dispatch-driven frame fixtures with top-level `:fixture/dispatches` and `:fixture/expect`. The live corpus now has a second substantial mode: pure/direct-call fixtures driven by `:fixture/calls` with call-local expectations.

Evidence:
- `spec/conformance/README.md:35-52` shows only the dispatch-style map with top-level `:fixture/dispatches` and `:fixture/expect`.
- `spec/conformance/README.md:150-153` mentions `:fixture/calls` only later in the lifecycle walkthrough, after the reader has already been taught the top-level shape.
- Many fixtures now use `:fixture/calls` instead of `:fixture/expect`, for example `spec/conformance/fixtures/machine-transition.edn`, `spec/conformance/fixtures/routing-match-url.edn`, and `spec/conformance/fixtures/ssr-render-to-string.edn`.
- The README's comparison paragraph at `spec/conformance/README.md:153` refers to `:expected-fx-emitted`, while the documented dispatch-style example and live fixtures use `:effects-routed`.

Why it matters:
The conformance README is the implementor's entry point. A host author reading only that document can easily build the wrong harness abstraction, especially for machine, routing, and SSR pure-call fixtures.

Suggested improvement:
- Split the fixture-format section into two explicit modes: dispatch fixtures and pure/direct-call fixtures.
- Add one worked example for each mode.
- Normalise the expectation-key vocabulary in the README (`:effects-routed` vs `:expected-fx-emitted`) so the prose matches the corpus.
notes: Closed via PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/244 — §Fixture format restructured to introduce both Mode A (dispatch-driven, :fixture/dispatches + :fixture/expect) and Mode B (pure / direct-call, :fixture/calls with call-local expectations) on equal footing, with a worked example for each plus a second routing/SSR-shape Mode B example. Also fixed the lifecycle walkthrough's stale :expected-fx-emitted reference (corpus uses :effects-routed exclusively across 24 occurrences) and dropped the stale 'EDN-ish form' parenthetical next to capability tagging. Audit for the record: 71 Mode A fixtures, 25 Mode B fixtures, mutually exclusive (no fixture currently uses both).
close_reason: Closed

title: Consider aligning re-frame-pair2 watch modes and predicate docs with the actual implementation
description:
Why this suggestion exists:
The `watch/*` surface does not currently match its documented contract in two ways.

First, window mode and count mode are not actually separate. In `skills/re-frame-pair2/scripts/ops.clj:467-495`, `watch-op` always assigns a default `count-n` of 5 and checks both the elapsed-time and emitted-count stop conditions for every non-stream watch. That means `scripts/watch-epochs.sh --window-ms 30000` can stop after five matches instead of running for the full 30 seconds promised in `skills/re-frame-pair2/SKILL.md:151`.

Second, `--custom` is advertised to users but not implemented. `SKILL.md:156` lists it as a supported predicate and `SKILL.md:306` uses it in the machine-transition recipe, but `skills/re-frame-pair2/scripts/ops.clj:444-450` exits immediately with `:not-yet-supported` when the flag is passed.

Suggestions to consider:
- Make `--window-ms` and `--count` truly distinct modes, or set the inactive mode's limit to unbounded so `watch/window` behaves as documented.
- Either implement `--custom` or remove it from user-facing docs and examples until it exists.
- Add direct shim/integration coverage for watch mode termination semantics and for any supported predicate flags.
close_reason: Stale in_progress cleanup — fix commits on origin/main per git log sweep 2026-05-13. Bead status was never flipped after PR merge.

title: Make browser test harness resilient to occupied port 8021
description:
Why this issue exists:
implementation/scripts/serve-and-run-browser-tests.cjs hard-codes `const PORT = 8021` and assumes that port is available. In a workspace where another local repo or prior test server is already listening on 8021, `http-server` exits with `EADDRINUSE`, but the harness still waits through the readiness timeout before failing. That makes `npm run test:browser` brittle across concurrent local sessions and slows diagnosis.

What needs to be done:
- Allow the browser test port to be injected via env var and/or choose a free port dynamically.
- Detect child-process early exit, especially `EADDRINUSE`, and fail fast with a direct error instead of timing out after the readiness loop.
- Thread the selected port through to `BROWSER_TEST_URL` so the Playwright runner remains unchanged.

Notes:
Observed from this repo on 2026-05-10: `npm run test:browser` failed because another `http-server` process from a sibling repo was already bound to 8021, not because of a product regression.
close_reason: Stale in_progress cleanup — fix commits on origin/main per git log sweep 2026-05-13. Bead status was never flipped after PR merge.

title: Spec 005 gap: per-:invoke wall-clock :timeout-ms (spans retries)
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md gap M3. Spec 014 :rf.http/managed per-attempt timeout governs ONE HTTP attempt; if request retries N times the wall-clock can grow well past any sensible bound. Boot machine wants 'this whole step (including retries) times out at 30s'; no first-class way today. Workarounds: (a) sibling :timer machine racing the work; (b) embed timeout in retry math. Both imperfect. Direction: add :timeout-ms to :invoke spec. When spawned machine doesn't terminate within the wall-clock window, runtime cancels it and transitions parent via :on-timeout. Trace event :rf.machine.invoke/timed-out fires on expiry. Open question: does :on-timeout receive partial-progress context? Probably no (timeout = state unknown); document. Disposition: P2 pre-1.0 candidate. Composes with M1 (spawn-and-join also wants :timeout-ms) and M2 (timeout fires same cancellation cascade).
close_reason: Closed

title: Spec 005 gap: :invoke cancellation contract for in-flight :rf.http/managed requests
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md gap M2. When a parent machine cancels a spawned child mid-execution, what happens to in-flight :rf.http/managed requests the child kicked off? Spec 005 + spec 014 do not explicitly cover the cross-feature cancellation contract. Direction: spawned actor destruction MUST abort its in-flight HTTP requests; trace event :rf.http/aborted-on-actor-destroy fires. Spawned-actor-id propagates through :rf.http/managed dispatch envelope; frame's HTTP request registry filters by spawned-actor-id on destroy; late-bind hook from machines into http to clear actor's requests. Open question: non-actor sources (request dispatched directly from event handler with no spawned-actor parent) — no cancellation. Probably fine; confirm in spec wording. Disposition: P2 pre-1.0 candidate; cancellation contract affects every boot machine correctness.
notes:
PR opened: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/247

Implemented:
- spec/005 §Cancellation cascade — in-flight :rf.http/managed aborts (full contract)
- spec/014 §Abort on actor destroy (http-side details)
- spec/009 :rf.http/aborted-on-actor-destroy taxonomy entry
- spec/MIGRATION M-42 (additive, no migration burden — pre-release framing)
- Implementation: late-bind hook :http/abort-on-actor-destroy; in-flight registry extended with actor-id index; abort cascade fires from declarative :invoke destroy, :invoke-all teardown, imperative destroy, and frame-destroy paths
- 5 new JVM tests covering :invoke + state-exit, multi-in-flight from one actor, sibling isolation, direct-dispatch (NOT cancelled), :after cascade
- elision-probe + check-elision.cjs updated for the new trace event sentinel
close_reason: Closed

title: Spec 005 gap: first-class spawn-and-join (parallel-region state-machines)
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md gap M1. Both Dash8 and rf8 boot sequences are parallel-region state machines expressed via day8.re-frame.async-flow-fx :seen-all-of? / :seen-any-of? predicate DSL. Dash8 fans out 7 dispatches and joins on a 7-event set. The spawn-and-join pattern dominates; spec 005 has no first-class expression for it. Today authors fake it with siblings + counter + :always — mechanical, verbose. Direction: add :invoke-all (or extend :invoke) with :join-condition :all/:any/:n-of/predicate-fn. Trace events for join lifecycle. Cancel-on-decision semantics need design refinement (cancel siblings on first :all-complete or :any-failed). Disposition: P2 pre-1.0 candidate since boot-as-state-machine is flagship use case.

title: Spec corpus: scope multi-host portability to JS-cross-compile + React (drop Python/Ruby/Go; drop non-VDOM substrates)
description: Decision (Mike, 2026-05-10): re-frame2 commits to React + VDOM. Non-VDOM substrates (Svelte, Solid, vanilla DOM, Replicant, Lit) are out of scope for forward-looking discussion. Multi-host portability scopes to languages that cross-compile to JS and work with React in the browser — examples: ClojureScript (reference), TypeScript, Melange / ReScript / Reason, Fable (F#), Squint, Scala.js, Kotlin/JS, PureScript. DROP from spec language: Python, Ruby, Kotlin (non-JS), native Rust, Go, server-side languages. Sweep spec corpus to align: 000-Vision (the host-profile matrix, the §Pattern footnote, Goal 8 'language-agnostic'), 001-Registration:137, 002-Frames:426/440/649, 004-Views:13/51, 006-ReactiveSubstrate:61/117/897, 010-Schemas:242 (Python hashlib parenthetical), 014-HTTPRequests:24, Construction-Prompts:16, Conventions:180. Reword 'host language' → 'JS-cross-compile language' or similar where appropriate. Replace non-React substrate mentions (Vue/Solid/Svelte) with React-flavoured cross-compile examples (Melange-React, PureScript-React, etc.). Pre-alpha; no migration concern. Blocked-by rf2-agql to avoid spec/006 + Conventions conflict.
notes:
---

## Decisions (Mike, 2026-05-10) — open questions from findings/substrate-contract-portability.md

### Q1: Drop 'any reactive substrate' wording in spec/006? — YES

The preamble's 'any reactive substrate' framing is retired. Spec 006 explicitly commits to React + VDOM. The substrate-agnostic wording stays only where it accurately describes the contract that DOES generalise (the universal reactive-container contract — entries 1-5 + 9 of the 9-fn surface). The render-side (entries 6-8) is React-shaped and the spec should say so.

Concrete sweep targets in 006:
- §What re-frame2 separates — currently 'reactive / rendering substrate', narrow to 'React + VDOM substrate'.
- §What the adapter owns — entries 6-8 (subscribe-tracking, render, dispose-frame!) should call out the React+VDOM assumption.
- The boundary description — the universal-vs-React-shaped split should be explicit.

### Q2: 8-language list canonical — YES

Confirmed: ClojureScript, TypeScript, Melange / ReScript / Reason, Fable (F#), Squint, Scala.js, PureScript, Kotlin/JS.

Sweep removes Python, Ruby, Kotlin (non-JS), Go, native Rust, server-side languages from spec corpus.

### Q3: Shared React-context primitive — KEEP in core, document per-language port equivalent

The `re-frame.adapter.context/frame-context` lives in the core artefact (CLJS-only file, JVM build doesn't load it). Consistent with the React commitment.

For non-CLJS language ports (TS, Fable, Melange etc.), each port handles its own React.createContext equivalent. Document this contract pre-1.0:

- spec/006 §Frame propagation — note that the React-context primitive is host-language-specific. CLJS reference uses cljs-bean-style React.createContext; TS port uses native React.createContext; Fable/Scala.js/PureScript ports use their respective React-binding's context primitive.
- The contract is 'each port supplies a React-Context value carrying the current frame-id; views read it via the port's hooks-equivalent.' The mechanism varies; the contract doesn't.
- spec/006 should NOT prescribe the JS implementation details — that's port discretion.

## Sweep work scope (revised)

In addition to dropping non-React-VDOM substrates and non-cross-compile languages, the sweep:

1. Drops 'any reactive substrate' framing from spec/006 preamble + commit text.
2. Adds explicit React + VDOM commitment language to 006 §Reactive substrate.
3. Adopts the 8-language list as the canonical multi-host portability target.
4. Documents per-language React-context-primitive in spec/006 §Frame propagation (no relocation; clarification only).
5. Sweeps Python/Ruby/Kotlin-non-JS/Go/native-Rust references from corpus.
close_reason: Stale in_progress cleanup — fix commits on origin/main per git log sweep 2026-05-13. Bead status was never flipped after PR merge.

title: reg-view + frame-provider context resolution is dead code: subscribe never consults React context, and Reagent stringifies the Provider's value prop
description: spec/002 §Reading the frame from React context defines a 3-tier resolution chain (dynamic var → React context → :rf/default) for subscribe/dispatch. The CLJS implementation has TWO current-frame fns: re-frame.frame/current-frame (CLJS+JVM shared, dynamic var only) and re-frame.views/current-frame (CLJS, full chain including React context). subscribe (re-frame.subs.cljc) calls frame/current-frame — the React-context tier is silently bypassed. Discovered while implementing rf2-d3k3 (plain-fn warning) — the warning's contract assumes subscribe route to :rf/default for plain fns; in practice ALL fns under a non-default frame-provider also route to :rf/default because subscribe never consults the React context. Compounding issue: Reagent's convert-prop-value (reagent.impl.template) stringifies named values when passed as React props, so even if subscribe consulted (.-context cmp) on a reg-view-wrapped component, ctx would be a string (not a keyword) and views/current-frame's (when (keyword? ctx) ctx) check returns nil. Two fixes likely needed: (1) wire subscribe (and dispatch's frame default) to consult re-frame.views/current-frame on CLJS via late-bind so the React-context tier becomes live; (2) views/current-frame must accept stringified-keyword shape from Reagent's prop conversion (mirror the workaround rf2-d3k3 added in read-react-context-frame). Found by browser-runner test in cross_spec_cljs_test/plain-fn-under-non-default-frame: _currentValue on the React Context object is the string "tenant-plain-fn-warn" not the keyword :tenant-plain-fn-warn. Note: the existing reg-view + frame-provider integration is not currently exercised by any cross-namespace browser-runner test that asserts subscribe routes to the surrounding frame — that's why this has stayed hidden.

title: spec/010-Schemas.md re-enable-validation-in-prod escape hatches contradict C-000.35 strict elision
description: From rf2-wq0a (PR #169) drift survey. spec/010-Schemas.md has 3 sites that imply a re-enable-validation-in-prod escape hatch: L92 (reg-app-schema row 'prod (when re-enabled): log and proceed'), L94 ('post-handler app-db failures roll back in dev' implying a prod branch), L118 (per-step recovery 'Prod (when validation re-enabled)...'). C-000.35 (just landed in 000-Vision) ratifies strict elision: 'production elision MUST NOT alter the value of a frame's state nor the order or identity of dispatched events.' These 010 escape hatches contradict C-000.35. The legitimate production-side validation surface is the boundary-validation interceptor at 010 L130-148; that should be the canonical home for any prod validation, not the dev-time validate-* fns being opted back in. Direction: align 010 to C-000.35 — either drop the re-enabling language entirely OR redirect 'prod validation' references to point at the boundary-interceptor. Mike's call which framing is clearer.
close_reason: Closed

title: Cover machine guard / action 3-arity escape hatch (rf2-hbt1 follow-up)
description: From rf2-o423 test coverage audit (top-3 #3 + most surprising finding). The 3-arity guard/action escape hatch is canonically documented in 'implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines.cljc:78-118' with a runtime arglist-inspection dispatcher ('declares-3-arity?'). Documentation explicitly flags variadics (e.g. (constantly ...)) as a footgun. **Not a single test in the suite uses 3-arity guards or actions.** Every machine test is 2-arity. A 'declares-3-arity?' refactor — especially around variadics — would be invisible to the 1413-assertion test suite. Required tests: (a) register a machine with a 3-arity guard, exercise transitions, assert the third arg (the spec's expected ':data'/'event'/extra binding) reaches the guard correctly. (b) Same for 3-arity ':action'. (c) Variadic edge cases: '(constantly true)' as guard (variadic-fn case mentioned in docstring as footgun). Live tests, not just JVM unit. P2 because it is silent coverage drift on a documented public surface.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #156 — 3-arity guard/action escape hatch coverage merged (commit ea271fc). Real-bug follow-up rf2-l04j was filed during this work and has also been addressed via PR #159.

title: Late-bind missing-artefact error path coverage for all 6 per-feature splits
description: From rf2-o423 test coverage audit (top-3 #2). All per-feature splits (schemas/machines/routing/flows/http/ssr) raise a documented error when a consumer calls a re-exported surface but the artefact is absent from the classpath (e.g. ':rf.error/flows-artefact-missing'). NONE of the splits have a test asserting this error contract. The contract is currently only documented in prose; broken absent-handling would not be caught in CI. Test shape: per-artefact, simulate the artefact-absent classpath state, call the re-exported surface, assert the documented error is raised with the documented payload shape. Probably implemented as a JVM test that ad-hoc strips the artefact's deps from classpath OR a CLJS test that registers the late-bind hook as nil and exercises the surface. Per-artefact: schemas, machines, routing, flows, http, ssr. P2 because the absent-artefact contract is the pre-beta promise to consumers picking artefacts a la carte.

title: Permanent bundle-isolation CI gate for per-feature splits (counter as canonical no-feature app)
description: From rf2-o423 test coverage audit (findings/test-coverage-audit-2026-05-09.md, top-3 #1). Each per-feature split (schemas / machines / routing / flows / http / ssr / epoch-when-landed) was verified at PR-time with a one-shot bundle-isolation grep against the counter example bundle. None has a permanent CI test asserting the isolation contract. A future change accidentally re-importing a split-out namespace into core would not be caught. Counter is the canonical no-feature app — it imports zero per-feature artefacts. CI test should: (1) shadow-cljs release counter, (2) grep the produced main.js for each artefact's namespace markers, (3) assert 0 hits per artefact (or a stable allow-list of consumer-side preset-map keywords). Lives ideally as a step in test.yml gating the topological deploy DAG. P2 because bundle-size guarantees are a load-bearing pre-1.0 promise.
