title: Add goal: hierarchical FSM substrate with implementor-chosen completeness level
description:
Mike's framing: re-frame2 wants a sufficiently complete FSM-and-actor substrate; implementors choose their capability set. Mike clarified that the actor side is largely done already (spawn, destroy, own state at [:rf/machines <id>], message ports via dispatch/sub-machine, cross-actor send via :fx). Parallel states and history states are explicitly OUT of pattern scope (locked, this iteration) — re-frame2 has clean substitutes for both that didn't exist in xstate's framing.

Reframed as orthogonal capability axes (not tiered levels):

FSM-richness axis (locked targets for v1 pattern):
  - Flat FSM (guards, actions, entry/exit, wildcard :*) — ✓ specced
  - Hierarchical states (compound) — add (main work)
  - Eventless :always — add (small)
  - Delayed :after — add (small; timing semantics need care for SSR/testing)
  - Parallel regions — SKIP. Substitute: separate machines per region, coordinated via cross-actor dispatch. All atomicity, inspectability, and undo benefits ride on the existing single-store machinery.
  - History states — SKIP. Substitute: snapshot-as-value capture (the snapshot at [:rf/machines <id>] is already a value; copy it on leave, restore on re-enter). xstate needs history states only because its runtime doesn't have first-class snapshot-as-value semantics — re-frame2 does.

Actor-model axis (largely already specced):
  - Own state + message ports (actor identity) — ✓ specced
  - Imperative spawn — ✓ specced
  - Destroy — ✓ specced
  - Cross-actor send via :fx — ✓ specced
  - Declarative :invoke (sugar over spawn) — add (trivial; runtime translates :invoke on a state's :on-entry to entry/exit actions that spawn/destroy)
  - SCXML compatibility — out of scope (post-v1, possibly never)

Goal text:
'The pattern anticipates a hierarchical FSM substrate with full actor semantics. Implementations declare which capabilities they support; conformance is graded against the claimed capability list. Parallel regions and history states are explicitly out of pattern scope; documented substitutes (separate machines per region; snapshot-as-value capture) cover their use cases.'

Action items:
1. Add goal to 000-Vision near AI-first amenability and revertibility.
2. Define a §Capabilities matrix in 005, listing each capability with its required prose / schema / fixture coverage.
3. Add §'Substitutes for skipped features' to 005 — documents the separate-machines-per-region pattern (with a worked example like a media player) and the snapshot-capture pattern.
4. Lock the v1 CLJS reference's capability set: all actor capabilities + flat FSM + hierarchical states + eventless :always + delayed :after + declarative :invoke. NOT parallel or history.
5. Update 005's v1-grammar binary table — replace with capabilities matrix; mark parallel and history as 'out of pattern scope; see substitutes.'
6. Conformance corpus: each fixture declares which capabilities it requires.
7. Host-profile matrix in 000-Vision lists each capability as required / recommended / optional / out-of-scope.
8. Worked example: include a media player demo showing separate-machines pattern as a substitute for parallel regions.
9. AI-Audit gets 'capability conformance clarity' graded against whether each spec section names the capabilities it depends on.

Pairs naturally with rf2-5pt (one-shot-implementability) and rf2-tqv (Circle Drawer inspectability audit).
close_reason: Added Goal 6 (Hierarchical FSM substrate with implementor-chosen capabilities) to 000-Vision §Goals. Added §Capability matrix to 005 listing each FSM-richness and actor-model capability with required prose/schema/fixture coverage. Added §Substitutes for skipped features with worked media-player example for parallel regions and snapshot-capture sketch for history states. Added §Declarative :invoke (sugar over spawn). v1 grammar subset table aligned with capability matrix. Host-profile matrix gains capability-list rows. Conventions.md gains reserved state-node keys table. Conformance README gains capability-tagging convention. Hierarchical-states schema work in Spec-Schemas marked as TODO with follow-up bead filed below.

title: Add goal: spec corpus is sufficiently complete that an AI can one-shot the v1 reference implementation
description:
Currently this claim appears only in passing in README.md as a description of what the conformance corpus verifies. It is not stated as a goal in 000-Vision alongside AI-first amenability, frame state revertibility, etc.

Per project memory, this IS the bar Mike has set for re-frame2 ('AI can one-shot the v1 reference implementation'). The bar deserves to be a stated goal because it's a different kind of claim from AI-first amenability:

- AI-first amenability is about design quality from the AI's perspective (regularity, named-things, low hidden context, etc.).
- 'AI can one-shot the implementation' is about the spec corpus's *completeness*. It's the acceptance test for the entire spec effort. If an AI armed only with the spec docs + conformance fixtures can produce a working v1 reference implementation, the spec is done. If not, something is missing.

These are related but distinct. The first is a design property; the second is a meta-property of the spec itself.

Action items:
1. Add to 000-Vision §Goals as a top-level goal. Suggested name: 'AI-implementable from the spec alone' or 'One-shot implementability'. Place near AI-first amenability since it's the natural sibling.
2. Rationale paragraph: this is the acceptance test for the spec corpus. The conformance corpus exists to verify it. Trade-off: forces the spec to be precise where it might otherwise leave details to the implementer.
3. Implications:
   - Every spec must be self-contained enough that an AI doesn't need to consult re-frame v1 source code.
   - Open Questions must be resolved or explicitly bracketed as 'CLJS-reference choice; document your host's choice'.
   - Conformance fixtures must be runnable test cases, not aspirational sketches.
   - Schemas (Spec-Schemas.md) define every shape the spec references.
   - The host-profile matrix names what each host must provide vs what it can choose.
4. Cross-reference from the conformance corpus README (which already mentions the claim).
5. AI-Audit gets a new top-level audit dimension: 'spec self-containedness' — graded against whether each spec relies on external knowledge.
6. Update the existing AI-first principles section if appropriate to distinguish 'amenability' (design) from 'one-shot-implementability' (spec completeness).

Status: open. Worth doing as part of any 000-Vision goal-list pass.
close_reason: Added Goal 2 (AI-implementable from the spec alone) to 000-Vision §Goals near AI-first amenability. Includes rationale, what-this-implies, connection to other goals, failure mode. Cross-referenced from conformance/README.md as the verification mechanism. Goal numbers renumbered (old 2 -> new 3, etc.). New audit row added to AI-Audit.md cross-cutting goals.

title: Spec 006 reactive-substrate adapter — verify revertibility (no observer state outside frame value)
description:
AI-Audit flagged Spec 006 (ReactiveSubstrate) as ◐ for the new top-level Goal 'Frame state is fully revertible' (per 000-Vision). The concern: a parallel-substrate adapter could legally put observer/subscription/reaction state *outside* the frame's value (e.g., in a Reagent track-cache, in adapter-private mutable cells), which would mean undo / time-travel / snapshot-restore of the frame value would not fully restore the runtime — observer state would survive the revert.

Action items:
1. Audit the existing 006 adapter contract (make-state-container, subscribe-container, make-derived-value, render, render-to-string, register-context-provider, dispose-adapter!) and identify any place an adapter is allowed to hold mutable state that isn't part of the frame's persistent value.
2. Add a §'Revertibility constraints on adapters' to 006 making the rule explicit: adapter-internal state used for performance (memoisation, reaction caches) is allowed if and only if it is *derivable* from the frame's value — i.e., dropping the adapter cache and recomputing from the frame value yields equivalent output. Anything that *adds information* not in the frame value violates the goal.
3. Verify Reagent reference adapter compliance — Reagent's track-cache is derivable; OK. The plain-atom adapter is trivially compliant. Note any adapter primitives that need extra care.
4. Update AI-Audit row for 006 from ◐ to ✓ once the §Revertibility section lands and the audit verifies no violations.
5. Cross-reference back to 000-Vision §Frame state revertibility from the new 006 section.

Status: open. Discovered during the goal-landing pass; not blocking but should be resolved before any v1 lock of Spec 006.
close_reason: Added §Revertibility constraints on adapters to Spec 006. Rule: adapter-internal state is allowed iff derivable from frame value. Audited primitives + reference adapters (Reagent, plain-atom). Flipped AI-Audit Goal-3 row for 006 from ◐ to ✓.

title: Document persistent-data-structure dependency for other-language implementors
description:
Re-frame2's design assumes structural-sharing persistent data structures: app-db value-equality is cheap (used by sub-cache invalidation), immutable snapshots are zero-copy (used by undo/time-travel/epoch-history), structural sharing keeps hot-path swaps cheap. For CLJS this is free (Clojure's defaults). For other-language ports, persistent data structures are a strong soft-requirement.

Host implications:
- JS/TS: Immer or Immutable.js; native JS without a library would have prohibitive deep-copy cost.
- Python: pyrsistent / immutables.
- Rust: im-rs (or similar).
- F#/Scala/OCaml: native PDS — zero ceremony.
- Go: requires a library or copy-on-write conventions; a strict idiomatic-Go port would struggle.

Action items:
1. Add a section to 000-Vision (Goals / Pattern constraints) calling this out as a strong recommendation.
2. Add a row to the host-profile matrix in 000-Vision listing 'persistent data structures (PDS)' as a pattern-required capability for dynamic hosts and a 'strongly recommended' for static hosts.
3. List standard PDS libraries per host in Conventions.md or a new Implementor-Notes.md doc.

Status: open. Don't forget to action before any v1-spec freeze.
close_reason: Enriched the host-profile matrix PDS row in 000-Vision with per-host options (Immer/Immutable.js, pyrsistent, im-rs, native FP, Go caveat), added a Note-on-PDS subsection explaining why Goal 2 makes it pattern-required, and pointed Conventions.md back at it.

close_reason: decision: remove historical-convention commentary from normative prose; edit: 002-Frames.md OQ-F-12 coda, 004-Views.md §Spec 003 subsumption + h-macro phrasing, 012-Routing.md §:on-match + R-3, 006-ReactiveSubstrate.md OQ-7 references rewritten to §Substrate decoupling, AI-Audit.md/Principles.md/Construction-Prompts.md per-the-reorient prefaces, 008-Testing.md:9 reorient-ordering note, 011-SSR.md status line

description: Suggestion from a spec-purity pass, not a decision: investigate scrubbing comments that read like project-management notes rather than specification text. Examples include `docs/specification/000-Vision.md:250-251` (`design pending`, `don't action skills yet`), `README.md:33` / `35` (`reframed per reorient`), and similar wording in deliverable/status tables. The spec should state what the artefact is and its contract, not how the team got there or whether design is still pending.

title: Investigate scrubbing drafting-history and reorientation narrative out of 000-Vision and related overview text
description: Suggestion from a spec-purity pass, not a decision: investigate removing or rewriting comments that describe the path by which decisions were made rather than the decisions themselves. Key examples include `docs/specification/000-Vision.md:3` (`Per the reorientation ... cleaned up in follow-up passes`), `000-Vision.md:212` (previous G1-G6 set), `000-Vision.md:276` / `309` (reorientation narrative), `000-Vision.md:372` (original framing vs current framing), and `README.md:13` (historical drafts that motivated re-frame2). The goal would be for the overview docs to read as specs, not as a design-journal or transition note.

title: Idea: Declarative subscriptions on a frame (Elm Sub-style) for external event sources
description:
Elm pattern: TEA's `subscriptions : Model -> Sub Msg` — at every step the program returns a *declaration* of which external sources should currently be feeding messages in (a websocket on this URL, an interval at this rate, the keyboard, a port). The runtime diffs declarations across steps and starts/stops the underlying machinery accordingly. The user never starts/stops a websocket — they declare its presence as a function of state. This is the Cmd/Sub *asymmetry*: Cmds are effects you produce (one-shot, fire-and-forget); Subs are message *sources* you currently want.

re-frame2's current position: re-frame's :fx is Cmd-shaped — effects you produce. The 'I want the runtime to feed me messages from source X while condition Y holds' case is currently solved ad-hoc: setup events that start a timer/websocket/eventListener, teardown events that close it, manual lifecycle bookkeeping. Pattern-RemoteData §Polling shows the workaround using :dispatch-later self-recursion. There is no first-class declarative subscription concept on a frame.

Why investigate: this is the missing half of the effect vocabulary. Concretely: a :subscriptions slot on reg-frame returning data — `[[:interval 1000 [:tick]] [:ws "wss://..." {:on-message [:msg]}] [:keyboard [:key-down]]]` — diffed each drain, with the runtime starting/stopping :sub-id-shaped sources. Solves websockets, intervals, keyboard listeners, document.visibilitychange, server-sent events, IntersectionObserver, and similar — which today are leaky setup-event patterns prone to forgotten teardown on frame destroy. Big AI-amenability win: the AI reads one declaration to know what's listening, not a scatter of setup/teardown events.

Fit: stays inside the closed effect model — :subscriptions is *declarative*, the runtime translates to an internal start/stop on registered subscription handlers (a new kind alongside :event/:sub/:fx). Frame-scoped lifecycle is automatic; :on-destroy stops everything declared. Hard constraint check: NO core.async — diffing is plain values, fire-and-forget callbacks dispatch events. Multi-frame check: each frame has its own :subscriptions slot.

Risks: registry-kind growth (a new :sub-source kind). Mitigation — could be modelled as a special idiom over reg-fx with explicit start/stop semantics rather than a new kind. Worth careful design — the asymmetry Cmd↔Sub is a deep idea and getting it wrong wastes the slot.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: Rejected exploration: does not fit the intended re-frame2 direction.

title: Idea: register the :assert/* event vocabulary as a public, enumerable assertion DSL
description:
Source: Storybook's @storybook/test exposes a small, named assertion vocabulary (within/expect/userEvent) that is deliberately a tiny surface — easy to learn, easy for AIs to generate. Storybook play functions are just sequences of those calls.

re-frame2's current position: 007-Stories.md §Play functions sketches :play [[:auth/email-changed ...] [:assert/path-equals [:auth :state-machine] :authenticated] ...] but does not enumerate the :assert/* vocabulary. The current text mentions :assert/path-equals as an example without committing to a list.

Why worth investigating: per Principle 'Public query surfaces' and 'Named things over anonymous things', the assertion vocabulary should be a *registered* set of events whose ids are enumerable via (rf/handlers :event) (filtered by :assert/ prefix). Concrete candidate vocabulary: :assert/path-equals, :assert/path-matches (predicate), :assert/sub-equals, :assert/dispatched? (event was dispatched during play), :assert/no-warnings, :assert/state-is (machine), :assert/effect-emitted. AI scaffolding (CP-10) would query the registrar for the :assert/* prefix and pick from the enumerable set rather than guessing names.

Fit/risks: lives in the post-v1 story library — these are story-tool-supplied events, not framework primitives. Risk: registering :assert/* events globally collides with user namespaces; mitigation is a reserved :rf.assert/ prefix. Cleanly composes with 'story-as-test duality' — same vocab works in :test mode (assertions fail loudly) and :dev mode (rendered as a checked-step list).

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
notes: Actioned: added 'Assertion vocabulary as a registered, enumerable DSL' note to 007-Stories.md §Play functions, with the candidate :rf.assert/* vocabulary. Bead remains open — vocabulary needs to be locked in alongside the post-v1 stories library.
close_reason: decision: :rf.assert/* is a reserved namespace (Conventions.md); the post-v1 stories library registers the canonical vocabulary at load time (path-equals, path-matches, sub-equals, dispatched?, state-is, no-warnings, effect-emitted); AI scaffolders enumerate via (rf/handlers :event ...); edit: docs/specification/007-Stories.md, Conventions.md

title: Idea: derive :argtypes from registered schemas (Spec 010) instead of hand-writing
description:
Source: Storybook 8 auto-derives argTypes from TypeScript types / JSDoc / propTypes, so authors rarely write the control-type table by hand. Histoire does the same from Vue prop definitions. Result: controls panel 'just works' once the component is typed/schema'd.

re-frame2's current position: 007-Stories.md §Argtypes describe controls requires the variant author (or AI) to hand-write {:argtypes {:placeholder {:control :text} :variant {:control {:type :select :options [...]}}}}. This duplicates information already present in Spec 010's view :spec / arg schemas (a Malli enum becomes a :select; a string becomes :text; a boolean becomes :boolean; a number with :min/:max becomes a number control with bounds).

Why worth investigating: pure AI-first win. The view already declares its argument shape via Malli (per Spec 010); auto-derivation removes a class of hand-written boilerplate where the AI is just transcribing schema → controls. Also: a single source of truth for arg shape — schema drift between view and argtypes is impossible.

Fit/risks: adds a 'malli → argtypes' resolver to the post-v1 stories library — no v1 framework hook needed. Risk: not every Malli shape has a clean control mapping (recursive schemas, multi-spec variants). Solution: auto-derive when possible, allow manual override for the rest. Fits 'post-v1 library scope vs v1 framework hooks' cleanly: lives entirely in the stories lib.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
notes: Actioned: added 'Auto-derivation from Spec 010 schemas' note to 007-Stories.md §Argtypes describe controls. Bead remains open — Malli→argtypes resolver design needs detail.
close_reason: decision: auto-derivation from Spec 010 schemas locked as principle (post-v1 library composes the resolver); inline note in 007 §Argtypes upgraded from 'under investigation' to 'locked principle; resolver post-v1'; edit: docs/specification/007-Stories.md

title: Idea: CP-10 — Construction Prompt for stories/variants/workspaces
description:
Source: Storybook CSF3 (story-as-data with declarative meta), devcards (cards-as-forms), Histoire (story-file-as-component). All have AI-friendly scaffolding shapes; CSF3 in particular is what AI agents converge on when writing Storybook stories.

re-frame2's current position: Construction-Prompts.md ships CP-1..CP-9 (event/sub/fx/view/machine/feature/route/schema/SSR). There is no CP for the story/variant/workspace artefact. 007-Stories.md positions reg-story/reg-variant/reg-workspace as post-v1 sugar but stories ARE one of the AI-first surfaces (they encode 'a component in a known state' as data — high-value AI input).

Why worth investigating: construction prompts are a 'deliverable principle' (Principles.md). Stories are arguably the highest-leverage AI deliverable (story-as-test, story-as-doc, story-as-agent-input — see 007 §Story-as-test duality). A CP-10 template would (a) preflight-check the parent view is reg-view'd; (b) preflight-check the events fired in :events are registered; (c) emit reg-story + N reg-variant forms with a documented :tags default; (d) cross-link CP-4 (view), CP-1 (events), CP-8 (schema for argtypes). Brings stories into the same construction-prompt regularity AI tooling expects.

Fit/risks: CP-10 is documentation, not runtime; sits cleanly with existing prompts. Risk: stories are post-v1 library, so CP-10 is forward-looking; can be marked 'CP-10 (post-v1)' and shipped when reg-story/reg-variant land. No constraint conflicts.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
notes: Actioned: added a 'Construction prompt (CP-10)' sketch section to 007-Stories.md and a forward-reference in Construction-Prompts.md header. Bead remains open — full CP-10 template lands when re-frame.stories ships.
close_reason: decision: CP-10 sketch in 007 finalised - emit Form B by default, pre-flight on registered ids/tags, auto-derive argtypes from Spec 010, draw assertions from registered :rf.assert/* vocabulary, AI-first checklist updated; CP.md header points to 007; full template lands when re-frame.stories ships; edit: docs/specification/007-Stories.md, Construction-Prompts.md

title: Idea: Pending-navigation state in app-db so views can render loading UI
description:
(a) Source ecosystem and pattern: React Router v6/v7's useNavigation exposes navigation state ('idle' | 'loading' | 'submitting') so any view can render a global progress bar or pending UI without manual bookkeeping. TanStack Router exposes isFetching/isLoading per route. Remix's pending UI story is one of its headline features.

(b) What 012 currently has: 012's :route slice is {:id ... :params ...}. There is no notion of 'a navigation is in flight,' no place a sub like :route/pending? or :route/transition can read from. Consumers wanting a progress bar would have to roll their own coordinated flag in app-db, which is exactly the kind of hidden context AI-first wants to avoid.

(c) Why investigate: real SPAs need pending UI. SSR-driven first-load also has a 'hydrating' moment that views may want to know about. A tiny addition to the :route slice — :transition with values like :idle / :loading / :error — and a registered sub :route/transition would make this a one-liner for views and an enumerable property for AIs. Especially powerful when combined with declarative loaders (rf2-5tf): the runtime knows when route-level fetches start and finish.

(d) Fit/risks: fits public-query-surfaces (named, queryable, deterministic). Plays well with state-machines (003/005) — :transition is itself a tiny FSM. Risks: if the runtime doesn't know what 'finished loading' means without declarative loaders, the application has to manage :transition itself, which is little better than a hand-rolled flag. So this idea is most powerful if rf2-5tf lands; otherwise leave it as a documented convention.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: ship :transition (:idle | :loading | :error) and :error in the :route slice; sub :route/transition and :route/error. Runtime-driven (set :loading while :on-match drains, :error if any errors). Spec edited at 012-Routing.md §The :route slice, §Reading the route is a sub; Spec-Schemas.md §:rf/route-slice.

title: Idea: Canonical machine snapshot persist/restore for SSR and Tool-Pair time-travel
description:
(a) xstate concept and concrete value: getPersistedSnapshot / restoreSnapshot are first-class — a snapshot serialises to JSON, reloads, and the machine resumes at the right state with context intact. xstate v5 enforces serialisability of context for this reason. Used for SSR hydration, replay, and dev-tools.

(b) re-frame2's current position: :rf/machine-snapshot is defined as a value shape (Spec-Schemas), and 011-SSR's hydration payload carries app-db. But there is no explicit contract for what 'a machine snapshot survives the wire and resumes correctly' means — e.g., context functions are forbidden? in-flight transition microstate? 002's epoch records also store db-before/db-after, which already brings machines along for the ride.

(c) Why worth investigating: the spec gets persistence almost for free because machines live at app-db paths — but 'almost' hides issues. If a child machine was mid-transition (waiting on an HTTP reply event), what's persisted? If a transition table version changes between snapshot and restore, what then? Tool-Pair epoch replay (rf-three-ai-layers) hits the same questions. A small named contract (snapshot-stable invariants, version tag, restore semantics) would lock these down.

(d) Fit / risks: aligned with closed effect-map, no new substrate — just clearer semantics on existing shapes. Could ship contract in v1 (stability rules) and tooling post-v1 (machine-snapshot-version / migrate-snapshot). Risks: adding a version field to the snapshot shape pressures the closed-shape principle; consider :meta-only.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: locked machine-snapshot stability rules — print/read round-trip, no in-flight microstate, stable across re-registration, optional :rf/snapshot-version under :meta; restore = replace-app-db-at-path semantics shared by SSR hydration and Tool-Pair epoch restore; edit: docs/specification/005-StateMachines.md §Machine snapshot stability, Spec-Schemas.md §:rf/machine-snapshot

title: Idea: Declarative per-route data requirements (loader-equivalent) on reg-route
description:
(a) Source ecosystem and pattern: React Router v6/v7, Remix, TanStack Router, and SvelteKit all colocate a route's data dependencies with the route definition (loader / +page.server.load / loader fn). The route declares 'this view needs X data'; the framework runs that loader on URL match (server and client) and the view consumes the result. Nested loaders compose; route-level revalidation is a first-class concept.

(b) What 012 currently has: 012 mentions a route->fetch-effects helper and a per-route-id multimethod as the conventional pattern; data fetches are emitted as ordinary :fx by :route/handle-url-change. There is no first-class slot on reg-route describing the data the route needs, and no enumerable surface telling tools 'these are the fetches associated with :route/cart'. CP-7 likewise leaves it implicit.

(c) Why investigate: AI scaffolding and tooling can do dramatically more if a route's data dependencies are declarative rather than buried in a multimethod. Declaring :fetch [[:cart/load-items] [:user/load-prefs]] on reg-route makes 'what loads when this route mounts?' enumerable via handler-meta, runs identically server- and client-side (matches goal #3), and lets a tool render route-loading dependency graphs. Compiles down to the existing :fx pipeline — no runtime change. Touches rf2-0fj.

(d) Fit/risks: strong fit with public-query-surfaces, named-things, and data-before-magic. Closed effect-map is preserved (the :fetch slot is metadata, not effects). Risks: revalidation semantics (when do we re-fire?), conditional fetches, parameterising the fetch by :route/params — these need a small DSL; do not over-engineer.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: ship :on-match as a vector of event vectors that the runtime dispatches on URL match (server- and client-side). Re-fires when params/query change; doesn't re-fire on hydration. No new effect substrate; pure event-and-fx. Spec edited at 012-Routing.md §Per-route data loading; CP-7 updated.

title: Idea: Model-based testing harness over machine-transition (xstate/test analogue)
description:
(a) xstate concept and concrete value: @xstate/test generates test cases from a machine — paths, coverage, shortest-path-to-state — by treating the machine as a graph. The pure transition function makes this cheap and the generated tests are exhaustive in a way handwritten cases never are.

(b) re-frame2's current position: 005 ships a pure (machine-transition definition snapshot event) → [next-snapshot effects] in v1. CP-5 demonstrates handwritten path tests. Nothing in the spec proposes generating those tests from the table.

(c) Why worth investigating: the highest-leverage, lowest-cost feature xstate has and a near-perfect AI-first match — the table is data, the transition fn is pure, an AI can ask the harness 'cover every transition' and get an EDN fixture corpus back. Plugs directly into the conformance corpus story (Spec-Schemas §Conformance). Can ship as a separate testing helper without touching the runtime.

(d) Fit / risks: zero conflict with machines-as-event-handlers, no core.async, no new effects — the harness reads transition tables and calls the pure fn. Risks: defining the coverage model (state-coverage vs transition-coverage vs guard-coverage) is non-trivial; effectful actions (HTTP) need stubbing in the harness. Naturally slots into the post-v1 library, but the v1 transition contract is already enough to build it externally.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
notes: Documented as forward-pointer in docs/specification/008-Testing.md §Future and 005-StateMachines.md §Future (post-v1 library item — sketch surface: rf/test/machine-paths, rf/test/shortest-path-to). Needs Mike's call: lock the API surface in v1 (so post-v1 library is constrained) vs leave fully open (post-v1 library defines its own API)?
close_reason: Design locked: post-v1 library 're-frame.machines.test'; transition coverage as default; :after timers via explicit time-advance using re-frame.interop test-clock; auto-stub fxs in test mode with opt-in recursive coverage of spawned children; EDN-fixture output in corpus shape. Spec-side acknowledgment landed in 005 §'Model-based testing harness — re-frame.machines.test' documenting the substrate guarantees the harness relies on: pure machine-transition, data-only tables, machine-scoped guards/actions as fns, conformance corpus shape. Library work post-v1; v1 substrate is sufficient.

description: Suggestion from the second spec review, not a decision: investigate whether machine definitions are meant to be separately registered via something like `reg-machine`, or whether they only exist as metadata and transition tables attached elsewhere. The current registration, machine, and API docs still leave that surface ambiguous.
close_reason: decision: machine definitions registered via reg-machine id ?metadata definition; surfaced in registrar via (handlers :machine) and (handler-meta :machine id); machine-handler accepts both definition value and registered id (id form preferred); edit: docs/specification/005-StateMachines.md §Machines are a queryable registry kind, API.md, Construction-Prompts.md CP-5

title: Investigate whether CLJS function-valued :fx-overrides should remain in the core reference contract
description: Suggestion from the second spec review, not a decision: investigate whether function-valued `:fx-overrides` should remain part of the main CLJS reference contract or be pushed to explicit test/story sugar. The pattern-level contract is already id-valued; keeping both forms in the main contract weakens portability, inspectability, and AI/tool regularity.
close_reason: decision: locked asymmetry — pattern contract is id-valued :fx-overrides; CLJS reference v1 accepts both id-valued (canonical) and function-valued (test convenience); other-language impls need only id-valued; edits: 002-Frames.md fx-overrides section, API.md banner

title: Handler-body DSL grammar not enumerated (used by every fixture)

title: match-url behaviour for params-validation-failure undefined
description: MEDIUM (completeness). 012-Routing:124-127 says match-url returns {:route-id :params} or nil. URL that matches a route whose :params schema validation fails: :route/handle-url-change returns :route/not-found, but match-url itself's behaviour for that case is unspecified. Define.
close_reason: 012 §Bidirectional URL ↔ params now specifies match-url's behaviour for params-validation-failure: returns the match map with :validation-failed? true and :validation-error fields (per 010); :route/handle-url-change treats validation failure same as no-match (routes to :route/not-found). route-url throws :rf.error/route-url-validation on bad caller-supplied params (added to error category list).

title: Path-pattern grammar undefined for cross-implementation conformance
description: MEDIUM (completeness). 012-Routing:36-42 says path syntax is 'implementation-specific (the CLJS reference uses bidi-flavoured syntax with :param segments and {...}? optional groups)'. Conformance fixture routing_match_url.edn uses /articles/:id and /search?q=... without specifying grammar. Cross-implementation conformance impossible. Action: pick a canonical path-pattern grammar (lean: subset of RFC 6570 URI Templates — well-known across languages, well-tested, codegen support exists). Document explicitly.
close_reason: decision: locked canonical path-pattern grammar — string DSL with literal segments, /:name params, {/...}? optional groups, /*name splat. Schema :rf/route-pattern registered. Spec edited at 012-Routing.md §Path-pattern grammar (canonical); Spec-Schemas.md §:rf/route-pattern.

Action: list v1 grammar (must support: :id, :initial, :context, :states/:on/:target/:cond/:actions/:entry/:exit/:meta); list post-v1 grammar (:always, :after, history, :invoke, parallel); specify error category for v1-user-supplies-post-v1-grammar.

Multiple Specs use different vocabulary for the same concept: 000:22 'isolated runtime boundary'; 002:9 'isolated runtime boundary'; 006:44 'frame contract'; 011:28 'isolated runtime boundaries'; Principles:68 'actor boundary'; 005:35 'actor-system boundary'. Pick one canonical phrase; cross-link from each Spec.
close_reason: 002 Abstract now has a 'Terminology' note clarifying 'isolated runtime boundary' is canonical; 'actor-system boundary', 'frame contract', 'per-request runtime' are different aspects of the same concept used in particular contexts (005, 006, 011 respectively).

title: reg-event-error-handler: :retry-count / :replacement runtime semantics undefined
description: MEDIUM (completeness). 009-Instrumentation:443-474 documents :recovery + :replacement + :retry-count + :notes return shape. Runtime contract for what runtime DOES with :retry-count (max retries? backoff? per-category?) is never specified. Action: define explicitly; either ship retry semantics or strike :retry-count from the documented return shape.
notes: PR #618 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/618. Spec-only change pinning :on-error return-map contract: :replacement shape made category-specific (effect-map for :rf.error/handler-exception, validated-value for :rf.error/schema-validation-failure, rejected for no-substitutable-slot categories); :retry-count struck (never was in contract; :retried recovery enum is reserved for :rf.http/retry-attempt only); policy-fn exceptions caught non-recursively. Two new error catalogue rows: :rf.error/bad-on-error-return (:logged-and-skipped) and :rf.error/on-error-policy-exception (:no-recovery). Impl untouched — no :on-error invocation site exists yet; emit sites will be wired in a follow-on bead. Do NOT close on merge — Mike retains decision authority.
close_reason: Closed

title: reg-view should auto-def the var (per V-1 lean)
description: MEDIUM (best-practice). API.md:33 + 004-Views require user to (def x (rf/reg-view :x ...)). React/Vue/Svelte/Solid all bind components by name implicitly. V-1 is open with lean toward 'user opts in explicitly.' Action: reverse V-1's lean — make reg-view a macro that defs the var by side-effect, the way defn does. Cite: every other reg-* doesn't make you re-bind anything.
close_reason: V-1 resolved: reg-view defs the Var as a side effect using the id's local name (after slash). Reverses the earlier 'opts in explicitly' lean per AI-first goal — AI scaffolding produces one form, not two. :as metadata key allows explicit naming or suppression for the rare cases that need it.

title: Envelope opts schema not formally defined
description: HIGH (completeness). 002-Frames:38-42 lists opts keys; API.md:67 names them. Spec-Schemas has :rf/dispatch-envelope but the *opts* map (the second arg to dispatch/subscribe) is not the same shape as the envelope itself — the runtime promotes opts into the envelope. The opts → envelope transformation is not specified. Action: add :rf/dispatch-opts schema in Spec-Schemas; document the opts → envelope mapping in 002 §Routing.
notes: Pre-flight check: schemas already exist in spec/Spec-Schemas.md. :rf/dispatch-envelope at lines 79-97 with all required keys (:event, :frame, :fx-overrides, :interceptor-overrides, :interceptors, :trace-id, :source, :origin, :dispatched-at). :rf/dispatch-opts at lines 99-115 with the same opts-side subset. Promotion semantics documented at line 117 ('(merge {:event event :frame :rf/default :dispatched-at (now)} opts)'). Cross-references from spec/002-Frames.md §Routing exist at lines 348 (:source enum) and 355 (opts schema link). Both action items from the bead description appear satisfied. No PR opened — no changes needed. Recommend closing with no-op resolution or, if a gap is identified, re-scope and re-file.
close_reason: Already satisfied on main since commit 1edbf791. Both :rf/dispatch-envelope (9 keys) + :rf/dispatch-opts (7-key subset) defined in spec/Spec-Schemas.md lines 79-117 with promotion semantics + cross-refs from spec/002-Frames §Routing. Bead was stale-on-arrival from the original 11-stream sweep.

title: Pattern-vs-CLJS view-targeting contradiction (004 vs 002)
description: MEDIUM (correctness). 004-Views:89 says 'A view always obtains its frame from React context...views never target a frame explicitly.' 002-Frames:248,289-297 explicitly presents the explicit-frame view shape (rf/subscribe [:count] {:frame frame}) as a valid pattern realisation, and OQ-F-12 confirms both are conformant. Action: rewrite 004's prose to acknowledge the dual contract: view body's unqualified dispatch/subscribe come from context (CLJS reference); the underlying contract is explicit-frame which other hosts realise differently.
close_reason: 004's contradicting prose at line 89 rewritten. Now says: inside reg-view body, unqualified dispatch/subscribe come from context (canonical), while explicit-frame addressing (qualified two-arg form) is the escape hatch for cross-frame work. Reconciled with 002's OQ-F-12 — both pattern realisations are conformant; the unqualified injection is the ergonomic default, not the only option.

title: Status messaging overstates how closed the spec is
description: README and specification/README claim 'pattern thesis, goals, minimal core locked' and '011/012 drafted and consistent', but 011 is explicitly a skeleton and the conformance corpus is skeleton. The bar 'AI can one-shot the implementation' is asserted alongside artefacts that are still in-flight. Reconcile the language: either ship the missing parts or acknowledge them as draft-with-gaps.
close_reason: Status messaging now distinguishes 'Locked' / 'Drafted but unimplemented' / 'Skeleton' layers. README's §Status section rewritten with explicit per-layer description: pattern thesis + 11 goals + minimal core + Goal #7 + per-area open questions all locked; reference implementation doesn't yet exist as code; conformance corpus 16 of ~22 fixtures (smaller error-variant cases pending). Conformance corpus README status moved from 'Skeleton' to 'Drafting (16 fixtures of a planned ~22)'. The bar 'AI can one-shot' is now explicitly scoped to v1-required Specs only; post-v1 Specs (005, 007) acknowledged as foundation-hooks-now-library-later.

title: 006-ReactiveSubstrate has Lean: answers other docs depend on
description: Spec 006 is v1-required and other Specs refer to concrete adapters / platform behaviour. But §Open questions S-1..S-3 still use 'Lean:' for adapter selection mechanism, current-adapter introspection, and multi-adapter coexistence. Resolve concretely: how is the adapter selected (compile-time conditional, runtime check, user-explicit), is rf/current-adapter shipped, can multi-adapter run in one process.
close_reason: 006 OQs all resolved with concrete decisions. S-1 adapter selection: compile-time conditional with reader conditionals; install-adapter! for explicit override before any frame exists; calling after rejected with :rf.error/adapter-already-installed. S-2 introspection: current-adapter ships, returns :reagent/:plain-atom/:custom — informational, not for behaviour-affecting branches. S-3 multi-adapter: NOT supported — one adapter per process; future-additive. S-4 cooperative substrate stays post-v1 (unchanged). API.md updated with install-adapter!, current-adapter, init-platform, configure.

title: Tool-Pair v1 runtime surface not in API.md or Spec-Schemas
description: Tool-Pair commits epoch-history / restore-epoch / :rf.epoch/* trace events / DOM source annotations as v1 surface, but they're not in the consolidated API reference (API.md) or the schema list (Spec-Schemas.md). Implementers can't tell whether these are normative or tool-local. Add: API.md tracing/testing/lifecycle sections; Spec-Schemas registers an :rf/epoch-record schema.
close_reason: Tool-Pair v1 surface integrated into the consolidated reference. API.md now has: a §Epoch history section (epoch-history, restore-epoch, configure :epoch-history :depth, :rf.epoch/snapshotted, :rf.epoch/restored trace events); a §DOM source-coord annotations section (configure :dom-source-annotations?). Error contract updated with :rf.error/no-such-sub and :rf.error/duplicate-url-binding. Schema list updated to include :rf/epoch-record (with full Malli definition added to Spec-Schemas) and notes :rf/effect-map is closed. :rf/frame-meta extended with :url-bound? per 012 R-4.

title: 012-Routing has Lean: suggestions where the spec claims to lock the contract
description: Open questions R-1..R-4 are still 'Lean:' answers but the doc-level banner says 'Routing is part of Goal #7... this Spec locks the contract.' Resolve concretely: R-1 (route-id-only vs URL-string in :route/navigate?), R-2 (explicit route-link vs auto-intercept of <a>?), R-3 (same-route param change = remount or update?), R-4 (multi-frame routing — which frame binds to the browser URL?). Different answers produce different runtimes.
close_reason: 012-Routing OQs all resolved with concrete decisions. R-1 navigate accepts both forms (route-id canonical, URL-string escape hatch); schema is :or. R-2 explicit route-link only — no auto-interception of <a>; route-link uses href + on-click with modifier-key respect. R-3 same-route-id-different-params is UPDATE not remount; views read :route/params reactively; no built-in :on-route-enter; convention is fetch effects from :route/handle-url-change. R-4 each frame has its own :route; default frame URL-bound; non-default frames opt in via reg-frame :url-bound? true; runtime errors on duplicate URL bindings.

title: Spec 006 — Reactive Substrate not yet drafted
description: reorient.md §Working design implications and 000 §Scope both say Spec 006 is v1 territory now (no longer feasibility-gated; SSR forces it). README still lists Spec 006 as 'Not yet drafted (v1 territory; SSR forces it).' The substrate-decoupling contract — how re-frame2 stays substrate-agnostic at the architecture level while shipping Reagent as the default CLJS adapter — needs a focused Spec doc covering: the public API of subs/derivations (no Reagent-specific types leaking), the adapter pattern, JVM-runnable subscription computation, the boundary between substrate-agnostic core and Reagent-specific bits. Pulled toward v1 by Spec 011 (SSR). ~250-300 lines.
close_reason: Spec 006 — Reactive Substrate drafted (~280 lines). Sections: Abstract; The boundary (3-layer diagram); What the core owns (registrar, frame, drain, sub topology, sub computation, effect-map interpretation, trace); What the adapter owns (state container, derived values, render, render-to-string, context provider); The adapter API contract (5 functions); CLJS reference: Reagent as default adapter (with sketch implementation); Subscription topology vs subscription tracking distinction; SSR-specific behaviour (plain-atom adapter); 4 open questions; Disposition (v1 ships Reagent + plain-atom adapters). Cross-refs 000 OQ-7, 011, 008, 002, 004.

title: Spec 001 — Registration: standalone doc not yet drafted
description: reorient.md §Consequences for the current document set says each per-area Spec should reflect the new orientation. Spec 001 is a special case: README and 000 acknowledge that the contract is 'locked in 000 §Registration shape' but a dedicated 001 doc has never been drafted. The metadata-map registration shape is described in 000 (and used throughout) but doesn't have its own focused Spec doc covering: the metadata schema, the macro-expansion contract for source-coord capture, named handler functions, schema-versus-type duality, query API on the registry. Worth ~150-200 lines as its own Spec.
close_reason: Spec 001 — Registration drafted (~210 lines). Sections: Abstract; The metadata map (standard keys table); The handler function (named-fn preference); Two forms of the middle slot (legacy vector vs new map); Source-coordinate capture (CLJS macros + per-host alternatives); The query API (handlers, handler-meta, frame-ids, frame-meta); Hot-reload semantics; Per-kind quick reference table; Schema integration; Open questions (R-1 require :doc, R-2 per-kind metadata schemas); Disposition. Cross-refs 000, 002, 009, 010, Construction Prompts, Spec-Schemas.

title: Note three-layer AI-amenable surface in 000-Vision and README
description: Update 000-Vision §Scope and the README to reflect that re-frame2 ships three layers of AI-amenable artefacts: (1) AI-targeted docs (Construction Prompts, Spec-Schemas, MIGRATION, conformance corpus); (2) runtime pair tool re-frame2-pair (Layer 2); (3) meta-improvement Claude skill re-frame2-pair-improver (Layer 3). Layer 3 closes the feedback loop. Also: the spec should make explicit that Claude-skill-shaped artefacts are first-class deliverables of re-frame2 alongside docs/code/fixtures.
close_reason: Three-layer AI-amenable surface noted in 000-Vision §The three-layer AI-amenable surface (after Scope) and in the root README's intro and tree. Layer 1 (AI-targeted docs) lives at docs/specification/. Layer 2 (runtime pair tool) is TBD library, v1 design pending. Layer 3 (pair-improver Claude skill) lives at /skills/, v1 design pending, 'don't action skills yet'. Cross-references: github.com/day8/re-frame-pair (Layer 2 source), github.com/mike-thompson-day8/re-frame-pair-improver (Layer 3 source).

title: Spec re-frame-pair equivalent for re-frame2
description: Per Mike: re-frame2 should ship with the equivalent of re-frame-pair (nREPL-attached AI/REPL agent). EP 009 already discusses forward-compat with re-frame-pair (see §Forward compatibility for tools); design and ship a re-frame2-native version. Capabilities: enumerate registries, read frame app-db, dispatch-sync, hot-swap handlers, observe trace events, structured-error reporting.
close_reason: Tool-Pair.md drafted (~220 lines). Specifies the runtime contract re-frame2 commits to so that pair-shaped AI tools (equivalent to upstream day8/re-frame-pair) can attach to a running app. Sections: What this Spec is and isn't (runtime contract, not the tool itself); What pair-shaped tools do (consolidating upstream's capability surface from the README); What re-frame2 commits to (table mapping each capability to existing Specs); Time-travel (NEW: per-frame epoch snapshots, restore-epoch API); REPL-eval (host's responsibility); Source-mapping (NEW: optional DOM source-coord annotations); What pair-shaped tools NOT to ship (Claude integration, nREPL middleware — separate); Future-compat commitments; Open questions (TP-1 multi-frame UX; TP-2 snapshot cost; TP-3 pair-improver session log); Disposition (v1 ships epoch snapshots + source annotations; the tool itself ships separately).

title: Spec a re-frame2-pair-improver skill (Claude-skill-shaped retrospective analyser)
description:
Per Mike (https://github.com/mike-thompson-day8/re-frame-pair-improver): re-frame-pair-improver is a Claude skill (not a runtime tool) that reviews a user's session with re-frame-pair and turns the session into concrete product-improvement proposals — for the pair tool itself. Diagnosis-first: identifies frictions (retries, confusion, workarounds, stale results, missing observability, brittle platform behavior, hidden prerequisites, trust gaps). Files improvements at multiple layers: SKILL.md / scripts / warnings/results / tests/fixtures / upstream issues. Never files without user approval. Reference repo contents: SKILL.md (the skill itself); references/analysis-lenses.md (friction taxonomy); references/known-frictions.md (recurring patterns); references/issue-template.md (issue drafting). 

Spec the equivalent for re-frame2: a Claude skill (and ideally other-agent-framework equivalents) that reviews re-frame2-pair sessions and proposes improvements to the re-frame2-pair tool. Closes the feedback loop. Pre-alpha is fine — this is meta-tooling that improves over time. Re-titling task: 'Spec a re-frame2-pair-improver skill for retrospective analysis of pair sessions'.
notes:
deferred per Mike: 'don't action skills yet' (session memory rf2-skills-location)

Reversed (Mike, 2026-05-09): action it. Scope expanded — create BOTH re-frame-pair2 AND re-frame-pair-improver2 using v1 originals as base.

Originals:
- C:/Users/miket/code/re-frame-pair
- C:/Users/miket/code/re-frame-pair-improver

Constraint: re-frame-pair2 must NOT depend on re-frame-10x (the v1 dev tool). Use re-frame2's own surfaces — Tool-Pair (epoch history, register-epoch-cb, restore-epoch), trace-buffer ring, source-coords, machine introspection, schema reflection, etc.

This bead splits into:
- rf2-ps4 — re-frame-pair-improver2 (this bead)
- new sibling bead — re-frame-pair2

Both dispatched as parallel background agents.
close_reason: Stale-satisfied: rf2-ps4 + rf2-9ra3 = skills exist at skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/ + skills/re-frame-pair2/ (both substantial SKILL.md). rf2-h44t = ai/findings/xstate-advanced-features-2026-05-13.md exists and ranks features per the bead's brief (concluded: 7 already have / 3 won't ship / 1 defer / 0 ship).

title: mcp-conformance: hermetic live-pair2 overflow variant (auto-spawn shadow-cljs against example)
description:
Follow-on from rf2-ynaoc PR. The current live-overflow conformance variant (test/live-pair2-overflow.js) is gated on $SHADOW_CLJS_NREPL_PORT — set locally on Mike's machine, unset on CI. CI therefore SKIPs the live path; only the degraded-mode end-to-end-pair2.js runs in CI for pair2-mcp.

This bead: build a hermetic variant that spawns shadow-cljs against a worked example (e.g. examples/reagent/tiny-todo) inside the CI job, sets $SHADOW_CLJS_NREPL_PORT pointing at the spawned runtime, runs the existing live-pair2-overflow.js (which becomes path-1 hermetic), then tears it down.

Catches everything the gated-SKIP version misses on CI: cap-trigger threshold drift, marker shape regressions only firing on real payloads, SDK strict-schema rejection of cap-marker shapes — but verified on CI's clean ephemeral runtime, not just Mike's machine.

Scope: new job mcp-conformance-pair2-hermetic in .github/workflows/test.yml that compiles the example, spawns shadow-cljs in the background (background step with PID capture + cleanup trap), exports the nREPL port, and runs npm run test:pair2-live-overflow. Plus auxiliary tweaks to live-pair2-overflow.js if any (e.g. allow form selection via env so the example app dispatching is exercised instead of the generic eval-cljs path).

Source: rf2-ynaoc.

title: [pair2-mcp] server-side hash precheck for cache (skip nREPL round-trip on cache-hit)
description:
Follow-on from rf2-3rt1f.

The rf2-3rt1f cache saves wire bytes but still pays the nREPL round-trip on every call: tool runs server-side, result is computed, then hashed client-side, then either emitted or replaced with a :rf.mcp/cache-hit marker.

A full optimisation would precompute a stable hash on the runtime side (e.g. (hash app-db) — or per-frame, or per-slice — at the point of state-mutation, exposed via a cheap accessor). The MCP server's first call to a tool would:

1. Ask runtime for the current hash via a single bencode round-trip.
2. If hash matches the LRU entry for (tool, args), short-circuit with the :rf.mcp/cache-hit marker. No body fetch.
3. Else proceed with the full tool eval and replace the cached hash with the new one.

That collapses the cost of the byte-identical second-inspect from 'full snapshot eval + transform pipeline' to 'one bencode round-trip with a number'.

The wire-protocol vocabulary stays the same — :rf.mcp/cache-hit is the contract. Only the implementation shifts from 'compute result then compare' to 'compare then maybe compute result'.

## Subtleties

- The runtime hash needs to be cheap to read. (hash app-db) on every dispatch is too expensive; better to cache it at the point of mutation (next-db wrap).
- Per-frame: each frame's app-db hash separately, since the snapshot/get-path tools key by frame.
- Path-sliced reads (rf2-tygdv) need their own sub-hash — cache per (frame-id, path-vec, db-hash).
- The probe op needs to live in re-frame-pair2.runtime — e.g. (re-frame-pair2.runtime/app-db-hash frame-id) returning the cached value.

## Acceptance

- Runtime exposes a stable per-frame app-db-hash accessor.
- pair2-mcp's apply-cache short-circuits with the marker when the prepass hash matches, without running the tool eval form.
- Latency observation: a same-state snapshot/get-path call is one bencode round-trip; the dispatch path skips snapshot-state + transform pipeline.
- Tests: probe hash matches before/after no-op; mismatches after dispatch; benchmarks confirm short-circuit.

title: day8/de-dupe: tools.build pom emitter ignores :mvn/scope — provided-scope semantics lost vs lein
description:
rf2-ucbuz follow-on. After the lein→tools.build swap, the published pom now lists org.clojure/clojure and org.clojure/clojurescript as ORDINARY (non-provided) dependencies. Previously (project.clj) they carried :scope "provided", meaning consumers wouldn't transitively pull a specific Clojure/CLJS version.

Root cause: clojure.tools.build.tasks.write-pom/to-dep only handles :exclusions / :optional, NOT :mvn/scope. Even adding :mvn/scope "provided" to deps.edn won't affect the emitted pom — the emitter silently drops it.

For a CLJC library this is a minor semantic change (consumers usually have Clojure/CLJS already, and version resolution picks the higher), but worth tracking. Options:
1. Accept — lib is small, consumers have these anyway.
2. Post-process the emitted pom.xml in build.clj to inject <scope>provided</scope> on the Clojure deps before jar.
3. Wait for upstream tools.build to honor :mvn/scope (raise an issue at https://github.com/clojure/tools.build).

Recommend option 2 (cheap, explicit). Implement in build.clj's jar fn between write-pom and copy-dir: parse + modify pom.xml on disk.

Source: rf2-ucbuz agent's final report, 2026-05-13 ~20:48 AUSEST.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/de-dupe/pull/6 — build.clj post-processes pom.xml to inject <scope>provided</scope> on Clojure + CLJS deps. Verified locally: jar's META-INF/maven/day8/de-dupe/pom.xml carries the scope tags. No version bump (0.3.0 already tagged; next release picks it up).

title: [story] :play step-through scrubber on :test pane (10x epoch wiring)
description:
## SB9 parity (rf2-v05qb)

Storybook 9's debug panel lets you step through the play sequence interactively — click a step in the timeline and the canvas re-renders at that step. Story's :test pane today shows the assertion record + source-coord per failure (tools/story/spec/009-Test-Mode.md §'Per-test rows') but no step-by-step scrubber.

The runtime data is all there: each event in :play is a dispatch, the trace bus emits each, and re-frame-10x already does this for the host app via its epoch buffer. Story has the 10x epoch panel registered as :rf.story/10x-epoch (per tools/story/spec/005-SOTA-Features.md §'10x epoch panel embed').

## Proposed shape

Wire the 10x epoch buffer to the :play step indices when the user is viewing the :test pane:

1. On :test-pane mount, the variant's frame's epoch buffer becomes the data source.
2. Each event in :play maps to one epoch (the trace bus already emits :event/dispatched per dispatch).
3. The :test pane shows a thin timeline strip above the per-row table: one tick per :play event, with status colouring (pass = the row's assertions passed; fail = at least one assertion failed at that step).
4. Click a tick → 10x's load-epoch event re-positions the variant's app-db at that step; the canvas re-renders against it.

## Where this lives

- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/test_mode.cljs (timeline strip)
- tools/causa or day8/re-frame2-causa (10x epoch integration — depends on Causa's load-epoch surface staying stable; per tools/story/spec/005-SOTA-Features.md §'10x epoch panel embed')
- tools/story/spec/009-Test-Mode.md (new §'Step-through scrubber')

## Depends on

- :rf.story/10x-epoch panel registration (shipped per spec/005)
- Causa's load-epoch / next-epoch / previous-epoch surface (per tools/causa/)

Storybook 9 advertised a 48% bundle-size drop vs SB8. Story's production-elision sentinel (per tools/story/spec/005-SOTA-Features.md §'Production elision under :advanced') means the Story bundle is zero bytes in production — but the dev bundle is what compares, and we haven't measured it.

## Proposed shape

A one-shot benchmark:

1. Build a representative re-frame2 project with Story enabled in dev (e.g. examples/reagent/counter_with_stories per spec/007 §See also).
2. Measure the dev bundle size (`npm run story:build` or shadow-cljs report).
3. Build an equivalent skeleton SB9 install with one matching component.
4. Report the comparison as a row in tools/story/spec/005-SOTA-Features.md (new subsection or appended to the existing performance discussion).

title: [story] Watch-mode auto-re-run :test on variant content change
description:
## SB9 parity follow-up (rf2-v05qb)

Storybook 9's Vitest addon ships a watch-mode toggle (eye icon) that re-runs only the changed stories on file save. Story's :test pane explicitly defers this (tools/story/spec/009-Test-Mode.md §'Out of scope at v1') because the runtime does not yet expose a stable signal for 'this variant's :play sequence changed since the last run' — the hot-reload signal fires on registration changes generally but doesn't differentiate :play / :events drift from docstring drift.

## Proposed shape

Two changes:

1. **Registrar refinement.** `re-frame.registrar/handlers` emits a stable :rf/content-fingerprint per registration (already shipped via snapshot-identity for variants per spec/007 §Variant snapshot identity). Watch mode subscribes to fingerprint deltas and triggers re-run only when the variant's serialised body (events + play + decorators) actually changes.
2. **Watch-mode toggle.** An eye-icon button on the chrome-level test widget (depends on rf2-q0irb landing first) toggles auto-re-run. When on: a variant whose fingerprint changed re-runs in the background; its sidebar status dot transitions through 'running' to the new pass/fail.

## Where this lives

- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/state.cljc (watch-mode flag + fingerprint subscription)
- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/sidebar.cljs (eye-icon toggle on the widget)
- tools/story/spec/009-Test-Mode.md (move watch-mode out of 'Out of scope at v1')

## Depends on

- rf2-q0irb (chrome-level test widget — watch-mode is the toggle on the widget; without the widget there's no host for the affordance)
- snapshot-identity already shipped per spec/007 §Variant snapshot identity, so the fingerprint primitive is in place.

title: [story] Sidebar tag-as-badge affordance on variant rows
description:
## SB9 parity (rf2-v05qb)

Storybook 9's badges addon renders tag chips inline next to each sidebar entry — high-signal for 'this is alpha' / 'this is deprecated' at-a-glance scanning. Story today only shows tag chips in the filter row, not on the variant rows themselves.

## Proposed shape

Each variant row in the sidebar renders a row of small tag-chip badges to the right of the variant id, colour-coded by axis (when reg-tag carries :axis — depends on rf2-frtec) and showing only the tags the variant carries beyond the canonical `#{:dev :docs}` baseline.

Visual: one chip per tag, monospace, the same palette the filter row uses, smaller font, no hover affordance (the filter row owns the interaction).

## Where this lives

- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/sidebar.cljs §variant-row (new badge subcomponent)
- tools/story/spec/005-SOTA-Features.md (add line item to v1.1 ship list or to a new sidebar polish section)

## Depends on

Best paired with rf2-frtec (`:axis` slot) so badges colour-code by axis. Without :axis the badges are still useful but visually flat.

title: [story] reg-tag: add :axis + :default-filter slots (status/role/team facets)
description:
## SB9 parity (rf2-v05qb)

Storybook 9 explicitly names status / role / team / feature as the four canonical tag axes; `.storybook/main.ts` declares tags with `defaultFilterSelection: 'include' | 'exclude'`. Story's seven canonical tags are flat (no axis), and every tag starts unfiltered (no default-exclude pattern), so :internal / :experimental show up alongside :dev in the sidebar until manually toggled off.

## Proposed shape

Extend reg-tag body shape:

```clojure
(rf/reg-tag :alpha
  {:doc "Pre-release status."
   :axis :status                      ;; new — toolbar grouping hint
   :default-filter :exclude})          ;; new — pre-applied to the sidebar filter

(rf/reg-tag :auth-team
  {:axis :team})
```

The sidebar tag-filter row groups by :axis (mirroring 010-Toolbar.md §Optional grouping for reg-mode) and renders each axis as a collapsible facet group. Tags with :default-filter :exclude pre-exclude their variants from the dev sidebar at boot.

## Where this lives

- tools/story/spec/001-Authoring.md §reg-tag (body-shape extension)
- tools/story/src/re_frame/story.cljc (reg-tag macro / validation)
- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/sidebar.cljs §tag-filter-row (group by axis; honour default-filter)
- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/state.cljc (initial tag-filter state from registered :default-filter)

title: [story] Test Codegen: assertion-inserter affordance mid-recording
description:

Storybook 9's Test Codegen addon lets the user click 'Add assertion' during recording and pick a Testing-Library matcher. Story's recorder (shipped rf2-5fc15) emits captured events as the :play body, but has no UI affordance to scroll-pick from the seven canonical :rf.assert/* events mid-recording — the author currently hand-types the assertion after stop.

## Proposed shape

- Keyboard / button affordance on the REC chip: 'Add assertion' opens a small picker.
- Picker enumerates registered :rf.assert/* events via (rf/handlers :event) per spec/007 §Assertion vocabulary.
- Selected assertion appears in the captured stream alongside dispatched events.

title: [skills] Codify leaf-size ceiling in skills/README.md
description:
Optional follow-up from rf2-94gpb audit.

Today the 'leaves ≤250 L, target ~150 L' rule is restated in 7+ places (per-skill spec/authoring-prompt.md and spec/design.md files). The corpus-wide skills/README.md does not carry it.

Audit finds the rule is sound (max leaf is 203 L, p95=148 L, p90=129 L, p80=110 L). The previously-cited 365 L / 300 L violators in re-frame-migration are gone — those files have been split already.

Proposed: append a 'Leaf size discipline' subsection to skills/README.md§Layout convention with:
- Each leaf SHOULD be ≤250 lines AND ≤16 KB (target ~150 lines / ~10 KB).
- SKILL.md orchestrators SHOULD be ≤500 lines (target ~300-400).
- No SKILL → A → B chains; one level deep.
- Catalogue-shaped leaves (e.g. re-frame-pair2/references/recipes.md, currently 203 L / 21 procedures) may exceed the ceiling if splitting would multiply file-handle overhead without reducing tokens-per-session. Test: would splitting reduce total tokens loaded per session?

Audit doc: ai/findings/skill-leaf-size-audit-20260513.md (local-only, gitignored).

Discretionary — Mike may prefer to leave the rule per-skill. Filing as P3 since it's a polish/normalisation, not a correctness issue.

title: mcp-conformance: live-pair2 variant exercising cap-markers under real overflow conditions
description:
Follow-on from rf2-cum40 PR #702. The harness today drives pair2-mcp in degraded mode (no nREPL connected). Cap markers (:rf.mcp/overflow, :rf.size/large-elided, etc.) are testable in shape but not in their natural overflow trigger.

Add a live-pair2 conformance variant: gated on $SHADOW_CLJS_NREPL_PORT env, boots a real shadow-cljs runtime, forces a payload over the 5K cap to trigger overflow markers in their natural setting. Asserts the client correctly parses the marker structure.

This catches: cap-trigger threshold drift; marker shape regressions that only fire on real payloads; client-side parse failures on cap-marker shapes the SDK's strict CallToolResultSchema doesn't yet recognise.

Scope: tools/mcp-conformance/test/live-pair2.js (new) + .github/workflows/test.yml step (gated/optional since real-runtime tests are flakier).

title: spec/011 SSR: pin :rf.server/request storage substrate (defonce side-channel atom, MUST NOT ride app-db)
description:
Drift sweep observation from rf2-2d1f3 audit:

The :rf.server/request impl (rf2-e825b, implementation/ssr/src/re_frame/ssr.cljc) uses a per-frame defonce side-channel atom keyed by frame-id (mirrors pending-error-traces). The docstring explicitly justifies this:

  '... not in app-db so the request never rides the hydration payload to the client.'

This is load-bearing — putting the request in app-db would leak Host/Cookie/Authorization headers to the client via the hydration payload. The spec (011-SSR.md §Server-only reg-cofx for request context, around line 316) defines the cofx contract but is silent on the storage substrate.

Decision: either (a) lock 'request MUST NOT be stored in app-db; ports MUST use a side-channel substrate that does not ride the hydration payload' as a normative MUST in spec/011, or (b) leave it as impl latitude and add a short DESIGN-RATIONALE note noting the precedent.

Recommendation: option (a) — too easy to get wrong in a fresh port. The privacy risk is concrete (auth headers leaking client-side).

Parent audit: ai/findings/spec-guide-sync-audit-20260513-1935.md (rf2-2d1f3).
notes: PR #692 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/692. Added '#### Request storage substrate' subsection (+11 lines) under §Server-only reg-cofx for request context. 8 MUST-level statements (5 MUST + 3 MUST NOT). Pins: MUST NOT ride app-db, MUST use framework-private side-channel keyed on frame-id (defonce atom canonical CLJS, ConcurrentHashMap canonical JVM, contract pinned not data structure), host adapter MUST populate before drain + clear after response, per-frame isolation under concurrent load. Anchors validated via check_doc_slugs.py.

title: story-mcp tests: drive a failing :rf.assert/* through the self-healing loop
description:
From rf2-h8z5l PR #687 follow-on gap #2. The live-server.js test exercises the happy path (vacuous pass) but doesn't drive a FAILING :rf.assert/path-equals through the run-variant → read-failures loop.

Add a test scenario that:
1. Registers a variant with a :play body that asserts something we know will fail (e.g. asserts :app-db slot value before any dispatch)
2. Calls run-variant — assertion fails, :passing? false, :assertions has the failed entry
3. Calls read-failures — returns non-empty :failures vector with structured failure data
4. Asserts the failure shape matches the :rf.assert/path-equals contract

This proves the full agent self-healing loop (write → run → fail → read-failures → refine) end-to-end. Currently the existing tests cover only the optimistic path.

Source: rf2-h8z5l PR #687 surfaced gaps section.

title: Causa :rf.causa/trace-buffer: same reactive-container fix as rf2-0vxdn
description:
From rf2-0vxdn PR #681 cross-cutting observation. The :rf.causa/trace-buffer sub thunks trace-bus/buffer (a plain atom outside the reactive sub-graph) — same architectural smell as the suppressed-sensitive-count fix.

Effect: trace-buffer-derived UI (trace panel rows, freshness badges) only updates on the next unrelated sub-graph recompute, not immediately on buffer push.

Fix pattern (lifted from rf2-0vxdn): move the buffer to app-db at [:rf/causa :trace-buffer] (or similar). trace-bus push becomes an event dispatch instead of a swap!. Dual-write from trace_bus.cljc if direct-call sites need JVM compatibility.

Scope: tools/causa/src/day8/re_frame2_causa/trace_bus.cljc + registry.cljs + targeted tests.
