title: [clarify] Spec 002 :test preset references optional Spec 014 fx with no port-omission carve-out
notes: spec/002-Frames.md:224-231 documents :test preset's :fx-overrides as {:rf.http/managed :rf.http/managed-canned-success}. Spec 014 is an optional capability (per Conventions §Reserved namespaces + §Packaging conventions). A port that omits Spec 014 and creates a :test preset frame would get :rf.error/no-such-fx on the first :rf.http/managed lookup. Direction: add a port-conformance row saying the :fx-overrides expansion is empty on ports that omit Spec 014; also clarify clock-stubbing path (interop layer, not preset).
close_reason: Closed

title: layout-debug toggle clicks don't update the rendered checkbox
description:
The :rf.story.panel/layout-debug panel renders three [:label] toggles (measure / outline / pseudo). Clicking a label fires (toggle-layout-debug! variant-id id) which swaps the layout-debug-toggles r/atom — but the rendered <input type='checkbox' readonly> never re-renders to reflect the new state.

Reproduced via Playwright in examples/reagent/counter_with_stories/counter_with_stories.spec.cjs (rf2-kljn): clicking the label and polling for isChecked() never succeeds; layout-debug-view's outer HTML is byte-identical before and after the click.

Discovered: 2026-05-12 while writing rf2-kljn Playwright coverage.

Likely cause: panel-host wraps each panel in [canvas/frame-provider-ns-safe {:frame variant-id} [view-fn variant-id]] and the as-element conversion may break Reagent's deref tracking; or the r/atom in layout-debug-toggles isn't observed because the layout-debug-view is registered via rf/reg-view* and converted to a static React element through that path.

Note also: per the v1 docstring the toggle is informational — the state records the user's preference and the canvas-side render-time merge that turns the preference into an applied decorator is a Stage-6-or-later refinement. So fixing this requires either (a) wiring the rendered :checked attribute properly so the user sees feedback, or (b) being explicit in the docstring that the toggle UI is purely advisory until the canvas-merge lands.

Also surfaces a separate (small) issue: when on /loaded variant the Story shell logs a React warning 'Encountered two children with the same key, :counter-with-stories/log-decorator' from controls/decorator-list — the same decorator id appears twice in the resolved stack (story-level + variant-level), and the (for) :key falls back to id which collides.
notes: Files involved: tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/panels.cljs (layout-debug-view), tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/controls.cljs (decorator-list duplicate-key).
close_reason: Closed

title: [tools/*] Polish: move bead-id references out of user-facing Vision prose
description:
Per-tool /spec audit (2026-05-12, ai/findings/per-tool-spec-audit-2026-05-12.md) found multiple tools have bead-id references in user-facing Vision prose, which the rf2-bfax convention says should live only in DESIGN-RATIONALE or footnotes.

Occurrences:
- tools/story/spec/000-Vision.md §Research lineage — names rf2-m6tu, rf2-94b0 inline.
- tools/template/spec/000-Vision.md opening blockquote — leads with 'Bead [rf2-lrtc]'.
- tools/10x/spec/000-Vision.md §Status — 'Spec corpus landed via rf2-1lls (2026-05-12)'.
- tools/template/spec/000-Vision.md §Cross-references — refs rf2-bfax via tools/README.md (this one is acceptable — refs tools/README.md which is the convention pointer).

Action — cross-cutting polish pass:
- Move each user-facing bead-id reference into the relevant DESIGN-RATIONALE.md (under a §Lineage or §Audit-trail subheading), or into a small footnote at the bottom of the Vision file.
- Vision prose then reads as evergreen documentation, not a project-management artefact.

Also noted: forward-references to tools/machines-viz/ (which does not yet exist) in tools/10x/spec/000-Vision.md — phrase consistently as 'future / planned / not-yet-implemented' across the corpus. Optional sub-task; can be deferred until machines-viz lands.
close_reason: Closed

title: [tools/template] Add spec/findings/ subdirectory to preserve audit trail
description:
Per-tool /spec audit (2026-05-12, ai/findings/per-tool-spec-audit-2026-05-12.md) found tools/template/spec/ is otherwise complete (000-Vision + 2 capability docs + API + Principles + DESIGN-RATIONALE — 303-line DESIGN-RATIONALE is exemplary) but missing findings/ subdirectory.

The DESIGN-RATIONALE captures the *outcomes* of the design investigation (clj-new vs deps-new, :edn-args ecosystem coercion, substrate-coercion logic) — but the trail-of-investigation is not committed. Lower priority than the story/ and 10x/ findings because the decisions are simpler and the rationale is sufficient on its own.

Action:
1. Check local ai/findings/ for any template-design audit findings (clj-new pilot investigation, :edn-args debugging session, etc.).
2. If extant findings exist, copy them to tools/template/spec/findings/.
3. If no extant findings, file a 'nf' finding bead to capture the design-investigation summary retroactively — short doc (~500 words) covering: why clj-new vs deps-new vs bb-wizard, the :edn-args strip-on-create discovery, the substrate-coercion ecosystem variance.

Reference shape: tools/story/spec/findings/ and tools/10x/spec/findings/ are the two existing examples — both are 2-file with substantive (~5,000-word) docs. Template's findings would be lighter; one summary doc is enough.
close_reason: Closed

title: [tools/story-mcp] Add Principles.md to spec/
description:
Per-tool /spec audit (2026-05-12, ai/findings/per-tool-spec-audit-2026-05-12.md) found tools/story-mcp/spec/ is materially complete (000-Vision + 3 capability docs + API + DESIGN-RATIONALE) but missing Principles.md.

Lift the load-bearing principles currently scattered across Vision and DESIGN-RATIONALE into a dedicated Principles.md:

- Thin adapter — Story-MCP adds zero new framework primitives.
- Transport-machinery isolation — stdio/JSON-RPC dependencies are isolated from Story core consumers.
- Stage-marker independence — re-frame.story-mcp.config/stage advances independently of re-frame.story/stage.
- Write-surface gated by default — opt-in via allow-writes? config.
- Protocol version pinned — deliberate bumps, not floating-version semantics.
- Agent-loop is the value-add — the self-healing loop (list → register-variant → run-variant → read-failures) is the user story.

Cross-reference DESIGN-RATIONALE for the WHY behind each. Use tools/template/spec/Principles.md (144 lines, P1–P7) as the structural cookie-cutter.

Optional: also consider whether any MCP-specific research belongs under tools/story-mcp/spec/findings/ (none identified during audit; verify before filing nf bead).
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix reg-view macro to return primary id (matches reg-* contract)
description:
The `reg-view` macro currently returns the auto-defed Var (its expansion ends with `(def sym (re-frame.core/view id))`, and `def` returns the Var, not the body value). Every other `reg-*` returns its primary id per Conventions §`reg-*` return-value convention. The deviation breaks the uniform pattern that lets call-site code thread the registration id without a separate literal.

Fix: change the macro's expansion to return the id (the auto-derived `(keyword (str *ns*) (str sym))` or the `:rf/id` override). One shape is wrapping the `do` so the final expression is `id`, e.g.

```
(do
  (binding [...] (reg-view* id ...))
  (def sym (re-frame.core/view id))
  id)
```

The Var auto-def is a side effect and remains intact; only the macro's terminal value changes.

Scope: implementation/core/src/re_frame/views_macros.clj `expand-reg-view`. Update or add a unit test asserting `(macroexpand-1 '(reg-view foo [x] body))` returns id at the call site, and an integration test asserting `(rf/reg-view foo [x] :body)` evaluates to `:my.ns/foo` (or the explicit `:rf/id` override).

`reg-view*` (the plain fn) already returns id — no change needed there.

Source: rf2-4far (spec PR pinning the uniform reg-* return-value contract).
close_reason: Closed

title: docs(guide): remove generic 'Just because you can, doesn't mean you should' quote — not re-frame-specific
description:
The generic aphorism "Just because you can, doesn't mean you should." appears in two guide chapters:

- \`docs/guide/01-why-re-frame2.md:49\`
- \`docs/guide/12-the-dynamic-model.md:71\`

It's a well-worn generic line — not re-frame-specific, doesn't earn its place. The surrounding prose probably says the same thing more vividly without the quote.

## Action

Remove both occurrences. For each:
1. Read the surrounding paragraph.
2. If the paragraph's argument depends on the line, rewrite the paragraph minimally so it stands without the quote.
3. If the paragraph reads cleaner without it, just remove the line + tighten.

## Discipline

- **Single PR**.
- **NO AI attribution** in commit or PR.
- **Do NOT commit** \`ai/\`, \`decision-beads.md\`, \`findings/\`.
- Touches only the two guide files.
- \`mkdocs build --strict\` should still pass.
close_reason: Closed

title: [adapter-docs] Document the imperative escape hatch per adapter (Form-3 / use-effect)
description:
Each CLJS adapter exposes a different imperative-escape-hatch shape for cases where a view genuinely needs to own a piece of host DOM lifecycle (animation listeners, third-party library mount, ref-based scroll, etc.). The shape exists in each adapter but isn't documented in a discoverable place.

**Edits — one subsection per adapter README/IMPL-SPEC:**

| Adapter | File | Shape |
|---|---|---|
| Reagent | implementation/adapters/reagent/README.md (create if missing) | Form-3 via reg-view* + reagent.core/create-class. Capture (rf/dispatcher) in render closure for frame-aware async dispatch. Cleanup in :component-will-unmount. |
| Reagent-slim | implementation/adapters/reagent-slim/FORM-3.md (already exists; add 'animations / listeners' worked example) | Same shape; 7-key cap; :component-did-mount attach + :component-will-unmount cleanup is canonical. |
| UIx | implementation/adapters/uix/README.md (create if missing) | uix.core/use-effect with deps + cleanup return. Capture (rf/dispatcher) in let above use-effect. |
| Helix | implementation/adapters/helix/README.md (create if missing) | helix.hooks/use-effect with deps + cleanup return. Capture (rf/dispatcher) in let above use-effect. |

Each subsection should include:
- a worked example wrapping an animationend listener (or similar — the canonical animation case)
- the cleanup-fn mandate (without it the listener leaks across re-mounts and hot-reloads)
- the dispatcher-capture pattern (dispatcher captured during render; lifecycle hook fires after commit but closure is established)
- cross-reference to Spec 002 line 373 (the silent fall-back-to-:rf/default rule) and rf2-i5n9 for the broader outer/inner pattern

**Findings:** ai/findings/animation-pattern-audit-2026-05-12.md §1.2 + §5 row 7.

**Discipline:** these are adapter-local docs (close to the code), not spec-tier. Keep each adapter section short — one page maximum. The full spec-side discipline is in 004-Views and Pattern-AsyncEffect; adapter docs just show the per-substrate spelling.
close_reason: Closed

title: [spec] Add RAF / animation-loop instance to Pattern-AsyncEffect
description:
Pattern-AsyncEffect already covers HTTP, WebSocket, IndexedDB, native bridges, etc. Add a single bullet to §Concrete instances:

**RAF / animation loops** — a :ui/raf-loop fx owns the requestAnimationFrame cycle; per-frame replies dispatch a :tick-shaped event carrying delta-time. Cancellation is a sibling fx that cancels the RAF handle.

This is the canonical home for regime-B animations (continuous / physics / game loops) — the fx captures :frame at registration (per the existing Async fx capture rule); per-frame events arrive with the correct frame.

**Edit:** spec/Pattern-AsyncEffect.md §Concrete instances — single new bullet, ~3 lines, between the existing 'AI/LLM API calls' and 'Geolocation' bullets.

**Findings:** ai/findings/animation-pattern-audit-2026-05-12.md §5 row 3.
close_reason: Closed

title: Spec 014 :malli-error? field in decode-failure envelope is CLJS-specific
description: Spec 014 §Failure categories defines :rf.http/decode-failure with public fields including :malli-error?. This field name embeds Malli's identity into the cross-port HTTP failure envelope — a TS port using Zod, a Python port using Pydantic, etc. should not surface a :malli-error? key. The field should be renamed to something port-neutral (e.g. :schema-validation-failure? or :validator-rejected?) or moved into the explainer's free-form body. Discovered while making Spec 010 substrate-agnostic (rf2-osxa); the schemas Spec stripped Malli specifics from its normative core, leaving the HTTP failure envelope as the remaining cross-port leak in this category.
notes: PR #428 — rename :malli-error? to :schema-validation-failure? in spec/014 decode-failure envelope.
close_reason: Closed

title: [a11y] Story shell: color-contrast pass on chrome labels
description:
Discovered while landing rf2-xc65 (landmark + scrollable-region fixes). The remaining axe-core color-contrast violations are shell-attributable but the bead's direction-set was scoped to landmarks + scrollable-region.

axe-core 4.10.0 reports ~22 nodes failing the WCAG 2 AA 4.5:1 contrast threshold in the Story shell chrome:

- Panel section-headers using `color #888` on `background #252526` (controls, trace, scrubber, share, a11y, layout-debug — every panel's :section-h-class style). Computed ratio 4.32:1 (needs 4.5).
- Panel-host header strip badge text `color #666` on `background #2d2d30` — the panel-id badge next to each panel title. Ratio 2.39:1 (needs 4.5).
- Hint text (`color #666` italic) under layout-debug controls and a11y panel idle state. Ratio 2.66:1.
- Sidebar 'Workspaces' section header — same #888 on #252526 baseline.

Fix shape: bump #888 → #b0b0b0 (>= 7:1 on #252526), #666 → #9a9a9a, possibly introduce a chrome-text-muted token in a shared styles ns so the bump applies in one place. The panel/canvas/sidebar/trace/share/scrubber/controls/layout-debug ns each carry their own :section-h-style; a one-shot find-replace plus a sanity scan covers them.

Verify: run the counter_with_stories playground, click A11Y panel 'run', confirm color-contrast violations count drops to zero for shell-attributable elements (user content like the variant-rendered checkbox label still belongs to rf2-xc65's sibling counter-side bead).
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Ch.17: sharpen opening 'URL as a sub, not a routing library' framing + add closing-stakes paragraph
description:
Per audit rf2-53pr findings (ai/findings/guide-character-audit-2026-05-12.md §Ch.17).

ch.17 opens with the claim — 'Routing in re-frame2 is state plus events, not a separate subsystem' — but doesn't DEFEND it with v1-voice stakes. v1's Bidirectional-Routes.md had the framing 'stop thinking about your routing library, start thinking about your URL as a sub.' That rhetorical move is what the chapter opening needs.

Plus: the chapter (~585 lines) closes on §Cross-references with no rhetorical summit. By line 580 the reader has done real work; the chapter has earned a closing paragraph.

Scope:
1. Sharpen the opening (lines 1-10) of docs/guide/17-routing.md with the 'URL as a sub, not a routing library' framing — make the stakes explicit before the rest of the chapter does the work.
2. Add a closing-stakes paragraph at the end (before §Next links) — what routing-as-state buys you that a hook-based router never could.

Source: https://github.com/day8/re-frame/blob/master/docs/Bidirectional-Routes.md voice register.

Orientation note: v2 has deterministic route ranking, nav-tokens, can-leave guards, multi-frame routing — all data, all enumerable, all SSR-shared. v1 had to lean on bidi/secretary. The 'URL as state' framing is the load-bearing claim; v2 has earned it more fully than v1 did.
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Ch.15: restore mid-chapter 'what this does for you in practice' framings (source-coord, epoch, perf channel)
description:
Per audit rf2-53pr findings (ai/findings/guide-character-audit-2026-05-12.md §Ch.15).

ch.15's first ~70 lines (§The-architectural-punchline) carry the right v1 voice — direct, opinionated, making a case. rf2-1nw6 + rf2-6dyl already restructured well. The gap is that after the opening section, the chapter drops into reference. §Click-to-source / §Scrubbing-time / §Performance describe their surfaces but don't open with 'what this does for you in practice' the way v1's Debugging.md would have.

Scope: at the head of each of the three back-half sections (§Click-to-source, §Scrubbing-time, §Performance-the-prod-friendly-channel) in docs/guide/15-devtools-and-pair-tools.md, insert a short (1-2 sentence) 'what this does for you when you're debugging at 2am' framing before the reference content.

Source rhetorical move: v1 Debugging.md opening voice — short, direct, names where to look first when things go wrong.

Orientation note: v2's three surfaces (source-coord, epoch, perf channel) are all new in v2 — there's no v1 content to lift, just voice register. Tools mentioned (re-frame-causa, re-frame-pair2) are v2-specific; v1 had re-frame-10x only.
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Ch.14: add 'every error you've caught with console.error' framing + story-up six scenarios + close on stakes
description:
Per audit rf2-53pr findings (ai/findings/guide-character-audit-2026-05-12.md §Ch.14).

ch.14 is genuinely new (rf2-x5vd) and structurally complete, but dense-with-tables and light-on-stakes. Three lifts:

1. §Errors-are-structured-trace-events introduces the schema without motivating it. v1's framing would be: 'every error you've ever caught with console.error("something broke", e) has lost half its information. The half that matters.' Lift that voice as the section's setup.
2. The six scenarios (§Common-scenarios) are correct-and-walkable but read as a reference catalogue. Each one could open with a one-line 'the bug you've shipped' framing — Scenario 1: 'a feature flag flipped, your event id was renamed, you didn't catch the rename in every dispatch site.' That shifts each scenario from category to story.
3. Chapter closes on §Where-to-read-next links. Add a closing-stakes paragraph — what structured-errors give you that try/catch never could.

Scope: motivating paragraph in §Errors-are-structured-trace-events, scenario-opening lines in §Common-scenarios, closing-stakes paragraph in docs/guide/14-errors.md.

Source: v1 didn't have an errors chapter; voice register is EffectfulHandlers.md + the v1 README.

Orientation note: v2's error trace stream is per-frame, structured, additive-only. v1 had ad-hoc console.error and an event-error-handler hook. The story is 'structured + queryable + projection-able for SSR' vs 'string in a logger.'
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Ch.13: sharpen opening complaint + restore v1's 'I don't test view functions, here's why' honesty sidebar + v1-voice closer
description:
Per audit rf2-53pr findings (ai/findings/guide-character-audit-2026-05-12.md §Ch.13).

Three voice gaps in ch.13 (Testing):

1. Opening assumes the reader already values testing. v1's Testing.md opened with the COMPLAINT — tests in stateful React apps are painful — and then offered the alternative. Restore that framing.
2. v1's Testing.md had a 'View Functions — I don't tend to write tests for them, here's why' sidebar. That sidebar's value isn't its content but its HONESTY. ch.13 currently presents JVM-runnable view-content testing as straightforwardly Good; v1 admitted there's a class of test that's not worth writing. The admission earned trust. Restore it (adapted: in v2 we CAN run render-to-string headlessly, so the sidebar is 'when's it still not worth writing one?').
3. The §What-you-should-expect closer reads like a survey result. v1's voice would close on the VIBE — 'tests stop being a tax. You write more of them. They run in milliseconds.'

Scope: tighten opening + add the honesty sidebar (adapted to v2's render-to-string availability) + rewrite the closer in docs/guide/13-testing.md.

Source: https://github.com/day8/re-frame/blob/master/docs/Testing.md

Orientation note: v2's view-content tests via render-to-string are real (Spec 011). The sidebar is 'when even that isn't worth it' — the cases where the answer is Playwright, not a snapshot.
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Ch.11: sharpen narrative-half voice; add 'the seam vanishes' payoff after §The-hydration-handshake
description:
Per audit rf2-53pr findings (ai/findings/guide-character-audit-2026-05-12.md §Ch.11).

ch.11 opens well — 'For most of the SPA era, server-side rendering has been an awkward retrofit' is v1-voice. But then the §Why-this-works-at-all section lists the three structural properties without CELEBRATING the seam-vanishing the way v1 would have. v1 didn't have a first-class SSR chapter (out of scope) but the analog is re-frame.md's closing voice — 'the architecture survives contact with reality' register.

Scope: insert a 1-2 paragraph 'the seam vanishes' payoff section after §The-hydration-handshake in docs/guide/11-server-side.md, before the §Reference-and-advanced-topics divider. Cross-reference to ch.18 from-v1's note that v1 SSR was bolt-on.

Note: rf2-5hhk + rf2-6dyl already restructured the ref/narrative split; this is voice-only inside the narrative half.

Orientation note: v2's claim is 'SSR is the same code as client render, in another runtime' — the seam-vanishing IS the claim. v1 had no equivalent; this is voice-from-v1, content-purely-v2.
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Ch.10: restore 'we kept rewriting this' voice in §Why-it-exists + add closing-stakes paragraph
description:
Per audit rf2-53pr findings (ai/findings/guide-character-audit-2026-05-12.md §Ch.10).

Two voice gaps in ch.10 (Doing HTTP requests):

1. §Why-it-exists has the six questions every v1 team had to answer for itself. Currently a bulleted survey. v1's Talking-To-Servers.md voice had the personal-pronoun framing — 'we found ourselves writing the same five lines in every event,' 'every team's answer drifted in subtly different directions.' Lift that lived-experience voice.
2. The chapter ends at §Cross-references with no rhetorical summit. A closing-stakes paragraph — 'your codebase shouldn't carry HTTP archaeology' or 'the canonical envelope is the only thing that lets tooling see all your requests at once' — would give the chapter the close it earned.

Scope: tighten §Why-it-exists in docs/guide/10-doing-http-requests.md + add a one-paragraph closing-stakes section before §Cross-references.

Source: https://github.com/day8/re-frame/blob/master/docs/Talking-To-Servers.md voice register.

Orientation note: v2's :rf.http/managed is the canonical answer; v1 had each team rolling their own (or reaching for re-frame-http-fx / re-frame-fetch-fx). The 'we kept rewriting this' framing is true and the reason :rf.http/managed exists.
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Ch.09: sharpen 'convention not library' rationale + embody form-error failure modes
description:
Per audit rf2-53pr findings (ai/findings/guide-character-audit-2026-05-12.md §Ch.09).

Two voice gaps in ch.09:

1. 'The reason to converge on a convention rather than a library' is passive-voice. v1's voice would lead with 'every team that's tried to build a <Form> component has watched it grow tentacles' — the lived-experience opener.
2. The §Error-visibility section names the two failure modes correctly ('all errors on first render' / 'no errors after submit') but DESCRIBES them rather than EMBODYING them. v1's EffectfulHandlers.md style would put the reader inside the failure ('you've all filled out a form that...').

Scope: tighten the convention rationale + rewrite the §Error-visibility failure-mode setup in docs/guide/09-forms.md.

Source rhetorical move: v1 EffectfulHandlers.md voice + general v1 README convention-justification voice.

Orientation note: v1 didn't have Pattern-Forms; this is voice-only, no content lift from v1.
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Ch.08: sharpen state-machine opening with 'you've been writing these all along' framing
description:
Per audit rf2-53pr findings (ai/findings/guide-character-audit-2026-05-12.md §Ch.08).

ch.08's current opening (lines 1-9) is direct and informative but voice-flat. v1 didn't have a state-machine chapter, but where v1 talked about state-shapes the rhetorical move was 'you're already writing one, you just don't know it yet.' The chapter's §When-to-reach-for-a-machine (line 593+) already carries the right voice; the opening should match it.

Scope: rewrite the opening (lines 1-9) of docs/guide/08-state-machines.md to lead with the 'you've already done this with cond/case/if-let' provocation before reaching the FSM term-of-art.

Source rhetorical move: v1 README + EffectfulHandlers.md voice register.

Orientation note: machines are per-frame in v2 (`[:rf/machines <id>]` per frame). The provocation works equally for both worlds — what people scatter across cond clauses today is what a machine names.
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Ch.06: frame the Signal Graph with 'who finds out, in what order?' question

Per audit rf2-53pr findings (ai/findings/guide-character-audit-2026-05-12.md §Ch.06 #2).

v1's subscriptions.md opened the four-layer Signal Graph by posing the QUESTION — 'when app-db changes, who finds out, in what order?' That question is what makes the four-layer answer land. ch.06's §The-Signal-Graph (after rf2-6ijg landed) has the right content but arrives without the motivating question.

Scope: add a short setup paragraph at the head of §The Signal Graph in docs/guide/06-views-and-frames.md, before the four-layer table.

Source: https://github.com/day8/re-frame/blob/master/docs/subscriptions.md

Orientation note: v1's framing was process-global; v2's is per-frame. The question 'who finds out' is per-frame in v2 ('every layer-2 extractor in this frame re-runs').
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Ch.06: restore 'all I want my view to do is X' refrain in §Views-compute-hiccup-only
description:
Per audit rf2-53pr findings (ai/findings/guide-character-audit-2026-05-12.md §Ch.06 #1).

v1's correcting-a-wrong.md had a refrain — 'all I want my view to do is display the time' — that came back when the refactor was complete. ch.06's §Views-compute-hiccup-only currently lands the before/after correctly but flatly. Restore the v1 rhetorical move: name what the view SHOULD answer, contrast with what it's doing now, return to the SHOULD when the refactor lands.

Orientation note: v1 talked about ratoms + manual deref; v2 has `reg-sub` chaining via `:<-`. The refrain shape is what to lift, not the technical setup.

title: docs(mkdocs): rewrite docs/index.md home page — summary of README; drop 'Three doors' and 'Source' sections
description:
The mkdocs home page at `docs/index.md` should be a tighter **summary of the repo README.md**, not the current "Three doors" / "Status" / "Source" enumeration.

## Drop these from `docs/index.md`

- **"Three doors into this site"** table (Guide / Spec / API) — overengineered; the mkdocs left-nav already exposes these.
- **"Source"** section — repo URL, issues link, implementation link. Belongs in the repo README itself, not duplicated on the docs site.

## Keep / borrow from the repo README

The main `README.md` (commit `acc2e94`, just committed) is the canonical pitch. Borrow its wording and structure to make the docs home page a **summary** of it:

- The Pratchett axe quote (already there).
- The "What is it?" framing (clone substance from README's "What is it?" + "What's novel about it" sections, but **compressed**).
- The three pillars (Spec-first / Views-are-derivative / Tooling-is-first-class) — same three Mike has refined in the README. The docs home can carry tighter one-liner versions; the README has the longer text.
- The Alan Perlis "Turing tar-pit" quote and the "Your language of choice should be Turing complete; your architecture shouldn't be" snark — both newly added to README and worth on the home page.

## Final shape (suggestion — refine in implementation)

```markdown
# re-frame2

> [Pratchett axe quote]

[One-paragraph "what is it?" — lifted/compressed from README]

## What's novel about it

Three things.

**1. Spec-first.** [one tight paragraph; link to spec/]

**2. Views are derivative, not central.** [one tight paragraph]

**3. Tooling is first-class.** [one tight paragraph]

> [Perlis tar-pit quote]

## Status

Alpha. [keep current shape — short status line]
```

Aim: shorter than the current home page, not longer. The home page is the elevator pitch; the guide carries the story; the spec carries the contract.

## Constraints

- Single PR.
- Touches **only** `docs/index.md`.
- **NO AI attribution** in commit or PR.
- Voice: tight, declarative, matches README's voice.
- `mkdocs build --strict` must pass (zero broken-link warnings).

## Cross-references

- `README.md` (repo) — the source the home page summarises. Don't duplicate verbatim; borrow + compress.
- `mkdocs.yml` — verify `docs/index.md` is still the nav's home target; no change needed unless rf2-jucc (skills tab, in flight) restructures the nav.

## Coordination

rf2-jucc (skills tab) is in flight and touches `mkdocs.yml`. This bead touches `docs/index.md` only — no conflict. Safe to dispatch in parallel, but if rf2-jucc lands first the home page should mention the new Skills tab in its intro / nav guidance.
notes: **Status line locked by Mike 2026-05-12**: 'Alpha. But it works.' — that's the entire Status section. No further prose. Already applied in commit on main. The full rewrite covered by this bead should preserve that exact wording (or shorter — but don't expand back to multi-sentence).
close_reason: Implemented directly in commit e73f779 per Mike's accumulated directives (status at top, drop Pratchett, target Reagent/UIx/Helix adapters, drop Spec-first pillar, drop Three doors + Source sections, keep punchy).

title: docs(mkdocs): add Skills top-nav tab with brief per-skill sub-pages
description:
Add a **Skills** entry to the mkdocs top navigation (sibling of "Guide", "Spec", etc.) — each skill in the repo gets a short sub-page explaining what it does and when to reach for it.

## Scope

### mkdocs.yml

Add a new top-level nav entry:

```yaml
nav:
  - Guide: ...
  - Spec: ...
  - Skills:
    - Overview: skills/index.md
    - re-frame2: skills/re-frame2.md
    - re-frame2-setup: skills/re-frame2-setup.md
    - re-frame-migration: skills/re-frame-migration.md
    # ... one per skill folder under /skills
```

(Or whatever shape matches the existing mkdocs nav style — verify with the current mkdocs.yml.)

### New files under `docs/skills/`

One markdown file per skill folder under `/skills/`. Each is **brief** (Mike's emphasis) — pitch + when-to-use + a paste-ready kickoff hint + pointer to the skill's full files. Aim for ~80-150 lines per sub-page, not the full SKILL.md text.

Required sub-pages (verify the current `/skills/` directory listing):
- `docs/skills/index.md` — Overview: what skills are, how to use them in Claude Code, the list of available skills with one-line teasers.
- `docs/skills/re-frame2.md` — the authoring skill (when you want Claude to author re-frame2 code).
- `docs/skills/re-frame2-setup.md` — setup skill.
- `docs/skills/re-frame-migration.md` — v1→v2 migration skill (landed via rf2-n78f).
- Any other skill folders under `/skills/` get a sub-page.

### Content per sub-page

- **One-paragraph pitch** — what the skill does, in plain English.
- **When to reach for it** — a couple of trigger phrases / situations.
- **Kickoff** — paste-ready prompt (lift from the skill's `SKILL.md` or its `reference/kickoff-prompt.md` if one exists).
- **Where the skill lives** — link to the actual `/skills/<name>/` folder.

Don't duplicate the SKILL.md verbatim — these are entry-ramps, not the full skill.

## Discipline

- **NO AI attribution** in commits, PR title, body.
- **Do NOT commit** `ai/`, `decision-beads.md`, `findings/`.
- Verify `mkdocs build` passes with the new nav + new files.
- **Single PR**.
- If the skills are not currently in mkdocs's `docs_dir`, two options: (a) copy the SKILL.md content into the new sub-pages (duplication risk), or (b) add an mkdocs include / symlink. Pick the cleaner path — likely (a) with the brevity constraint, or use `mkdocs-include-markdown-plugin` if it's already enabled.

## Depends on

- rf2-aqys (chapter renumber) — landed.
- rf2-aswf + rf2-t462 + rf2-15fz (v1 mega) — in flight; affects mkdocs.yml nav shape. Wait for that to merge before dispatching this bead so the agent sees the final nav structure.

## Rationale

Skills are a first-class part of re-frame2's surface (re-frame2 authoring, re-frame2-setup, re-frame-migration, and growing). They deserve discoverability from the docs site, not just from `skills/` directory browsing.

title: Forms page (05a/09): evaluate colocated handlers for HTTP + flows for error-state consolidation
description:
Design-review for `docs/guide/05a-forms.md` (post-rf2-aqys: `docs/guide/09-forms.md`).

Two questions:

## Q1 — Should colocated handlers be used for the HTTP work?

The Forms page demonstrates form submission. Currently the HTTP submission uses standalone `reg-event-fx` handlers (likely registered at namespace top level, separate from the view that dispatches them).

**Question**: would **colocated** handlers — handlers registered inline with the view (or alongside it in the same namespace, near the dispatch site) — read more cleanly for the Forms pattern? The submission handler is specific to that form; lifting it to a top-level registration adds distance between where it's defined and where it's used.

Evaluate:
- Does re-frame2's surface support colocated registration cleanly? (`reg-view` + `reg-event-fx` in the same form?)
- What's the trade-off between "registration lives near use" and "registration lives at namespace boundary"?
- Does the existing Forms page implicitly recommend one shape via its example? Should it explicitly pick a side?

## Q2 — Could flows be used to automatically consolidate error state?

The Forms page's `Pattern-Forms` shape carries error state in `:errors` (per-field) + `:submit-error` (transport-level). Currently these are written by handlers (validation handler writes `:errors`; HTTP failure handler writes `:submit-error`) and read by per-field-error subs.

**Question**: could a **flow** (Spec 013) declaratively consolidate these? A flow could observe both `:errors` and `:submit-error` paths and produce a single `:all-errors` derived slot, eliminating the per-field-error sub's need to OR across multiple sources.

Evaluate:
- Is this a good fit for flows, or does it stretch them? Flows are declarative derivations; per-field errors are a derivation, but the question is whether the derivation justifies the flow machinery.
- Would it simplify or complicate the Pattern-Forms idiom?
- What's the runtime cost of a flow for this case vs the current sub-based approach?
- Does the answer change for `:form-region` machines (which already use machine `:data`)?

## Output

A `bd` note appended to this bead with the recommendation per question:
- Q1: Should the Forms page recommend / show colocated handlers? Y/N + rationale.
- Q2: Should the Forms page recommend / show a flow for error consolidation? Y/N + rationale.

If the answer to either is yes, **file a follow-up bead** scoping the actual page edits. Don't edit the page in this bead — this is design-review only.

## Discipline

- Read-only review.
- Output: notes appended via `bd update --notes` OR a recommendation file in `ai/findings/forms-page-design-review-<date>.md` (local-only).
- **Do NOT commit** `ai/`, `findings/`.
- No PR for this bead — the deliverable is the recommendation.

## Cross-references

- `spec/Pattern-Forms.md` — the canonical Pattern-Forms shape.
- `spec/013-Flows.md` — flows spec.
- `examples/reagent/realworld/auth.cljs` — runnable Pattern-Forms example.
- `examples/reagent/realworld/settings.cljs` — runnable `:form-region` machine variant.

## Status note

Held by rf2-aqys (chapter renumber in flight). After the renumber, the target file becomes `docs/guide/09-forms.md`.
notes:
@
Design review complete. Findings doc: ai/findings/forms-page-design-review-2026-05-12.md (local-only, gitignored).

TERMINOLOGY NOTE first: "co-located handler" already means something specific in chapter 10 / Spec 014 — one event id handles BOTH request and reply via the :rf/reply sentinel. Pattern-Forms current shape (three sibling handlers, explicit :on-success / :on-failure) is chapter 10s documented escape hatch. The bead Q1 reads "colocated" in the namespace-organisation sense, which is a different concept. Both senses considered below.

== Q1 — Colocated handlers for HTTP work in the Forms page ==

RECOMMENDATION: NO. Keep the three-handler shape (:submit, :submit-success, :submit-error). Optionally add a §Variations paragraph naming chapter-10s co-located-reply shape as an alternative for very small forms with convergent paths.

Sub-question A (ns-local registration — handlers live near the view): already present in the chapter and in auth.cljs / settings.cljs. No change.

Sub-question B (chapter-10 §co-located reply — collapse the three submit handlers into one via :rf/reply):
1. The seven-event lifecycle is the conventions teachable spine; collapsing breaks it.
2. :submit-success and :submit-error paths DIVERGE non-trivially (snapshot vs cond-> over structured-vs-transport) — exactly the case chapter 10 names as a reason to opt out of co-located default.
3. Three handlers map 1:1 onto three state transitions; the §"round-trip walk-through" leans on that mapping.
4. The :form-region machine variant (settings.cljs) routes via three sibling events broadcasting three machine transitions — it cannot consolidate the same way. Pages adopting handler-side consolidation for the slice variant would teach two shapes; net pedagogical loss.

== Q2 — Flows for error-state consolidation ==

RECOMMENDATION: NO. A flow watching :errors and :submit-error to produce an :all-errors slot fails three of Spec 013s four §When-to-use-a-flow gates:

- value is part of app-state, not view input — NO, only views consume it
- other handlers / machines / schemas need to read it as plain app-db — NO
- value should survive SSR / time-travel / serialisation — IRRELEVANT (form runtime errors arent SSR-hydrated)
- derivation is stable enough to register — yes, but isolated

Spec 013 is explicit: "The derived value is consumed only by views → use a subscription (lighter, sub-cache native, no app-db write)." This is exactly that case.

Additional reasons:
- A flow doesnt eliminate the gating sub (the touched-OR-submit-attempted rule still has to run somewhere). It would be an intermediate cache, not a replacement.
- The existing :field-error sub already merges three sources (:errors, :touched, :submit-attempted?); adding :submit-error is a one-line addition to its signal vector — no flow needed.
- :errors and :submit-error are DELIBERATELY separate per Pattern-Forms §":submit-error vs :errors" (renderable validation vs opaque transport failure, rendered through different view code paths). A consolidated :all-errors flattens a distinction the pattern works to maintain.

Answer doesnt change for the :form-region machine variant — same analysis applies. The machine variant tilts MORE against the flow because the :data map is already the consolidation boundary; a flow that re-projected pieces of :data into another app-db slot would split the locality the machine pattern relies on.

== Follow-up beads ==

None filed. Both recommendations are NO.

Optional candidate (not filed by default — flag for Mikes decision):
- Forms page §Variations: name the chapter-10 co-located-reply shape as an alternative for very small forms where success and failure converge into the same slice writes. One-paragraph addition. Low priority.
@
close_reason: Design review complete. Q1 (colocated handlers): NO — current 3-handler shape is right per Spec 014 co-located-reply opt-out conditions. Q2 (flows for error consolidation): NO — fails 3/4 of Spec 013's 'when to use a flow' gates; view-only derived state belongs in subs. Optional flagged: §Variations note in 09-forms.md naming ch.10's :rf/reply pattern for small convergent forms (not filed as bead — Mike's call if to surface). Findings doc: ai/findings/forms-page-design-review-2026-05-12.md (local-only).

title: JVM flake: async-dispatch-resolves-after-current-drain timing race (drain_test.clj:317)
description:
Sister flake to the one rf2-iosc stabilised (`destroy-frame-discards-pending-events`). Different test, same family.

## Symptom

`async-dispatch-resolves-after-current-drain` in `implementation/core/test/re_frame/drain_test.clj:317` fails intermittently on JVM CI. Captured on PR #354 (rf2-fgvi, docs-only — zero impl changes, so the failure cannot be code-caused):

```
FAIL in (async-dispatch-resolves-after-current-drain) (drain_test.clj:317)
  expected: (= :ok (deref done 2000 :timeout))
    actual: (not (= :ok :timeout))

FAIL in (async-dispatch-resolves-after-current-drain) (drain_test.clj:319)
  expected: (true? (:outside? (rf/get-frame-db :rf/default)))
    actual: (not (true? nil))

FAIL in (async-dispatch-resolves-after-current-drain) (drain_test.clj:322)
  expected: (some #{:outside-async} (clojure.core/deref order))
    actual: (not (some #{:outside-async} [:sync-only :sync-only]))
```

The test waits 2000ms for an async dispatch to complete. Under CI load, the 2s budget sometimes runs out before the async resolves.

## Likely fix shape

Same approach as rf2-iosc — `with-redefs [interop/next-tick ...]` to make the async path deterministic in this test. Or, alternatively, bump the timeout (less principled — papers over the race).

Look at how rf2-iosc fixed `destroy-frame-discards-pending-events` and apply the same pattern.

## In the meantime

Admin-merge affected docs-only PRs (no real code risk) and re-run on CI when the test is touched.

## Cross-references

- **rf2-iosc** (closed via #327) — fixed the destroy-frame flake with the same shape. Same intervention pattern likely applies.
- Mentioned in session summary as "the JVM core flake family" alongside the destroy-frame one.
close_reason: Closed

title: Call-site source-coords for inject-cofx / subscribe / dispatch — macro-stamp the invocation lines
description:
Follow-up to **rf2-3nn8** (handler source-coord on error traces).

rf2-3nn8 attaches the **registration site** of the triggering handler to error events — i.e. for `:rf.error/no-such-cofx` fired inside `(reg-event-fx :user/save ...)`, the trace gets the line where `:user/save` was registered. That's free because registrations already carry source-coord.

This bead adds the **call site**: the specific line *inside* the handler body where the failing call lives — e.g. the `(inject-cofx :foo)` line, the `(subscribe [:bad-sub])` line, the `(dispatch [:missing-event])` line. With both pieces, tooling can render two clickable links per error: "handler defined at X:142" and "failed call at X:147".

## Why P3

- Registration-site coord (rf2-3nn8) is the bigger immediate win — that lands first.
- Call-site coord is precision — diminishing returns for the cost.
- Cost is real: every callable surface that can emit an error (`inject-cofx`, `subscribe`, `dispatch`, `dispatch-sync`, `re-frame.core/->fx`, etc.) needs a macro form that captures `(meta &form)` at the call site.

## Scope

- Decide which call surfaces get macro-form variants vs stay function-only.
- For each macro form, capture `(meta &form)` and bind it to a dynamic var or pass via an internal arg so the runtime can pull it on error.
- Add `:rf.trace/call-site` to the error event shape (sibling of `:rf.trace/trigger-handler`).
- Decide elision behaviour — same as trigger-handler (kept in production) or different.

## Open questions for spec walkthrough later

1. Macro form on existing fn surfaces, or new `dispatch!` / `subscribe!` variants?
2. Single key `:rf.trace/call-site {:ns :file :line :column}` or nest call-site coord under trigger-handler?
3. Elision: same as rf2-3nn8 (production), or dev-only?

## Cross-references

- **rf2-3nn8** (P2) — registration-site coord on error events. Land first. This bead is the precision follow-up.
- spec/Spec-Schemas.md will need the new key added once the questions above are locked.
notes: Decisions 2026-05-13: Q1=C (existing-name macro + dispatch* fn variant per established convention), Q2=A (flat :rf.trace/call-site key), Q3=B (dev-only elision). Pre-alpha green-light. Hold dispatch until queue capacity opens.
close_reason: PRs merged 2026-05-13 batch landing

title: rf2-hoiu spec: re-frame.core.X collides with goog.provide; use re-frame.core-X
description:
Implementing rf2-hoiu (clarity refactor: split optional-artefact wrappers out of re-frame.core) revealed a hard CLJS constraint: goog.provide for re-frame.core unconditionally overwrites the parent object, wiping out any previously-loaded re-frame.core.X namespace.

Reproduction:
  goog.provide('re_frame.core.machines'); // re_frame.core.machines.reg_machine = fn
  goog.provide('re_frame.core');           // re_frame.core = {} -- WIPES the nested ns

So having both 're-frame.core' and 're-frame.core.flows' as cljs namespaces is structurally impossible in the goog.provide model. (Empirically verified in this branch: re_frame.core.machines.reg_machine was defined, then immediately undefined after re_frame.core loaded.)

The bead's spec said Q6 'flat re-frame.core.X (not .impl.X)' -- intent was clearly to avoid deep .impl. paths. The next-cleanest flat form that ACTUALLY WORKS on CLJS: 're-frame.core-X' (dash, not dot). Other valid options: 're-frame.api.X', 're-frame.optional.X', 're-frame.X-api'. Single-hyphen 're-frame.core-flows' preserves Q6's flatness intent and the user-facing 'rf' alias still resolves rf/reg-flow via re-export.

Decision applied in PR (clarity-core-cleanup branch): rename re-frame.core.{flows,machines,routing,schemas,ssr,epoch,http} -> re-frame.core-{flows,machines,routing,schemas,ssr,epoch,http}. Public surface unchanged (rf/reg-flow etc. still resolve via def-aliases in re-frame.core).

References: clojurescript.org/about/differences (lists goog.provide as the canonical reason cljs cannot have a.b and a.b.c both as namespaces); rf2-hoiu bead.
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Ch.09: add three v1 mental-model angles — event-sourcing, perpetual-reduce, whole-app-as-FSM
description:
V1's `all-models-are-wrong.md` offers three powerful framings that re-frame2 ch.09 doesn't carry: event-sourcing, perpetual-reduce, and FSM-as-control-for-the-whole-app.

**Scope (quoted from audit B-IMP-9):**

V1's `all-models-are-wrong.md` offers three powerful framings that re-frame2 ch.09 doesn't carry:

- **Event sourcing**: "to debug, you need the checkpoint state + the events since then. That's just data. Pure, lovely loggable data." Re-frame2's epoch system (ch.11) makes this *more* true than v1, but ch.09 never names it. A paragraph would land.
- **`app-db` as the temp storage of a perpetual reduce over events**: "the value in app-db is the result of performing a reduce over the entire collection of events dispatched up until that time. The combining function is the set of event handlers." This is one of the most powerful mental models in re-frame and it's missing from re-frame2's pitch.
- **The whole app as a finite state machine** (not just registered machines — *the whole app*): "events act like the triggers in an FSM; event handlers act like the rules governing transitions." Re-frame2 ch.05 has formal FSMs but ch.09 misses the higher-level "the whole app is a FSM" framing.

**Specific edits:** add three short sections to ch.09, each one paragraph, in the §"Less powerful by design" arc.

**Source material:** v1 `all-models-are-wrong.md` (https://github.com/day8/re-frame/blob/master/docs/all-models-are-wrong.md).

**re-frame2 chapter affected:** `docs/guide/09-the-dynamic-model.md` — three new paragraphs in §"Less powerful by design".

**Cross-references:**
- B-IMP-10 (events-as-assembly-language) and B-IMP-11 (interconnections framing) are sibling ch.09 additions; consider as a coordinated content drop.
- ch.11 epoch system is the v2 surface that makes event-sourcing concrete.

**Full audit context:** `ai/findings/re-frame1-docs-audit-2026-05-12.md` §5 B-IMP-9.
close_reason: Closed

title: [guide] Add callout about 'events.cljs never required' gotcha — registrations silently don't load
description:
V1's `App-Structure.md` calls out the recurring trap: if `events.cljs` is never `(:require)`-d by any reachable ns, the Google Closure dep tracker won't load it, and registrations silently won't happen. Worth a callout.

**Scope (quoted from audit B-IMP-8):**

V1's `App-Structure.md` calls this out explicitly. Re-frame2 has the same gotcha — if `events.cljs` is never `(:require)`-d by any reachable ns, the Google Closure dep tracker won't load it, and your registrations silently won't happen. Two minutes of "why doesn't my counter work" is the outcome. Worth a one-paragraph callout in ch.02 or ch.13.

- Specific edits: a callout in ch.02 §"Mount", or in ch.13 §"Build something with it."

**Source material:** v1 `App-Structure.md` (https://github.com/day8/re-frame/blob/master/docs/App-Structure.md).

**re-frame2 chapter affected:** `docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md` (§"Mount") OR `docs/guide/13-where-next.md` (§"Build something with it").

description:

**Scope (quoted from audit B-IMP-7):**

V1's `breaking-it.md` is the brave move of saying what's hard. Re-frame2 ch.01 has objections-and-answers but no equivalent "this isn't the right tool for these jobs" honesty. The candidates re-frame2 should acknowledge: very-high-event-rate streaming apps (telemetry firehose); apps where the SPA is a thin veneer over server-side rendering with no client state to speak of (Hotwire-shaped territory); contexts where the team is fully committed to React hooks idioms and integrating re-frame2's pattern would be cultural friction.

- The point isn't to scare readers off — it's to position re-frame2 honestly so the wins land more credibly.
- Specific edits: a small sidebar in ch.01 after §"What re-frame2 keeps."

**Source material:** v1 `breaking-it.md` (https://github.com/day8/re-frame/blob/master/docs/breaking-it.md). Note: v1's section is unfinished; this is an opportunity to finish it for re-frame2's context.

title: [guide] Teach the subscription Signal Graph — four layers, Layer 2 extractors, propagation pruning
description:
Re-frame2's ch.02 introduces `reg-sub` and shows chaining with `:<-`. Neither chapter teaches the **Signal Graph**: the ground-truth → extractors → materialised-view → leaves structure. V1's `subscriptions.md` is the canonical introduction.

**Scope (quoted from audit B-IMP-5):**

Re-frame2's ch.02 introduces `reg-sub` and ch.02 shows chaining with `:<-`. Neither chapter (nor any other) teaches the **Signal Graph**: the ground-truth → extractors → materialised-view → leaves structure that governs *why* you write extractor subs as small as possible, *why* `:<-` exists at all, and *why* tooling like `sub-topology` (ch.11) makes sense. V1's `subscriptions.md` is the canonical introduction.

- Specific edits: add §"The Signal Graph and the four layers" to ch.04, or a new ch.04a "Subscriptions in depth." Carry the `subscriptions.png` image (B-INF-4) and the propagation-pruning rule. Adapt all "app-db" to "frame's app-db."

**Source material:** v1 `subscriptions.md` (https://github.com/day8/re-frame/blob/master/docs/subscriptions.md). Image: `subscriptions.png` (see B-INF-4 — adaptation needed: "app-db" → "frame's app-db").

**re-frame2 chapter affected:** `docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md` new section, or new chapter ch.04a "Subscriptions in depth".

**Cross-references:**
- rf2-ccnp — has an opinion on *when* in the sequence the Signal Graph teaching belongs (probably after ch.04 since views are the consumer); the progressive-disclosure audit will validate placement.
- B-INF-4 (subscriptions.png image port).
- ch.11 `sub-topology` (which currently assumes reader knows the graph).

**Full audit context:** `ai/findings/re-frame1-docs-audit-2026-05-12.md` §5 B-IMP-5.

title: [guide] Ch.03: lift 'Doing vs Causing' + Et-tu-React + Banana issue framing from v1 EffectfulHandlers.md
description:

V1's `EffectfulHandlers.md` opens with a memorable rhetorical move: "I proudly claim this event handler is pure: `(reg-event-db :my-event (fn [db _] (assoc db :flag true)))`" — then shows it's only possible because re-frame itself is doing the side-effect of `reset!`-ing app-db. The "Doing vs Causing" framing then becomes natural. Plus the **"Et tu, React?"** pivot ("Reagent does the same thing — your view returns a description; the framework does the side effect") is *the* pedagogical bridge to "and so we do the same with events."

title: Guide ch.02 'The Frame' section: add reassurance note that frames aren't normally globals
description:
In docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md, the "The Frame" section walks through the canonical counter example which defines a frame as a top-level def. A new reader might worry "wait, is this a global? Is re-frame2 telling me to define globals?"

Add a quick clarifying note at the bottom of "The Frame" section. Something like:

> Note: In a real app, frames are rarely defined as top-level globals. They're typically either:
> - **Unit-test specific** — created inside `(with-frame [f (rf/create-frame :test/X)] ...)` and destroyed when the test exits, OR
> - **Attached to a view** — created once when a parent view mounts, then accessed by all child views via `current-frame` (the dynamic binding pattern, see ch.04 / Pattern-MultiFrame for the canonical shape)
>
> The top-level def in this example is a minimal-mount shortcut — fine for the counter, but you wouldn't ship app code that way.

(Adjust the wording to match the chapter's voice. Verify the names of the patterns / chapters referenced.)

Verify against `examples/reagent/realworld/core.cljs` or another multi-frame example for the "attached to view" idiom — confirm the prose matches reality.

Discovered by Mike 2026-05-12.

Scope: docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md only.
close_reason: Inline note added in commit d9ec707

title: [a11y] Story shell: landmark wrapping + scrollable-region keyboard access — fix axe-core violations from the shell
description:
Discovered by Mike 2026-05-12 alongside the counter-side a11y bead. Axe-core's run from the A11Y panel inside the Story playground showed violations that are the Story shell's responsibility (not the rendered variant's).

Likely violations:

1. **All page content should be contained by landmarks** — the Story shell renders into a generic <div>. Wrap the top-level layout in a <main> and possibly <nav> (sidebar) / <aside> (right panel) so screen readers can navigate by landmark.

2. **Scrollable region must have keyboard access** — some scrollable container (probably the canvas or the right-panel) lacks `tabindex="0"` to make it focusable for keyboard users.

3. Possibly more — re-run the scan in a freshly-cleared shell to enumerate.

Scope: tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/{shell,sidebar,panels,canvas,workspace}.cljc/cljs — wherever the layout DOM is structured. Don't touch the rendered variants (those are user content and a11y is their author's concern; we surface violations via the panel).

Verify after fix: run the counter_with_stories playground, click A11Y panel 'run', confirm shell-attributable violations gone (landmark + scrollable-region rules). Counter-side violations are separately filed.

Discovered alongside the counter-side a11y bead.
close_reason: Closed

title: [a11y] examples/counter_with_stories: button labels + contrast — fix axe-core violations in the counter UI
description:
Discovered by Mike 2026-05-12 while running the Story playground; clicked the A11Y panel's 'run' button (axe-core scan) and saw violations.

Violations attributable to the counter UI itself (examples/reagent/counter_with_stories/views.cljs):

1. **Form elements must have labels** — the +/- buttons render visual glyphs only ('+', '-'). Add `aria-label="Increment count"` / `aria-label="Decrement count"` (or equivalent — verify against axe-core's exact rule). May want a visually-hidden text alternative too.

2. **Minimum colour contrast** — the hint span uses #888 on white (just changed in commit ed52254 / rf2-bnn7). Below WCAG AA's 4.5:1 ratio for normal text. Pick a darker grey (#555 or #444) that still reads as 'secondary' but passes contrast.

3. Likely also surface other violations on the counter view itself (parity-badge styling? count display?) — scan via the playground first, file specifics.

Scope: examples/reagent/counter_with_stories/views.cljs only. Don't touch the Story shell — its violations are filed separately as rf2-? (the Story-shell a11y bead, sibling).

Verify after fix: run the playground locally, click 'run' on A11Y panel, confirm violations gone for the counter view's nodes.
close_reason: Closed

title: Test: unsubscribe-then-no-recompute under value-change
description:

title: Guide ch.05: :record-error action signature shown differently from runnable example
description:
Chapter 05 §Guards and actions shows `:record-error` as:
  (fn [data [_ err]]
    {:data (-> data (update :attempts inc) (assoc :error (or (:message err) "Login failed.")))})

The runnable companion at examples/reagent/state_machine_walkthrough/core.cljc:73-76 has:
  (fn [data [_ {:keys [failure]}]]
    {:data (-> data (update :attempts inc) (assoc :error (or (:message failure) "Login failed.")))})

The difference is the destructure key: the inner event vector folded by the managed-HTTP extras-fold is shaped `[<event-id> {:kind :failure :failure m}]` per Spec 014, so the action sees a map under the :failure key — not a raw err. The guide oversimplifies; readers who copy the snippet will see `(:message err)` always return nil and the fallback string always render. Either (a) match the runnable example's destructure, or (b) add a one-line note that this is a simplified shape and link the runnable. The chapter §Dispatching to a machine §'extras-fold' DOES describe the {:kind :success :value v} / {:kind :failure :failure m} envelope, so updating the snippet to match is consistent with the chapter's own explanation.
close_reason: Closed

title: Guide ch.02: slim/fast adapter (counter_slim_and_fast example) is not mentioned anywhere in the guide
description:
examples/reagent/counter_slim_and_fast/ exists — a counter example mounted on the day8/reagent-slim rewrite (bead rf2-5lbx; binds S3-008 contract from reagent-slim IMPL-SPEC §1.4/1.8/6/12). implementation/adapters/reagent-slim/ holds the slim adapter. Per its core.cljs, the bundle contains no reagent.impl.* nor react-dom/server symbols — a real bundle-size win for apps that don't need full Reagent.

The guide currently mentions three CLJS adapters (Reagent, UIx, Helix) in chapter 02 §'Same pattern on other adapters' and chapter 02's `init!` discussion. It does not mention the slim adapter, nor the bundle-isolation contract it carries. For an audience that cares about ship-size, this is a worth-knowing affordance — a one-paragraph note under §'Same pattern on other adapters' linking to the slim example and the reagent-slim adapter would close the gap.

P3 because most users won't reach for slim until they hit ship-size pressure, and the existing Reagent adapter is the canonical 'first app' pick the chapter promises.
close_reason: Closed

title: Guide ch.05: missing mention of :fsm/parallel-regions / :fsm/tags capability flags and initial-entry cascade fix
description:
Chapter 05 (State machines) covers `:type :parallel` / `:regions` (parallel regions) and `:tags` (state tags) at depth in its narrative sections, but never mentions:

1. The capability-flag namespace these are claimed under (`:fsm/parallel-regions`, `:fsm/tags`) — spec/005-StateMachines.md §1120 + §1383 makes these the names ports declare claim against. The guide audience is Mike's narrative users, not port implementors, so this is genuinely optional — but the §Capability matrix cross-reference is useful for advanced readers wanting to know which features are reference-vs-extension.

2. The initial-state entry cascade fix (rf2-0z73, implemented at machines.cljc:1171-1284). Pre-fix, neither the singleton bootstrap nor `:rf.machine/spawn` ran initial `:entry` actions — apps used workaround `:on :rf.machine/spawned` self-transitions. Now initial `:entry` fires as part of the bootstrap cascade. The guide's `:submitting` state has `:entry :issue-request`, which works correctly under the fix; but a careful reader writing a fresh machine where the INITIAL state has `:entry` should know that path now fires correctly. A one-paragraph note in §What's in a transition or §Wiring a machine would close the gap.

Neither is critical; both are quality-of-life improvements for readers writing non-trivial machines.
close_reason: Closed

title: router.cljc: extract drain-step / drain-settled tail-paths from drain!
description:
implementation/core/src/re_frame/router.cljc lines 252-336 (~85 LoC). drain! has:
- depth-limit branch with atomic rollback + epoch-discard
- empty-queue settle branch with epoch settle!
- per-event recur

All inside one loop body inside try/finally.

ACTION: extract handle-depth-exceeded! (rollback + emit + discard) and handle-drain-settled! (emit + epoch capture) as named tail-paths. drain! body becomes a cleaner cond.

Discovered during clarity review 2026-05-12; see ai/findings/clarity-review-2026-05-12.md §F24.

title: cofx.cljc: replace println no-cofx warning with structured :rf.error/no-such-cofx trace
description:
implementation/core/src/re_frame/cofx.cljc lines 78 and 88 (inside inject-cofx's 1-arity and 2-arity paths):

  (do (println "re-frame2: no cofx registered for" cofx-id)
      ctx)

This is inconsistent with how fx.cljc handles the same case (line 197):

  (trace/emit-error! :rf.error/no-such-fx
                     {:fx-id    fx-id
                      :fx-args  args
                      :frame    frame-id
                      :recovery :no-recovery})

The println:
- bypasses production elision (println isn't gated on debug-enabled?)
- won't be picked up by trace listeners / 10x / re-frame-pair
- inconsistent with the surrounding error contract (every other framework error category is structured)

ACTION: replace with (trace/emit-error! :rf.error/no-such-cofx {:cofx-id cofx-id :event-id (when (vector? event) (first event)) :recovery :no-recovery}) and document the category in the error-contract section of Spec 009.

Discovered during clarity review 2026-05-12; see ai/findings/clarity-review-2026-05-12.md §F22.
close_reason: Fixed in https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/346 — println in inject-cofx replaced with structured :rf.error/no-such-cofx trace mirroring fx.cljc's no-such-fx. Spec 009 + Spec-Schemas updated; cofx_test.clj pins both arities.

title: Guide ch.02: counter example walkthrough diverges from runnable examples/reagent/counter/core.cljs
description:
Chapter 02 §'The whole thing' shows the counter as a 40-line program using `(rf/reg-frame :rf/default {:on-create [:counter/initialise]})` plus `(rf/reg-view counter [] ...)`. The actual runnable file at examples/reagent/counter/core.cljs (linked from line 5 of the chapter):
- Uses `(rf/dispatch-sync [:counter/initialise])` in `run` rather than `reg-frame :rf/default :on-create` (the chapter mentions this at lines 192-199 in the 'Minimum-viable mount' callout, but the section above it is misleading)
- Uses `reg-event-fx` (mixing :db + :fx) so the perf example exercises the rf:fx:* perf bucket; the chapter says `reg-event-db`
- Imports `reg-view` from `(:require-macros [re-frame.views-macros :refer [reg-view]])` rather than from re-frame.core (related to bead rf2-7whh — `reg-view` IS re-exported via core's :require-macros, so `rf/reg-view` should work; just confirm the example uses the canonical import path)
- Uses two views (`counter-buttons` and `counter-app`); chapter shows one

Not wrong per se — the chapter is a teaching artefact, the example is a perf-instrumented runnable. Worth noting in the chapter that the runnable file is intentionally richer for perf-bucket reasons, and that both the `:on-create` form and the `dispatch-sync` seed form are equivalent.
close_reason: Closed

title: core.cljc + views_macros.clj: deduplicate metadata-free-symbol rationale (9 verbatim copies)
description:
The following comment block appears verbatim (or with one-word delta) at 9 sites:

  ;; Construct a fresh, metadata-free symbol. (ns-name *ns*) returns the
  ;; ns-symbol but in CLJS macro context that symbol may carry the consumer
  ;; namespace's :doc metadata, which would then get serialised into the
  ;; bundle and defeat production elision.
  ns-sym (symbol (str (ns-name *ns*)))

Sites:
- core.cljc reg-event-db (line 173)
- core.cljc reg-event-fx (line 193)
- core.cljc reg-event-ctx (line 213)
- core.cljc reg-sub (line 233)
- core.cljc reg-fx (line 253)
- core.cljc reg-cofx (line 273)
- core.cljc reg-frame (line 293)
- core.cljc reg-flow (line 474)
- core.cljc reg-route (line 506)
- core.cljc reg-app-schema (line 546)
- core.cljc reg-machine (line 663)
- core.cljc reg-error-projector (line 836)
- core.cljc reg-view (line 384)
- views_macros.clj reg-view (similar)

ACTION: either deduplicate by extracting a (metadata-free-ns-name-form) macro/fn, or collapse to one top-of-section block-comment with a "see CLJS-macro-ns-meta rationale above" pointer at each call site.

Note: rf2-p7da addresses the broader reg-* defmacro shape dedup.

Discovered during clarity review 2026-05-12; see ai/findings/clarity-review-2026-05-12.md §F20.
close_reason: Closed

title: warned-non-dom-roots atoms survive across tests on three CLJS adapters
description: Three CLJS files carry defonce'd warn-once caches that reset-runtime-fixture does NOT clear: implementation/core/src/re_frame/views.cljs:272, implementation/adapters/helix/src/re_frame/adapter/helix.cljs:330, implementation/adapters/uix/src/re_frame/adapter/uix.cljs:324. Each is swap!ed inside per-adapter warn-non-dom-root! on first encounter of a registered view whose render-fn returns a non-DOM root (Fragment, fn/class component, non-element value). Contract is 'warn once per id', which is correct as a user-facing UX promise — but the cache is per-process and the fixture only resets the analogous warned-plain-fn-frame-pairs atom (via views/clear-plain-fn-warned-pairs!). A test asserting 'registering :foo with a Fragment root produces :rf.warning/...' passes in isolation but silently green-lights if a sibling test fires the same warning first.
design: Mirror the clear-plain-fn-warned-pairs! shape: (1) Add clear-warned-non-dom-roots! in each of the three files. (2) Publish each through a late-bind hook (e.g. :adapter/clear-warn-once-caches!). (3) Invoke from reset-runtime-fixture alongside the existing trace/clear-trace-cbs! step. ~3 lines per adapter + ~5 lines in test_support.cljc + ~3 lines registering hooks. Severity P3 because no runtime behaviour breaks — only test assertions over the warning surface are affected. See ai/findings/functional-purity-review-2026-05-12.md §P3-1.
notes: Found by functional-purity-review-2026-05-12.md — read-only review of implementation/.
close_reason: Fixed via https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/347 — three adapter namespaces now publish a clear-step on the chained :adapter/clear-warn-once-caches! late-bind hook; reset-runtime-fixture invokes the top of the chain so every loaded adapter's warned-non-dom-roots defonce clears between tests.

title: frame.cljc: renumber destroy-frame! steps (docstring drift since rf2-wvkn / rf2-d656)
description:
implementation/core/src/re_frame/frame.cljc lines 196-264. destroy-frame! docstring lists steps (1)-(3) but the body now does more:

1. Fire :on-destroy.
2. Signal machine teardown.
2a. Fire HTTP abort cascade hook (rf2-wvkn) — interleaved with step 2.
3. Mark frame destroyed.
4. Dispose sub-cache.
5. Emit :frame/destroyed.
6. Dissoc from frames atom.
7. Unregister frame.
8. Fire epoch frame-destroyed hook (rf2-d656).

Numbered comments in the body (;; (1) ..., ;; (2) ..., ;; (3) ...) match the docstring but not the actual count.

ACTION: either renumber the docstring step list to match the body, or break the body into named local fns (fire-on-destroy-event!, notify-machine-destruction!, tear-down-sub-cache!, notify-epoch-listeners!) so the body matches the docstring 1-to-1.

Discovered during clarity review 2026-05-12; see ai/findings/clarity-review-2026-05-12.md §F18.
close_reason: Closed

title: machines.cljc: replace ((resolve `machine-transition) ...) with direct call via existing declare
description:
implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines.cljc line 1045 (inside drain-raises):

  (let [step-result ((resolve \`machine-transition) machine snap args)]
    ...)

This goes through clojure.core/resolve at runtime against a syntax-quoted symbol. There's already (declare machine-transition machine-transition-single) at line 1286 — but it's declared AFTER drain-raises.

ACTION: move (declare machine-transition ...) above drain-raises and replace the resolve form with a direct call:

  (let [step-result (machine-transition machine snap args)]
    ...)

Inconsistent style otherwise — drain-raises is the only call site in this ns using resolve for a forward reference.

Discovered during clarity review 2026-05-12; see ai/findings/clarity-review-2026-05-12.md §F17.
notes: Verified on rf2-rr4z-drop-runtime-resolve worktree (origin/main @6e45205): the (resolve `machine-transition) anti-pattern no longer exists. drain-raises now lives at implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines/transition.cljc:933 (post-split into transition/parallel/timer/lifecycle-fx by rf2-5hnn #359), with a properly-positioned (declare machine-transition-single) at line 931 and a direct call (machine-transition-single machine snap args) at line 957. Comment above the declare even cites the same late-bind anti-pattern this bead warned about. The fix was actually already in 23842ad (Implementation: std-interceptors + machine :always microsteps) — the bead's filing was based on a stale audit snapshot. Machines test suite: 114 tests / 307 assertions pass on this worktree. Closing as already-resolved; no PR needed.
close_reason: Already resolved in main (commit 23842ad / preserved through rf2-5hnn split #359). drain-raises at implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines/transition.cljc:933-970 calls machine-transition-single directly via a (declare ...) at line 931 — no runtime resolve. Machines tests pass (114/307). No PR needed.

title: router.cljc: extract named helpers from process-event*
description:
implementation/core/src/re_frame/router.cljc lines 105-237 (~130 LoC). process-event* interleaves:
- handler resolution
- :phase :run-start trace
- step-1 event validation (rf2-jwm4)
- overrides resolution
- chain assembly
- initial-ctx construction
- performance bracket
- error capture
- :db effect commit
- post-commit schema validation
- run-flows!
- fx walk
- :phase :run-end trace
