title: http-managed supersede! fires :on-failure reply on the prior request
description:
## Problem

`implementation/http/src/re_frame/http_managed.cljc:155-167` — `supersede!`:

```clojure
(defn- supersede! [request-id]
  (when-let [prev (lookup-in-flight request-id)]
    (clear-in-flight! request-id)
    (try
      ((:abort-fn prev) :request-id-superseded)
      (catch ... _ nil))))
```

The `:abort-fn` was constructed by `run-attempt-cljs!` / `run-attempt-jvm!` capturing the original ctx. On invocation it calls `finalise-failure!`, which fires `dispatch-reply!` against the captured `:origin-event` / `:explicit-on-failure` — i.e. the PRIOR request's reply target.

If the prior request's `:on-failure` was a real event vector, that event fires with `{:kind :failure :failure {:kind :rf.http/aborted :reason :request-id-superseded ...}}` even though the user's intent was "the new request replaces the old one." Two replies arrive at the original target: first the abort, then the success/failure of the new request. Invites race bugs in debounce-search patterns.

## Spec status

Per Spec 014 §`:request-id` (internal): "a fresh request with the same `:request-id` supersedes the previous one." The contract is silent on whether the prior request fires its reply.

## Fix — Mike-decision flagged

Two options:

1. **Suppress the reply on superseded aborts.** In `finalise-failure!` (cljs + jvm variants), skip `dispatch-reply!` when failure kind is `:rf.http/aborted` AND `:reason = :request-id-superseded`. Matches the debounce-search mental model.

2. **Document the dual-reply behaviour.** Update Spec 014 §`:request-id` to call out that supersede fires a failure reply on the prior request; users handle the abort-replay explicitly.

Option 1 is more user-friendly; option 2 is more conservative. Mike's call.

## Scope

Internal to `re-frame.http-managed`. Spec touch: Spec 014 §`:request-id` (internal). Test coverage: at least one supersede-flow test should assert the chosen behavior.

Found by 2026-05-12 correctness review: ai/findings/correctness-review-2026-05-12.md §P3-3.
notes: Decision 2026-05-13: A = suppress reply on superseded aborts. Pre-alpha green-light. Semantic alignment with 'supersede' = 'replaces'; trace bus retains telemetry path via register-trace-cb!; matches debounce-search mental model.

title: subs layer-2+ inputs leak ref-count on Reagent auto-dispose
description:
## Problem

`implementation/core/src/re_frame/subs.cljc:166-284` — `compute-and-cache!` for a layer-2+ sub recursively calls `subscribe` on each `:<-` input (line 199-201). Each `subscribe` increments the input sub's ref-count via `compute-and-cache!`-on-miss (`:ref-count 1`) or via the hit-path inc.

When the parent reaction is later disposed by Reagent (because its sole watcher — a view — unmounted), the parent's `add-on-dispose!` callback (line 278-283) dissocs the parent's cache slot. But it does NOT call `unsubscribe` on the input subs whose ref-counts it incremented during construction.

## Impact (mostly observability, mild correctness)

- Inspector reading `(rf/sub-cache)` between hot reloads sees lingering high `:ref-count` integers that don't reflect actual subscribers.
- Tests that subscribe imperatively (incrementing ref-count) and then have the Reagent reaction disposed externally (`(interop/dispose! r)`) find the cache slot disappeared without the integer decrementing.
- Hypothetical leak: if the parent reaction were ever reconstructed (hot-reload, `clear-subscription-cache!` followed by new subscribe) AND the input subs' cache slots were still present with elevated ref-counts, those slots would never be disposed even after all subscribers detached. `invalidate-sub-on-replace!` (line 497-522) doesn't reset ref-counts on subs that weren't themselves re-registered.

## Fix — Mike-decision flagged

Two options:

1. **Tighten the contract.** Document that `:ref-count` is dev-introspection-only when Reagent autodispose is in play. Production apps shouldn't read it. Touch the Spec 006 doc and the `subs.cljc` docstring.

2. **Wire `compute-and-cache!`'s `add-on-dispose!` to walk `:inputs` and decrement each input's ref-count before clearing the parent slot.** Mechanically simple; brings the integer in line with the disposal model.

Option 2 is more work but closer to "what the integer says, the reality reflects." Mike's call.

## Scope

Internal to `re-frame.subs`. Likely a behavioral change for any test reading `:ref-count` directly.

Found by 2026-05-12 correctness review: ai/findings/correctness-review-2026-05-12.md §P3-2.
notes: Decision 2026-05-13: B = decrement on dispose. Pre-alpha green-light: 'what the integer says, reality reflects' invariant restored; Causa sub-cache panel + machine-inspector will read accurate counts; mechanical fix (one callback edit). Test behaviour changes for direct :ref-count reads are correct churn.
close_reason: PRs merged in batch landing wave 2026-05-13

title: interceptor chain :rf/interceptor-error overwrites prior errors
description:
## Problem

`implementation/core/src/re_frame/interceptor.cljc:46-62` — `invoke-before` and `invoke-after` both write `:rf/interceptor-error` to the context unconditionally on catch:

```clojure
(catch ... e
  (assoc context :rf/interceptor-error
         {:phase :before :id (:id interceptor) :exception e}))
```

If interceptor #2's `:before` fails and stamps an error, then interceptor #1's `:after` also fails, the second `assoc` overwrites the first. The router's downstream `:rf.error/handler-exception` trace surfaces only the LATER error; the original cause is lost.

Additionally, when a `:before` interceptor fails BEFORE a schema-validation interceptor (which sets `:rf/skip-handler?`), the handler-as-interceptor's skip check doesn't fire — the handler still runs against partial context, only to be reported as a generic interceptor-error later.

## Fix

1. Skip downstream `:before` stages once `:rf/interceptor-error` is set (mirror the existing `:rf/skip-handler?` short-circuit pattern in events.cljc / cofx.cljc). Continue running `:after` for proper teardown — that's the documented chain-continues guarantee.
2. Append errors to a `:rf/interceptor-errors` vector rather than overwrite. Keep `:rf/interceptor-error` for the FIRST error so existing tracing code reads "what went wrong first" without surprise.

## Scope

Internal to `re-frame.interceptor`. Test impact: any existing tests that assert on `:rf/interceptor-error` shape need to be updated to read the singleton (first-error) or the vector (all-errors) variant.

Found by 2026-05-12 correctness review: ai/findings/correctness-review-2026-05-12.md §P3-1.
close_reason: Closed

title: core.cljc: extract optional-artefact wrappers to sub-namespace
description:
implementation/core/src/re_frame/core.cljc is 1506 LoC. Roughly lines 405-1390 are late-bind wrappers for 7 optional artefacts (flows, routing, schemas, machines, epoch, ssr, http). Each wrapper follows:

  (defn X [...]
    (if-let [f (late-bind/get-fn :Y/X)]
      (f ...)
      (throw (ex-info :rf.error/Y-artefact-missing ...))))

The actual public API (dispatch, subscribe, current-frame, dispatcher, with-frame, get-frame-db, snapshot-of, ->interceptor, register-trace-cb! etc.) is a small fraction. Readers searching for the canonical surface wade through ~600 lines of optional-artefact wrapping.

ALSO: the (:require ...) form at lines 19-119 carries ~100 lines of block comments per-artefact explaining why each is NOT statically required. The reason composes (Mike's feedback_downstream_eps_consume_foundation.md principle: 'no new registries... no new effect substrates'); one top-of-section block-comment + 1-line per-entry pointer would suffice.

ACTION: extract optional-artefact wrappers into re-frame.core.optional-artefacts (or per-artefact: re-frame.core.flows, re-frame.core.routing, re-frame.core.machines, re-frame.core.schemas, re-frame.core.epoch, re-frame.core.ssr, re-frame.core.http) and re-export from re-frame.core.

OPEN QUESTION: do we preserve the v1 single-import contract (apps require only [re-frame.core :as rf])? If yes, the new sub-ns are internal and re-export through `core`. If no, per-artefact import is the migration story.

Discovered during clarity review 2026-05-12; see ai/findings/clarity-review-2026-05-12.md §F10, §F21, §Q5.

title: core.cljc: deduplicate the six reg-* defmacros via shared helper
description:
implementation/core/src/re_frame/core.cljc lines 166-304: reg-event-db, reg-event-fx, reg-event-ctx, reg-sub, reg-fx, reg-cofx, reg-frame are 7 near-identical defmacros. Each ~20 lines binding *pending-coords* around a delegate call. Same shape:

  (let [m       (meta &form)
        ns-sym  (symbol (str (ns-name *ns*)))
        file    *file*]
    `(binding [source-coords/*pending-coords*
               (cond-> {:ns '~ns-sym}
                 ~file        (assoc :file ~file)
                 ~(:line m)   (assoc :line ~(:line m))
                 ~(:column m) (assoc :column ~(:column m)))]
       (TARGET-FN ~id ~@args)))

With the same 'metadata-free symbol' rationale comment x6.

ACTION: helper macro defmacro-with-coords that generates the binding shape from (target-fn id & args). The seven reg-* macros become one-liners.

OR: keep the defmacros but factor a build-pending-coords-form fn that returns the cond-> shape, and inline that.

Sub-concerns identified:
  1. LCA + exit/entry refs (lines 575-595)
  2. Run action cascade + handle action-failure sentinel (lines 596-612)
  3. Compute new state + epoch bump (lines 622-655)
  4. Build :after-cancel-fx / destroy-fx / spawn-fx / after-fx vectors (lines 656-989)

Step 4 alone is ~330 lines and includes :invoke spawn-args build with :on-spawn callback wiring, plus :invoke-all join-state seed.

description:
implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines.cljc is 3152 LoC, 60+ fns. Many independent surfaces interleaved:

- pure transition engine (chase-ref, resolve-guard, resolve-action, state-path, pick-transition, apply-transition-once, drain-raises, machine-transition[-single]) — ~1000 LoC
- parallel-region engine (parallel?, region-machine, parallel-machine-transition, commit-tags-parallel) — ~300 LoC
- :after timer machinery (after-timer-key, classify-delay-source, resolve-delay-ms, schedule-after-timer!, on-sub-changed!, after-schedule-fx, after-cancel-fx) — ~400 LoC
- lifecycle fxs (spawn-fx, destroy-machine-fx, invoke-all-init-fx, abort cascade) — ~600 LoC
- registration boundary (create-machine-handler, reg-machine*, machines, machine-meta, framework subs, late-bind installs) — ~350 LoC

title: Realworld: collapse :auth.session/store + :auth.session/clear into one fx
description:
The realworld auth.cljs registers two fxs for the localStorage token
seam (:auth.session/store + :auth.session/clear) plus one cofx for the
read side (:auth.session/token). A more idiomatic shape is one fx
(:auth.session/persist) whose arg is {:token <token-or-nil>} — write
on truthy, remove on nil.

File: examples/reagent/realworld/auth.cljs:29-48

Fix: merge store + clear into one fx with arg-shape dispatch; the
auth machine's :store-session / :clear-session actions then call the
same fx with different args. Halves the registrar entries and gives
a single localStorage seam to mock.

See ai/findings/best-practice-review-2026-05-12.md §F-MP-1.
close_reason: Fixed in https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/345

title: adapters: extract route-hook! helper to deduplicate rf2-0d35 routing pattern across 4 adapters
description:
Each CLJS adapter (reagent, reagent-slim, uix, helix) repeats the same 7-9 routed late-bind-hook installations at its bottom-of-file. Per-adapter ~150 lines; ~600 lines total of structural copy with near-identical comment blocks.

The pattern (rf2-0d35 routing — installed adapter's impl runs only when it is the (rf/init!)-installed one; otherwise chains to previously-registered reader):

  (let [previous (late-bind/get-fn :adapter/X)]
    (late-bind/set-fn! :adapter/X
      (fn adapter-X-routed [args]
        (if (identical? adapter (substrate-adapter/current-adapter))
          (impl args)
          (if previous (previous args) fallback)))))

Repeated for :adapter/current-frame, :adapter/current-component (where applicable), :adapter/ratom, :adapter/ratom?, :adapter/make-reaction, :adapter/add-on-dispose!, :adapter/dispose!, :adapter/reactive?, :adapter/after-render.

ACTION: extract a route-hook! helper into re-frame.substrate.adapter (preferred — alongside current-adapter) or re-frame.late-bind. Signature:

  (route-hook! adapter-spec :adapter/X impl-fn fallback-result)

Adapter ns bottom-of-file shrinks from ~150 to ~30 lines.

OPEN QUESTION: helper placement — substrate.adapter, late-bind, or new substrate.adapter.routing?

Discovered during clarity review 2026-05-12; see ai/findings/clarity-review-2026-05-12.md §F3 and §Q4.
close_reason: Closed

title: Realworld: replace repeated (current-time-ms) calls in handlers with a :rf/now coeffect
description:
Six realworld namespaces each define a per-ns current-time-ms that
calls (.getTime (js/Date.)) and invoke it directly inside
reg-event-fx handlers (e.g. :articles/loaded, :comments/loaded,
:profile/loaded). Reading wall-clock time inside the handler makes
the handler impure — output depends on execution-time and breaks
reproducible tests / SSR hydration.

Files:
- examples/reagent/realworld/articles.cljs:31
- examples/reagent/realworld/comments.cljs:16
- examples/reagent/realworld/favorites.cljs:12
- examples/reagent/realworld/profile.cljs:40
- examples/reagent/realworld/settings.cljs:50
- examples/reagent/realworld/tags.cljs:39

Fix: register one :rf/now (or :realworld/now) cofx in realworld.http
or realworld.time; have every handler that writes :loaded-at carry
:inject-cofx and read now from coeffects. Realworld is the canonical
SSR demo so the pure-handler discipline matters here in particular.

See ai/findings/best-practice-review-2026-05-12.md §F-IC-1.
close_reason: Fixed in https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/345

title: Chapter 08 (from re-frame v1) assumes deep v1 familiarity; add upshot box for skimmers

title: Circle Drawer: layer subs off a :drawer Layer-2 sub instead of raw db reads
description:
Every sub in circle_drawer reads directly from db ([:drawer :circles],
[:drawer :dialog], [:drawer :undo], [:drawer :redo]). Idiomatic re-
frame splits into a Layer-2 :drawer slice sub and Layer-3 readers
chained via :<-, matching the realworld pattern (:articles →
:articles/data → ...). The current style re-traverses [:drawer ...]
on every recompute.

File: examples/reagent/7Guis/circle_drawer/circle_drawer.cljs:158-163

Fix:
  (rf/reg-sub :drawer (fn [db _] (:drawer db)))
  (rf/reg-sub :drawer/circles  :<- [:drawer] (fn [s _] (:circles s)))
  ;; and similarly for :dialog, :can-undo?, :can-redo?

See ai/findings/best-practice-review-2026-05-12.md §F-SL-1.

Currently every schema gets its own (rf/reg-app-schema [path] schema) line. title: Counter example :counter/initialise mixes :db + :fx; document the perf-bucket exception
description:
The smallest counter example uses reg-event-fx for an :initialise
handler that only writes :db {:count 5} plus a :counter/log fx call.
The doc comment notes the :fx exists to exercise every perf bucket on
a single dispatch, but a reader skimming the smallest counter takes
this as the canonical initialiser shape and propagates :db + :fx mixes
to their own code.

File: examples/reagent/counter/core.cljs:43-46

Fix options:
- Keep reg-event-fx but add a clearer header comment naming this as a
  deliberate widening for the perf-instrumented build, not the
  canonical shape.
- Split into two events: a plain reg-event-db :counter/initialise and
  a separate :counter/log-init that runs in the perf-on variant only.

See ai/findings/best-practice-review-2026-05-12.md §F-FV-1.

title: Chapter 02 mentions (keyword *ns* "counter") without explaining *ns* for Clojure novices
description: docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md line 152 mentions 'auto-derived id (keyword *ns* "counter")' as part of the reg-view explanation. A reader new to Clojure won't know what *ns* is — it's a dynamically-bound var in Clojure pointing at the current namespace.\n\nFix: either one-sentence definition ('*ns* is Clojure's reference to the current namespace; the auto-derived id is a namespaced keyword pointing at this counter') or omit the parenthetical and just say 'a namespaced keyword auto-derived from the registration site.'
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F18.
close_reason: Closed

title: Chapter 12 introduces ':rf/route' slice schema before any routing example
description: docs/guide/12-routing.md introduces the :rf/route slice schema (lines 70-90, with a 12-key map) before the reader has seen a routing example in motion. A reader scanning the chapter hits the slice shape with no context to attach it to.\n\nFix: open the chapter with the smallest possible routing example (a reg-route, a navigate, a sub) THEN introduce the slice shape as 'here's what's in app-db after that navigation.' Same teaching move chapter 02 uses for app-db.

title: Add rf.http/get / post / put / delete helpers that build :rf.http/managed args maps
description: The :rf.http/managed args map carries 12+ keys. Even the most common case (GET this URL, dispatch this event with the decoded body) requires several keys. Apps that issue many similar requests carry boilerplate at every call site. Ship rf.http/get / post / put / delete as small fns that take url + partial-args and synthesise the canonical [:rf.http/managed args-map] entry. Result: {:fx [(rf.http/get "/api/items" {:on-success [:items/loaded]})]}. Reduces every call site by 4-6 lines. ADDITIVE. Ships in day8/re-frame2-http. Findings: ai/findings/user-ergonomics-review-2026-05-12.md §3.4.
close_reason: PRs merged 2026-05-13 batch landing

title: Chapter 07 server-fxs introduced in narrative prose; convert to table
description: docs/guide/07-server-side.md §'The server response is more than HTML' (lines 122-145) introduces five new fx ids in close succession: :rf.server/set-status, :rf.server/set-header, :rf.server/append-header, :rf.server/set-cookie, :rf.server/redirect, plus a multiple-set-status conflicts trace event. This is reference-shaped material delivered in narrative prose.\n\nFix: convert to a table with columns 'fx-id | semantics | platforms | notes'. The prose intro can stay; the enumeration belongs in tabular form.
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F16.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add rf/with-overrides macro for lexical-scope fx-override binding
description: Test code that runs N dispatches with the same :fx-overrides map repeats the override map at every call site. (rf/with-overrides {:http :http/stub} (rf/dispatch-sync ev1) (rf/dispatch-sync ev2) ...) would bind a dynamic var consulted by router/build-envelope to merge in for the lexical scope. Composable with with-frame. Test-support is the canonical caller. The macro itself can live in re-frame.core (no new namespace). ADDITIVE — no break. Findings: ai/findings/user-ergonomics-review-2026-05-12.md §3.3.
notes: Decision 2026-05-13: SHIP. Pre-alpha green-light. Small macro (~10 lines), composes with with-frame, addresses catalogued repetition pain in tests (ergonomics review §3.3). **Per Mike 2026-05-13: update /examples to demonstrate (test files showing with-overrides in place of inline :fx-overrides maps).** Hold dispatch until rf2-1pqc (in-flight, same core.cljc + API.md surface) lands.
close_reason: merged in 2026-05-13 wave

title: Replace js/setTimeout in demo-stub fxs with framework :dispatch-later path
description:
Four example demo-stub fxs use raw js/setTimeout to delay a canned
reply so the :loading UI state is observable:
- examples/reagent/realworld/core.cljs:255-258
- examples/reagent/boot/core.cljs:93-96
- examples/reagent/login/core.cljs:136-153
- examples/helix/login_helix/core.cljs:65-82 (and uix/login_uix
  /core.cljs:62- mirror)

The deferred work is an fx invocation (:rf.http/managed-canned-success),
not a dispatch, so :dispatch-later is not a 1:1 swap. But the timer
bypasses framework time controls (Tool-Pair time-travel, the
documented :dispatch-later nil-override seam used by timer.cljs tests)
and the pattern is replicated four times verbatim.

Fix options:
(a) Coin a small helper that internally :dispatch-later's to a private
    ::deliver-canned event whose handler calls the framework canned-
    success fx; reuse across the four examples.
(b) Accept current shape as a documented exception and add a comment
    naming the trade-off so readers don't copy js/setTimeout into
    production app code.

See ai/findings/best-practice-review-2026-05-12.md §F-ST-1..4.
close_reason: Fixed in https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/345

title: Chapter 14 (Stories) doesn't strongly signal its optionality at the top
description: docs/guide/14-stories.md is listed under 'optional deep dives' in the README, but the chapter itself doesn't signal optionality strongly. The chapter opens with 'You've seen the runtime in chapter 03, the views in chapter 04, the test machinery in chapter 10, and the tooling pitch in chapter 11' — but doesn't say 'if you haven't read those, come back later.'\n\nFix: add a one-paragraph 'this chapter assumes you've absorbed chapters 03, 04, 10, 11; come back later if not' box at the top of the chapter.

title: Document or split :rf.error/no-such-handler — it covers two distinct failure modes
description: :rf.error/no-such-handler is emitted for (a) event dispatched with no registered handler, AND (b) frame name lookup against a destroyed/missing frame (under epoch restore-epoch / reset-frame-db!). The trace event's :kind tag (e.g. :event vs :frame) disambiguates, but spec/API.md row 398 documents only case (a). A user grepping logs sees the same operation keyword for two distinct failure modes. Action: either (1) document the :kind discriminator in API.md's error table, or (2) split the operation: keep :rf.error/no-such-handler for events; use :rf.error/frame-destroyed (already exists) for the frame-lookup case. Prefer (2) — cleaner taxonomy, but ssr.cljc:778-784 already routes off :rf.error/no-such-handler for 404 status. Findings: ai/findings/user-ergonomics-review-2026-05-12.md §2.7.
close_reason: Closed

title: Chapter 11 reference-half transition is abrupt; add a one-sentence bridge
description: docs/guide/11-devtools-and-pair-tools.md has a divider at line 213 ('---' followed by '## Reference') but the narrative half ends abruptly at line 211. A reader transitioning between halves should be told who the reference half is for.\n\nFix: insert one sentence between the halves: 'The narrative is above; the reference below is for tool authors and edge-case investigators — read top-down for the story, jump to the bottom when you need exact shapes.'

title: Chapter 06 'schema reflection' subsection is tooling metadata; demote to reference

A first-pass reader learning HTTP-requests doesn't need this — it's opt-in tooling-shaped metadata.\n\nFix: move to a 'Reference and tooling' section at the chapter end, or to chapter 11 (tooling) where reflection-shaped surface lives naturally.

title: Register reg-app-schema for [:boot/staging] slot in boot example
description:
The boot example's staging slot ([:boot/staging <key>]) is the hand-off
between :invoke-all children and the :enter-hydrating action. Its shape
is the canonical Pattern-Boot staging-slot pattern; readers copy the
schema block as a template.

Realworld is the canonical Spec 010 schema demo. Every other slice has

title: Pin return-value contract for every reg-*: returns primary id
description: Some reg-* fns return their id consistently (reg-event-db/-fx/-ctx, reg-fx, reg-sub, reg-view*, reg-route, reg-flow). Others return whatever the late-bind impl returns: reg-machine / reg-machine* / reg-app-schema / reg-error-projector. API.md does not document the return value of any reg-*. Action: (1) document in spec/API.md that every reg-* returns its primary id; (2) audit reg-machine / reg-machine* / reg-app-schema to actually return their id consistently (machine-id for machines; path for reg-app-schema). ADDITIVE — no break, just adds a documented guarantee. Findings: ai/findings/user-ergonomics-review-2026-05-12.md §2.5.
notes: ERRATUM 2026-05-13: prior 'SHIP' note was misapplied. This bead is about reg-* return-value contract (already closed). The plural-schemas SHIP decision belongs to rf2-jzs9. Ignore the SHIP note here.

title: Chapter 04 Form-1/Form-2 distinction is a digression for readers new to Reagent
description: docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md §'dispatch and subscribe are auto-injected' (lines 71-86) introduces the Form-1 vs Form-2 distinction. A reader new to Reagent doesn't yet know what Reagent forms are; the digression interrupts the chapter's flow on reg-view.\n\nFix: either footnote the Form-1/Form-2 explanation or label it as 'advanced — skip on first read.' The macro's compile-time error message at line 86 is informative enough that readers who need this material will discover it from the error.

title: Chapter 10: add 'when to use which' comparison between with-fresh-registrar and reset-runtime-fixture
description: docs/guide/10-testing.md §'Registrar isolation: with-fresh-registrar' (lines 83-95) introduces both with-fresh-registrar AND reset-runtime-fixture close together. A reader new to test fixtures will struggle to remember which is which.\n\nFix: add an explicit comparison line at the end of the section — 'Use with-fresh-registrar when only the registrar might change; use reset-runtime-fixture when tests also touch trace listeners, schemas, or other process-wide state.' (The chapter already has the data; it just needs the explicit choosing rule.)

title: Move client-side validation message off :submit-error to :errors/:_form in comment-form
description:
Pattern-Forms §:submit-error vs :errors keeps the two slots distinct:
:errors holds renderable validation errors (both per-field and the
reserved :_form key), :submit-error is reserved for transport/non-
field failure. The comment-form's blank-body branch currently writes
the validation message into :submit-error.

File: examples/reagent/realworld/comments.cljs:169-172

Fix: write the validation message into [:comment-form :errors :_form]
(or [:comment-form :errors :body]); leave :submit-error for the HTTP
failure path.

See ai/findings/best-practice-review-2026-05-12.md §F-PF-3.

title: Move chapter 01 'What re-frame2 adds / keeps' enumerations to chapter 13 as a recap
description: docs/guide/01-why-re-frame2.md ends with 'What re-frame2 adds to the original re-frame' and 'What re-frame2 keeps from the original re-frame' enumerations (lines 104-120). These preview features the reader hasn't yet seen — flows, schema-attached contracts, deep instrumentation, etc. — and read as marketing rather than orientation. A net-new reader can't tell whether any of them matters to them.\n\nFix: move both lists to chapter 13 (where-next), reframed as 'what you've now seen' recap. Chapter 01 should set up the argument; chapter 13 should consolidate the surface.
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F10.
close_reason: Closed

title: Decision: per-adapter start! convenience fns (e.g. reagent-adapter/start!) wrapping rf/init!
description: Per rf2-agql there is no default-adapter registry — every app must (rf/init! reagent/adapter). That's structurally correct for bundle isolation but produces an identical boot dance in every Reagent app. Proposed: each adapter ns ships a zero-arg start! fn that does the equivalent of (rf/init! reagent.adapter/adapter). The user requires the adapter ns (which is already required for the Var) and calls (r/start!). Same explicit-on-classpath invariant; just one less symbol to type. ADDITIVE convenience. Mike may prefer the explicit boot to read identically across adapters; this changes that. Findings: ai/findings/user-ergonomics-review-2026-05-12.md §3.1. Mike-blocked: API surface decision.
notes: Mike-blocked. From user-ergonomics review (ai/findings/user-ergonomics-review-2026-05-12.md §3.1).
close_reason: won't-ship per rf2-hyh5w pre-alpha audit decision 2026-05-13: explicit (rf/init! adapter/adapter) reads uniformly across adapters; convenience saves 2 chars at cost of three different entry-point shapes

title: Add :submit-attempted? to realworld :comment-form and :editor slices
description:
Same shape as rf2-ctn2 (F-PF-1) but for the comment-form and article-
editor slices. Pattern-Forms expects :submit-attempted? as the load-
bearing flag for full-form-error reveal; the editor currently works
around the omission by re-touching every field on validation failure.

Files:
- examples/reagent/realworld/comments.cljs:18-24 (comment-form-defaults)
- examples/reagent/realworld/article_editor.cljs:46-58 (editor-slice)

Fix: same as rf2-ctn2 — add :submit-attempted? false; flip on submit;
remove the editor's re-touch workaround once it's in place.

See ai/findings/best-practice-review-2026-05-12.md §F-PF-2.
close_reason: Fixed in https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/345

title: Chapter 01 introduces undefined vocabulary cloud — add 'we will meet each of these in 02-04' footnote
description: docs/guide/01-why-re-frame2.md introduces multiple terms a net-new reader hasn't yet seen: app-db, :fx, *frame*, *machine*, *Reagent*, *Malli*, *hiccup*, *re-frame-10x*. The terms are unavoidable in an argument chapter, but a reader has to absorb the vocabulary cloud while also processing the argument.\n\nFix: add a one-sentence footnote on first use of each term — 'we'll meet [term] in chapter 0X' — so the reader knows the gap is intentional and they're not expected to know the term yet.
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F9.
close_reason: Closed

title: Promote chapter 12 'Navigation tokens' into the basics half
description: docs/guide/12-routing.md splits into basics (top half) and reference (bottom half). 'Navigation tokens — stale-result suppression' (line 275) is currently in the reference half — but a reader writing a route's :on-match continuation that fetches data will hit stale-suppression as a real bug. The concept is more pedagogically critical than its placement suggests.\n\nFix: promote nav-tokens to the basics half, or strengthen the pointer from :on-match (currently a single sentence in §'Per-route data loading') to a more prominent forward-reference.
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F8.
close_reason: Closed

title: Decision: ship rf/cofx-from-sub helper to reduce subscribe-value-in-handler boilerplate
description: App authors writing event-fx handlers reach for rf/subscribe-value when they need a sub's current value. The recommended pattern is to register a cofx that wraps the sub; users skip the cofx step because it's per-feature boilerplate (5 lines to wire one sub into a cofx). A one-liner helper (rf/cofx-from-sub ::name [:sub-id args]) would make the canonical pattern obvious. ADDITIVE but pattern-policy: Mike may prefer authors NOT subscribe inside handlers at all (subs are for views; cofx are for handler input). Either ship the helper to legitimise the pattern, or document the cofx-wrapping pattern more visibly and refuse the shortcut. Findings: ai/findings/user-ergonomics-review-2026-05-12.md §3.2. Mike-blocked: pattern decision.
notes: Mike-blocked. From user-ergonomics review (ai/findings/user-ergonomics-review-2026-05-12.md §3.2).
close_reason: won't-ship per rf2-hyh5w pre-alpha audit decision 2026-05-13: legitimises subscribing-inside-handlers anti-pattern; cofx wrapping is canonical. Ergonomics handled by documenting the wrapping pattern (new bead filed)

title: Add :submit-attempted? to realworld auth :login-form and :register-form slices
description:
Per Pattern-Forms §Error visibility, the per-field-error sub MUST reveal
every error after the first submit click independent of :touched. The
two realworld auth form slices follow the Pattern-Forms shape but omit
:submit-attempted?, so a user pressing Sign-in on an empty form sees no
per-field errors.

Files:
- examples/reagent/realworld/auth.cljs:187 (:auth.login-form/initialise)
- examples/reagent/realworld/auth.cljs:210 (:auth.register-form/initialise)

Fix:
- Add :submit-attempted? false to both initial slices
- Flip to true in :auth.login-form/submit / :auth.register-form/submit
  before branching on errors
- Teach per-field-error sub to OR on :submit-attempted?

See ai/findings/best-practice-review-2026-05-12.md §F-PF-1.
close_reason: Fixed in https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/345

title: Trim chapter 03 'Patterns: the next layer up' — eight Pattern docs is too many at chapter end
description: docs/guide/03-events-state-cycle.md §'Patterns: the next layer up' (lines 196-211) enumerates eight Pattern docs (AsyncEffect, RemoteData, Forms, Boot, WebSocket, LongRunningWork, StaleDetection, NineStates) at the bottom of chapter 03. The reader has just absorbed effects-as-data; absorbing eight more conventions on top adds cognitive load with no immediate payoff.\n\nFix: trim to the two that bottom out on chapter 03 directly (Pattern-AsyncEffect, Pattern-RemoteData). Move the rest to chapter 13 (where-next) as 'look these up when you hit the matching shape.'
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F7.
close_reason: Closed

title: Decision: rename register-epoch-cb / remove-epoch-cb to add ! suffix
description: Public re-exports rf/register-epoch-cb and rf/remove-epoch-cb lack the bang that rf/register-trace-cb! / rf/remove-trace-cb! carry. The internal re-frame.epoch ns already uses the bang form (register-epoch-cb! / remove-epoch-cb!); only the public re-export in re-frame.core drops it. Per docstring in core.cljc:1202 the listener API 'mirrors register-trace-cb!' but the public spelling does not. Cheatsheet papers over by writing neither with bang; running fn names diverge. BREAKING rename: rf/register-epoch-cb -> rf/register-epoch-cb!; rf/remove-epoch-cb -> rf/remove-epoch-cb!. Surface is dev-only so blast radius is small. spec/API.md rows 359-360 require parallel update. Findings: ai/findings/user-ergonomics-review-2026-05-12.md §2.1. Mike-blocked: any public-API change is his call.
notes: Mike-blocked. From user-ergonomics review (ai/findings/user-ergonomics-review-2026-05-12.md).
close_reason: merged in PR #495 (API.md batched cleanup) and PR #492 (bang rename); both landed in 2026-05-13 batch landing wave

title: Move chapter 02 'Same pattern on other adapters' + 'init! and adapter wiring' to chapter 13 or Adapters doc
description:
docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md ends with two sections — 'Same pattern on other adapters' (lines 279-326) and 'init! and how the adapter gets wired' (lines 201-216) — that disrupt the chapter's tight 'counter end-to-end' scope.

A reader meeting their first app doesn't yet have context for comparing Reagent / UIx / Helix, and the init! deep-dive is a digression from the counter narrative.

Fix: move both sections to chapter 13 (where-next), or to a separate Adapters reference doc. Keep chapter 02 ruthlessly focused on the counter.
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F6.
close_reason: Closed

title: Chapter 11: swap tools-enumeration with architectural-punchline section — pitch first, tools as evidence
description: docs/guide/11-devtools-and-pair-tools.md leads with the tools enumeration (re-frame-pair2, re-frame2-story, re-frame-10x v2, machine visualisers) and only then delivers the chapter's load-bearing pitch — 'one observation surface, every tool consumes it' — in section 'The architectural punchline.' A scanning reader hits the tools first and misses the pitch.\n\nFix: swap order. Lead with the punchline (the framework commits to a single observation surface; multiple tools coexist on it); use the tools enumeration as evidence that the surface works. This way the load-bearing claim arrives first, with concrete tools as substantiation.
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F5.
close_reason: Closed

title: Move Flows and Pattern-NineStates out of chapter 04 (views and frames)
description:
Chapter 04 (Views and frames) currently includes two top-level sections that are off-topic for the chapter's scope:

1. 'Computed values as state — the flow escape hatch' (lines 210-224). Flows are explicitly described as a 'niche convenience' but given top-level section weight. A reader meeting frames for the first time doesn't need flows yet.
2. 'Making the common UI states explicit — Pattern-NineStates' (lines 230-250). NineStates is a Pattern with its own normative doc and example; it's introduced with table + state list + render-priority rules + nine canonical render keywords mid-chapter.

Recommended: Move Flows to an addendum or fold into chapter 03's app-db note. Move NineStates to chapter 05 (after parallel regions are introduced) or chapter 13 (where-next). Replace with 2-line pointers in chapter 04.
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F4.
close_reason: Closed

title: Re-order chapters 05a (Forms) and 05 (State machines) — 05a first
description: Chapter 05a (Forms) is currently sequenced *after* chapter 05 (State machines), with README framing as 'skippable on first read.' But pedagogically, 05a is the more accessible introduction to the slice + events + subs pattern that 05 then builds on — and chapter 05's login-flow example literally sits on top of the form slice 05a introduces.\n\nRecommended: re-order to 05a → 05. Update the README's framing — 05a is more pedagogically load-bearing than 05's first half, not 'skippable side-track.'
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F3.

title: Chapter 05 (state machines) is 877 lines covering six topics; split into 05 (flat) + 05b (advanced)
description:
docs/guide/05-state-machines.md is 877 lines — almost twice the length of the next-longest core-path chapter — and tries to teach six things at once: flat machines, the xstate vocabulary lift, parallel regions, child-machine spawn/destroy, the :system-id slot, and three full Pattern walkthroughs (WebSocket, Boot, LongRunningWork with a 60-line worked example).

A first-pass reader meeting state machines for the first time hits cognitive overload before the Patterns section even starts.

Recommended split: 05 covers flat machines + xstate lift + 'when to reach for a machine' (load-bearing for the mental model); 05b covers parallel regions, child machines, :system-id, and points at the Pattern docs for WebSocket/Boot/LongRunningWork rather than reproducing them. Most apps don't need 05b on day one.
notes: Filed from tutorial-clarity-review-2026-05-12.md F2.

title: examples/counter_with_stories: prevent card reflow when 'hold to repeat' hint appears on -button hover
description:
In examples/reagent/counter_with_stories/views.cljs (counter-buttons Form-2 view, around line 43-60), hovering the '-' button triggers a Reagent local-atom toggle that conditionally renders a 'hold to repeat (not implemented — UI hint only)' span. The span's appearance widens the variant card on hover.

The behaviour itself is intentional (demonstrates Form-2 + the app-db / local-state boundary). The reflow is a cosmetic side-effect: when the span mounts, the inline-flex container grows; when it unmounts, it shrinks. Cards in :Workspace.counter/auto-grid all reflow.

Fix direction (pick whichever is smallest):
1. Reserve space for the hint via a non-rendered placeholder span at the same length (visibility: hidden when @hint? is false; visible when true). Keeps the card width stable.
2. Render the hint with position: absolute + a fixed-height parent so it overlays rather than displaces.
3. Use opacity transition (hint always rendered, opacity 0 vs 1) — simplest CSS-only fix; takes width permanently.

Recommend option 1 (placeholder reserve) — minimum visual impact, no new CSS animations.

Scope: examples/reagent/counter_with_stories/views.cljs only. Don't touch tools/story or anything outside the example.

Verify: hover the - button in a variant card. Card width should not change. Hint should still appear on hover, disappear on leave.

Discovered by Mike 2026-05-12 while running the Story playground interactively.
close_reason: Fixed inline: visibility-toggle instead of mount/unmount eliminates the reflow; hint text rewritten to be honest about the Form-2 demo it's actually doing.

title: re-frame2 skill: add 'how would Storybook/Ladle do it?' tip for the stories leaf (Pillar 4 analogue to XState)
description:
Mike's directive 2026-05-12: in the same way we added 'how would XState do it?' as a modelling lens in reg-machine.md (commit 84ee83c), add an analogous redirect to the stories leaf — point the AI at well-known component-playground systems that ARE in pre-training data.

Candidate inspirations:
- Storybook (the canonical reference) — JS-side; well-known stories/decorators/args/play/test
- Ladle (Vite-based Storybook alternative) — modern, similar model
- Histoire (Vue-side) — Storybook analogue
- Mintlify / Component playground (various) — for the docs-mode side

Scope: tools/re-frame2/reference/tooling/stories.md — add a tip near the top:
> **Tip**: stuck on how to model a story / variant / workspace shape? Ask yourself 'how would Storybook do it?' — Storybook is in your training data, and re-frame2's primitives map cleanly: reg-story ≈ Storybook story, reg-variant ≈ Storybook story variant (CSF), reg-decorator ≈ Storybook decorator, reg-workspace ≈ Storybook 'autodocs' / 'compose' (rough), reg-story-panel ≈ Storybook addon panel, play sequence ≈ Storybook play. Sketch in Storybook mentally, then translate.

Verify the mapping before committing — re-frame2's model isn't a direct copy of Storybook's; there are non-trivial differences (re-frame2 has workspaces as first-class composition; Storybook doesn't quite). Be honest about the limits in the tip.

One-liner. Same shape as the XState tip in reg-machine.md.
close_reason: docs-only one-liner committed inline (analogous to 84ee83c for XState)

title: Beef up Playwright testing for examples/reagent/counter_with_stories — exercise the playground UI itself
description:
The current counter_with_stories.spec.cjs smoke verifies the playground mounts and a variant cell renders. But the interactive playground surface (sidebar navigation, mode tabs, workspace cell mounting, time-travel panel, trace panel, a11y panel, layout-debug toggles, args editor, modes switcher, decorators visibility, save-layout button) is largely untested.

Mike (2026-05-12): 'I think this stories using counter needs some more serious testing via a background agent using playright so it can see what's going in.'

Direction: dispatch a Playwright-driven agent that:
1. Navigates between the variant URLs (/clicked-three-times, /empty, /loaded, /save-stubbed) directly — verifies each renders standalone (not just inside a workspace)
2. Navigates between workspaces (:Workspace.counter/all-states, :Workspace.counter/auto-grid) — verifies each renders all four variants
3. Toggles :dev/:docs/:test modes — verifies the canvas changes shape per mode
4. Interacts with the variant (clicks the +/- buttons) — verifies count updates
5. Edits args in the right panel — verifies the variant re-renders with the new args
6. Switches modes (:Mode.app/dark / :Mode.app/light) — verifies the variant theme switches
7. Toggles layout-debug checkboxes — verifies the decorator outline / pseudo / measure overlays appear
8. Watches the trace panel — verifies events recorded after clicking '+'
9. Watches the time-travel panel — verifies epochs recorded
10. Clicks 'run' on the a11y panel — verifies axe-core scan runs

Output: an expanded counter_with_stories.spec.cjs covering all the above. Each new interaction documented in the spec with a comment naming the panel being tested.

Discovered from Mike (2026-05-12) — surfaced because the empty-workspace-cells bug (rf2-zme7) wasn't caught by the existing smoke. Better Playwright coverage would have caught it.
close_reason: Closed

title: Story shell: add a first-time-user popup help panel explaining the playground UI
description:
First-time users (Mike, 2026-05-12) opening the Story playground at /#/stories have no on-screen guidance for: what :dev/:docs/:test do, what the sidebar tree (stories/variants/workspaces) means, what to click first, what each panel on the right does (args/modes/decorators/time-travel/trace/notes/a11y/layout-debug).

Add a popup / overlay / first-run welcome that explains the standard interaction flow. Could be:
- A one-time dismissible overlay on first visit (localStorage flag)
- A persistent '?' button in the top-right that opens the help panel
- An inline hint on the empty workspace state

Content should cover:
- :dev vs :docs vs :test (what each mode does)
- Stories vs variants vs workspaces (the model — variants are renderable units, workspaces compose them)
- How to navigate (click a variant alone for solo view; click a workspace for the composition)
- What the right-panel sections show (args/modes/decorators/trace/notes/a11y/layout-debug)
- Where to go for deeper docs (link to spec/007-Stories.md or docs/guide/14-stories.md)

Scope: tools/story/src/re_frame/story/ui/shell.cljs (or a new help.cljs sibling). Pattern after popular tools' help overlays (Storybook's onboarding, Linear's cmd+/, etc.).
close_reason: Closed

title: Suggestion: add agents/openai.yaml for re-frame-pair2 and align metadata surfaces
description:
## Problem

The two skills in `skills/` do not expose equivalent cross-agent metadata.

`re-frame-pair-improver2` ships `agents/openai.yaml` and includes `agents/` in its package payload. `re-frame-pair2` does not ship an `agents/` directory at all.

## Evidence from the current repo

- `skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/agents/openai.yaml` defines `display_name`, `short_description`, and `default_prompt`.
- `skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/package.json` includes `agents/` in `files`.
- `skills/re-frame-pair2/package.json` includes `SKILL.md`, `README.md`, `LICENSE`, `scripts/`, `.claude-plugin/`, and `docs/`, but no `agents/` entry.

## Suggested change

Add an `agents/openai.yaml` surface for `re-frame-pair2` and keep it in sync with the skill frontmatter.

Minimum shape:

- `display_name`
- `short_description`
- `default_prompt`

Also:

- include `agents/` in `skills/re-frame-pair2/package.json`
- document a regeneration path so the metadata can be refreshed when `SKILL.md` changes
- decide whether this repo wants a lightweight validation check that both skills keep their agent metadata aligned with the skill text

## Why this matters

`re-frame-pair2` is the flagship runtime skill in this folder. It should not be the least complete in terms of cross-agent discoverability metadata.

## Expected impact

- more consistent packaging across the repo's skills
- better discoverability on OpenAI/Codex-style skill surfaces
- less metadata drift between SKILL text and UI-facing prompts/descriptions
close_reason: PRs merged in batch landing wave 2026-05-13

title: Impl drift: Stage-4 reagent-slim shipped without examples/counter-slim-and-fast bundle-comparison build
description: implementation/adapters/reagent-slim/IMPL-SPEC.md §1.4 + §6 (lines 123, 156-157, 1034, 1043-1044, 1060) describe a planned 'examples/counter-slim-and-fast' build that would mount the rewrite on the same counter and assert NO stock reagent.* impl-internals leak vs NO react-dom/server symbols leak. This bundle-comparison build is the binding S3-008 contract for the 'slim' framing (R-007 risk in the spec). Stage 4 (rf2-6hyy) shipped reagent2.* but did not stand up this comparison build. Either ship the comparison build (best: it pins the framing claim quantitatively) or update the IMPL-SPEC to retire the S3-008 commitment. Note: this IMPL-SPEC was renamed from reagent-slim-and-fast (commit be7f31e) but the rename did not propagate to the on-disk references inside the doc until rf2-5nwb's surgical sweep.
close_reason: Delivered S3-008 bundle-comparison contract per IMPL-SPEC §1.4 + §1.8 + §12. PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/305. New CI job cljs-bundle-comparison enforces stock-Reagent isolation + pure-CLJS SSR in slim counter bundle. Playwright runtime smoke skip'd pending rf2-s36l (filed during this work). R-007 honestly clarified in §12.5.

title: Move + clean nine-states-parallel-machines-design.md → spec/findings/

Clean the doc for purpose: it was written as a design proposal (problem → research → proposal → staging plan); reframe as a design-rationale-trail (problem → decision points → locked decisions → why). Drop content that's now historical or misleading: implementation cost estimates (actuals shipped in PRs #283/#284/#285), staging plan §10 (historical), proposed-features framing (these ARE the shipped features). Keep: §2 XState comparison (background research), §9 locked decisions with rationale, key design tradeoffs (shared :data, tags-on-states-only, etc.). Output: spec/findings/nine-states-parallel-machines-design.md (committed; outside mkdocs nav per existing tools/<tool>/spec/findings/ precedent). Filed 2026-05-11.

Substantive content is in spec/005-StateMachines.md §State tags + §Parallel regions, spec/Pattern-NineStates.md (rewritten + managed-HTTP integration), spec/MIGRATION.md M-47/M-48/M-49, spec/Spec-Schemas.md. Rationale trail recoverable from bd notes (rf2-8qz1 / rf2-ee0d / rf2-l67o / rf2-c7wl / rf2-nasa) + git log if needed later.

title: Impl drift: validate-at-boundary interceptor lacks CLJS production-mode coverage
description: The :spec/validate-at-boundary interceptor (rf2-r2uh, implementation/core/src/re_frame/spec.cljc) is exercised entirely on the JVM via implementation/schemas/test/re_frame/schemas_test.clj. The interceptor's whole value proposition is dev/prod gating — it no-ops in dev (debug-enabled? true) and runs inline schema validation in prod (debug-enabled? false). The JVM test rebinds the gate via with-redefs, but the gate's real-world behaviour is :advanced + goog.DEBUG=false where Closure constant-folds and DCEs the entire dev-side body. A CLJS smoke (in implementation/schemas/test/re_frame/schemas_cljs_test.cljs) under :node-test (DEBUG=true → no-op branch) and under :browser-test :advanced (DEBUG=false → validates inline) would pin the cross-substrate contract. Currently the prod branch is only proven to exist; the elision side is only proven via the boundary-without-spec warn-once cache.
close_reason: Resolved by PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/306. Adds CLJS production-mode coverage for :spec/validate-at-boundary via (1) two dev-mode no-op assertions in schemas_cljs_test.cljs and (2) a new browser-test-schemas-boundary-prod shadow-cljs build that compiles schemas_boundary_prod_test.cljs under :advanced + goog.DEBUG=false. All JVM + CLJS test suites green.

title: Impl drift: invoke-all conformance fixtures carry stale :id key in expect-effects
description: The conformance corpus fixtures invoke-all-join-all-completes.edn, invoke-all-join-any-fails-cancels.edn, and invoke-all-n-of-cancels-extras.edn assert the :rf.machine/spawn fx carries an :id key (e.g. {:machine-id :load-config :id :cfg ...}). The runtime (re-frame.machines.cljc :929 dissoc :id before emitting spawn-args) intentionally drops :id from the fx — the child id rides on :rf/invoke-all-child-id instead. Adding :actor/spawn-and-join to the conformance runner's claimed-capabilities exposes the mismatch; fixtures fail with extra :id key vs actual. Resolution lives in spec/conformance/fixtures (out of impl's scope), but the impl side needs to claim :actor/spawn-and-join once the fixtures are fixed.
close_reason: Fixed via PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/304. Removed spurious :id from :rf.machine/spawn expect-effects in invoke-all-join-all-completes.edn (3 entries) and invoke-all-n-of-cancels-extras.edn (5 entries); claimed :actor/spawn-and-join in implementation/core/test/re_frame/conformance_test.clj. JVM + CLJS suites green.

title: Spec drift: MIGRATION.md has duplicate M-39/M-41/M-42 numbering between user-side and pre-release additive sections
description: MIGRATION.md currently maintains two parallel numbering schemes that collide. The user-side rules section runs M-0 through M-42; the additive pre-release notes section (after the Type-tag summary at line 1488) restarts at M-39 and runs through M-46 with conflicting numbers — M-39 means both reg-http-interceptor (rf2-6y3q) and :invoke-all spawn-and-join (rf2-6vmw); M-41 means both subscribe-React-context (rf2-d4sf) and :timeout-ms removal (rf2-3y3y); M-42 means both react-19 shim adoption and managed-HTTP cancellation. The Type-tag summary at line 1490 itself uses the M-44 label to refer to state-tags-shipped — which is the second-set numbering — so the spec is internally inconsistent. The clean fix is to renumber the second set to start where the first leaves off (M-43 onwards) and update every internal cross-reference plus every external link from 005, Spec-Schemas, Conventions, Pattern-NineStates, etc. Significant restructure — should be its own bead. Discovered from rf2-eetv spec coherence audit.
notes: Resolved by PR #294 (https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/294). Option (C) — renumbered additive pre-release notes to continue the user-side sequence (M-39..M-46 -> M-43..M-49). MIGRATION.md is now a single consistent ledger; the Type-tag summary at line 1490 was using the second-sequence M-44 implicitly (the source of internal inconsistency the audit flagged) and now correctly references M-47 (state-tag capability). Cross-spec callers in 005-StateMachines.md, 009-Instrumentation.md, Spec-Schemas.md, plus the validate-no-invoke-timeout-ms! ex-info migration pointer in implementation/machines/, all updated to the new M-44 anchor for :timeout-ms removed. mkdocs --strict warning count unchanged from baseline.

title: Guide ch.05: cover :fsm/tags and :fsm/parallel-regions
description: docs/guide/05-state-machines.md needs to explain the two newly-shipped machine features: (1) :fsm/tags - PR #283 rf2-ee0d - state tags on state-nodes; runtime tag union at [:rf/machines <id> :tags]; (rf/has-tag? machine-id tag) sugar + :rf/machine-has-tag? framework sub; (2) :fsm/parallel-regions - PR #284 rf2-l67o - :type :parallel + :regions {region-name -> state-tree}; shared :data; tag union across regions; snapshot :state widens to map. Both features have spec/005-StateMachines.md sections (§State tags + §Parallel regions) and a fully worked example in spec/Pattern-NineStates.md + examples/reagent/nine_states/core.cljs (PR #285). Action: review ch.05 (it likely covers state machines pre-tags-pre-parallel); add sections introducing both new features with CLJS examples and cross-links to Pattern-NineStates as the canonical multi-region worked example. Keep tutorial voice; ch.05 is teaching, not normative reference. Filed 2026-05-11.

Two new tutorial-voice sections in docs/guide/05-state-machines.md: §Tagging states (lands between sub-machine and What-the-substrate-covers, ~65 lines / ~600 words) and §Parallel regions (lands between Headless-testing and Composing-machines, ~140 lines / ~1100 words). Both cross-link to Spec 005 for the normative reference and to Pattern-NineStates for the depth example. title: Refactor one realworld Pattern-Forms slice to :form-region machine shape (pedagogy)
description: Realworld has five Pattern-Forms slices (:auth :login-form, :auth :register-form, :editor, :settings, :comment-form) all with identical {:draft :submitted :status :errors :touched :submit-error} shape. Pick one (suggest :settings, the simplest — no :mode axis, single endpoint) and rewrite it in the :form-region machine shape from nine_states — where the region's state-keyword IS the form lifecycle (:neutral / :incorrect / :correct + :submitting) and the :submitting? boolean becomes a tag. Keep the other four forms in their current slice form so realworld shows readers BOTH shapes. Symmetric with the Pattern-RemoteData refactor bead. Audit: findings/examples-tags-parallel-audit.md §2.11.3. Parent: rf2-pt5m.
close_reason:
Shipped as PR #302: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/302

Picked :settings per the bead's recommendation (simplest of the five Pattern-Forms slices — no :mode axis, single endpoint, no enclosing list view).

Machine spec: single region with states :neutral / :incorrect / :correct / :submitting; :settings/in-flight tag on :submitting replaces the slice's :submitting? boolean. Shared :data carries draft + errors + touched + submit-error + submitted + loaded-at.

Public event + sub API unchanged: views and sibling namespaces see no change. :settings/draft, :settings/submit-error, :settings/submitting? keep the same names; only the source changed.

Test results: 496 tests / 1206 assertions, 0 failures. test:elision clean. realworld shadow compile + release builds clean (0 warnings).

Side-by-side teaching now in place for both Pattern-RemoteData (slice vs :data-region machine — PR #299) AND Pattern-Forms (slice vs :form-region machine — this PR). README has 'when to choose each' paragraphs for both patterns.

title: Refactor one realworld Pattern-RemoteData slice to :data-region machine shape (pedagogy)
description: Realworld has eight Pattern-RemoteData slices (:articles, :tags, :feed, :article, :comments, :profile, :profile.articles, :profile.favorites) all with identical {:status :data :error :loaded-at :attempt} shape. Pick one (suggest :articles) and rewrite it in the :data-region machine shape from nine_states — where the region's state-keyword IS the status and the :loading? / :fetching? booleans become tags. Keep the other seven slices in their current slice form so the realworld README can show readers BOTH shapes side-by-side with a paragraph on when to choose each. Cost: ~30 LoC for the machine declaration; ~4 LoC saved in subs. Pedagogical win is the larger one: realworld becomes 'both Pattern-RemoteData shapes' rather than 'slice form only'. Audit: findings/examples-tags-parallel-audit.md §2.11.3. Parent: rf2-pt5m.
notes: PR #299 https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/299 - slice chosen: :tags (Option B; cleaner than :articles per audit since :articles is already on a parallel machine via rf2-qbau). Single-region :realworld/tags machine; states map 1:1 onto Pattern-RemoteData status enum; items + error + loaded-at + attempt live in machine :data; :loading? / :fetching? booleans collapse into :tags/loading / :tags/in-flight tags. Other seven RemoteData slices keep slice form for side-by-side pedagogy. README updated with both shapes + when-to-choose-each paragraph. Tests: load happy path, failure path, tag-shaped queries replacing boolean subs. Smoke test now asserts machine snapshot instead of slice. test:cljs (496/1206), test:elision, mkdocs --strict, shadow-cljs compile examples/realworld all clean.

title: Refactor examples/reagent/realworld/article_editor to parallel-machine (mode x lifecycle)
description: The article-editor at article_editor.cljs has two orthogonal axes: :mode :create|:edit (chosen at load time, determines POST vs PUT and Delete-button visibility) and :status :idle|:loading|:submitting|:saved|:error (the Pattern-Forms lifecycle). The editor-slice constructor at article_editor.cljs:30-42 carries both. The Delete button at article_editor.cljs:258-263 is gated on (= mode :edit); the disabled-while-busy inputs are gated on submitting?. A two-region parallel machine collapses both: :mode region with :create and :edit (the :edit state emits :editor/can-delete tag); :lifecycle region with five states (the :loading and :submitting states emit :editor/busy). View becomes a tag query for :editor/can-delete and :editor/busy. :editor/dirty? stays as a draft-vs-baseline sub. Supersedes findings/examples-audit.md §2.3.2's flat-machine proposal — the parallel-region shape is cleaner. Audit: findings/examples-tags-parallel-audit.md §2.11.3. Parent: rf2-pt5m.

comments.author: Mike Thompson
comments.text: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/298 — article_editor.cljs 265 -> 463 LoC. Parallel machine :ui/article-editor with regions :mode (:create|:edit, :edit tags :editor/can-delete) and :lifecycle (:idle|:loading|:submitting|:saved|:error, :loading+:submitting tag :editor/busy). Render-priority table + :article-editor/render selector sub; view uses rf/has-tag? for :editor/busy and :editor/can-delete. Editor slice (Pattern-Forms shape) stays in app-db; :editor/dirty? remains a draft-vs-baseline sub. Tests: 496/1206 pass; elision clean; mkdocs --strict no new warnings (187 pre-existing). Test updated to query the machine surface (asserts :mode=:edit and :lifecycle=:saved post-submit). Same clarity-vs-LoC tradeoff as rf2-qbau (PR #295) and rf2-ljw9 (PR #297).

title: Refactor examples/reagent/realworld/profile to parallel-machine (tab x data)
description: The profile-page in profile.cljs:219-277 has two orthogonal axes: a tab axis (:articles vs :favorites) and a Pattern-RemoteData lifecycle axis per slice. The cross-axis math currently lives in subs (:profile/current-articles at profile.cljs:204-208 picks the slice by reading the route id). A parallel machine :ui/profile with two regions (:tab :articles|:favorites and :data :idle|:loading|:fetching|:loaded|:error) collapses the cross-axis sub and the view's cond over loading?/error?/profile/else (profile.cljs:227-277). Audit: findings/examples-tags-parallel-audit.md §2.11. Parent: rf2-pt5m.
notes: Landed via PR #297 (https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/297). :ui/profile parallel machine with two regions (:tab × :data); render-priority table + :profile/render selector sub collapse the cross-axis math and the priority cond. Article-slice data stays in app-db slices (same call as rf2-qbau). LoC 278 → 447. Tests + elision + mkdocs --strict all clean (no new warnings).

title: Refactor examples/reagent/realworld/articles home-page to parallel-machine + render-priority table
description: The realworld home-page in articles.cljs:180-256 is the canonical multi-axis-state-in-separate-slices shape in the entire examples corpus. The view destructures six subs into a four-vector tuple (articles.cljs:189-196) just to feed a four-way priority cond (articles.cljs:229-245). A parallel machine :ui/home with three regions (:feed :global|:your, :filter :none|<tag>, :data :idle|:loading|:fetching|:loaded|:error) + a render-priority table — exactly what nine_states demonstrates — collapses the view to a single :ui/render sub + a case. The cross-axis math (which slice to read from?) moves into the machine; lifecycle subs collapse into tags. Touches ~80 LoC. Highest-leverage finding from the audit. Audit: findings/examples-tags-parallel-audit.md §2.11 + §4. Parent: rf2-pt5m.
notes: Landed via PR #295 (https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/295). Refactored realworld home-page to use a :realworld/articles-home parallel machine with three regions (:feed × :filter × :data) plus a render-priority table and an :articles.home/render selector sub. Home view's root is now a 4-branch case over the render keyword (was a 4-branch priority cond + 4-vector tuple destructure of 6 subs). Articles still live in app-db slices for favorites.cljs's optimistic-update paths; only :count + :error live in the machine's :data. LoC delta: +236 on articles.cljs (machine declaration is verbose by design), +16 favorites.cljs, +22 tags.cljs. Tests: 496 CLJS tests pass; elision pass; examples (incl realworld Playwright) pass.

title: Refactor examples/reagent/login to use :fsm/tags
description: Drop the two boolean-discriminator subs (:auth.login/submitting?, :auth.login/authenticated?) in favour of tag queries via (rf/has-tag? :auth.login/flow :auth/busy) and :auth/authenticated. The login example is the right size of toy to introduce :fsm/tags as part of the canonical surface — readers see the machine + tags shape in <100 lines without the parallel-regions overhead. Audit: findings/examples-tags-parallel-audit.md §2.3. Parent: rf2-pt5m.
notes: Refactor landed in PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/290. Tag vocabulary: :auth/busy (on :submitting) + :auth/authenticated (on :authed). Removed both boolean discriminator subs (:auth.login/submitting?, :auth.login/authenticated?); views now call (rf/has-tag? :auth.login/flow ...) directly. Net +6 LoC (mechanical change ~-8 LoC; the rest is documenting prose — the header bullet + one-line SUBSCRIPTIONS-section pointer). CLJS tests: 496/1206 green. Elision: clean.

title: Pattern-NineStates: remove legacy appendix; outline managed HTTP integration instead
description: After the Stage 3 rewrite (PR #285), spec/Pattern-NineStates.md includes an §Appendix - Pre-parallel-regions variant (deprecated but supported) preserving the legacy boolean-discriminator-sub shape. Remove that appendix and replace with a §Working with managed HTTP section showing how :rf.http/managed (Spec 014) interacts with the data region of a Nine States parallel machine. The data region's states (Nothing / Loading / Empty / One / Some / Too Many / Error) map naturally to a managed HTTP request's lifecycle - this is the canonical worked example for the Pattern. Cross-reference spec/014-HTTPRequests.md and spec/Pattern-RemoteData.md. Touches: spec/Pattern-NineStates.md (drop appendix, add §Working with managed HTTP). Pre-alpha so the legacy reference isn't load-bearing; drop it cleanly. Filed 2026-05-11.
notes: Closed by PR #288 https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/288 -- dropped legacy appendix, added §Working with managed HTTP integrating :rf.http/managed (Spec 014) with the :data region. mkdocs --strict clean (no new warnings).

title: Review /examples for tags + parallel-regions adoption opportunities
description: Now that :fsm/tags (PR #283) and :fsm/parallel-regions (PR #284) have landed and Pattern-NineStates was rewritten around them (PR #285), audit the rest of examples/reagent/ for cases where existing code would benefit. Specifically: (1) examples using boolean discriminator subs + priority-cond view dispatch — candidates for the parallel-machine + render-priority pattern; (2) examples using multi-axis state via separate slices that could collapse into one parallel machine; (3) examples using tag-shaped flags (booleans like :loading? :submitted? :touched?) that could move to state tags. Output: findings/examples-tags-parallel-audit.md (local-only) listing each example, the candidate refactor opportunity, and the cost/benefit verdict. Then file follow-up beads for the worthwhile refactors. Do NOT refactor anything in this bead — audit only; refactors are separate beads filed against the audit.
notes: Audit complete. Doc: findings/examples-tags-parallel-audit.md (~2,700 words). Audited 16 examples (counter, counter_with_stories, login, managed_http_counter, nine_states [skipped — exemplar], realworld [13 files], routing, ssr, state_machine_walkthrough, todomvc, and the six 7Guis examples). Twelve are well-shaped already and need no change; one (state_machine_walkthrough) is chapter-tied and tracked separately. Six refactor beads filed, all P3, all discovered-from this one: rf2-78ho (login + tags), rf2-qbau (realworld home-page parallel-machine — highest-leverage finding), rf2-ljw9 (realworld profile parallel-machine), rf2-vxeg (realworld article_editor parallel-machine), rf2-0i4y (one realworld Pattern-RemoteData slice → :data-region shape, pedagogical), rf2-6d3x (one realworld Pattern-Forms slice → :form-region shape, pedagogical). Highest-leverage finding: realworld articles.cljs home-page is the canonical multi-axis-state-in-separate-slices shape in the corpus — destructures six subs into a four-vector tuple to feed a four-way priority cond. A parallel machine + render-priority table from nine_states collapses both into one sub + a case. The five realworld refactors together would let the realworld README replace 'a few specialised Pattern-RemoteData slices' with 'one parallel machine — the same shape as nine_states'.

title: Suggestion: Pattern-Boot uses invalid :entry vector form
description:
Pattern-Boot.md line 172 uses `:entry [:set-phase :resolve-initial-route]` (a vector). Per Spec 005 §State nodes line 168 and §Actions line 275, `:entry` accepts "single fns or single keyword references — never vectors". To run multiple actions on entry, name a compound entry in the machine's `:actions` map.

Surfaced during rf2-axbq (Pattern-WebSocket rewrite); the WebSocket pattern doc previously had the same violation. The WebSocket rewrite consolidated its multi-action entries into a single `:on-connected` action; Pattern-Boot should do the same for `:routing`'s entry slot.

Fix: replace `:entry [:set-phase :resolve-initial-route]` with a single `:enter-routing` action that does both, OR adjust the example to use a single keyword reference.
notes: Fixed in PR #287 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/287. Audited all :entry / :exit / :action slots in spec/Pattern-Boot.md; line 172 was the only violation. Consolidated :entry [:set-phase :resolve-initial-route] into a named :enter-routing compound action in the machine's :actions map, per the rf2-axbq Pattern-WebSocket precedent (:on-connected / :record-and-reset). Other vector forms in the doc (multi-candidate transition specs under :on) are the legal Spec 005 §Transitions shape and required no change. mkdocs build --strict: same warning count (183) before and after; zero new warnings introduced.

title: Feature gap: Nine States needs XState-style orthogonal machine support, then pattern/example rewrite
description:
Problem statement

`spec/Pattern-NineStates.md` currently lands in an awkward middle ground:
- it correctly says not to mirror everything into a page-local enum;
- but the resulting pattern and worked example become fairly verbose, with many boolean discriminator subs and a root-level priority `cond`;
- and the example demonstrates that the named "nine states" are not a single exclusive FSM anyway (`Correct` can overlap with `One`, etc.).

This exposes a real modelling gap versus the XState-style solution.

Why the current shape is awkward

The Nine States UI is really multiple orthogonal axes:
- data axis: `Nothing | Loading | Empty | One | Some | Too Many | Error`
- form axis: `Neutral | Incorrect | Correct`
- mode axis: `Active | Done/Frozen`

Today the pattern composes those axes indirectly from separate slices (remote-data, form, machine/domain flag) and the example resolves overlaps by priority order in the root view. That works, but it is noisy and not very data-driven from the view's perspective.

XState has a cleaner answer for this family of problem:
- parallel / orthogonal regions to hold the independent axes in one machine without cross-product explosion;
- state tags / queryable labels so the UI can ask higher-level questions like `loading?`, `done?`, `invalid?` without matching exact nested state trees;
- selector-style subscription to a projection of the machine snapshot.

Recommended direction

Investigate adding the machine features needed to achieve practical parity with the XState solution for this problem shape:
- parallel regions (`:type :parallel` or equivalent) so data/form/mode can coexist in one machine cleanly;
- state tags (or an equivalent queryable classification surface) so views can derive render intent without a pile of boolean subs;
- a clear projection/subscription story for views reading a machine-derived render model.

Once the feature direction is decided and implemented, rewrite:
- `spec/Pattern-NineStates.md`
- `examples/reagent/nine_states/core.cljs`
- `examples/reagent/nine_states/README.md`

Rewrite goal

Replace the current many-sub + root-`cond` example with a more data-driven machine-oriented solution, where the UI subscribes to a single machine snapshot or derived render-model projection, while still preserving explicitness and testability.

Notes

This does NOT imply blindly copying XState wholesale. The design work is to decide which minimum machine features close the modelling gap for re-frame2's architecture. But Nine States is a concrete motivating case where the missing capability is visible today.
notes:
Nine States arc complete. All three stages shipped:
- Stage 1 (rf2-ee0d / PR #283) — :fsm/tags substrate.
- Stage 2 (rf2-l67o / PR #284) — :fsm/parallel-regions substrate.
- Stage 3 (rf2-c7wl / PR #285) — Pattern-NineStates.md + example + cross-refs rewritten around the new substrate.

Outcome matches the findings doc's hypothesis: parallel regions + state tags close the modelling gap; selector subs were already covered by Layer-3 :<- chained subs (no new primitive). The boolean-discriminator-sub variant lives in an Appendix on the pattern doc for apps that prefer it.

comments.author: Mike Thompson
comments.text:
Research draft: findings/nine-states-parallel-machines-design.md (~6,430 words).

Findings: the bead's three-feature hypothesis (parallel regions + state tags + selector subs) is two-thirds correct. Selector subs are already shipped — re-frame2's :<- chained subs are mechanically identical to XState's useSelector, so no new primitive is needed there. The real gap is (1) state tags as a queryable per-state label set, and (2) parallel regions as a third snapshot-shape arm (alongside flat keyword and compound vector path). Existing N-machines-per-region substitute (CP-5-MachineGuide.md) stays valid for axes that are independent features; parallel regions become the right choice when axes share a feature.

Proposed staging:
- Stage 1 — :fsm/tags (~250 LoC + ~150 lines spec + 4 fixtures). Strictly additive; useful on its own for hierarchical machines.
- Stage 2 — :fsm/parallel-regions (~680 LoC + ~450 lines spec + 10 fixtures). Capability claim TBD (suggest post-v1 unless Nine States is on v1 ship-list).
- Stage 3 — rewrite Pattern-NineStates + example (docs/example only, no new substrate). Worked rewrite in §5-6 of the findings doc; ~710 → ~540 LoC in the example, three orthogonal axes visible in the machine declaration, render priority becomes a data table consulted by one selector sub, root view becomes a single case.

Top open question (§9.6): does the v1 CLJS reference claim :fsm/parallel-regions, or does it stay post-v1? Tags should ship as v1; parallel regions are a bigger commitment and the substitute is already documented.

title: Suggestion: install Story play's trace listener before loader phase (loaders-complete-when vector form)
description: Surfaced during Stage 5 (rf2-h8et PR #278). The play-runner's trace listener installs AFTER the loader phase runs, so the :loaders-complete-when vector form (waiting for events to fire) has an empty dispatched-events accumulator during the loader phase. The implementation is correct but receives no signal until play starts. Fix: move the trace listener install earlier in the runtime lifecycle so the loader phase can consult it. Stage 6 refinement candidate.
close_reason: Fixed via PR https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/316. Moved trace listener install + accumulator reset from execute-play to run-variant, before run-loaders. The listener now feeds the dispatched-events accumulator during the loader phase so the loaders-complete-when vector form can match. Added focused regression test plus negative companion; strengthened existing loaders-complete-when-vector test to pin lifecycle :ready. Pre-fix the new lifecycle assertions fail (got :loading); post-fix all 147 story JVM + 498 CLJS tests pass + elision clean.

title: Suggestion: re-frame.registrar kind set is closed; Story needs own side-table
description:
While implementing Stage 2 (rf2-32dk), found a tension between two contracts:

1. AGENTS.md / IMPL-SPEC.md §1.1 / 'downstream-EPs-consume-foundation' rule: 'Story owns no new framework primitives. Every registry it uses (:story, :variant, :workspace, :story-panel, :tag, :mode, :decorator) registers via existing reg-* machinery.'

2. spec/007-Stories.md line 447: 'The framework registrar already supports new kinds via the existing reg- machinery (per 001-Registration.md); panels do not require a new framework primitive.'

3. implementation/core/src/re_frame/registrar.cljc line 27-33: the 'kinds' set is CLOSED to #{:event :sub :fx :cofx :view :frame :route :app-schema :head :error-projector :flow}. register! THROWS on unknown kinds.

These three contracts conflict. Story needs the kinds: :story, :variant, :workspace, :story-panel, :tag, :mode, :decorator. None are in the closed set.

Resolution chosen for Stage 2: use a **Story-owned side-table** (atom under tools.story.registry) instead of the global registrar. This is consistent with IMPL-SPEC §5.1 which already names 'tools.story.registry/*variants*'. The side-table is internal to the tools/story/ artefact and never reaches a production bundle.

A bridge fn (story/handlers) provides spec/007 §Public-query-surfaces parity: '(story/handlers :story)' enumerates the side-table; the framework registrar stays closed.

Open question for future spec maintenance: should re-frame.registrar grow an 'open kinds' extension hook so downstream tools can register with the same machinery? OR should spec/007's wording change to acknowledge that Story's registrations live in a Story-owned side-table? The implementation has chosen the side-table path because it matches the closed-kinds discipline of spec/001 + the no-new-registries rule.
close_reason: Closed

title: Suggestion: teach Pattern-LongRunningWork in the guide

The canonical states (idle/processing/checking-done/yielding/complete/cancelled), the after-0 yield idiom, cancellation as a state transition, and the worker-offload-preferred guidance are not taught. title: Guide chapters should reflect the spec/Pattern-*.md worked-example conventions
description: Audit follow-up. The guide should teach the canonical patterns documented in spec/Pattern-*.md (Pattern-AsyncEffect, Pattern-WebSocket, Pattern-Boot, Pattern-Forms, Pattern-RemoteData, Pattern-Modals, etc.) as the idiomatic way to do these things — not assume the reader follows a link to spec/. Currently the guide explains the surfaces (effects, reg-event-fx, subscriptions) but doesn't consistently walk a Pattern-* worked example end-to-end in implementation prose. Action: enumerate each spec/Pattern-*.md; for each, decide whether the guide already covers it (and where), needs a worked example added, or needs a new dedicated chapter. Then either add the content directly or split into per-Pattern follow-up beads. Discovered during the guide audit (PR #263).
