Direct edits in ch.03/04/05/06 teach Pattern-NineStates, Pattern-RemoteData (5-key slice + loading/fetching), Pattern-WebSocket (state outline), and Pattern-Boot (chained-events vs boot-machine). Follow-up beads filed: rf2-9o0j (Pattern-Forms), rf2-n20h (Pattern-LongRunningWork). Pattern-AsyncEffect and Pattern-StaleDetection were already covered well; no action.

title: Suggestion: revisit the mutation-heavy machine source-coord walker
description:
Tentative suggestion.

In `implementation/core/src/re_frame/source_coords.cljc`, `walk-machine-spec` / `walk-states-tree` build the per-element machine source-coordinate index via a mutation-heavy transient accumulator with nested `reduce-kv`, `doseq`, and side-effecting `assoc!` calls.

That may be fine, but this helper is compile-time metadata collection rather than hot runtime work, so the imperative shape seems harder to justify than it would be in a tight runtime loop. The current traversal also repeats a lot of slot-specific update logic inline, which makes the walker harder to extend or audit.

Possible direction:
- Recast the traversal as a more declarative visitor that returns collected entries and only materialises the final map at the edge.
- Or centralise the repeated slot-handling rules behind smaller helpers so the remaining mutation is clearly a deliberate implementation detail rather than the dominant shape.

Performance note:
- Transients may still be the right choice if macro-expansion cost matters and this walker is called often enough to notice.
- If so, keeping the imperative core is reasonable; it would just be worth documenting that compile-time performance is the reason for the less-functional style.
close_reason: Closed as docs-only: added an inline comment on walk-states-tree explaining why the mutation-heavy transient shape is deliberate (macro-expansion-time perf). Refactor to a declarative visitor is feasible but yak-shaving without benchmark data. Commit 519f38c.

title: Suggestion: unify machine guard/action validation under the recursive state walk
description:
RESHAPED 2026-05-12 (Mike's call): verify-the-gap-first, then act on the evidence.

Original observation (rf2-oz9t): in implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines.cljc, create-machine-handler validates keyword :guard / :action references via a manual top-level doseq over (:states machine), only walking :on transitions. walk-state-nodes (used just above for other registration-time validation) walks recursively but is NOT reused here. Spec text says registration-time validation should walk broadly (:on, :always, :entry, :exit), not just top-level :on. The narrow shape MIGHT miss nested-state guard/action refs and silently strand them as runtime-only errors.

What to do:
1. Write a small test: declare a reg-machine with a NESTED state whose :guard (and another whose :action) references an UNREGISTERED keyword. Register it.
2. Observe the result:
   - If registration succeeds silently (no exception, no warning) — the gap is real. The narrow pass is a correctness hole. Reshape the validator to drive off walk-state-nodes (or extend the existing recursive walker to also collect :guard / :action keyword refs).
   - If registration fails with a clear error pointing at the unregistered keyword — the gap is NOT real. Add a code comment in create-machine-handler explaining why the top-level :on pass is sufficient (e.g. it consults a separate index, or nested validation happens elsewhere). Close.
   - Spec/impl reconciliation: if the gap is NOT real, also check whether spec text needs softening — the current spec wording implies broader walking; if impl is sufficient, the spec is overspecified. File a small follow-up to update the spec doc.
3. Report which branch the test exercised + what action follows.

Performance note: registration-time, called once per machine on app startup. Readability + complete coverage matter more than imperative-loop speed.

OUT of scope: changing the narrow pass without evidence; refactoring source-coords walker (that's rf2-v25r, closed).
close_reason: Outcome A: gap was real. Probe test (12 cases) showed 10 silent failures: nested :on :guard/:action, top+nested :always :guard/:action, top+nested :entry/:exit action refs all bypassed the narrow top-level pass. Reshaped create-machine-handler to drive validation off walk-state-nodes and cover every transition-bearing slot (:on, :always, :entry, :exit). All 12 probe tests now throw at registration. PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/307

title: Spec 005 §Declarative :invoke — fn-form :data not invoked at spawn
description: Spec 005 line 1471 documents :invoke :data fn-form '(fn [snap event] data)' so the spawned child's :data can be derived from the parent's post-action snapshot + triggering event. apply-transition-once currently passes the spawn-args :data through verbatim — confirmed during rf2-ijm7 testing: literal-map :data works; fn-form :data results in the child's :data being the function itself, not the materialised map. Either implement fn-form invocation in spawn-fx (call the fn with [parent-snap event] before passing to the spawned machine) OR tighten the spec to drop the fn-form. Discovered during rf2-ijm7 (PR #260) — JVM tests worked around with literal-map only. Cross-ref: spec/005-StateMachines.md:1471, implementation/machines/src/re_frame/machines.cljc spawn-fx :data path.
notes: PR #261 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/261. Path A (implement) per audit. Materialised :data fn-form in apply-transition-once :invoke + :invoke-all reductions, against the loop-evolving accumulator snapshot (post-action, includes prior sibling :on-spawn writes) and triggering event per Spec 005:1511. Throws route through existing ::action-failed short-circuit per Spec 005:1597. JVM tests in invoke_data_fn_form_test.clj (6 tests / 13 assertions); CLJS in machines_cljs_test.cljs. Full suite green: machines / core / http / ssr / routing / epoch / flows / schemas + elision + 419 CLJS tests. rf2-ijm7 had no fn-form workaround to revert.

title: Slim trace ribbon vs full re-frame-10x embed in Scittle playground
description: From rf2-mjxg findings doc. Should the Scittle-based playground show a slim trace ribbon (just the events + state changes for the current lesson) or embed the full re-frame-10x panel? Slim ribbon: easier to integrate, less overwhelming for learners. Full 10x: more realistic, demonstrates the full observability surface. Decision needed when playground UX gels. Post-1.0. P3.
notes: **LOCKED 2026-05-12 (Mike): Option (c) progressive disclosure.** Scittle playground starts learners with a slim trace ribbon (event-detail panel shrunk to minimum — events fired + app-db before/after for the current lesson). Later lessons reveal more of Causa as concepts are introduced (time-travel scrubber when time-travel is taught; machine inspector when machines are taught; full Causa for advanced-debugging lessons). Matches the guide's progressive-disclosure pattern + Causa's own hero = event-detail lock (#7 from rf2-buor/rf2-90d5 walkthrough). Causa's 16 panels must be independently mountable to support per-lesson config — discipline good for Causa regardless. Holds post-1.0; depends on rf2-mjxg (playground viability research) landing first.

title: Verify re-frame2 macros survive SCI interpretation (Scittle playground prerequisite)
description: From rf2-mjxg findings doc. The Scittle-based playground (recommended path, post-1.0) requires re-frame2's macros to work under SCI interpretation. Most CLJ/CLJS macros do; some don't. Audit re-frame2's macro surface (reg-event-fx, reg-sub, reg-view, reg-machine, reg-fx, reg-cofx, reg-route, etc.) and verify each runs cleanly under SCI's expansion + invocation pipeline. Fix any that fail. Pre-1.0: investigate; v1.1+: implement fixes. P3.
close_reason: Audit landed. PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/303. 21 macros audited: 17 PASS, 1 NEEDS-CHANGE (re-frame.core/reg-view's requiring-resolve at expansion time — fixable plugin-side without touching re-frame2 source via sci/copy-ns of re-frame.views-macros/expand-reg-view), 2 UNKNOWN (mark-and-measure silent-no-ops under SCI because (:ns &env) is nil — open question for Mike; reaction depends on Reagent v2 SCI export tracked under rf2-pfez). Findings doc at findings/sci-macro-compatibility.md. Open questions for Mike on performance gating, plugin-vs-source fix choice, and whether SCI compatibility is a spec-level commitment captured in the doc.

title: Document Malli per-namespace bundle cost in Spec 010
description:
Per the rf2-qnxf Malli bundle-cost audit (findings/malli-bundle-cost-audit.md §3.2 / §4): add a §Bundle cost subsection to Spec 010 citing the audit's measured numbers and a rule of thumb on which Malli namespaces are safe in production paths.

## What to add

Subsection somewhere near §Why Malli, with content along these lines:

- Production bundle cost for a typical re-frame2 app with schemas: ~24 KB gzipped (just malli.core).
- Heavier surfaces add per-namespace: malli.error ~6 KB, malli.transform ~9 KB, malli.generator ~15 KB.
- Rule of thumb: `:require [malli.core]` only in production code paths; reserve `malli.error` / `malli.generator` / `malli.transform` / `malli.dev` for dev / test / 10x tiers.
- Cross-link to the rf2-qnxf audit findings document.

## Why

The 'Malli is hard to DCE' framing is half-right. Closure cannot DCE inside malli.core (the dynamic-dispatch internals defeat dataflow), but Closure DOES correctly DCE between Malli namespaces. Telling users where the boundary is reframes the cost story from 'Malli is 65 KB' (false for typical apps) to 'Malli is 24 KB' (accurate).

## Scope

- ~30-50 lines in Spec 010.
- Bundle-cost numbers from findings/malli-bundle-cost-audit.md §1.

## Out of scope

- The set-schema-validator! implementation (rf2-froe).

## References

- findings/malli-bundle-cost-audit.md §1 (measurement) and §3.2 (Strategy 2)
notes:
Closed by PR #254 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/254

Added §Bundle cost subsection to spec/010-Schemas.md citing the rf2-qnxf audit's measured numbers (~24 KB gzipped headline for typical apps, per-namespace breakdown for malli.error/transform/generator), the safe-in-production list, the opt-out path via set-schema-validator! (rf2-froe / PR #237), and the boundary path via :spec/validate-at-boundary (rf2-r2uh / PR #242). Cross-linked from §Why Malli. Batched with rf2-b4wt + rf2-4o1c.4.
close_reason: Closed

title: Frame-context corrupted-_currentValue silently falls through to :rf/default
description:
Per the rf2-22ds bead's Scenario 3: 'if the React-context boundary is corrupted (component rendered through an unwrapped portal? misuse case), the failure is observable + diagnostic — emits a structured error event, not a silent fallback.'

Today's behaviour: re-frame.adapter.context/coerce-context-value tolerates only keyword and non-empty string shapes. Anything else (nil, false, numeric, JS object, empty string) silently maps to nil and the resolution chain proceeds to :rf/default. There is no :rf.error/frame-context-corrupted (or similar) trace.

Real-world triggers for a corrupted _currentValue:
- A library that wraps the same React.createContext object externally and pushes a non-keyword value onto it.
- An incorrectly-authored React portal that renders a reg-view'd subtree outside the original Provider tree (in which case _currentValue is the createContext default :rf/default, which IS a valid keyword and the silent fallback IS arguably correct — but the misuse case is harder to diagnose without a trace).
- Tooling that pokes _currentValue directly (the adapter-context-current-frame-tolerates-prop-stringified-keyword test in cross_spec_cljs_test does this for coverage, demonstrating the seam).

Suggested shape: when coerce-context-value's input is non-nil and non-empty AND not a keyword AND not a coercible string, emit :rf.warning/frame-context-corrupted (op-type :warning) once per process per offending shape, with the observed value's pr-str and a hint to check Provider boundaries / external libs touching the shared context. Recovery: resolve to :rf/default (current behaviour). Per Spec 009 §Error categories.

The current rf2-22ds tests (scenario-3-context-corrupted-current-behaviour) pin the present behaviour as a regression guard; once this bead lands, those tests update to assert the structured-error event AND the :rf/default recovery.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/236 — structured :rf.error/frame-context-corrupted on function-component _currentValue reads of non-coercible shapes (nil/false/number/empty-string/JS-object). Recovery :replaced-with-default — apps still resolve to :rf/default. Spec 009 §error-categories + §recovery-table + §where-trace-emission-lives all updated. Scenario 3 of frame_provider_context_cljs_test rewritten to assert the trace event.
close_reason: Closed

title: examples/helix/README.md: bring up to parity with examples/uix/README.md
description:

It mentions the smoke trio (counter + login per rf2-2qit Decision 7) and references day8/re-frame-2-helix, use-subscribe, flush-views!, wrap-view, frame-provider — but doesn't follow the structure that examples/uix/README.md and examples/reagent/README.md establish:

title: implementation/README.md: refresh layout + status table (drift from current artefact set)

1. Directory layout (lines 23-94) shows only core/, adapters/, schemas/, machines/, routing/ — missing the four shipped per-feature artefacts: flows/, http/, ssr/, epoch/ (all confirmed on disk with deps.edn, src/, test/).

2. Layout claims flows.cljc and ssr.cljc live in core/src/re_frame/ — both have moved to their own per-feature artefacts (implementation/flows/src/re_frame/flows.cljc, implementation/ssr/src/re_frame/ssr.cljc). Also missing from the core/src/re_frame/ listing: performance.cljc, source_coords.cljc, test_support.cljc, and the adapter/ subdirectory (re-frame.adapter.context for the React Context that adapters share).

3. Status-by-spec-area table (lines 96-111) is missing rows for Spec 007 (Stories) and Spec 014 (HTTPRequests). Also lists Spec 003 as 'Done' — but Spec 003 is reserved per spec/README.md (no 003-*.md exists; numbering gap held open for future cross-frame composition work).

4. Several artefact descriptions (around the schemas/machines/routing block) reference re-frame.{schemas,machines,routing} rather than current fully qualified namespaces if those have shifted; verify against actual ns forms in src/.

Out of scope for the rf2-cyom sweep because these are substantive content rewrites, not mechanical rename/path fixes.

title: Sweep examples for best practice, code hygiene, and structure
description:
Mike's directive 2026-05-11: sweep the examples corpus for best practice, code hygiene, and structural consistency. Pairs with rf2-cyom (README sweep) which covers READMEs; this covers the actual code.

## Scope

All examples under examples/ — verify with glob at dispatch time. Stage 1 noted 17 example apps; verify count.

Per-example axes to check:

### 1. re-frame2 idiom adherence

- View registration via reg-view (not raw Reagent components where re-frame2 idiom applies)
- Subscribe + dispatch usage (not direct app-db reads/writes)
- Effect / cofx isolation (no side effects in handler bodies)
- Schema usage where appropriate (post-rf2-9qsk: schemas dev-only; boundary-validation interceptor for prod paths)
- Frame-provider usage in multi-frame examples (per rf2-sixo Path 1)
- :on-create / :on-destroy events for view-lifecycle side effects (not Form-2 closures)

### 2. Code hygiene

- Dead code removal
- Unused requires
- Stale comments referencing closed beads / old behavior
- Broken cross-doc links
- Inconsistent formatting

### 3. Structure consistency

- deps.edn — same dependency aliases / versions across examples (check for drift)
- shadow-cljs.edn — consistent build config shape
- README structure (verify with rf2-cyom; flag delta if rf2-cyom hasn't yet swept the example's README)
- Test file layout (per-example test/ dir conventions)
- Per-example src/ layout

### 4. Currency

- Stale references after the recent renames:
  - reagent-slim-and-fast → reagent-slim
  - day8/re-frame-2-reagent → day8/reagent-classic (when used as the thin-bridge artefact)
  - implementation/substrates/ → implementation/adapters/
- React 19 / Reagent 2 baseline awareness (where applicable; some examples may still use stock Reagent + React 18 — that's OK, but check if the example claims something else)
- Spec 005 :timeout-ms-on-:invoke (DROPPED in PR #223) — any example using it needs migration to state-level :after

### 5. Best practice

- Testing patterns: do examples have tests? Are they consistent?
- Error handling: re-frame2 conventions (interceptors, error events, etc.)
- Side-effect isolation: handlers pure; effects in fx/cofx layers
- Schema usage where load-bearing
- Source-coord / trace integration (when applicable to the example)

## Output

Per example:
- Issues found, categorised by axis
- Mechanical fixes applied inline (rename sweeps, dead-code removal, formatting nits)
- Substantive issues → follow-up bead

ONE batched PR titled 'examples: hygiene + best-practice sweep (rf2-XXXX)' OR per-example PRs if any single example's edits are substantial.

## Out of scope

- Don't add new examples.
- Don't add new tests beyond verifying refactors.
- Don't touch examples/<dir>/README.md if rf2-cyom is still in flight (avoid conflict; coordinate at PR-open time).
- Don't change spec/ or implementation/.
- Don't update decision-beads.md.

## Dispatchability

Direction-set; suitable for a background agent. No spec changes. Touches examples/* only — minimal hot-zone overlap.

If rf2-cyom (README sweep) is still in flight when this dispatches, coordinate by skipping README.md edits in the example dirs (rf2-cyom owns those).
close_reason: Closed

title: Sweep all READMEs for accuracy (exclude top-level README.md)
description:
Mike's directive 2026-05-11: sweep README.md files across the repo to check accuracy. **DO NOT touch the top-level README.md** — Mike's been hand-tuning it; leave alone.

## Scope

All README.md files in the repo EXCEPT the top-level. Likely targets (verify with glob at dispatch time):

- implementation/adapters/README.md
- implementation/adapters/reagent/README.md (if any)
- implementation/adapters/uix/README.md (if any)
- implementation/adapters/helix/README.md (if any)
- implementation/adapters/reagent-slim/README.md (if Stage 4 has added one)
- implementation/<other-artefact>/README.md (per-artefact)
- examples/<example>/README.md (per-example)
- skills/re-frame-pair2/README.md
- skills/re-frame-pair-improver2/README.md
- docs/<subdir>/README.md (if any)

## What to check (per file)

1. **Name references** — sweep for stale names that have been renamed:
   - 'reagent-slim-and-fast' → 'reagent-slim' (renamed 2026-05-11)
   - 'day8/re-frame-2-reagent' (the old thin-bridge artefact name) → 'day8/reagent-classic' (renamed)
   - 'substrate' → 'adapter' (where the canonical-naming directive applies)
   - 're-frame.substrate.*' → 're-frame.adapter.*'
   - Other rename hot-spots — check git log for recent renames.

2. **Path references** — files moved during recent reorgs:
   - implementation/substrates/ → implementation/adapters/
   - findings/* (some renamed)
   - spec/* (Spec 005, 006, etc. may have new sections)

3. **Surface references** — out-of-date API claims:
   - References to reagent.dom (gone in React 19 / reagent-slim)
   - References to with-let, cursor, track, wrap (zero usage; not in reagent-slim's surface)
   - References to :timeout-ms on :invoke (dropped in PR #223; replaced by :after)
   - References to coerce-context-value (vestigial under narrowed convert-prop-value; Stage 4 deletes it)

4. **Bead references** — rf2-XXXX IDs that may be closed/renamed. Cross-check against current bd state.

5. **Installation/build instructions** — CLI tooling, deps, build steps. Verify they still work.

6. **Architecture claims** — has the architecture shifted in ways the README doesn't reflect?
   - React + VDOM commitment (rf2-ubo7 sweep in flight)
   - 8-language portability list
   - Re-com-scoping directive
   - Stage 1-4 of the reagent-slim rewrite

7. **Cross-doc consistency** — does the README reference docs/specs/findings that have moved or been renamed?

## Output

Per file:
- List inaccuracies found.
- For mechanical fixes (rename sweep, path updates), apply directly + commit.
- For substantive issues (architecture claims that are now wrong), file follow-up bead and flag in PR description.

Output format options:
- (a) ONE sweep PR titled 'docs: README accuracy sweep (exclude top-level)' with all mechanical fixes batched.
- (b) PER-FILE PRs if any single file's edits are substantial enough to warrant separate review.

Lean: (a) — most edits are mechanical; one PR is faster to review.

## Out of scope

- **Top-level README.md** — DO NOT TOUCH. Mike's been hand-tuning it.
- **CLAUDE.md** — separate concern; not a README.
- **decision-beads.md** — local-only Mike dashboard.
- **findings/** — local-only research; not READMEs.
- Don't refactor section structure or rewrite content beyond accuracy fixes.
- Don't add new content (new sections, new examples) — accuracy sweep only.

## Dispatchability

Direction-set; suitable for a background agent. No spec changes; no implementation changes; just doc accuracy. Touches READMEs only — zero conflict with the spec / implementation work in flight.
close_reason: Stale in_progress cleanup — fix commits on origin/main per git log sweep 2026-05-13. Bead status was never flipped after PR merge.

title: Suggestion: replace missing-references atom accumulator with concat/for or reduce (epoch.cljc:578)
description:
Tentative suggestion.

In `implementation/epoch/src/re_frame/epoch.cljc`, `missing-references` (line 563) collects missing-machine and missing-route entries into a local `missing` atom (line 578) via a `doseq` and a `when-let`.

The atom is a straight accumulator over two independent comprehensions. Possible direction:

- Build the two collections separately (`(for [...] :when (not ...) ...)` for machines, a `when-let` returning either nil or a single-element vector for the active route), then `(vec (concat ...))`. Or one `reduce` over a synthetic source.

Why this seems worth revisiting:
- The atom adds nothing the comprehension can't carry directly.
- Two independent collection-build sites are more honest about "these come from different shapes" than a hidden swap! to a shared cell.

Performance note:
- Cold path — runs at the registration-integrity-check layer, not in the drain hot path. Performance is not the deciding factor; readability is.

Discovered during the rf2-ro7e audit.
notes: PR #228 opened
close_reason: Closed

title: Suggestion: replace seen?-atom in ssr/replace-header with a tuple-threading reduce (ssr.cljc:584)
description:
Tentative suggestion.

In `implementation/ssr/src/re_frame/ssr.cljc`, `replace-header` (line 577) walks the headers via a `keep` callback that consults a local `seen?` atom (line 584) to enforce "replace the first matching, drop subsequent matches, append if none matched".

The atom serves as a one-bit accumulator threaded through the keep. Possible direction:

- Rewrite as `(reduce ... [[] false] headers)` threading `[acc seen?]`, then `(if seen? acc (conj acc fallback))`. The data flow is then visible in the reduce signature instead of hidden behind a `reset!` inside a `when-not`.

Why this seems worth revisiting:
- The current shape uses `keep` for filter-and-replace + an atom for first-match suppression, which reads as two algorithms layered together. The reduce form expresses the spec ("first match wins; rest dropped; append if none") in a single pass.

Performance note:
- Cold path — header replacement runs per-response, not per-event. Performance is not the deciding factor here; readability is.

Discovered during the rf2-ro7e audit.
notes: PR #228 opened
close_reason: Closed

title: Suggestion: thread last-event through router/drain! loop binding instead of an atom (router.cljc:284)
description:
Tentative suggestion.

In `implementation/core/src/re_frame/router.cljc`, `drain!` opens a `(loop [depth 0] ...)` and tracks the most-recently-processed event in a local `last-event-a` atom (line 284). The atom is mutated on every iteration and read once on the depth-exceeded branch to populate the `:last-event` slot of the `:rf.error/drain-depth-exceeded` trace.

The atom expresses a value that is naturally a loop-binding: "the last event we just processed". Possible direction:

- Rewrite as `(loop [depth 0 last-event nil] ...)` and read `last-event` directly in the depth-exceeded branch. The recur in the work branch becomes `(recur (inc depth) (:event envelope))`.

Why this seems worth revisiting:
- Reads more like the algorithm it implements ("track depth and last event together").
- One less allocation per drain (no atom box).
- Loop binding is at least as fast as atom mutate-and-deref on every iteration, so this is unlikely to regress the drain hot path.

Performance note:
- This sits on the drain path (the hottest path in re-frame2). The PR for this refactor should include a before/after sanity check (e.g. `pattern_smoke_test.clj` or a microbenchmark) so the change is demonstrably neutral or positive on perf.

Discovered during the rf2-ro7e audit.
notes: PR #228 opened
close_reason: Closed

title: Suggestion: revisit the imperative fallback JSON parser in http_managed
description:
Tentative suggestion.

In `implementation/http/src/re_frame/http_managed.cljc`, the JVM fallback `json-read*` parser is a correctness-sensitive pure helper, but it currently uses a mutable cursor atom plus nested `while`/`loop` scanners.

Because this parser is only the fallback path when Cheshire is absent, it is not obvious that low-level imperative style is buying enough to outweigh the maintenance cost. Hand-rolled parsers are easy to get subtly wrong, and the current implementation is more imperative than the rest of the namespace.

Possible direction:
- Replace the fallback with a tiny well-tested library path, if dependency policy allows.
- Or keep the no-extra-deps fallback but rework it toward a clearer functional parser shape and add dedicated edge-case fixtures.

Performance note:
- If the current cursor-based parser is materially faster or smaller than the alternatives, keeping it is reasonable.
- In that case, it would be useful to say so in the comment block and back it with a couple of focused parser tests, because the imperative shape otherwise looks accidental.
notes: PR #228 opened
close_reason: Closed

title: Suggestion: revisit mutation-heavy route pattern parsing/building
description:
Tentative suggestion.

In `implementation/routing/src/re_frame/routing.cljc`, the pure path-pattern helpers (`pattern-shape`, `compile-pattern`, `collect-param-names-in-group`, and `route-url`) currently do manual char-by-char scanning with local atoms and nested loops.

That may be fine, but this part of the code now reads like a hand-written mutable parser, and several delimiter-scanning rules are repeated in multiple places. The style makes the grammar harder to extend or audit than a shared functional walker/tokenizer would.

Possible direction:
- Extract one shared segment/group walker and let the ranking, regex compilation, and URL rendering stages consume the same parsed representation.
- Or, if keeping the low-level scanner is important, centralise the repeated scan-to-delimiter logic and add a short comment that the imperative style is intentional.

Performance note:
- The current scanner may well be faster than a more declarative parse tree, especially on route registration and URL generation.
- If so, keeping it is reasonable; the tradeoff would just be worth documenting explicitly so the style choice looks deliberate rather than accidental.
notes: PR #228 opened
close_reason: Closed

title: Suggestion: consider a more functional topo-sort in re-frame.flows
description:
Tentative suggestion.

In `implementation/flows/src/re_frame/flows.cljc`, `topo-sort` is a pure registration-time helper, but it currently threads state through three local atoms (`order`, `ready`, `remaining`) plus a `while` loop.

That works, but it reads more like mutable algorithm code than idiomatic data transformation, and the invariants are a little harder to audit than they need to be.

Possible direction:
- Re-express the Kahn walk as a pure `loop/recur` or reducer that threads `{order ready remaining}` explicitly.
- If the current atom/while shape turns out to be measurably faster on large flow graphs, keep it and document that the imperative style is a deliberate performance tradeoff.

Why this seems worth revisiting:
- This path runs at registration time, not in the hot event-drain path.
- The helper is pure enough that a more functional shape may improve readability and reviewability without changing semantics.
notes: PR #228 opened
close_reason: Closed

title: Audit local-atom-as-scratch-accumulator usage across the codebase; refactor to functional shape where idiomatic
description:
Mike directive 2026-05-11: prefer functional programming over imperative when given the choice; efficiency matters where it's measurable.

Mike spotted an instance at implementation/routing/src/re_frame/routing.cljc:79 (pattern-shape) — three atoms (i, depth, seen) used as scratch accumulators inside a loop. Imperative-flavour code that would read more idiomatically as loop/recur threading state through.

Likely a class issue, not a one-off. Initial grep found 52 files matching the broad pattern (let [... (atom ...) ...] ...) but most are legitimate uses (registry atoms, test fixtures, async queues, anything where state persists or crosses thread boundaries). The smell is specifically: **local atoms used as scratch accumulators inside a tight loop, never escaping the function, where loop/recur or reduce/transducers would express the same algorithm functionally**.

## Audit scope

For each of the 52 files:
- Identify each (atom ...) call site at top of let binding.
- Classify:
  - LEGITIMATE: state genuinely needs to persist beyond the function (registry, fixture, queue), cross a boundary (async, thread), be observable to watchers, or back a public reactive API.
  - SCRATCH-SMELL: local accumulator inside a tight loop or recursive walk, never escapes, no concurrency.
  - HOT-PATH-EFFICIENCY: scratch-shape but materially faster than the functional alternative on a measurable hot path (worth keeping with a one-line rationale comment).

For each SCRATCH-SMELL site, propose a refactor (typically loop/recur threading the accumulator state, or a reduce/reduce-kv form).

## Principle (Mike's directive 2026-05-11)

Prefer functional over imperative. Trade-offs to weigh:
- **Readability**: functional is usually clearer. Win.
- **Aliasing safety**: functional is safer (immutable bindings can't be aliased). Win.
- **Performance**: imperative atom-mutate is sometimes faster on tight loops, but only when the loop is genuinely hot. For 99% of code paths, the difference is invisible.
- **Pre-release**: no back-compat concerns; refactor freely.

Rule: prefer functional unless there's measurable evidence the imperative form is needed for performance. If kept imperative for efficiency reasons, leave a rationale comment.

## Concrete starter

routing.cljc:79 (pattern-shape) — refactor to loop/recur threading [i depth seen]. ~15-line edit.

## Output

- findings/imperative-atoms-audit.md — per-file classification + refactor proposals.
- A bd bead per SCRATCH-SMELL site (or one bead per file, depending on volume).

## Dispatchability

Direction-set. Background agent can do the audit. The refactor work splits into per-bead PRs after Mike reviews the audit findings. Could batch into a single sweep PR if the volume is manageable.
notes:
2026-05-11: Cross-link to pre-existing Codex-filed specific-instance beads. Three sites already surfaced + filed by Codex 2026-05-10:
- rf2-w0am: topo-sort in flows.cljc:65
- rf2-lckc: routing.cljc pattern-shape:79 + compile-pattern:225 + collect-param-names-in-group:404 + route-url:435
- rf2-d6a2: http_managed.cljc json-read*:291

Audit scope: SKIP these three; cover the rest of the 52 files matching the broad pattern. Output findings doc + file follow-up beads ONLY for sites not already covered by rf2-w0am/lckc/d6a2.
2026-05-11: Audit complete. findings/imperative-atoms-audit.md (~2700 words). Filed 3 follow-up beads for SCRATCH-SMELL sites in production source: rf2-8jmn (router.cljc:284 last-event-a — drain hot path), rf2-9hxy (ssr.cljc:584 replace-header seen?), rf2-2fny (epoch.cljc:578 missing-references). Together with the three Codex-filed beads (rf2-w0am, rf2-lckc, rf2-d6a2) the roadmap covers all SCRATCH-SMELL sites in re-frame2: ~6 specific sites across 5 files. No HOT-PATH-EFFICIENCY sites found — every imperative-atom site has a functional alternative at parity. Test files all use atoms as legitimate assertion fixtures; not flagged. Two borderline test-infra sites (conformance_test.clj:188 + :885) noted in the doc but not filed, per Mike's test-bias guidance.

title: Decide: keep or drop source-coord non-DOM-root warning (Stage 4 reagent-slim-and-fast)
description: IMPL-SPEC.md §14.6 + §11.3 noted: the current views.cljs/inject-source-coord-attr uses warn-non-dom-root! to emit a one-shot warning per id when a reg-view returns a non-DOM root (a fn/class component, fragment, etc.). Under the rewrite's renderer-side stamping (§5.4), the equivalent would re-implement the warning inside reagent2.impl.template/as-element. Audit didn't show evidence of the warning triggering in production. Stage 4 decides: keep the warning by re-implementing it in the renderer, or drop it as redundant pair-tooling guidance? Pair-tools (re-frame-pair, re-frame-10x) use :rf/id fallback per Spec 006 §Source-coord annotation; the warning is informational, not load-bearing.
close_reason: Closed

title: Decide flush-views! ordering with React 19 Suspense (Stage 4 reagent-slim-and-fast)
description: IMPL-SPEC.md §14.5 noted: a child component throws a Promise (Suspense pattern); the parent's flush-views! is called from a test. Should the flush wait for the suspended promise to resolve? React 19's act drains Suspense, but the precise sequencing (microtask-microtask vs microtask-then-act vs act-then-microtask) is non-trivial. Stage 4 picks an order and tests it; Stage 3 doesn't pre-commit. Need: this bead captures the design choice + test as Stage 4 makes it.
notes:
RESOLUTION (Stage 4-B, PR #234):

Chosen ordering: microtask -> act(flush!) -> microtask.

Rationale:
- Step 1 (microtask tick): lets any in-flight rea-schedule that's already
  queued run before we hand off to act. Converts pending reactive work
  to React-visible state changes BEFORE act drives React.
- Step 2 (act(flush!)): synchronous drain of the dirty-set + after-render
  queue inside act. React's commit-phase + Suspense Promise resolution
  composes inside act's scope. If a child suspends mid-render, act
  awaits the Promise; on resolution act re-renders the now-ready subtree.
- Step 3 (microtask tick): settles tail-cascade Reactions triggered
  during the React commit (e.g. a useSyncExternalStore subscriber that
  fires during commit and downstream reactions cascade). Without this
  step a test's post-flush-views! assertion could miss the tail value.

Why not microtask-microtask: skips React commit drainage entirely.
Why not act-then-microtask: reactive work already queued pre-flush-views!
gets a chance to run inside act's bounds, but the trailing tail-cascade
outside act isn't drained.
Why not microtask-then-act (no trailing): tail-cascade triggered during
commit goes unobserved by the caller.

Implementation: reagent2.dom.client/flush-views! gates on goog.DEBUG so
:advanced + goog.DEBUG=false DCEs the body to zero. The body looks up
react/act (top-level export under React 18.3+ / 19+); falls back to a
plain synchronous batching/flush! if act is unavailable (e.g. some node
test environments where the React module hasn't been initialised).

Tests pinning the ordering:
  reagent2.dom.client-cljs-test/flush-views-suspense-composition-ordering
  reagent2.dom.client-cljs-test/flush-views-suspense-composition-cascade

The first asserts the OBSERVABLE effect: after flush-views! returns, a
Reaction has recomputed, its component has re-rendered, and the
after-render tail-effect has fired. The second pins the
no-flatten-cascade rule (a render-time mutation queues a fresh turn,
matching React 19's transition semantics per IMPL-SPEC §4.5).

A real-DOM Suspense test (a child component that throws a real Promise
during render) is out of scope for the node-test target — it belongs on
the browser-test target alongside Stage 4-D's hiccup interpreter, where
a real React tree can be mounted.
close_reason: Closed

title: Spec error-boundary test cases for reagent-slim-and-fast Stage 4
description: IMPL-SPEC.md §14.4 noted that React 19's error-boundary contract has subtle edge cases (errors during componentDidMount of a child caught vs errors during render of a sibling not necessarily caught). Stage 1/2 didn't enumerate the exact cases. Stage 4's test coverage in §6.5 needs to enumerate them precisely. Initial test list in §12.1 is a starting point only. Need: file an enumeration spec for the error-boundary cases under React 19 before Stage 4 implements them.
notes:
Closed by Stage 4-C (rf2-6hyy). The error-boundary test enumeration shipped in implementation/adapters/reagent-slim/test/reagent2/impl/component_cljs_test.cljs covers:

  1. error-boundary-component-did-catch-fires — basic plumbing: cDC fires with (this, error, info).
  2. error-boundary-get-derived-state-auto-installed — getDerivedStateFromError auto-installed when :component-did-catch supplied; the default returns `{cljsHasError true}` so React 19 actually re-renders with the boundary state.
  3. error-boundary-no-derived-state-without-did-catch — no silent boundary semantics: a class without :component-did-catch does NOT silently intercept errors.
  4. error-boundary-nested-isolation — inner boundary catches; outer boundary does NOT fire (cascade isolation contract).
  5. error-boundary-during-commit-via-component-did-mount — boundary's cDC receives commit-phase errors with the correct (error, info) shape; the precise React-19 commit-phase wiring (componentDidMount throw → boundary catches) is a real-DOM contract that the Stage 4-D browser-test target covers end-to-end.
  6. error-boundary-rethrow-bubbles-via-cDC — a user :component-did-catch fn that throws cascades; the rewrite plumbing does not swallow user-fn rethrows.

The remaining real-DOM integration test (a child throws during render → React 19 unmounts the subtree → boundary's getDerivedStateFromError flips state → boundary re-renders with fallback) needs a mounted React tree. That test belongs on the browser-test target where Stage 4-D's hiccup-translation pipeline + a real `react-dom/client.createRoot` is wired.

Pinned in IMPL-SPEC §6.5. The 4 unit-test cases pin the contract sufficiently for Stage 4-C; the real-DOM end-to-end ride-along lands with 4-D.
close_reason: Closed

title: Decide: defview macro vs folding Form-detection into reg-view (Stage 4 reagent-slim-and-fast)
description: Stage 3 spec for reagent-slim-and-fast (rf2-60le) flagged this as an open question for Mike. The S3-003 input from Stage 2 specs an optional 'defview' macro that compile-time-classifies Form-1/2/3, sitting alongside the runtime detection in reagent2.impl.component/wrap-render. But re-frame2 already ships reg-view as the canonical view-registration macro. Two paths: (a) ship 'defview' as a Reagent-flavoured user surface separate from reg-view; (b) fold the compile-time classification into reg-view's expansion (so users get the perf hint for free with no new macro to learn). Stage 3 IMPL-SPEC §14.1 recommends (b). Mike's call needed before Stage 4-B.
close_reason: Closed

title: Design-rationale doc for reagent-slim-and-fast (why these decisions made it slimmer)
description:
Per Mike's directive 2026-05-11: ship a design-rationale document for reagent-slim-and-fast that explains each design decision and how it contributes to a slimmer / faster Reagent.

Audience: adopters evaluating slim-and-fast vs reagent-classic. Distinct from:
- IMPL-SPEC.md (Stage 3, rf2-60le) — for implementers; what was built + how
- FORM-3.md (rf2-pe4u) — Form-3 cap specifics + worked examples (Google Maps, SpreadJS)

Coverage (each section: decision + rationale + slim/fast impact + migration note):

1. React 19 floor (D5). reagent.dom removed; pre-React-18 surfaces gone. Bundle/runtime savings + clean concurrent-mode story.

2. Re-com-scoping directive. Surface = re-com ∪ re-frame2 usage. Surfaces outside intersection don't ship — empirically validated by rf2-cgcv + rf2-kfpf audits (zero usage of with-let, cursor, track, wrap, rswap!, partial, merge-props, unsafe-html, adapt-react-class, reactify-component, create-element, after-render, next-tick, flush, force-update, dom-node, class-names, is-client, ratom/run! across re-com, 10x, Dash8, rf8). Massive surface-area shrink.

3. 7-key Form-3 cap (D3). Cap: did-mount, will-unmount, did-update, reagent-render, display-name, get-snapshot-before-update, did-catch. Other keys throw. Empirically the exact set used by re-com / 10x / Dash8 / rf8.

4. Narrowed convert-prop-value (D2 — option iii). HTML-attribute prop names only; non-HTML props pass keywords through. Removes rf2-d4sf root cause; retires coerce-context-value defensive workaround.

5. No reagent.dom shipped (forced by React 19). Throw-on-call shims for the legacy mount path with migration messages.

6. SSR split (D6). render-to-string NOT shipped (SSR seam at day8/re-frame-2-ssr is canonical for hydrate-able SSR). render-to-static-markup IS shipped (Dash8/rf8 use it for offline HTML report export — no hydration involved).

7. Compat namespace (D4 outcome — TBD). Reagent-shape compat surface in  (or whatever ns settles); apps not using it don't pay for it.

8. CAN-DEPRECATE warning stubs (D7 outcome — TBD). Class A vs Class B split per React-19 reality.

Each section: decision → why → estimated bundle/runtime impact → migration path for affected users.

Closing section: 'How this compares to reagent-classic' table. Side-by-side surface area comparison; bundle size delta; runtime perf delta (filled in once Stage 2 measures).

Natural home: implementation/adapters/reagent-slim-and-fast/DESIGN-RATIONALE.md (alongside IMPL-SPEC.md). Cross-link from MIGRATION.md, the rewrite's README, and the day8.com blog when the rewrite ships.

Blocked-by Stage 2 (rf2-142b — efficiency analysis fills in the bundle/perf numbers) and Stage 3 (rf2-60le — final implementation decisions land). Can draft against Stage 1 immediately; Stage 2/3 land the quantitative content.
notes:
PR #225 opened: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/225

Branched from reagent-slim-and-fast-impl-spec (PR #222) so DESIGN-RATIONALE.md lands alongside IMPL-SPEC.md in implementation/adapters/reagent-slim/. PR opens against main; rebases naturally when PR #222 merges.

Doc length: 4,647 words (in the 4,000-6,000 target). Twelve sections covering Stage 1's eight decisions framed as Decision -> Why -> Slim impact -> Re-frame2-fit impact -> Migration note, plus the three drivers (slim, React-19-native, re-frame2-fit), native re-frame2 integration, quantified expectations, side-by-side comparison.

Cites: Stage 1 (rf2-ui6g), Stage 2 (rf2-142b), rf2-cgcv audit, rf2-kfpf audit, IMPL-SPEC.md, FORM-3.md (future, rf2-pe4u).
close_reason: Stale in_progress cleanup — fix commits on origin/main per git log sweep 2026-05-13. Bead status was never flipped after PR merge.

title: Document the 7-key Form-3 cap for reagent-slim-and-fast
description:
Per rf2-5djt D3 (resolved 2026-05-11), the rewrite ships a 7-key Form-3 cap. Cap: :component-did-mount, :component-will-unmount, :component-did-update, :reagent-render, :display-name, :get-snapshot-before-update, :component-did-catch. Out-of-cap keys throw :rf.error/create-class-key-unsupported.

This needs documentation. Suggested coverage:

1. The cap itself — what works, what throws, why these 7 (empirical: re-com 5 + 10x +1 + Dash8/rf8 +1; zero usage of UNSAFE_/legacy keys across all 4 codebases).

2. Migration FROM stock Reagent — apps using out-of-cap keys (UNSAFE_component-will-mount, etc.) need to adapt. The throw is the migration trigger; per-key migration recipes.

3. The Google Maps / SpreadJS pattern — canonical worked example for imperative DOM library integration. Form-3 + :ref callback (since r/dom-node is gone in React 19) + closure-captured atom for instance + four lifecycle keys. ~30-line template.

4. Why Form-3 stays — explicit rationale for keeping Form-3 in 'slim and fast': error boundaries (:component-did-catch) and pre-commit hooks (:get-snapshot-before-update) have NO Form-1/Form-2 equivalent. Imperative DOM library integration in Reagent shape genuinely needs Form-3 (Options A-C in conversation explored alternatives; only D — switching to UIx/Helix — works without Form-3).

5. When NOT to use Form-3 — re-frame2's :on-create / :on-destroy events for app-state side effects; :ref callbacks for DOM access in Form-1.

Natural home: implementation/adapters/reagent-slim-and-fast/docs/FORM-3.md (or similar). Cross-link from MIGRATION.md.

Blocked-by Stage 4 (rf2-6hyy) — docs reference the actual implementation.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Mike Thompson
comments.text: PR opened: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/255 — adds implementation/adapters/reagent-slim/FORM-3.md (2983 words). Covers all five suggested areas: the cap + throw, migration recipes, Google Maps + Vega-Embed worked examples, why Form-3 stays (:component-did-catch + :get-snapshot-before-update have no Form-1 substitute), and when NOT to use it (steers to frame :on-create / :ref / subscribe idioms). Cross-references IMPL-SPEC §6, DESIGN-RATIONALE §4, and the two audits (rf2-cgcv, rf2-kfpf).

title: re-frame-pair2: streaming subscription channel for trace + epoch events
description:
Today watch-epochs polls (the 'pull-mode only' state per pair2's STATUS.md). Streaming via nREPL :out (or sidecar WebSocket) lets Claude observe events as they fire, no polling.

## Scope

- pair2 runtime registers a trace-cb / epoch-cb that pushes events to a stream.
- MCP server receives the stream and surfaces events to Claude as notifications (or as readable lines from a long-running tool call).
- Claude consumes the stream via Monitor-tool-shaped reads (one event per line).
- Replaces the current polling loop in watch-epochs.sh.

## Disposition

P2-P3 — the most architecturally involved of the four sub-beads. Composes with rf2-5b8e (MCP holds the stream) but is the largest piece individually. Blocked-by rf2-5b8e + ideally also rf2-???-snapshot (so streamed events join cleanly with snapshot reads of state).
close_reason: PRs merged 2026-05-13 batch landing

title: Cross-Spec Interactions could reconcile its status vocabulary with the entries it now contains
description:
Suggestion to consider:

`spec/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md` defines a two-state status model (`Pinned` and `Provisional`), but the document now contains a third status marker (`Locked`) without explaining it.

Evidence:
- `spec/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md:18-29` says each interaction's status is either `Pinned` or `Provisional`, and the status legend only defines those two markers.
- `spec/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md:223` marks interaction 21 as `Locked`.
- `spec/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md:29` also describes the corpus state as "The remaining 13 stay Provisional," which only makes sense if the `Locked` entry is treated as outside the documented model.

Why it matters:
This is a small but visible clarity leak in one of the corpus's orientation docs. A reader cannot tell whether `Locked` means "normatively settled but not fixture-backed," "not a cross-spec interaction in the same sense," or simply a doc-only note.

Suggested improvement:
- Either add `Locked` to the status legend with a precise meaning, or restate interaction 21 using the existing vocabulary.
- If interaction 21 is intentionally a family-note rather than a fixture-trackable interaction, consider moving it to a short appendix so the main status model stays clean.
notes: Resolved by https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/248. Added Locked to the canonical status legend in spec/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md with semantics matching how interaction 21 actually uses it (a normatively-settled, family-level architectural rule owned by the cited Spec, with no run-time scenario for a fixture to capture). The 'remaining 13 stay Provisional' count was already consistent with treating Locked as outside the fixture-trackable model (7 Pinned + 1 Locked + 13 Provisional = 21) — the corpus-state paragraph now mentions the Locked entry explicitly.
close_reason: Closed

title: Refresh spec index and AI audit scaffolding after Spec 014 landed
description:
Suggestion to consider:

The corpus-navigation docs still describe the numbered spec set as if it ends at 013, and the AI audit still scopes itself to the pre-014 corpus. That makes the spec's own scaffolding look older than the normative set it is supposed to help readers navigate.

Evidence:
- `spec/README.md:12` says the contract is defined by numbered Specs `(000–013)` even though the same file now lists `014-HTTPRequests.md` as a capability spec.
- `spec/README.md:23` says "The numbering 000–013 has one gap" while the actual numbered set in the directory is 000–014 with 003 reserved.
- `spec/AI-Audit.md:4` scopes the audit to numbered Specs `(000–013)`.
- `spec/AI-Audit.md:9-12` and `spec/AI-Audit.md:51+` omit a stand-alone scoring pass for Spec 014, and the audit's per-Spec coverage is also still selective in a way that no longer matches the current corpus shape.

Why it matters:
These docs are the reader's orientation layer. When they lag the live spec set, it raises the question of whether 014 and the newer companion material have actually been integrated into the corpus-thinking or merely appended.

Suggested improvement:
- Sweep the index/audit scaffolding docs so they describe the live numbered set consistently.
- Either add Spec 014 to the AI audit properly, or explicitly mark the audit as historical / partial until a re-audit lands.
- If selective per-Spec scoring remains intentional, state that policy plainly rather than implying whole-corpus coverage.
notes: Resolved by https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/248. spec/README.md (bucket-axis line + About-Spec-003) and spec/AI-Audit.md (header scope) now describe the numbered set as 000-014. AI-Audit's Scope section now lists 013 and 014 alongside 001/006/007 as indirectly-graded; added a Coverage policy paragraph stating plainly that Per-Spec scoring is selective and lags the live numbered set by design.
close_reason: Closed

title: Independent research + refinement of re-frame-2-story SOTA features (compares vs rf2-m6tu)
description:
Follow-up to rf2-m6tu. Different framing: 'what features would make re-frame-2-story state-of-the-art?' rather than 'what should it minimally support?'

Two-phase work for one agent:

## Phase 1 — Independent research (no peeking at rf2-m6tu)

Agent does its own survey of component-development tools focused on the SOTA question:
- What's the highest-bar capability set? Not 'minimum viable.'
- Which tools (Storybook + non-Storybook) ship features that feel best-in-class?
- Cross-tool: what's been borrowed, refined, surpassed?
- Storybook addons (https://storybook.js.org/addons) — which are state-of-the-art? a11y? play / interactions / test? composition? designs? viewport with breakpoints? args + controls type-generation?
- Beyond Storybook: Histoire's vue-specific magic, Ladle's perf claims, React Cosmos's fixture model, Bit's component-as-package, Lookbook's Rails-flavoured ergonomics.
- The answer should reach for SOTA, not the average.

Agent writes its independent answer to a scratch file: findings/re-frame-2-story-sota-draft.md (or holds in agent memory).

## Phase 2 — Comparison + refinement

After own answer is written:
- Read findings/re-frame-2-story-feature-set.md (the rf2-m6tu output).
- Compare:
  - Where do they agree? (Confidence: both reached the same conclusion independently.)
  - Where does rf2-m6tu under-claim vs SOTA? (Add to refined doc.)
  - Where does rf2-m6tu over-claim vs realistic? (Question.)
  - Where does the independent SOTA framing surface features rf2-m6tu missed entirely?
- Refine the rf2-m6tu document in place: findings/re-frame-2-story-feature-set.md gets the synthesis.
- Drop the scratch draft after merging if used.

The refined doc should:
- Carry forward rf2-m6tu's organisation (don't restructure for its own sake).
- Add SOTA features the original missed.
- Surface tensions where SOTA-pursuit conflicts with re-frame2's ethos (e.g. 'Storybook composition is SOTA but conflicts with our spec-first / single-source-of-truth ethos — recommend skip').
- Preserve attribution: 'Refined 2026-MM-DD against independent SOTA research; the following sections were strengthened: ...'

## Why two-phase

Pre-loading the agent with rf2-m6tu's findings biases the research. Mike wants the independent eyes first — different agent, different framing prompt, no shared context — to surface SOTA features the original missed.

Blocked-by rf2-m6tu (can't compare to a doc that doesn't exist yet).
notes: findings/re-frame-2-story-sota-refinement.md (5324 words). Phase discipline maintained — Phase 1 written before reading rf2-m6tu. CONVERGENCE: both passes agree on 12 settled ship items including (1) MCP server + manifest, (2) EDN-first variants + :rf.assert/* vocabulary, (3) embed re-frame-10x epoch panel. Phase 1 ADDITIONS: in-canvas perf ribbon, mode primitive (theme×viewport×locale tuples with per-cell snapshot identity), layout-debug overlay trio. DISAGREEMENTS: rf2-m6tu wins on 10x embed (vs Phase 1's custom scrubber), pure-EDN variants (vs CSF Factories), federation deferral (Phase 1 over-weighted).

title: Research Storybook + JS-world component-dev tools to design re-frame-2-story artefact feature set
description:
Research-only. Survey Storybook, Histoire, Ladle, React Cosmos, Lookbook, devcards, Day8's workspaces (re-frame-7), Pattern Lab, etc. Identify the most-appreciated advanced features across them. Produce a feature spec for a new day8/re-frame-2-story artefact that adopts best practice + the most valued advanced features, while honouring re-frame2's ethos (spec-first / AI-implementable / frame-as-actor-system-boundary / trace-as-first-class-output / Tool-Pair integration / multi-substrate / production-elision / strict additive contract).

Output: findings/re-frame-2-story-feature-set.md.

Note: spec/007-Stories.md already exists — read first for context. May already define the surface partially; this research extends with cross-tool best-practice.
notes: findings/re-frame-2-story-feature-set.md (4842 words). Top-3 SOTA features: (1) Storybook MCP/agent integration via re-frame2's queryable schemas/tags/assertions; (2) devcards-style atom time-travel scrubber via epoch-history + restore-epoch; (3) Nubank/workspaces grid + transit-shareable layouts. 7 architectural calls flagged for Mike (MCP placement / substrate handling / assert failure mode / loader completion / workspace persistence / DCE policy / 10x integration model). rf2-94b0 is the follow-up independent re-survey + comparison.

title: Guide ch.04 could narrow its scope to views and frames, then spin supporting topics out
description:
Suggestion to consider:

Chapter 04 has a strong core subject, but it currently carries several adjacent topics that dilute the through-line.

Evidence:
- docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md:87-93 includes a significant implementation caveat about frame propagation.
- docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md:206-223 introduces source-coordinate DOM annotations.
- docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md:225-245 shifts into general app-db shape guidance.
- docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md:247-267 introduces flows.
- docs/guide/04-views-and-frames.md:269-273 previews routing in detail even though routing has its own dedicated chapter 12.

Why it matters:
A chapter named "Views and frames" sets an expectation of a tight narrative around rendering, registration, frame scoping, and multi-frame mental models. The later sections broaden into app architecture and feature-specific topics, which makes the chapter feel heavier than its title promises.

Suggested improvement:
- Keep the chapter tightly focused on view registration, frame creation, frame targeting, and frame-provider composition.
- Move or shorten the app-db, flows, and routing preview sections unless they are directly needed for the chapter's example.
- If those topics stay, present them as short forward pointers so the main narrative remains about views and frames.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/251 — ch.04 tightened around views+frames through-line. Source-coord section spun out to ch.11 (forward-link). Frame-propagation caveat trimmed. app-db / flows / routing minimised in place; follow-up rf2-q2x0 filed for the eventual fold into ch.03. Heading anchor for flows preserved (ch.08 link).
close_reason: Closed

title: Guide ch.02 could defer substrate/package detail until after the first runnable example
description:
Suggestion to consider:

Chapter 02 is supposed to be the first practical walkthrough, but it front-loads adapter selection and Maven artefact detail before the reader sees the counter itself.

Evidence:
- docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md:7-38 spends the opening section on substrate choice, package coordinates, and the feature-opt-in artefact story.
- The actual app goal does not appear until docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md:40.
- The first complete code sample does not appear until docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md:46.
- The chapter already has a later callout for real-world mount wiring at docs/guide/02-your-first-app.md:216-230, which suggests some of the packaging/adapter context can also be handled as a focused sidebar rather than as the opening gate.

Why it matters:
The current structure increases cognitive load before the reader has any concrete picture of the re-frame2 loop. For a first-app chapter, the golden path is easier to learn if the first thing the reader sees is a runnable, single-substrate example.

Suggested improvement:
- Start with one canonical stack (Reagent) and get to the counter sooner.
- Move UIx/Helix and artefact-selection detail into a shorter sidebar, appendix, or end-of-chapter "same pattern on other adapters" section.
- Keep the explicit init!/adapter notes, but treat them as support for the example rather than the chapter's front door.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/251 — ch.02 reordered to lead with Reagent counter; adapter/Maven detail moved to 'Same pattern on other adapters' appendix.
close_reason: Closed

title: Guide README could separate the core learning path from optional deep dives
description:
Suggestion to consider:

The guide currently presents one flat chapter sequence in docs/guide/README.md, but the later chapters mix different reader intents: migration (08), optional long-form philosophy (09), practical testing/devtools material (10-11), and routing (12).

Evidence:
- docs/guide/README.md:20-24 lists chapters 08-12 as one continuous path.
- docs/guide/README.md:28 already hints at alternate behaviour ("If you're impatient, read 01 and skip to 02").
- docs/guide/09-the-dynamic-model.md:6 explicitly says chapter 09 is skippable for readers who just want to write code.
- docs/guide/10-testing.md:425-428 and docs/guide/11-devtools-and-pair-tools.md:433-437 continue the linear "Next" chain even though those chapters are better treated as practical reference material for many readers.

Why it matters:
New readers do not get a clearly signposted "core path" through the guide. The current order makes the practical onboarding story compete with optional essays and later reference material.

Suggested improvement:
- Reframe README around 2-3 tracks, for example: core onboarding, migration/reference, and deeper system/philosophy.
- Update chapter "Next" sections to support those tracks instead of implying every reader should proceed linearly through 08 -> 09 -> 10 -> 11 -> 12.
- At minimum, add explicit jump guidance for readers who need routing/testing before they need the long-form essay.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/251 — README restructured into core path (01-07) + optional deep dives (08-12). Chapter filenames unchanged.
close_reason: Closed

title: Remove stale implementation/substrates README or relocate it under adapters
description:
Why this issue exists:
The worktree currently contains an untracked implementation/substrates/README.md even though the implementation has already standardized on implementation/adapters/ as the canonical location and terminology. Keeping a substrates/ doc around reintroduces the old name, duplicates the packaging explanation already captured in implementation/README.md, and its relative links (reagent/, uix/, helix/) are broken from the implementation/substrates/ directory because those subdirectories do not exist there.

What needs to be done:
- Decide whether this content should live at implementation/adapters/README.md or be dropped entirely in favor of the existing top-level implementation README.
- If it is kept, move it to the canonical directory and fix its relative links.
- Remove the stale substrates/ path so the repo does not present both names for the same concept.

Notes:
This is documentation/package-structure drift rather than runtime behavior, but it directly affects discoverability and makes the implementation layout look less settled than it actually is.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #187. README relocated to implementation/adapters/README.md.

title: Rewrite findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md after spec 005 gap beads complete
description: When the spec 005 gap beads filed from this doc all complete, rewrite findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md to: (a) reflect the now-canonical spec 005 idiom (use :invoke-all per rf2-6vmw, use :timeout-ms per rf2-1lop, use the cancellation cascade per rf2-wvkn, optionally invoke :rf.http/managed as a machine per rf2-ijm7); (b) drop the 'spec 005 gaps' / mismatch sections — those gaps are closed; (c) keep the Dash8 + rf8 boot diagrams + auth-machine sketch as the canonical worked example showing best-practice idiom; (d) cross-link the canonical retry-ownership pattern (rf2-zg4g decision (c) hybrid). Result: a rewritten doc that's pure pedagogical guide to boot-as-state-machine, not a gap-analysis. Blocked-by rf2-6vmw (just merged via PR #185), rf2-wvkn, rf2-1lop, rf2-ijm7 — when all complete, dispatch the rewrite. rf2-zg4g already closed.
close_reason: Doc removed 2026-05-11. Spec edits: spec/014-HTTPRequests.md §Retry — clarify :retry is for transport-level only (function-of-attempt-count policies); explicit guidance to escape to state-machine retry for response-conditional / state-conditional retry. spec/Pattern-Boot.md (or relevant Pattern doc) — show the auth-machine pattern from findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md as the canonical 'semantic retry via machine' example (5xx-retry-with-backoff, 401-fatal, refresh-vs-init distinction). spec/Pattern-AsyncEffect.md (if exists) — cross-reference the boundary. spec/005 §Cross-spec interactions — cross-link. Disposition: P3. Blocked-by rf2-6y3q (HTTP middleware bead in flight, spec/014 hot zone) and rf2-6vmw (spec/005 hot zone).

Resolves rf2-zg4g.

title: Spec 014 gap: per-frame request middleware (Bearer auth header, instrumentation, common request transforms)
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md L272 — Dash8's :configuring phase fetches with Bearer token via custom XHRIO wrappers. Both Dash8 and rf8 have to manually attach Authorization headers per-request. spec 014 :rf.http/managed has no middleware concept. Direction: add per-frame request middleware chain — (rf/reg-http-middleware frame-id name (fn [req] (assoc-in req [:headers :authorization] ...))). Each middleware fn transforms the request map before dispatch; fires in registration order. Useful for: Bearer auth (Dash8/rf8 both need this), CSRF token, request-correlation-id, instrumentation/tracing, base-URL rewriting in dev. Concrete shape options: (a) fn-list per frame; (b) interceptor-chain matching re-frame2 event-handler shape (composes with what authors already know); (c) :rf.http/middleware reg-fx that wraps requests structurally. (b) most likely natural fit. Spec 014 §Middleware new section. Disposition: P3 — non-gating but Day8 apps demonstrate it's load-bearing.

title: Spec 014 gap: :rf.http/managed not invokable as a child machine (machine-shape surface for :invoke integration)
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md auth-machine sketch L312: the proposed boot pattern shows machines invoking :rf.http/managed AS A CHILD MACHINE — '{:invoke {:machine-id :rf.http/managed :data ...}}' — and observing :succeeded / :failed terminal events. Today :rf.http/managed is an fx, not a machine. To use it from a state machine you must (a) emit the fx as part of an :entry action, (b) route :on-success / :on-failure events back to the machine, (c) manually thread request correlation. Verbose and indirect. The findings sketch shows the cleaner shape: :rf.http/managed registered as a machine whose terminal states are :succeeded / :failed (with response data attached); machines compose via :invoke. Direction set if we want machines as the canonical async-orchestration shape: register a thin machine-flavoured wrapper around :rf.http/managed exposing :requesting → :succeeded / :failed transitions plus the response payload as data on terminal entry. Spec 014 + spec 005 cross-spec interaction; spec/Conventions §Per-feature substrate clearly. Disposition: P3. Boot-as-state-machine is the flagship pattern; not gating beta but landed cleanly here, machines-as-orchestration becomes the canonical async-effect shape.
close_reason: Already implemented and merged via PR #260 (https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/260). The wrapper machine is registered in implementation/http/src/re_frame/http_managed.cljc (machine-shape wrapper section, around L1628). Spec 014 has the §Machine-shape wrapper section. JVM + CLJS tests in implementation/http/test/. The bead's OPEN status was stale — closing now.

title: Decision: should the boot machine OWN per-bucket retry, or delegate to :rf.http/managed?
description: From findings/boot-as-statemachine-dash8-rf8.md top-open-question. Dash8 auth has business logic (5xx-only retry, 401-fatal, refresh-vs-init distinction) that fits a custom state machine. Non-auth fetches fit :rf.http/managed :retry cleanly. Mike must rule on canonical guidance for spec 005 + spec 014. Options: (a) Always delegate to :rf.http/managed — one source of truth for retry; (b) Always state-machine — :rf.http/managed dumb, machine wraps with retry transitions; (c) Hybrid — managed-HTTP for transport-level retry (network errors, 5xx), state machines for semantic retry (401 fresh-token, business-rule fallbacks). Affects Pattern-AsyncEffect.md, Pattern-Boot.md, every author's mental model, plus the M2 (HTTP cancellation) bead's framing. Disposition: P3 design call; not gating beta but shapes canonical patterns going forward.
notes:
---

## Decision (Mike, 2026-05-10): Option (c) — Hybrid

`:rf.http/managed` owns transport-level retry. State machines own semantic retry.

The teachable boundary: if retry policy is a **function of attempt count** (5xx → retry up to N times with backoff), it's managed-HTTP. If it's a **function of prior responses + business state** (401 → refresh token → re-init; multi-tenant fallback URL chain; etc.), it's a state machine.

## Implementation

Tracked under separate impl bead — see linked.
close_reason: Decision (c) Hybrid recorded in notes; impl tracked under separate bead.

title: Interactive playground / tutorial for re-frame2 (inspired by Svelte's tutorial) — viable with self-hosted ClojureScript?
description:
Mike (2026-05-10): Svelte's tutorial at https://svelte.dev/tutorial is a brilliant interactive learning experience — edit code on the left, see live output on the right, compiled in the browser. Would love to create something like that for re-frame2.

## The question

Is self-hosted ClojureScript complete enough to power this?

## Background — what's needed

A Svelte-tutorial-like experience needs:
- In-browser code editing (Monaco / CodeMirror).
- In-browser compilation of user-edited code.
- Live execution into a sandboxed re-frame2 runtime.
- Tutorial step navigation with progressive concept reveal.

## Options — evaluate

### A. Self-hosted ClojureScript (cljs.js bootstrap)

CLJS can compile itself in-browser via cljs.js. Used by Klipse, Lambda Island, etc.

Open questions to research:
- Does Reagent work under self-hosted CLJS today? (Klipse demos suggest yes.)
- Does re-frame2's macro layer (reg-event-fx, reg-machine, reg-view, source-coord stamping) survive self-hosted bootstrap?
- Bundle size of cljs.js + Reagent + re-frame2 — is it acceptable for a tutorial site? (Likely 1-3MB minified.)
- First-compile latency — typical 'hello world' compile time in self-hosted CLJS is sub-second; complex deps slower.
- Macro packaging — re-frame2's macros need to ship as analyser metadata (cljs.analyzer) for self-hosted to expand them.
- Does the macro-rich code re-frame2 emits (reg-machine source-coord stamping, reg-view source-coord stamping) introspect okay under self-hosted?

### B. Klipse

Pre-built self-hosted CLJS playground. Mature. Used by lambdaisland.com and others. Could be embedded.

Open questions:
- Does Klipse handle re-frame2's adapter init (rf/init! adapter)?
- Per-step state isolation — Klipse cells share global state by default; may need adaptation.

### C. Pre-compiled snippets per step

Each tutorial step is a separately-compiled bundle. User clicks 'Next', the right-pane swaps to the next pre-compiled bundle. No in-browser compilation.

Pros: no self-hosted CLJS dep; bundle-per-step is small.
Cons: user can't edit code, defeating the Svelte-tutorial inspiration.

### D. Hybrid — pre-compiled lessons + Klipse for the user-edits-code part

Lesson narrative + hard-coded examples are pre-compiled; the 'try it' boxes are Klipse-powered. Best of both.

### E. Squint (CLJS-flavour to native JS)

https://github.com/squint-cljs/squint — fast in-browser compile. But: not full ClojureScript (subset). Macros work differently. Re-frame2's macro-heavy registration would likely need adaptation. Worth surveying.

## Likely answer

Self-hosted CLJS is viable but slow + large for a polished tutorial. A hybrid (lessons pre-compiled, Klipse for try-it boxes) is the pragmatic shape. Worth a research pass to confirm Reagent + re-frame2's macros work under self-hosted before investing.

## Disposition

P3 — post-beta. Outreach / DX investment, not pre-1.0 blocker. But the answer to 'self-hosted CLJS works?' affects what tooling we can ship in the guide chapters too (interactive snippets in docs/guide/).

## Recommended next step

A small spike: build one tutorial step ('your first counter') using self-hosted CLJS or Klipse, see if it works end-to-end. ~1 day of investigation. File as a separate bead when ready to spike.
notes: Findings landed: findings/svelte-style-playground-feasibility.md (~3447 words). Top recommendation: Scittle (SCI in browser) + real re-frame2 + Reagent v2 — drops in via the same sci/copy-ns pattern Scittle uses for re-frame 1.3.0. Bundle ~280 KB gzipped, no compile step. Effort 3-5 weeks for MVP. Timing post-1.0. Surprise: Klipse in soft maintenance (last release Jan 2022); Squint disqualified by mutable-JS data model breaking Reagent RAtom protocol; Replicant has its own Scittle plugin. Agent suggested 3 follow-up beads (verify re-frame2 macros survive SCI; upstream-vs-vendor for Reagent v2; slim trace ribbon vs full 10x embed) — Mike to file if interested. Note: agent's worktree landed in re-frame v1's worktrees/ dir (isolation:worktree mis-rooted again); findings doc was manually copied into re-frame2/findings/.

title: declares-3-arity? misclassifies CLJS variadic 2-plus-rest fns as 3-arity
description: From rf2-1e0n test work (PR #156). On CLJS, '(fn [data event & rest] ...)' mis-classifies as 3-arity in 'declares-3-arity?' (machines.cljc) — the CLJS check '(or (= 3 (.-length f)) (some? (unchecked-get f "cljs")))' returns true for compiled 2-plus-rest fns because the latter property is set on variadic fns. Result: user's '& rest' receives '(ctx)' rather than being empty. JVM matches the docstring at machines.cljc:88-90 (returns false for 2-plus-rest). Behaviour-vs-docstring divergence. Tests in PR #156 pin actual current behaviour per platform. Fix likely needs a smarter CLJS check that distinguishes a true 3-arity dispatch entry from variadic-with-arity-3-property. Also flagged: live initial-snapshot synthesis (machines.cljc:808) doesn't propagate spec-level ':meta' into the snapshot — minor secondary issue. P3 — not user-facing for the canonical 2-arity guards/actions, but the documented escape hatch behaves differently across platforms.
close_reason: Resolved via PR #159 — declares-3-arity? CLJS check distinguishes variadic-2-plus-rest fns (commits 4a1d42d, db89594).

title: Suggestion: review browser-harness coverage gaps for documented cross-spec interactions
description:
Suggestion for further agent review on suitability and possible action.

Review finding:
Several documented cross-spec interactions are still represented by placeholders or partial TODO coverage in the CLJS test suite, especially where a real browser / DOM / timer harness is required. Current examples include the SSR :after no-op end-to-end path, frame-destroy-during-render, plain-function warnings under non-default frames, and fuller server error-projection coverage.

Observed surface:
- implementation/reagent/test/re_frame/cross_spec_cljs_test.cljs contains placeholders / TODO notes pointing at f2-443l.

Why this may merit action:
These are spec-documented behaviours without full CI enforcement in the browser harness, so regressions in those paths may go undetected.

Suggested review questions:
- Which of the remaining placeholder cases are truly in scope for the current browser-test runner?
- Should these be split into smaller issues by interaction, or handled as one harness-completion pass?
- Is f2-443l still the right umbrella, or should the missing cases be tracked more granularly?

Possible action, if the review agrees:
Replace the placeholders with browser-test coverage where feasible, or explicitly narrow the documented contract and track the remaining cases with concrete sub-issues.
notes:
PR #243 — https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/243

The remaining cross-spec coverage gaps the bead anticipated (':after' no-op, frame-destroy-during-render, plain-fn warning under non-default frame) are all live coverage today — see findings/browser-harness-coverage-audit.md for the per-interaction audit.

title: Conventions reserved-name table inconsistencies: :rf.machine vs :rf/machine sub-id, :rf/url-changed vs :rf.route/url-changed, dangling cross-refs
description:
Audit rf2-smpt findings F1, F3, F15, F22 — bundled because they all describe the same root cause: Conventions' tables and cross-references contain dangling/inconsistent reserved-name entries that have not been swept against their citation graph or against the per-feature specs that own each underlying decision.

Note: F2 (:destroy-machine row), F5 (:rf/pending-navigation row), F6/F11 (error-id grammar), F18 (collision-audit cross-ref) — confirmed merged via PR #141 and skipped per task brief.

F22 — :rf.machine vs :rf/machine sub-id (audit-conventions table line 74): 'Conventions.md §Reserved namespaces table — :rf.machine/* row (line 22): "machine framework subs ([:rf.machine <id>])". Note :rf.machine (no slash, no kw-namespace separator). 005-StateMachines.md:1453 registers the sub as :rf/machine (line 1453 of 005 — (rf/reg-sub :rf/machine ...)). API.md:61 says (rf/sub-machine machine-id) is "Sugar over (subscribe [:rf/machine machine-id])". So the sub-id is :rf/machine (single-segment) not :rf.machine (sub-namespace). Conventions' table cell is inconsistent with 005 and API.md.'

F15 — :rf/url-changed vs :rf.route/url-changed (audit-conventions table line 67): 'These two events are documented in API.md (lines 102-103) as :rf/url-changed / :rf/url-requested, AND :rf.route/handle-url-change. But Conventions's §Reserved namespaces table (:rf.route/* row, line 24) lists :rf.route/url-changed (with the dot). One of these is wrong — either the event is :rf/url-changed (matching the :rf/* "pattern-level event" row) or :rf.route/url-changed (matching the routing sub-namespace row). API.md and the substrate-adapter section say :rf/url-changed; the Reserved-namespace table says :rf.route/url-changed.'

F1 — "Conventions.md §Reading machines" doesn't exist (audit-conventions line 53): '005-StateMachines.md:1446 cites "Conventions.md §Reading machines" — the section does not exist. Dead reference.'

F3 — :story.* / :Workspace.* prefixes claimed reserved but absent from table (audit-conventions line 55): '007-Stories.md:39: "The :story.<...> and :Workspace.<...> prefixes are reserved (see Conventions §Reserved namespaces)". Neither :story.* nor :Workspace.* appears in the reserved-namespace table. The table opens with "re-frame2 reserves one root keyword namespace for framework-owned ids: :rf/*" — :story and :Workspace violate that single-root claim if reserved at all. Either 007's claim is wrong, or Conventions' single-root invariant has two undocumented exceptions.'

Cross-spec inconsistency per audit-criterion #2 + dangling references. Resolution per audit R-S6/R-S7/R-S1/R-M1 — small fixes per item; coordinated sweep recommended.
notes: 2026-05-09 bookkeeping: PR #164 (commit d701079) merged the dangling-references portion only — F1 (Conventions §Reading machines), and the dead-cross-ref entries. Naming-inconsistency portion (F22 :rf.machine vs :rf/machine sub-id, F15 :rf/url-changed vs :rf.route/url-changed, F3 :story.* / :Workspace.* prefix table inconsistency) remains OPEN pending rf2-ljw6 resolution — those are corpus-wide naming decisions that the wider :rf/route vs :route bead will determine. Leaving open.
