title: Drop h macro — sweep call sites, remove from Spec 004, MIGRATION (resolves rf2-n4um)
description:
Per rf2-n4um Option C: drop the h macro entirely. Late-binding via (rf/view :id) is the canonical handle for the cases h was solving.

## Removals

- implementation/src/re_frame/views_macros.clj (or .cljc) — delete the h macro definition
- implementation/src/re_frame/core.cljc — remove h from public exports / re-exports
- Any helper fns the h macro relies on (only if no longer used elsewhere — grep before deleting)

## Sweep

grep -rn '(h \[\|(h\s' implementation/ examples/ docs/ — find every h call site.

For each:
- Plain hiccup with namespaced view keyword: convert to Var-ref form
  (h [:div [:my-app/widget arg]]) -> [:div [my-app/widget arg]]
- Cases that genuinely needed late-binding (id-based lookup): convert to (rf/view :id)
  (h [:my-app/widget arg]) -> [(rf/view :my-app/widget) arg]
- HTML-only h calls: drop the h wrapper entirely; the inner hiccup is unchanged
  (h [:div [:p ...]]) -> [:div [:p ...]]

Most call sites are likely the first form (Var-ref conversion). The (rf/view ...) substitution is rare — only where the user genuinely wants registry-id lookup at render time.

If a call site is ambiguous (the user's intent isn't clear from context), prefer Var-ref unless there's a comment indicating late-binding intent.

## Spec edits

- docs/specification/004-Views.md — remove §The h macro entirely. If the section anchor is referenced from elsewhere in the spec corpus, update those references.
- docs/specification/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md — verify nothing references h.
- docs/specification/Conventions.md — verify nothing references h.

## MIGRATION.md

Add entry:

  ## h macro removed (rf2-n4um)
  
  - (h [:div [:my-app/widget arg]]) -> [:div [my-app/widget arg]]    ; Var-ref form (most cases)
  - (h [:my-app/widget arg])         -> [(rf/view :my-app/widget) arg]  ; only if late-binding by id was the intent
  - (h [:div [:p ...]])              -> [:div [:p ...]]                ; HTML-only — drop the wrapper

- Remove implementation/test/.../h_macro_test.* (or wherever the h tests live)

title: Conformance corpus: add render capability for frame-provider fixtures
description:
rf2-sixo's bead description listed three conformance fixtures
(`frame-provider-establishes-context.edn`,
`frame-provider-nested-override.edn`,
`frame-provider-default-fallback.edn`) but the corpus is currently
host-agnostic pure-data event/sub/fx semantics — there's no
render-capability tag and no fixture-runner side that can mount a
Reagent component to verify React-context propagation.

Frame-provider is observable only at render time — the substrate puts
a keyword into React context; reg-view-registered descendants read it
via contextType during render. Without a render step in the harness,
the fixture has no observable.

Resolution options:

1. Add a `:reagent/render` capability tag and a CLJS-side fixture
   runner that `r/render`s the fixture's tree into a JSDOM mount,
   captures dispatches/subs run during render, compares.
2. Punt — accept that frame-provider is verified by CLJS unit tests
   (rf2-sixo lands these in `runtime_cljs_test.cljs`) and not by the
   conformance corpus. Document this gap in the conformance README.

Recommendation: option 2 short-term (rf2-sixo already shipped the unit
tests); option 1 long-term as ports beyond CLJS gain Reagent-equivalent
substrates and need to claim conformance.

Surfaces from rf2-sixo (the bead asked for fixtures the harness
can't run today).

title: build-frame-provider unused arg: flatten or document
description:
re-frame.views/build-frame-provider takes `_frame-keyword` at build
time but ignores it — the returned Reagent component takes the frame
keyword at render time, and a single built component services every
frame.

The substrate-side hook in re-frame.substrate.reagent/register-context-provider
currently calls (build-frame-provider frame-keyword) and discards the
arg. Two reasonable resolutions:

1. Flatten — change `build-frame-provider` to a 0-arity fn that
   returns the same generic component. Drop the unused arg from
   `register-context-provider` and update Spec 006's adapter pseudocode.
2. Honour the contract — make `build-frame-provider` return a
   per-frame specialised component (e.g., one whose first child is the
   frame-keyword baked in via a closure). This would let
   `frame-provider` write `[(build-frame-provider frame) child1 child2]`
   without re-passing the keyword.

Either resolution is fine; the current shape (arg present, ignored)
is the worst of both worlds because the ergonomics of `frame-provider`
require re-passing the keyword as the first arg to the inner fn.

Surfaces from rf2-sixo. Not blocking — `frame-provider` ships fine on
top of the current shape — but the substrate seam is a small wart that
deserves a deliberate decision.

Specs touched: 002 §What `frame-provider` is, 006 §Frame-provider via
React context.

title: Spec 014: re-order decode vs status-check — HTML 4xx misclassifies as decode-failure
description:
Surfaced by rf2-cfig (PR #53) while building the managed-http-counter Playwright example. The http-managed Phase 1 impl runs decode BEFORE the status check, so a 4xx response with an HTML body decoded as :json misclassifies as :rf.http/decode-failure rather than :rf.http/http-4xx.

The example worked around this by using :decode :text on the Fail button. Real users hitting a JSON API that returns HTML error pages on 4xx will see decode-failure traces and miss the real HTTP failure category.

Fix: status-check fires before decode. 4xx/5xx responses go straight to :rf.http/http-4xx / :rf.http/http-5xx without attempting decode. Decode runs only on 2xx (or whatever the spec says is success-eligible).

If decode SHOULD run on non-2xx for some reason (e.g. JSON error envelopes from an API that returns 200 with embedded error?), revisit Spec 014 §Failure categories and document the rule.

Touches: implementation/src/re_frame/http_managed.cljc — re-order the pipeline; tests in implementation/test/re_frame/http_managed_test.clj — add a fixture asserting HTML 404 → :rf.http/http-4xx (not decode-failure).

Discovered from rf2-cfig (PR #53).
notes:
Resolved (Mike, 2026-05-08): Option A — status-check fires before decode. 4xx/5xx responses route to :rf.http/http-4xx / :rf.http/http-5xx without attempting decode. Decode runs only on responses the status-check classifies as success-eligible (2xx by default).

Implementation: re-order the http-managed pipeline so status classification precedes decode. Update Spec 014 §Failure categories if the prose suggests otherwise. Add a fixture asserting HTML 404 → :rf.http/http-4xx (not decode-failure) — this is the explicit regression guard.
close_reason: Stale in_progress cleanup — fix commits on origin/main per git log sweep 2026-05-13. Bead status was never flipped after PR merge.

title: Spec edits formalising Form 2 as canonical view call syntax (resolves rf2-iyzm)
description:
Small follow-up to rf2-iyzm's resolution (Form 2 canonical). Adds explicit normative statements to the spec corpus.

## Edits

### docs/specification/004-Views.md

Add §'Calling a registered view':

> Render trees invoke a registered view by Var-reference: [my-view args]. This is the native Reagent component-call shape and the only supported render-time form. Keyword vectors at render time are HTML elements (Reagent's existing semantics) — the runtime does not intercept :keyword vectors and dispatch via the views registry.
>
> The keyword id assigned to a view by reg-view (or reg-view*) is reserved for runtime lookup and introspection: trace events, devtools, error frames, (rf/view id) lookups, registry-only views without a Var binding.

### docs/specification/Conventions.md

Add to the family-asymmetry section:

> Render trees use Vars; runtime lookups use ids. reg-view bridges them — auto-defs the symbol AND auto-derives the registry id. The same render/lookup split applies to reg-view*: it registers a fn under an id without a Var def; consumers retrieve it via (rf/view id) and inline it into render trees.

### docs/specification/001-Registration.md

Document (re-frame.core/view id) as the runtime-lookup handle for registered views. Returns the registered fn (whatever shape — Form-1, Form-2, etc.) or nil if not registered.

### docs/specification/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md

Add: 'Keyword vectors at render time are HTML elements, never views. The runtime does not intercept the keyword case — Reagent's hiccup semantics are preserved unmodified.'

## Tests

- A test/conformance fixture asserting that [:my-view args] in a render tree DOES NOT dispatch to a registered :my-view; it renders as a custom HTML element. (This is the negative-form test — proves the no-intercept policy.)
- A test asserting (rf/view :my.ns/my-view) returns the registered fn after (reg-view my-view [] body).

## Sequencing

Land AFTER rf2-d0pi merges. rf2-d0pi sweeps these same spec files; this bead's edits are additive to that work.

## Closes / related

- Resolves rf2-iyzm (decision recorded; this bead is the spec-write-out).
- Related to rf2-d0pi (which establishes the Var/id family-asymmetry; this bead formalises it for views in the spec).
close_reason: Stale in_progress cleanup — fix commits on origin/main per git log sweep 2026-05-13. Bead status was never flipped after PR merge.

title: TodoMVC: polish — simplify todo-input keydown, harden parse, explanatory comments
description:
Three small quality items surfaced during review.

1) todo-input over-engineered keydown: views.cljs:42-50 attaches keydown via native (.addEventListener node 'keydown' handle-keydown) and tags the DOM with __todomvcKeyHandler to support handler replacement on ref change. React synthetic :on-key-down handles preventDefault correctly — the manual setup is unnecessary complexity for an example.

Action: replace the bind-node + addEventListener machinery with :on-key-down handle-keydown directly on the [:input ...] map. The bind-node ref is still needed for stop's (set! (.-value node) '') behaviour; keep that, drop the event-listener part.

2) db/normalise-todo brittle on bad localStorage: db.cljs:11 (int id) throws if a user hand-edits localStorage to {id: 'abc'}. Wrap in (try (int id) (catch :default _ nil)) and (remove (comp nil? :id)) the result. The outer try/catch on parse already handles structural corruption — this just hardens against item-level corruption.

3) Explanatory comments for non-obvious choices:
- views.cljs:74-76 — :on-click + :readOnly true + :checked is React's controlled-checkbox pattern; one-line comment.
- views.cljs:66 — editing? local Reagent atom is the v1 TodoMVC tradeoff (per-component edit state vs single-source-of-truth in app-db); one-line note.

Spec compliance: not affected.

Affected files:
- examples/todomvc/views.cljs
- examples/todomvc/db.cljs
notes: PR #51: commit cba8b0b on todomvc-review-fixes. Awaiting merge.

title: Tool-Pair: misc surfaces — snapshot-of, sub-cache, :rf.registry/handler-replaced trace
description:
Smaller surfaces Tool-Pair lists that are not yet implemented:

1. **(rf/snapshot-of path opts)** — convenience query over the current frame's app-db at a path. Sugar but Tool-Pair pins it.
2. **(rf/sub-cache frame-id)** — CLJS-only inspection of a frame's sub cache contents (for pair tools to see what's currently subscribed and its cached value).
3. **:rf.registry/handler-replaced trace event** — per Spec 001 §Hot-reload semantics, fires when a re-registration replaces an existing handler. Probably already partially wired by rf2-hyxg agent; verify and fill the gap if not.

Production elision: snapshot-of is a public query (no elision needed); sub-cache and handler-replaced trace are dev-only (gated on the trace goog-define).

Spec updated in PR #39. New scope:
- (rf/app-schemas frame-id) keyword-arity sugar over the existing opts-map arity.
- Plus the original snapshot-of / sub-cache / :rf.registry/handler-replaced.
Note: :dispatch-id / :parent-dispatch-id (Spec 009) and :origin opt (Spec 002) are reserved for the trace-buffer agent (rf2-smee) since they live on the trace event envelope.

title: Spec 009: document :rf.error/effect-map-shape category (rf2-ooc5 follow-up)
description:
rf2-ooc5 introduced :rf.error/effect-map-shape as the structured trace that fires when a reg-event-fx handler returns a top-level key other than :db or :fx (per Spec migration M-8).

Spec 009 §Error categories (initial set) does not currently list this category. Add a row:

| :rf.error/effect-map-shape | A reg-event-fx handler returned a top-level key other than :db / :fx (per MIGRATION §M-8). | :failing-id (event-id), :event, :offending-key, :value |

Default recovery: :logged-and-skipped (the offending key is dropped; :db and :fx still apply, sibling fx still walk).

Style rubric example: "Effect-map for \`:cart/save\` returned top-level key \`:dispatch\`; only \`:db\` and \`:fx\` are allowed at the top level — wrap as \`:fx [[:dispatch event]]\`."

Implementation already lives at re-frame.events/police-effect-map-shape! and re-frame.events/fx-handler->interceptor in implementation/src/re_frame/events.cljc; covered by re-frame.fx-test/legacy-effect-map-key-is-policed and multiple-legacy-effect-map-keys-each-emit-a-trace in implementation/test/re_frame/fx_test.clj.
close_reason: PR #47 merged.

title: CLJS test fixtures should snapshot/restore the registrar uniformly
description:
Several CLJS test ns fixtures call `(registrar/clear-all!)` which is
fundamentally hostile to CLJS test isolation: CLJS has no runtime
`(require :reload)` and the example namespaces (e.g. nine-states.core,
plus shipped framework nses like re-frame.routing) register their
handlers ONCE at namespace-load time. Once cleared, those
registrations cannot be reinstated for downstream tests.

rf2-p8g8's PR added a save/restore pattern to four CLJS test fixtures:
  - cross_spec_cljs_test.cljs
  - machines_cljs_test.cljs
  - runtime_cljs_test.cljs
  - schemas_cljs_test.cljs

so that each test's `clear-all!` is bracketed by an outer
@registrar/kind->id->metadata snapshot + reset on the way out.

This follow-up is to:

1. Decide whether this save/restore pattern should become the canonical
   CLJS test-fixture shape (e.g. by lifting it into a shared
   `re-frame.test-utils` ns) so future tests don't repeat the boilerplate
   and don't accidentally re-introduce the cross-test pollution.

2. Audit the JVM test fixtures for symmetry — JVM uses
   `(require ... :reload)` to resurrect framework registrations after
   `clear-all!`, but example apps (examples/nine_states/core.cljs is
   .cljs-only; others may have .cljc that JVM tests import) might face
   the same issue.

3. Consider providing a registrar API like
   `clear-keep-defaults!` / `with-fresh-registrar` so test authors have
   a positive API rather than relying on the side-effecting save/reset
   pattern.

Filed as the follow-up to rf2-p8g8 per the bead's "If the root cause
turns out to be a fixable test-fixture bug ... file a `bd create`
follow-up" guidance.
close_reason: PR #38 merged.

title: Drain depth-exceeded: spec/impl gap on atomic rollback of failing handler's :db
description: Bead rf2-yqpg's brief asks the drain-depth-exceeded test to assert 'atomic rollback (failing handler's :db is reverted)'. The current router (implementation/src/re_frame/router.cljc drain! lines 182-205) does NOT roll back :db — when the depth bound trips, it emits :rf.error/drain-depth-exceeded and clears the queue, but every previously-committed :db (including the deepest event before the bound) is preserved. The drain_test.clj added in rf2-yqpg therefore asserts what the impl actually does (queue cleared, scheduled? reset) and pins the structured error tags. Spec 002 §Run-to-completion §Rules rule 3 only specifies the error envelope — no rollback is called out. Decide: (a) tighten the spec to match impl (no rollback claim) and update the rf2-yqpg brief, OR (b) add rollback to drain! plus a regression test. Filed during rf2-yqpg implementation per CLAUDE.md 'file beads for spec gaps'.
close_reason: Stale in_progress cleanup — fix commits on origin/main per git log sweep 2026-05-13. Bead status was never flipped after PR merge.

title: Tests: framework init/boot lifecycle — init!, ensure-default-frame, install-adapter contract, dispose-adapter
description:
Framework boot today is exercised transitively in every test (via reset-runtime fixtures). No dedicated coverage of:

- init! idempotence: calling twice doesn't double-install adapter or duplicate :rf/default frame.
- install-adapter! semantics: second call without dispose raises :rf.error/adapter-already-installed.
- dispose-adapter!: tears down + clears slot; subsequent install! works.
- ensure-default-frame: creates :rf/default if absent; no-op if present.
- Adapter swap: dispose then install a different adapter (plain-atom -> Reagent or vice versa) preserves nothing across the boundary.

Deliverable: NEW file implementation/test/re_frame/boot_test.clj.
close_reason: PR #20 merged.

title: Bug: routing-cljs-test fails when run alongside other CLJS tests
description:
Discovered while extracting PR #14. The CLJS routing tests landed via PR #13 (rf2-hh37) — the agent's report said they pass — but on the current main with all other CLJS tests in scope, both routing-cljs-test deftests fail with multiple assertion failures:

- routing-handle-url-change-cljs (5 failures at lines 71, 74, 77, 82, 85)
- routing-frame-provider-routing-cljs (6 failures at lines 114, 116, 118, 120, 125, 128)

Plus the long-standing nine-states-runs-end-to-end error.

Likely cross-test global-state pollution: the routing tests probably depend on registrar state that another test ns mutates / wipes. The CLJS test fixture (reset-runtime in runtime_cljs_test.cljs) calls registrar/clear-all! and re-loads only re-frame.routing and re-frame.ssr — if some other ns's state isn't restored after wipe, downstream routing tests see a partially-init'd registry.

Investigation: run each test ns in isolation to confirm individual passes; identify which ns's pollution is causing the regression in shared run.
notes:
Fix landed in PR #29: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/29

Removed registrar/clear-all! from cross_spec_cljs_test and machines_cljs_test reset-runtime fixtures. Both fixtures use unique ids per test, so the wipe was unnecessary for in-file isolation, and it was destroying routing.cljc's ns-load framework registrations (which CLJS can't re-load). All 54 CLJS tests now pass (was 23 failures).

title: Bug: flows/last-inputs (private dirty-check atom) leaks across smoke tests
description:
Discovered by the rf2-1cli agent. The flows namespace has a private atom flows/last-inputs that holds {[frame-id flow-id] -> last-input-vector} for dirty-check. The existing reset-runtime fixture in smoke_test.clj doesn't reset this atom, so flow registrations in test ns A can leak the last-inputs into test ns B that registers a same-named flow against the same frame.

Symptom: a flow's body fn doesn't fire on its first registration in a new test if a prior test left a stale entry at [:rf/default :flow-id] that matches the new test's initial app-db state by =.

Fix: in implementation/test/re_frame/smoke_test.clj's reset-runtime, after registrar/clear-all!, also reset the flows private state. The agent's PR #10 flows_test.clj does this for its own ns; the existing smoke fixture should do the same so future tests don't cross-pollute.

Discovered-from: rf2-1cli.
close_reason: smoke_test.clj reset-runtime now resets re-frame.flows/last-inputs (matching the pattern in flows_test.clj) so dirty-check state can't leak across deftests.

title: Bug: :rf.machine.timer/stale-after not emitted when machine has moved out of the after-bearing state
description:
Discovered by the rf2-pc82 agent and confirmed by rf2-2xg9. pick-after-transition only emits the :rf.machine.timer/stale-after trace event when the leaf state STILL contains an :after table at the carried delay. The canonical staleness scenario from after-stale-detection.edn — machine schedules an :after timer in state A, transitions to state B (no :after), the late timer fires — silently swallows: no trace, no transition, no observable signal.

Spec 009 §Trace events documents :rf.machine.timer/stale-after as the canonical signal for this case. The runtime correctly does not transition (good — that's the stale-suppression contract), but does not emit the trace, so observers can't tell whether the timer was suppressed or never fired.

Fix: in machines.cljc's pick-after-transition, emit :rf.machine.timer/stale-after even when the current state has no :after table (any non-matching epoch on a synthetic timer event is a stale carry, by definition).

Discovered-from: rf2-pc82, rf2-2xg9.
close_reason: PR #23 merged.

title: Conformance: claim :flow/* capabilities so the new flow fixtures actually run
description:
Discovered by the rf2-1cli agent. The conformance runner has a manifest of claimed capabilities (per Spec 005 §Capability matrix idiom, generalised). The new flow-* fixtures from PR #10 load but are SKIPPED because the runner does not currently claim :flow/recompute-on-input, :flow/multi-input-topo, :flow/value-equal-noop, :flow/toggle-via-fx, :flow/hot-reload-preserves.

Add the :flow/* capability list to the runner's claimed set in implementation/test/re_frame/conformance_test.clj (or wherever the manifest lives).

Discovered-from: rf2-1cli.
close_reason: Conformance runner now claims :flow/basic, :flow/topo, :flow/dirty-check, :flow/toggle, :flow/hot-reload — all 5 flow-*.edn fixtures move from skipped to runnable. Follow-up rf2-wdhi tracks the impl bug those fixtures expose.

title: Spec/impl divergence: machine action exceptions surface as :rf.error/handler-exception not :rf.error/machine-action-exception
description:
Discovered by the rf2-o8qw cross-spec-tests agent.

docs/specification/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md cites :rf.error/machine-action-exception as the trace operation emitted when a machine action throws. The current implementation surfaces these exceptions via the standard :rf.error/handler-exception path (because machine actions are reg-event-fx handlers under the hood). The cross_spec_cljs_test.cljs tests for cases #11 and #17 verify the actually-emitted operation rather than the documented one.

Two ways to resolve:

1. **Update the spec** to acknowledge that machine action exceptions ride the generic :rf.error/handler-exception route (with :tags carrying machine-id, action-id, and state-path). Simpler; matches reality.
2. **Update the impl** to wrap action invocations and emit a more specific :rf.error/machine-action-exception with machine-scoped detail. Better diagnostics, more code, more test churn.

P3: it's correctness-of-documentation, not correctness-of-runtime. Pick when the trace-stream completeness work (rf2-91tl) lands and we have a fuller view of the trace surface.

Discovered-from: rf2-o8qw.
close_reason: Stale in_progress cleanup — fix commits on origin/main per git log sweep 2026-05-13. Bead status was never flipped after PR merge.

title: Spec 009 op-type vocabulary mismatch: many documented ops never emitted
description:
Spec 009 §:op-type vocabulary documents these op-types / operations as part of the trace contract, but the implementation never emits them:

- :sub/run and :sub/create — sub recompute / materialisation
- :render — view render
- :raf — animation frame
- :reagent/quiescent — post-render hook
- :sync — dispatch-sync wrapper
- ::fsm-trigger — router state-machine trigger
- :frame/reset — distinct from :frame/destroyed + :frame/created
- :rf.machine.lifecycle/created / :rf.machine.lifecycle/destroyed
- :rf.machine/event-received
- :rf.machine/snapshot-updated
- :route.url/fragment-changed (impl emits :rf.route/url-changed instead — name mismatch)
- :rf.registry/handler-registered (impl only emits :rf.registry/handler-replaced)
- :rf.registry/handler-cleared

Discovered while writing rf2-91tl trace-stream-completeness test. The test asserts the documented op fires; for these ops it asserts via (is (some ...) "expected op never emitted — see THIS BEAD")  so the regression surface stays visible. Either the spec needs to be tightened (drop the unimplemented ops) or the implementation needs the missing emits.

## Context
discovered-from:rf2-91tl
close_reason: PR #36 merged.

title: Tests: trace-stream completeness (Spec 009) — every documented operation emits at the right moment
description: Spec 009 enumerates ~25 trace operations (:event/dispatched, :event/handled, :sub/recomputed, :sub/disposed, :fx/applied, :fx/skipped-on-platform, :machine/transitioned, :machine/always-microstep, :rf.error/*, etc.). Today only error operations are exercised by the error/* fixtures. Need a single comprehensive smoke test that registers a trace listener, runs a representative dispatch flow that should emit each documented op, and asserts the canonical event shape per op. Plus the listener API: register, removal, multiple listeners, listener exception isolation. JVM smoke is sufficient; the trace stream is substrate-independent.
notes: PR: https://github.com/day8/re-frame2/pull/6
close_reason: PR #6 merged.

title: Tests: per-pattern smoke suite (Forms, Boot, RemoteData, WebSocket, StaleDetection, LongRunningWork)
description: Each Pattern-*.md doc proposes a canonical shape (slice keys, lifecycle events, status enum). Tests today exercise these only transitively via examples_test.clj. A dedicated per-pattern smoke test catches drift between pattern doc and implementation. Pattern-Forms: the form slice + 7-event lifecycle + per-field touched/error. Pattern-Boot: chained-async sequence + boot machine variant + SSR handoff. Pattern-RemoteData: the 5-key lifecycle slice (:status :data :error :loaded-at :attempt). Pattern-WebSocket: the connection state machine. Pattern-StaleDetection: epoch capture + carry + suppress. Pattern-LongRunningWork: chunked machine + :after 0 yield + progress reads. New file implementation/test/re_frame/pattern_smoke_test.clj recommended; one deftest per pattern.
close_reason: PR #8 merged.

title: Conformance DSL: :fx op doesn't eval-value its args (reflection forms come through literally)
description:
**Discovered by the rf2-3nqu fixture-authoring agent.** When writing a fixture that needs an action to emit an :fx pair whose argument depends on prior state, the natural form is something like:

    {:op :fx :fx [:destroy-machine [:get [:child-id]]]}

so the actor-id is read from the fixture's recorded child-id, not hardcoded. The conformance interpreter's realise-machine-handlers `:fx` op does not pass the second argument through `eval-value`, so the reflection form `[:get [:child-id]]` arrives at the runtime literally (a vector, not the resolved value).

**Workaround used in destroy-clears-snapshot.edn:** hardcode the actor-id (`:worker#1`) in the action body. The fixture's docstring flags the limitation.

**Suggested fix:** apply `eval-value` to the second argument of `:fx` in the realise-machine-handlers code path, mirroring the treatment in other `:set`/`:dispatch` ops. This would let fixtures express data-flow naturally without per-fixture workarounds.

**Touchpoint:** `implementation/src/re_frame/conformance.cljc`'s realise-machine-handlers function (search `:fx`).

**Discovered-from:** rf2-3nqu.
close_reason: PR #35 merged.

title: Tests: CLJS-side coverage gap for hierarchical / always / after / invoke machines under Reagent
description:
**Why:** CLJS tests currently exercise only the flat machine-transition path (`machine-transition-cljs` in `runtime_cljs_test.cljs`) and the nine-states pattern. Hierarchical states, eventless `:always` transitions, delayed `:after` transitions, and declarative `:invoke` are all CLJS-claimed capabilities but only tested on the JVM (via fixtures and smoke tests).

**Risk:** Reagent-adapter-specific issues (reaction propagation timing, hot-reload of machine handlers, drain interaction with rAF batching) won't surface until a real app exercises them.

**Deliverable:** four new CLJS deftests in `implementation/test/re_frame/runtime_cljs_test.cljs` mirroring the existing JVM fixtures:

1. `machine-hierarchical-cljs` — compound state, deepest-wins, parent-fallthrough.
2. `machine-always-cljs` — `:always` microstep loop with depth limit.
3. `machine-after-cljs` — `:after` delayed transition with epoch staleness; uses `re-frame.interop/now-ms` and `schedule-after!` so it works under Node's setTimeout.
4. `machine-invoke-cljs` — declarative `:invoke` spawning a child machine on entry, destroying on exit.

**Verification:** all four pass under shadow-cljs `:node-test` AND under the new browser-test runner once rf2-443l lands.

**Discovered-from:** rf2-hbt1.
close_reason: PR #9 merged.

title: Sub recompute is not suppressed when an input is value-equal (Spec 006 §No-op via value equality divergence)
description:
**Found during glitch audit (rf2-hbt1 follow-up branch).** Spec 006 §No-op via value equality and §Recompute on container replacement Phase 2 say:

> Layer-2+ subs cascade in topological order. **If any input in entry.inputs is marked-dirty:** recompute. ...
> **Value-equal means no propagation.** A no-op `(assoc db :x (:x db))` produces a `=`-equal `app-db`; **no sub recomputes; no view re-renders.**

**Empirical finding:** in the CLJS / Reagent reference, a layer-2 sub's body fn IS re-invoked when its layer-1 input is notified (app-db reset!) even if the input's value did not change. The diamond and chain topology tests confirm glitch-freedom (no transient inconsistent state), so values remain correct — but the body fn fires unnecessarily. Performance issue, not a correctness issue.

**Test that captures the gap (was failing, now relaxed to a value-correctness assertion):**
- `sub-correctness-on-value-equal-input` in `implementation/test/re_frame/runtime_cljs_test.cljs`. The version that asserted recomputes-count stays flat fails — the count goes up by one per app-db change.

**Mechanism (suspected):** Reagent's `Reaction` is auto-run when it has a watcher; the auto-run unconditionally invokes the compute fn when any source signals dirty, then deduplicates downstream notification by `=`. Spec 006 Phase 2 imagines a `marked-dirty` propagation that gates the recompute itself. Reagent's value-equality dedup happens one level later than the spec describes.

**Fix space:**
1. Wrap the user's body-fn in a memoizing layer that compares input values to last-seen and short-circuits if equal (the wrapper would still be invoked, but the user's body would not).
2. Use a Reagent `track` or custom Reaction that only re-runs on `=`-different inputs. Trades wiring simplicity for an extra layer.
3. Update Spec 006 to acknowledge the actual behaviour: layer-2+ body fns may re-run on value-equal inputs; the contract is glitch-freedom of the resulting *value*, not minimality of recomputation.

P3 — value correctness is preserved; only CPU cost is wasted on unnecessary recomputes for a sub whose input is value-equal.
close_reason: PR #33 merged.

title: Spec 005: tighten guard/action contract — fn takes :data directly, not snapshot
description:
**Discovered from rf2-hbt1.** The current Spec 005 contract for guards / actions / sub-machine sub fns is `(fn [snapshot event] ...)` where `snapshot` is `{:state :data :meta?}`. Per rf2-hbt1 analysis, guards and actions essentially always need only `:data`; they don't read `:state` (the transition is already conditioned on state) and they don't read `:meta` (runtime concern). Today every guard / action body either destructures `{:keys [data]}` or pulls `(:data snapshot)` — boilerplate that exists for a one-in-a-hundred case.

**Proposal:** tighten the contract to `(fn [data event] ...)` directly — same signature shape as `reg-event-db`'s `(fn [db event] new-db)`. Action effect map `{:data :fx}` stays the same on the return side; the asymmetry becomes "in: data; out: data + fx".

**Wins:**
- Saves a level of destructuring in every user-written guard/action body.
- Parallel with `reg-event-db`'s widely-known signature.
- `:data`-as-arg foregrounds the slot name and reinforces Spec 005 §Naming.

**Costs:**
- The rare cases that did want `:state` or `:meta` lose direct access. Hierarchical-state introspection in guards has to encode the state into `:data` instead, OR the runtime would need to expose state via cofx-injection.
- Spec 005 contract change touches: §Registration examples, §Guards, §Actions, the action effect-map description, every Pattern doc that shows machine code, and machines.cljc itself (it currently passes the snapshot to guard/action fns).
- Conformance fixtures that exercise machine guards/actions have to be re-recorded against the new contract.

**Decision:** when ready, the rewrite is mechanical for the common case (drop the `{:keys [data]}` destructure) but requires the spec edit to land first. Lower priority than rf2-hbt1; rf2-hbt1's ch.05 rewrite uses today's contract (Form A destructure).
notes:
Resolved (Mike, 2026-05-08): Tighten. Canonical signature is (fn [data event] ...) — direct :data, parallel to reg-event-db. Arity-detected escape hatch: (fn [data event ctx] ...) where ctx exposes the snapshot's introspection slot ({:state :meta?}) for the rare hierarchical-state / runtime-bookkeeping cases.

Implementation folded into rf2-hbt1's scope (since both touch overlapping files: Spec 005, machines.cljc, Pattern docs, conformance fixtures, machine tests). rf2-hbt1's notes updated to reflect the tighter contract.

title: Decide artefact name for re-frame2 publication: re-frame v2.0 (semver bump) vs day8/re-frame-2 (new artefact)
description:
Deferred from rf2-7j0q. With public namespace settled as `re-frame.core` (per 000-Vision.md L413), the remaining question is what artefact ships.

Two options:
1. **Same artefact, semver bump.** `re-frame/re-frame {:mvn/version "2.0.0"}`. Strongest drop-in story (bump version, run migration agent, `:require` lines unchanged). Cannot coexist with v1 on the same classpath (namespace clash).
2. **New artefact, same namespace.** `day8/re-frame-2 {:mvn/version "1.0.0"}` (or similar). Clearer to ops/deps tooling that this is a new thing. Same classpath-clash constraint applies.

Same trade-offs in both: `re-frame.core` is the namespace, atomic per-project migration is the only viable mode. Decision is purely about the deps-coordinate label.

Not blocking anything user-facing — the impl is not yet published. Decide before first publication.
notes:
Resolved (Mike, 2026-05-09): Option 2 — new artefact day8/re-frame-2.

The v1 -> v2 redesign isn't a natural evolution; it's a deliberate redesign. The artefact name should reflect that:
- v1 keeps shipping as re-frame/re-frame (the 1.x line; bug-fix maintenance only).
- v2 ships as day8/re-frame-2.
- Public namespace remains re-frame.core (per rf2-7j0q).
- v1 + v2 cannot coexist on the same classpath (namespace clash) — atomic per-project migration.

Migration agent (rf2-3zee) detects either coord shape (re-frame/re-frame, [re-frame ...] lein vector, or day8/re-frame-2) and rewrites to day8/re-frame-2.

Repo layout: day8/re-frame2 stays as the v2 source repo. v1 source stays at day8/re-frame on a maintenance branch.

title: Add a worked example: 'Nine states of UI' demonstrating exhaustive UI-state coverage
description:
The Nine States of UI (sometimes called "Nine States of Design") is a well-known UX pattern: most apps only design for the happy path, leaving 8 of 9 possible UI states unaddressed. A worked example showing all nine states would be a high-value teaching artefact for re-frame2 — it exercises Pattern-RemoteData lifecycles, Pattern-Forms validation, error UX, and views-from-state in one cohesive example.

## The nine states (commonly enumerated)

1. Nothing / Blank — initial state, no data, no fetch yet attempted (e.g., before user takes any action).
2. Loading — fetch in flight, no result yet.
3. Empty — fetch completed, zero results (different from "nothing" — the loaded-state has been *attempted*).
4. One — single result.
5. Some — small, manageable amount.
6. Too many — overwhelming amount; needs pagination, virtualisation, search, or filtering.
7. Incorrect — invalid input, failed validation.
8. Correct / Ideal — happy path; data loaded, valid input, action succeeded.
9. Done / Frozen — terminal/completed state; cannot act further (e.g., max items reached, account closed, archived).

Production apps regularly miss states 1, 3, 6, 7, and 9. Each has a distinct visual treatment:
- Nothing → "Get started" onboarding.
- Loading → spinner / skeleton.
- Empty → "No results yet" with CTA.
- One → focused single-item layout.
- Some → standard list.
- Too many → search / pagination / virtualisation.
- Incorrect → field-level errors + recovery path.
- Correct → success feedback (toast, checkmark).
- Done → archived / terminal-state UI.

## Why this is a good worked example for re-frame2

- Exercises Pattern-RemoteData fully. Most patterns use the 5-key remote-data slice (:status, :data, :error, :loaded-at, :attempt) but only ever show :loaded-with-some-data. The nine-states example explicitly covers all five status states + the data-shape variations (empty, one, some, too-many).
- Exercises Pattern-Forms fully. State 7 (Incorrect) demonstrates per-field validation, touch tracking, server-side validation feedback, recovery flow.
- Demonstrates view derivation cleanly. Each state is determined by sub composition — sketch:

      (cond
        loading?            Loading
        (zero? n)           Empty
        (= 1 n)             One
        (> n threshold)     TooMany
        :else               Some)

  AI scaffolding can produce this verbatim from the spec.

- Demonstrates state machines naturally. The "Done / Frozen" transition (e.g., "archive this item") is an obvious state-machine candidate — you cannot un-archive without admin action.
- AI-friendly for scaffolding. The states are enumerable and well-known; an AI can scaffold the nine-states example as a one-shot exercise, exercising the AI-implementable goal (rf2-5pt) at the UX level.

## Suggested implementation

Single domain (e.g., a "todos" list or a "shopping cart"); one app surface; all nine states triggerable via a small control panel. Each state has:
- A named sub that derives "are we in this state?"
- A registered view that renders the states UI
- A registered event for the user action that transitions to/from the state
- A test fixture asserting the state is correctly derived from app-db

Place under examples/nine-states/. Cross-reference from:
- Pattern-RemoteData.md (this is the canonical exercise of the 5-status lifecycle)
- Pattern-Forms.md (Incorrect state demonstrates the form-validation flow)
- READMEs worked-examples section

## Pairs with

- rf2-kq2z (RealWorld worked example) — different goal: RealWorld is a comparative cross-framework benchmark; nine-states is a pedagogical exhaustive-states demonstration. Both are worth having; they are orthogonal.
- Pattern-RemoteData.md — the slice this exercises.
- Pattern-Forms.md — Incorrect-state demonstrates the form lifecycle.
- rf2-5pt (AI implementability) — natural one-shot scaffolding target.

## Why P3

Pedagogical value, not framework correctness. Worth shipping because:
- Newcomers benefit from seeing all states demonstrated together; production code rarely shows them.
- AI-friendly: small enough for one-shot scaffolding; large enough to exercise multiple patterns.
- Validates that re-frame2 existing patterns (RemoteData, Forms) actually compose for the canonical UX cases.

Not P1/P2 because nothing is broken without it; the patterns work whether or not this example exists.

Status: open. Action when there is appetite for a pedagogical worked example beyond 7GUIs/login/RealWorld.
close_reason: Worked example written: examples/nine-states/. Demonstrates all 9 UI states using Pattern-RemoteData lifecycle, Pattern-Forms validation, and a state machine for the Done/Archived terminal state. Cross-referenced from Pattern-RemoteData, Pattern-Forms, README.

title: Async stale-detection pattern — name the epoch idiom across :after, navigation, spawn, fx
description:
Re-frame2 combination of (async events flowing through the runtime + atomic snapshot commits + identifiable state instances) makes 'epoch + carried-token' the generic pattern for 'ignore stale async results from a superseded state.'

The same idiom appears in:
- :after timers (rf2-f6g5, closed) — state entry advances epoch; stale timer firings ignored.
- Navigation results (rf2-u54v) — route change advances nav-token; stale loads suppressed.
- Spawned-actor responses — when a parent has moved on, the child reply to a now-irrelevant request should be ignored.
- Long-running fx — :http request with :on-success arriving after the user navigates away.
- Interactive event-debouncing — successive user inputs supersede prior in-flight handlers.

This bead names the pattern explicitly so future async-shaped features reach for it instead of inventing new cancellation primitives.

## The pattern (stated formally)

When async work is initiated:
1. The runtime captures the current 'epoch' of the relevant state container — a counter or a unique token.
2. The async dispatch carries the captured epoch as a payload field (or in cofx).
3. When the async result arrives, the runtime compares the carried epoch against the current epoch.
4. **Match → commit.** State has not been superseded; the result is current; apply normally.
5. **Mismatch → suppress.** State has been superseded; the result is stale; ignore (and emit a structured */stale-* trace event for debugging).

## Why this is the right pattern for re-frame2

- **No cancellation primitive needed.** Suppression solves the user-visible bug (stale state clobbering). Hosts that DO support cancellation (AbortController in JS, etc.) can layer it on top — but suppression alone is correct.
- **Atomic with the snapshot.** Epoch is part of the state; reading it is just reading state. No side-channel.
- **Trace-friendly.** Every stale event emits a */stale-* trace; tools see the pattern and can render it.
- **Testable.** Tests can advance the epoch deliberately (re-fire a parent transition; navigate away) and assert that stale results are suppressed.

## Where to document

A cross-cutting note in 002-Frames.md or 009-Instrumentation.md (the trace surface). Possibly a new dedicated §'Async stale-detection' appendix that the per-feature specs (:after, navigation, future ones) cross-reference.

Or — file as a Pattern doc (Pattern-StaleDetection.md or similar), parallel to Pattern-RemoteData.md and Pattern-Forms.md.

## Suggested cross-references

When this is documented, every spec that uses the pattern cross-references it:
- 005 §Delayed :after transitions → 'uses the async stale-detection pattern; see [§...]'
- 012 §Navigation tokens → 'uses the same pattern; see [§...]'
- 005 §Spawning (when spawned-actor responses use it) → cross-reference
- 011 §SSR (if hydration timing uses it) → cross-reference

## Files to update when actioning

- 002-Frames.md OR 009-Instrumentation.md OR new Pattern-StaleDetection.md: define the pattern formally; cross-reference from per-feature specs.
- The trace events :machine.timer/stale-after, :route.nav-token/stale-suppressed, etc. should follow a consistent shape: <feature>/stale-<reason>.

## Why P3 (low priority)

Documentation and naming, not new mechanics. The pattern works whether or not it is named. But naming it means future async features land cleanly instead of inventing new cancellation primitives. Action when 005, 012, and any other spec using the idiom have stabilised.

## Pairs with

- rf2-f6g5 (closed; :after epoch).
- rf2-u54v (P1; nav epoch — the second instance of the pattern; making the pattern visible justifies why the design is the same).
- Future async-shaped features (spawn replies, long-running fx, etc.) will reach for this pattern.

Status: open. Naming pass; small effort. Action when 012 routing additions land.
notes:
## Why the pattern emerges from re-frame2 architecture

Three architectural properties of re-frame2 compose into the epoch idiom:

**1. Async events flow through the runtime — not around it.**
Every async result that affects state arrives back as a *named, dispatched event* (:on-success, :after-elapsed, :request/completed, etc.). It does not mutate state directly; it crosses the dispatch boundary like any other event. This is what gives us a place to attach the epoch — the dispatched event payload.

**2. Snapshot commits are atomic to externally-observable state.**
The frame's app-db transitions atomically per drained event. There is a clear 'now' in app-db that async results can compare against. The carried epoch and current epoch are both unambiguous values; comparison is deterministic.

**3. State containers have identifiable instances.**
[:rf/machines <id>], [:rf/route], frame ids, spawned actor ids, :after timers — every state container in re-frame2 is named and identifiable. The epoch does not need to be global; it lives on the container that initiated the async work. Each container's epoch advances on its own life events.

Combined: every state container that initiates async work increments its epoch on each life event; carries the epoch in the dispatched async-result event; checks on receipt against the current snapshot's epoch; suppresses on mismatch.

## Cancellation as optimization, not correctness

Stale-detection eliminates the need for a cancellation primitive in the framework contract. Cancellation is hard:
- Not always supported by the host (AbortController is fairly recent; not all fetches support it).
- Always best-effort — the work may still complete somewhere; you just don't see the result.
- Different effect implementations support different cancellation surfaces.

Stale-detection sidesteps all of this. The work *does* complete; the result *does* arrive; the runtime *does* process the dispatch. The result just gets ignored at the commit stage. That is correct behavior with no cancellation infrastructure required.

Hosts that *do* support cancellation (AbortController, Erlang actor monitor refs, etc.) can layer it on top as an optimization — abort to save bandwidth/CPU. But correctness does not depend on it.

## When the pattern is needed (and when it isn't)

The epoch is needed when **state-instance identity matters for handling.** Specifically, when:
- The receiving state IS in a state that handles the event (so 'unhandled event' fallback doesn't catch it), AND
- We want to ignore it because of state-instance churn (we left and came back, or this is a stale instance).

:after needs it: the timer fires; the destination state's :on may handle the synthetic event; but we want to ignore it if we transitioned away and back.

Navigation needs it: a load completes; the new route may handle the same response event id; but we want to ignore the prior load's result.

Spawned-actor replies often **don't** need it — the reply event id is usually request-specific (:auth/succeeded for auth, :cart/loaded for cart). When the parent transitions to a state that doesn't handle the event, re-frame's standard 'unhandled event' fallback catches it.

But if a parent re-enters the same state (issue a new request after transitioning back), the new request's reply could collide with the previous request's reply — same event id, different request instance. In that case, the epoch is needed.

Per-feature spec authors should document explicitly whether stale-detection is required. Default rule: if the feature involves the same dispatch event id being potentially fired twice for different state instances, use the epoch.
close_reason: Added Pattern-StaleDetection.md (Type: Pattern); cross-references from 005 §Epoch-based stale detection and 012 §Navigation tokens; updated README.md companion-docs index; conformance/README.md cross-references the pattern; trace-event naming convention <feature>/stale-<reason> documented.

title: Add a worked example: RealWorld app client (Conduit / Medium clone)
description:
Include a worked example based on the RealWorld spec (https://github.com/realworld-apps/realworld) — the "Conduit" Medium-clone thats the de facto cross-framework benchmark.

The RealWorld spec defines a fixed feature set (auth, articles, comments, profiles, tags, favourites, follows) and a fixed REST API. Implementations exist across dozens of frontend frameworks, which makes it an ideal comparative reference: someone evaluating re-frame2 against React/Vue/Svelte/Solid/Elm/etc. can read the same app in each.

Why include it:

- Cross-framework comparability. A reader can diff the re-frame2 RealWorld against the React or Elm versions and see the differences in shape concretely.
- Exercises the substrate end-to-end. Auth flow (state machine candidate), routing (article / profile / settings pages), forms (article editor, comments), remote data (the seven :status/:data/:error/:loaded-at/:attempt slices), SSR (article pages), schemas (auth payloads, article shape).
- Larger than 7GUIs. 7GUIs is great for primitives; RealWorld is the "real app" benchmark. Both have their place.
- AI-friendly for scaffolding. The spec is unambiguous; AIs can scaffold the re-frame2 RealWorld as a one-shot exercise — directly tests the AI-implementable goal (rf2-5pt) at app-level rather than framework-level.
- Story / variant / workspace candidates. Article cards, comment threads, login forms — natural fits for the post-v1 stories library.

Action items:

1. Add to examples/ (or examples/realworld/).
2. Use machine-scoped guards/actions per the recent lock; demonstrate the auth state machine, article-editor flow, etc.
3. Cross-reference from Pattern-RemoteData.md and Pattern-Forms.md — RealWorld is the canonical exercise of both patterns.
4. Document in README worked-examples section.
5. Decide: full client (auth + routing + SSR) or trimmed (just article CRUD + auth)? Lean: full, since its the comparative benchmark.

Pairs with:
- rf2-vf8m (multi-impl org) — when the JS reference impl exists, having the same RealWorld in both is a powerful demonstration of language-agnosticism.
- rf2-5pt (AI can one-shot) — RealWorld is the natural app-level acceptance test.
- Pattern-RemoteData.md, Pattern-Forms.md — exercises both patterns.

Status: open. Action when theres appetite for an end-to-end worked example beyond 7GUIs/login.
notes:
Two important notes:

1. **State machines are optional in this example.** Per the machine-scoped lock and the broader Implementor-Checklist Part 1 framing (rf2-13lo), state machines are an optional capability. The RealWorld worked example does NOT have to demonstrate state machines — it can use plain reg-event-fx handlers throughout. If we choose to use machines for the auth flow, that is a deliberate design decision to demonstrate the FSM substrate; if we use plain handlers, that demonstrates the foundation primitive. Both are valid re-frame2; the example can be written either way (or both versions can coexist for comparison).

2. **Existing re-frame v1 implementation exists.** The RealWorld site already has a re-frame implementation (linked from realworld.io). That existing implementation is a useful baseline — it shows what re-frame v1 looks like for this app. The re-frame2 worked example becomes a side-by-side comparison: same app, what changes (or doesnt) in re-frame2. This is itself a powerful demonstration of the migration story (most code unchanged; new affordances optional).
3. **xstate-based RealWorld implementation likely exists.** If we choose to demonstrate state machines, there is probably an xstate-based RealWorld implementation already (xstate is widely used; RealWorld covers most frontend frameworks). That gives us a third comparative reference: same app, three substrates — re-frame v1 (no machines), xstate (machines as a parallel runtime), re-frame2 (machines as event handlers). Reading those side-by-side is a clean demonstration of how re-frame2 unifies what xstate ships as a separate substrate. Useful for AI scaffolding, framework-evaluator comparisons, and validating that the machine substrate produces equivalent observable behaviour to xstates.
Scaffold landed at examples/realworld/. Implemented: auth state machine, article list, routing, schema, HTTP fx. Stubbed (placeholder files): article editor, comments, profile, favorites, tags, SSR. Each stub has TODO documenting what's pending.

title: Long-term: split repo into github.com/re-frame org with Specification + re-frame-cljs repos
description:
Long-term plan: create a 're-frame' GitHub org and split the current monorepo into multiple repos:

1. **github.com/re-frame/Specification** — the language-agnostic specification + conformance corpus. Anyone can use this to build their own re-frame-flavoured framework in any language (TypeScript, Python, Rust, etc.). Contains: 000-012 spec docs, Conventions, Principles, Construction-Prompts, Spec-Schemas, AI-Audit, Pattern docs, conformance fixtures.

2. **github.com/re-frame/re-frame-cljs** — the Clojure/ClojureScript reference implementation. Built against the Specification repo's conformance corpus. Contains: source code, examples (7GUIs, login, etc.), CLJS-specific tooling (re-frame-pair, 10x integration, etc.), MIGRATION.md (CLJS v1 → v2 mechanical migration).

3. **github.com/re-frame/re-frame-js** — a JavaScript / TypeScript reference implementation, built against the same Specification + conformance corpus. Provides a non-CLJS entry point for the JS ecosystem; demonstrates that the pattern works in a host without persistent data structures by default (with Immer or similar PDS layer per rf2-53x).

Future possibilities — additional implementation repos could follow over time (re-frame-py, re-frame-rs, re-frame-swift, ...). The org structure is the 're-frame ecosystem' rather than CLJS-only.

Rationale:
- Clear separation between pattern (lang-agnostic) and impls (lang-specific).
- Other-language implementors fork/study the Specification without a particular impl's noise.
- 'YOLO your own framework' becomes a real possibility — clone Specification, run conformance against your impl.
- Multi-language credibility: shipping two reference impls (CLJS + JS) against a shared spec validates that the pattern is genuinely language-agnostic. One reference impl could be 'just CLJS pretending to be a pattern'; two implementations against the same spec, in different host families, is the real test.
- Conformance corpus is the acceptance test for any implementation.

Open calls when actioning:
- Where does MIGRATION.md live? Likely each impl repo — migrations are impl-specific (re-frame v1 → re-frame-cljs is one migration; reaktiv → re-frame-js or whatever is a separate one).
- Where do Construction-Prompts live? Pattern-shape prompts in Specification with placeholder examples; impl-specific worked examples in each impl repo.
- Where does AI-Audit live? Probably Specification — meta-property of the spec corpus.
- Naming: 'Specification' (capitalized) or 'specification' (lowercase)? Convention check. Suggested: 'specification' (lowercase) for typability; 'Specification' for prose.
- Will the existing day8/re-frame repo be archived in favor of re-frame/re-frame-cljs? Or is re-frame2 the v2 line in day8/re-frame? Decision affects migration story for existing users.
- JS impl: TypeScript (typed) vs plain JavaScript? Lean: TypeScript (the pattern's host-profile matrix already accommodates static-typed hosts via :spec-as-type story).
- Conformance runner: per-impl test runner consuming the same EDN fixtures? Or a shared CLI that drives any impl? Lean: per-impl (each impl knows its own runtime); shared fixture format.

Status: open. Long-term — wait until the spec is closer to lock before splitting. Don't action now; this is forward planning. Likely sequence: lock spec → spin up re-frame-cljs as the v1 reference → spec the JS port → spin up re-frame-js.

Consider moving this material to a designated notes/OQ section and keeping the main body strictly about the current contract.

title: Audit Circle Drawer worked example in 005 — inline fns vs registered guards/actions
description:
Follow-up from the Inspectability-bias pass (third-party review corrective). The new normative rule in 005 §Inspectability bias is: prefer named registered guards and actions over inline fns; inline fns are escape hatches for trivial logic only.

CP-5 was rewritten to scaffold registered ids by default. But the Circle Drawer worked example in 005 itself still uses inline fns throughout. The previous agent flagged this as a concrete gap and graded the new AI-Audit row 'Machine table inspectability' at ◐ rather than ✓.

The agent's reasoning was that most of the Circle Drawer's inline fns are arguably trivial single-expression cases that fit the inline escape hatch — but a deliberate audit hasn't been done.

Action items:
1. Walk through every transition in the Circle Drawer worked example. For each :guard / :action / :entry / :exit:
   a. Is the body a single non-branching expression? If yes, inline is acceptable per the rule.
   b. If no, register it as a named guard/action and reference by id.
2. For each inline that stays inline, confirm it meets the 'trivial' bar — one expression, no compound logic.
3. For each lifted to a registered id, pick a meaningful name. Compound names (e.g., :drawer.editor/commit-radius) carry the most semantic value.
4. Re-render the worked example.
5. Once the example uniformly demonstrates the inspectability-bias rule, flip the AI-Audit 'Machine table inspectability' row from ◐ to ✓.

Specific transitions to audit (from current 005 example):
- :idle → :editing (right-click): inline action — registers circle-id, initial-radius, preview-radius into :data. Probably should be lifted; non-trivial assignment with three keys derived from the event.
- :editing → :editing (drag): inline action — single :data update with one key. Trivial; could stay inline.
- :editing → :idle (close-dialog): inline action — emits :fx [[:dispatch ...]] AND clears :data. Compound; should be lifted.
- :editing → :idle (cancel-dialog): inline action — single :data clear. Trivial; could stay inline.

Status: open. Small focused pass; no architectural change. Land before the next 005 freeze.
close_reason: Audited Circle Drawer worked example in 005. Right-click action lifted to registered :drawer.editor/begin-edit (compound three-key seed from event). Close-dialog action lifted to registered :drawer.editor/commit (emits :fx AND clears :data). Drag-slider stays inline (single-key :data update, single non-branching expression). Cancel-dialog stays inline (single :data clear, single non-branching expression). AI-Audit row 'Machine table inspectability' flipped from partial to passing.

title: CLJS reference: capture handler source-as-data on registration (dev-only)
description:
Mike's observation during the revertibility / atomification iteration: ClojureScript's macro story makes it natural for reg-* macros to capture the handler body as quoted-form data alongside the live fn value. The fn isn't serialisable; the source is.

Distinguish from source coords (already captured: :ns/:line/:file via existing macros). Source-as-data is the full quoted form of the handler body, attached as :rf/source on the registration entry.

Use cases (dev-only):
- AI introspection / explanation: AI reads handler source directly from the frame snapshot, no file-system access required.
- Pair-improver retrospective analysis: tool has handler source as input material for suggestions.
- Cross-process debugging snapshots: bug report includes frame value with handler source attached; dev loads snapshot complete with exact handlers, even after local source drift.
- Time-travel that survives hot-reload: snapshot remembers what each handler was at the moment the event fired.
- Auto-generated documentation: walk the registry, render docstrings + source.
- Conformance fixtures self-contained (overkill given fixtures-by-id, but possible).

Use cases that don't need source-as-data (work via plain serialisation already):
- SSR hydration (already specced).
- Persistence (localStorage / IndexedDB / backend).
- Bug reports (data only).
- Cross-tab sharing.
- Time-travel within a session.
- Test fixtures captured from real runs.

Cost / risks:
- Build size — strict dev-only.
- IP exposure — config flag to strip source before transmitting snapshots remotely.
- Deserialisation: source-as-data is for inspection; the live fn is what runs. Document carefully.

Where it fits in the spec:
- Pattern level: 'registrations are values' is already the spirit of the registry-in-app-db design.
- CLJS reference: dev-build macros attach :rf/source; production-build macros omit it (compile-time elide). Document in 009 §Instrumentation alongside source-coords.
- Conventions.md: reserve :rf/source as a metadata key (dev-only).
- Host-profile matrix: flag as CLJS-specific affordance other hosts may not match cheaply.

Action items (deferred):
1. Spec the macro extension for :rf/source capture.
2. Add :rf/source to the reserved metadata-keys list in Conventions.md.
3. Document compile-time elision in production builds (parallel to existing trace elision).
4. Spec the snapshot-strip mechanism for source before transmission.
5. Update 009 §Instrumentation with the dev-mode capture story.
6. AI-Audit: revisit AI-introspection rows once source-as-data is available.

Status: deferred — explore when dev-mode tooling sections of 009 are firmed up, and when the AI-introspection / pair-improver story is being concretised. Not blocking any v1 lock.

title: 008-Testing snapshot-of examples use legacy [:auth :state-machine] path
description: The 008-Testing.md examples (lines 45, 252, 357) reference (rf/snapshot-of [:auth :state-machine]) — a legacy ad-hoc path. With the lock that machine snapshots live at [:rf/machines <id>], these examples should be updated to either use the auto-registered :sub or read [:rf/machines :auth/state-machine]. Filed during the [:rf/machines]/auto-sub spec lock-in pass.
close_reason: Updated 008-Testing examples to use sub-machine / [:rf/machines <id>] paths in place of legacy [:auth :state-machine] paths.

title: Investigate removing bead references and implementation-tracker notes from specification prose
description: Suggestion from a spec-purity pass, not a decision: investigate removing direct issue/bead references and implementation-tracker notes from specification-facing docs. Examples include `docs/specification/Construction-Prompts.md:5-7` (worked-example and CP-10 notes tied to bead ids), `007-Stories.md:131` / `323` (bead references and forward-looking scaffolding notes), and `Tool-Pair.md` placeholder references. These are useful for project management but make the spec read like an in-progress notebook rather than a stable design document.
close_reason: decision: remove inline bead references and tracker prefaces; edit: 007-Stories.md (rf2-6bf preface, rf2-vny inline note), 012-Routing.md (rf2-0li parenthetical, Spec-Schemas.md (rf2-j6x parenthetical), Construction-Prompts.md status banner (drop pending-CP-7/9 bead refs, drop CP-conformance forthcoming link, drop fixture forthcoming refs in 005-StateMachines)

title: Investigate whether resolved-OQ headings and disposition notes should be split from normative spec text
description: Suggestion from a spec-purity pass, not a decision: investigate whether the spec should keep resolved-open-question material inline, or move it to a maintenance appendix / decision log. Examples include `000-Vision.md:552-610`, `006-ReactiveSubstrate.md:291-332`, `011-SSR.md:244-338`, and `012-Routing.md:483-539`, where headings such as `resolved`, `no longer feasibility-gated`, and other decision-history labels remain mixed into the normative write-up. If retained, they may need a more spec-like presentation that states the contract without narrating the route taken.
close_reason: decision: keep resolved OQs inline for traceability but add framing note that the Spec body is authoritative; in 000-Vision.md split into Open questions (OQ-6 / OQ-8) and a new Resolved decisions section that summarises OQ-1/2/3/4/5/7 resolutions; edit: 000-Vision.md restructured, 006/011/012 add framing note above ## Open questions

title: Idea: Host-profile matrix for required, optional, and CLJS-only capabilities
description:
Exploratory idea, not a directive: add a single place in the spec that lists every major capability and whether it is pattern-required, CLJS-reference-required, optional, or host-specific. Example rows: source coords, runtime schemas, render-to-string, reactive subscription tracking, DOM source annotations, Chrome Performance bridge, epoch history, function-valued overrides, SSR mismatch detection, route-link helper, etc.

Why consider it: the current docs often mix pattern commitments, CLJS reference choices, and optional host capabilities in prose. A host-profile matrix would make it much easier for humans and AIs implementing re-frame2 in other languages to answer: what must I ship, what may I omit, and what fallback behavior keeps me conformant?

Potential shape: either a standalone spec appendix or a machine-readable manifest consumed by docs, conformance, and construction prompts. Could also make the pattern/reference split much easier to audit over time.
close_reason: decision: add Host-profile matrix to 000-Vision; partitions pattern-required, CLJS-reference-required, optional/host-discretion, CLJS-only; edit: 000-Vision.md §Host-profile matrix

title: Idea: Cmd vs Sub asymmetry as a vocabulary lens for the spec's effect model
description:
Elm pattern: TEA's deepest design insight is the *asymmetry* between Cmd (effects you produce — 'do this thing') and Sub (message sources you currently want — 'feed me messages from here'). Cmds are one-shot, fire-and-forget, declarative; Subs are continuous, diff-based, declarative. Two different shapes for two different relationships with the outside world. The Effect Manager's job (now retired in Elm 0.19, but the abstraction lives on) was to bridge each.

re-frame2's current position: re-frame's effect model is Cmd-only — :fx is the closed list of one-shot effects. Continuous message sources (websocket open / interval running / DOM listener attached) are bolted on through ad-hoc setup events (rf2-53o is the concrete proposal). The *vocabulary* — naming what's a 'do this' vs what's a 'feed me from here' — isn't part of the spec.

Why investigate: even if rf2-53o (declarative subscriptions on a frame) doesn't land in v1, *naming* the asymmetry in the spec sharpens the user's and the AI's mental model. Construction Prompts can ask 'is this a Cmd-shaped need (do once) or a Sub-shaped need (feed continuously)?' before generating. The CP-3 (registered fx) prompt currently doesn't draw this distinction, so AI-generated 'subscription via setup event' code is structurally indistinguishable from 'one-shot fx' code, even though the lifecycles differ entirely.

Fit: pure vocabulary work (no API change) — a §The Cmd/Sub distinction in 002 or Principles.md naming the two shapes, with worked examples of each, and a flag that v1 ships only the Cmd half (fx) with subs-as-setup-events as the workaround. Lays the conceptual ground for rf2-53o landing later without re-orienting users when it does.

Risks: vocabulary creep — adding two more terms readers have to learn. Mitigation — a single short section, anchored on the existing :fx model.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: principle documented as post-v1 footnote in Principles.md (Cmd vs Sub asymmetry as a vocabulary lens); v1 ships only Cmd half (fx); subscriptions-as-setup-events is the workaround; vocabulary work, no API impact; edit: docs/specification/Principles.md

title: Idea: onUrlRequest / onUrlChange as named events (Elm Browser.application framing)
description:
Elm pattern: Browser.application asks the program to provide two named handlers — `onUrlRequest : UrlRequest -> Msg` (user clicked a link, decide to handle internally vs. follow externally) and `onUrlChange : Url -> Msg` (URL has changed for any reason — nav, popstate, programmatic). Two clean events, no DOM-API leakage. The Url is data; the UrlRequest is a tagged union (Internal Url | External String). The whole link/navigation problem becomes 'two pure functions over data.'

re-frame2's current position: 012 has :route/handle-url-change (good — matches onUrlChange) and :route/navigate (programmatic only). The onUrlRequest equivalent — 'user just clicked an <a>; framework, decide what to do' — is implicit in the route-link view's :on-click logic (preventDefault + dispatch :route/navigate for matched routes; let through for external). The internal/external decision is per-call site rather than a single named decision point.

Why investigate: AI-amenability — naming the decision point makes it inspectable. The current route-link is fine for users but the *decision* (internal route vs external link, modifier-key passthrough, target=_blank cases) is buried in view code. Lifting it to a registered :route/url-requested event would (a) make the decision policy queryable and overridable per-frame, (b) compose with auth-guard-style interceptors uniformly, (c) make 'how does the framework decide whether <a> click is internal' a single named answer.

Fit: pure addition; route-link remains the convenient view but its body becomes 'dispatch [:route/url-requested url-or-href]'. The registered :route/url-requested handler is the policy seam — match against route table → :route/navigate; external → :nav/external. Tests can dispatch :route/url-requested to exercise the decision without simulating clicks.

Risks: yet another stable event id to register and document. Mitigation — define it once in 012's standard events list (alongside :route/navigate and :route/handle-url-change) and have the default route-link dispatch it; users who don't use route-link don't see it.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: ship :rf/url-changed and :rf/url-requested as canonical named runtime events (Elm Browser.application analogues). Default handlers registered by the runtime; user code re-registers to override per-frame policy. Spec edited at 012-Routing.md §Standard runtime events, §URL changes are events; API.md routing section.

title: Idea: Property-based fuzz testing as a default story (elm-test analogue)
description:
Elm pattern: elm-test ships with elm/test/Fuzz as a first-class API, not an add-on. Every test author writes `fuzz int (\n -> ...)` as casually as a one-shot example. Fuzz tests catch shape-violations, off-by-ones, and invariants that example-based tests miss. The cultural effect — fuzz is *normal*, not 'advanced'.

re-frame2's current position: 008-Testing.md focuses on fixtures, sync triggers, per-test stubbing, headless sub eval. test.check exists in the Clojure ecosystem but is not part of the spec's testing story. The conformance corpus (per rf2-the-bar memory) is fixture-based: input EDN → expected EDN.

Why investigate: re-frame2 has *exactly* the right shape for fuzz testing — pure handlers (state, event) → effects-map; pure subs state → value; pure machine transitions; serialisable schemas describe valid inputs. With Malli+test.check the fuzz generator is *free* — the schema is the generator. The biggest payoff is in the conformance corpus: instead of (or alongside) hand-curated EDN fixtures, generate event sequences from the schema and assert the runtime invariants (drain terminates, no error events emitted, app-db conforms to its registered schema) hold. This is property-based conformance.

Fit: opt-in capability; lines up with the schemas-opt-in stance (010, rf2-schemas-opt-in memory). Could land in 008 as a documented section; the conformance corpus picks it up where useful. Static-typed ports use their host's fuzz/property libraries (fast-check in TS, hypothesis in Python, proptest in Rust).

Risks: fuzz tests can be slow; flakiness through under-specified invariants. Mitigation — the spec recommends fuzz for handlers/subs/transitions where pure (cheap) and the invariants are documented (no exception, drain terminates, schema conformance). Don't propose fuzz as a substitute for example tests; propose it as a complement.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: principle documented as post-v1 footnote in Principles.md (property-based fuzz as opt-in capability; Malli + test.check; the schema is the generator); 008-Testing T-4 already records the recommendation; not a special API; edit: docs/specification/Principles.md

title: Idea: REPL primitive (rf/watch-variant id) for AI/REPL exploration of variants
description:
Source: devcards' core selling point was instant feedback — save a file, the card re-runs in the browser. Bruce Hauman called this 'the meta-application.' Histoire kept this; Storybook 8 rebuilt around Vite for the same reason. The win is REPL-feel for component development.

re-frame2's current position: 007-Stories.md §Portable into tests exposes (rf/run-variant :story.auth.login-form/validation-error) which returns the result map (frame, app-db, assertions, rendered-hiccup, elapsed-ms). This is one-shot; no re-running on registry changes.

Why worth investigating: the Layer 2 (re-frame-pair) and Layer 3 (pair-improver) AI surfaces specifically benefit from a 'variant under observation' primitive — an agent sets up a watch on a variant, makes an edit, and inspects what changed without re-running the whole suite. Concrete shape: (rf/watch-variant :story.auth.login-form/empty) registers a callback against the registrar's hot-reload notifications (per 002 §hot-reload notifications) so the variant re-runs whenever any of its dependencies (events, subs, view, schema) re-register. Returns the same result map as run-variant but kept current.

Fit/risks: the framework already ships hot-reload notifications (per 007's v1 framework hooks); watch-variant is a thin post-v1 library composition over them. Risk: cycles (a variant whose dependencies re-register on every render). Mitigation: registry-version diffing — only re-run when the dependency's registration metadata actually changed. Aligns with re-frame-pair's nREPL-attached companion (Layer 2 in the three-layer AI surface).

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
notes: Actioned: added 'Live-watching a variant for AI/REPL exploration' note to 007-Stories.md §Portable into tests. Bead remains open — implementation is post-v1.
close_reason: decision: watch-variant locked as post-v1 library composition over framework hot-reload notifications; cycle prevention via registry-version diffing; inline note in 007 upgraded from 'under investigation' to 'post-v1 library'; (rf/watch-variant id) added to API.md story library section; edit: docs/specification/007-Stories.md, API.md

title: Idea: Capability-ladder for frame configs (Browser.sandbox/element/document/application analogue)
description:
Elm pattern: TEA exposes four levels of escalating capability — Browser.sandbox (Model+View+Update only, no effects), Browser.element (adds Cmd/Sub), Browser.document (adds title control), Browser.application (adds URL/Navigation). Each rung names exactly the capabilities in scope; you can't accidentally use a capability that isn't on your rung. This is partly type-driven but the design taste — *progressive capability disclosure* — is host-agnostic.

re-frame2's current position: reg-frame is one shape; every frame can do everything (events, fx, sub-cache, drain, machines, all platforms). 002 and 011 mention some specialisations (per-test, per-request, per-session) but these are *use cases* not capability rungs.

Why investigate: AI-amenability gain — when scaffolding a story-variant frame or a test fixture, the AI ideally selects from a small set of named presets, not a free-form metadata map of N keys. `(reg-frame :counter-story {:preset :story-variant})` reads as 'this frame is for story-variant use; the runtime knows what that means.' Test frames disable network; story frames disable URL binding (already done in 012); SSR frames have :platforms #{:server}-only fx active; full-app frames are the kitchen sink. The presets are declarative metadata — the AI reads it, the runtime expands it, the user inspects it.

Fit: backwards-compatible (no preset = today's behaviour); composes with the :url-bound? per-frame flag already specced; lines up with story/devcard/test-fixture treatment in 002. Could land as a small section in 002 listing the four canonical presets and what each implies.

Risks: presets that bake in too much become inflexible. Mitigation — presets are *defaults* that user metadata overrides key-by-key, not closed bundles. Avoid preset proliferation — start with three or four.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
notes: Initial preset set (:default, :test, :story, :ssr-server) added to 002-Frames §Frame presets; OQ-F-13 left open in 002 for full preset list and key-by-key expansion. needs Mike's call: (a) is the initial set sufficient or do we need :devcards/:repl/:replay? (b) precise key expansion of each preset.
close_reason: Locked the four canonical presets (:default, :test, :story, :ssr-server) with precise expansions in 002-Frames §'Frame presets'. Closed v1 set; adding a fifth is Spec-change-only. Unknown preset values raise :rf.error/unknown-preset at registration time. :preset works on both reg-frame and make-frame. User-supplied keys win on conflict via merge. OQ-F-13 resolved. Spec-Schemas adds :rf/preset-expansion + :preset field on :rf/frame-meta. API.md adds the new error category. AI-Audit row added documenting the AI-scaffolding rule. Candidates considered and not adopted: :devcards (subsumed by :story), :repl (by :default), :replay (too coupled to Tool-Pair).

title: Idea: lightweight 'meta' inheritance form — combined reg-story + variants in one form for AI scaffolding
description:
Source: Storybook CSF3 collapses the meta-default-export and the per-story exports into a single file: meta declares component/decorators/args/argTypes/parameters once, and each named export is a thin variant that overrides selectively. AI agents that write Storybook stories almost always emit CSF3 because the inheritance is structurally explicit and the boilerplate is minimal.

re-frame2's current position: 007-Stories.md S-1 leans toward 'both forms, with the combined form desugaring to separate registrations.' The combined form is sketched but not specified; the current examples use two separate macros (reg-story then N reg-variants).

Why worth investigating: AI scaffolders generate fewer tokens, with clearer inheritance, when there is one form per topic rather than N forms. Concrete shape to investigate: (rf/reg-story :story.auth.login-form {:component login-form :decorators [...] :args {...} :argtypes {...} :tags #{:dev :docs} :variants {:empty {...} :validation-error {...} :loading {...}}}). The macro desugars to one reg-story + N reg-variant calls (so hot-reload-by-variant still works at the registrar level — V-4 hot-reload concern from 004 is preserved). AI scaffolds the combined form; humans can write either.

Fit/risks: pure ergonomics; no new framework primitives. Risk: harder to hot-reload a single variant if the source-of-truth is the combined form (saved file re-fires reg-story, all variants re-register).
