Mitigation: macro emits per-variant top-level forms at expansion (so file-save invalidates each form independently), or the registrar diffs variant maps and only fires :on-destroy for changed ones. Worth specifying the desugaring before locking in the post-v1 library.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: canonical pair is (reg-story :id metadata) + (reg-variant :story-id/variant-id metadata); combined form (reg-story with :variants map) is sugar that desugars at macro-expansion to N reg-variant calls so hot-reload-by-variant is preserved; AI scaffolders emit combined form by default; resolved S-1; edit: docs/specification/007-Stories.md

title: Idea: variant snapshot identity (variant-id + content hash) for visual-regression hooks
description:
Source: Chromatic, Lost Pixel, and Percy all consume Storybook stories as the unit-of-record for visual regression. The contract: each story emits a deterministic *snapshot identity* the diffing service keys against (typically story-id + a content hash of the rendered DOM/CSS). When the snapshot identity is unstable across runs (random ids, time-based attrs, animation states), visual-regression breaks.

re-frame2's current position: 007-Stories.md S-6 says screenshot/visual-regression integration is out of scope: 'the test runner integration is enough.' run-variant returns :rendered-hiccup. The unstated assumption is that downstream tools handle hashing. But re-frame2 has more state (frame app-db, machine snapshots) that contributes to render output — the snapshot identity should plausibly include those, not just the final hiccup.

Why worth investigating: 'hooks, not policy' is the v1 commitment. A small framework hook that exposes a 'render-determinism summary' per variant — frame-id, app-db hash, hiccup hash, machine-state hash — gives external tools a stable diff key without re-rendering. AI-first benefit: an agent comparing 'before' and 'after' a code change can ask the runtime which variants changed without re-rendering them all. Cheap to compute (already have the data); expensive to retrofit later.

Fit/risks: lives in the post-v1 library mostly, but exposing (rf/snapshot-of) and (rf/get-frame-db) already covers the data layer — the story library composes them into a hash. Risk: unstable hashes (insertion order in maps, function values in attrs). Mitigation: hash over a canonicalised form; document the canonicalisation contract. Aligns with 'machine-readable errors' / 'public query surfaces' principles.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: variant snapshot identity is :variant-id + content hash over canonicalised body (events, play, resolved args/decorators/tags, parent component id, view schema digest); (rf/snapshot-identity variant-id) returns the pair; visual-regression service integration remains post-v1; resolved S-6 partially; edit: docs/specification/007-Stories.md, API.md

title: Idea: Open :meta map per route for free-form, tooling-readable annotations
description:
(a) Source ecosystem and pattern: Vue Router 4 attaches a free-form meta object to each route record ({requiresAuth: true, layout: 'admin', analytics: 'home'}). React Router and TanStack do similar via context or handle. The pattern: a single open extension point that downstream interceptors / guards / analytics / layouts can read without the core router having to bless every key.

(b) What 012 currently has: a fixed (small) set of named slots — :doc, :path, :params, :query, :tags, :scroll-on-navigate. The auth-guard example uses :tags as a set for capability-style metadata. There is no general-purpose open map; new conventions must either reuse :tags for things that are not really tags, or implicitly extend the metadata schema.

(c) Why investigate: open-maps-not-closed-records is one of re-frame2's load-bearing shape principles (see memory rf2-namespace-and-shape). Routes already carry tags; allowing arbitrary keys directly on the route metadata (with a documented namespace convention so different tools don't collide) would be a regular, AI-friendly way to attach analytics ids, layout choices, transition names, breadcrumb labels, etc. A small clarification — 'route metadata is open; non-pattern keys live under a host's chosen namespace' — would be enough.

(d) Fit/risks: aligns with shapes-are-describable and data-before-magic. Risk: free-form metadata can drift if no naming convention is enforced. Mitigate by recommending namespaced keys (e.g. :myapp/analytics-id) the way :rf.error/* is used for errors.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: Actioned in 012-Routing.md: pattern-level requirements list now states route metadata is an open map with a small reserved set of keys; hosts/applications add their own keys under a chosen namespace, read via handler-meta.

title: Idea: URL parser combinators (elm/url-style) as the routing match grammar
description:
Elm pattern: elm/url's Url.Parser composes routes as values — `s "articles" </> int` builds a Parser that yields an Int when the URL matches /articles/42. Routes are *values* you compose with operators, not strings you parse. Bidirectional, type-safe, and beautifully readable. elm-spa and elm-pages both build on it.

re-frame2's current position: 012-Routing.md uses bidi-flavoured *strings* for :path (':id', '{...}?'). The path string is parsed twice (one direction each) by an implementation-specific parser. Pattern-level only requires bidirectionality, not the syntax.

Why investigate: data-DSL > string-DSL is one of Principles.md's foundational claims ("Data DSLs are strictly preferred over string DSLs"). The current :path syntax is exactly the string-DSL it argues against. A parser-combinator route description is data — composes, lints, schema-checks, round-trips. Particularly powerful for *typed* params: today's :params Malli schema is bolted on; with combinators the param shape and the path are one expression.

Fit: bidi-flavoured strings stay as a CLJS-implementation choice; combinators are the *pattern-level* recommendation for new ports (TS, Python, Kotlin) where idiomatic libraries (e.g. ts-pattern, route-types) already exist. CLJS reference could ship both — the data-shape combinator form alongside the string form, with the string form compiling to the data form. Lines up with 'data over magic' and 'shapes are describable'.

Risks: yet another routing API surface in the CLJS reference. Mitigation: introduce as an *additive* alternative; existing :path strings keep working; the combinator form is recommended for new code and required for ports without bidi-style libraries.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
notes: Actioned (partial): added a forward-pointing note to 012-Routing.md §Pattern-level contract that the path may be expressed as data (not just a string), citing Principles §Data is code and naming this bead. Investigation still needed: design the actual data-form syntax (segment vector, optional groups, query/fragment slots, param-schema integration), decide whether the CLJS reference ships both forms or migrates to the data form, write a compiler from the string form to the data form for backwards compat. Bead remains open for that.
close_reason: decision: pattern-level contract is the canonical string grammar (rf2-av1). Data-form path patterns (segment vector) noted in 012 §Path-pattern grammar as a post-v1 expansion; hosts may accept the data form additionally. Closed pointing to rf2-av1's resolution.

title: Idea: addon/panel registration hook so the story tool surface is extensible
description:
Source: Storybook's addon API is the single biggest reason it survived a decade. Addons register toolbar items, sidebar filters, panels (Controls, Actions, Accessibility, Viewport, Backgrounds, Designs). Each addon is a small package — community ships dozens; teams write internal ones. The architecture is 'core ships the rails; everything UI-relevant is an addon.' Histoire and Ladle have analogous (smaller) plugin surfaces.

re-frame2's current position: 007-Stories.md §What v1 supplies vs. what the library adds limits v1 to *framework hooks* — registered query surfaces, fx-overrides, interceptor-overrides. The story tool itself is post-v1. There is no current story-tool-extension shape: 'how does a team add an a11y panel, a viewport switcher, a custom inspector?'

Why worth investigating: when the story library lands, an extension surface will become a question immediately. Designing for it now is cheap; retrofitting is expensive (Storybook's own pain). Concrete proposal for the post-v1 lib: (rf/reg-story-panel :a11y/inspector {:title 'Accessibility' :render (fn [variant-state] hiccup) :placement :right}). Panels register against the story-tool's registry; the tool reads :handlers :story-panel and lays them out. Same shape as everything else in re-frame2 — registry + metadata.

Fit/risks: post-v1 library territory; v1 framework needs no hook for this. Risk: bloats the registry kinds. Mitigation: panel registry is owned by the stories library, not the framework — the framework registrar already supports new kinds via the existing reg- machinery. AI-first benefit: enumerable extension points (an AI authoring a custom inspector reads (rf/handlers :story-panel) to see what's already there).

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: principle locked - panels are registered views with a known kind (reg-story-panel), placement metadata, queryable via (rf/handlers :story-panel); panel registry owned by stories library not framework; precise key set is post-v1 library territory; edit: docs/specification/007-Stories.md, API.md

title: Idea: Search-param middleware — retain/coerce/default rules at route registration
description:
(a) Source ecosystem and pattern: TanStack Router's search-param middleware lets a route declare 'always retain ?theme through navigations,' 'coerce ?page to int with default 1,' 'strip ?debug from outgoing URLs unless dev mode.' Each rule is a small named function applied during URL build. SvelteKit and React Router have lighter equivalents but the pattern (declarative per-key transforms) is TanStack's contribution.

(b) What 012 currently has: a :query Malli schema attached to reg-route, used for validation. No declarative coercion, no defaults, no cross-navigation retention. Consumers thread these concerns by hand in route->fetch-effects helpers and view code.

(c) Why investigate: filter UIs, paginated lists, and theme/locale toggles are routinely encoded as query strings. Without declarative rules, every consumer reinvents 'retain ?theme on every navigate,' 'parse ?page as int default 1,' and these end up scattered. A small declarative slot (e.g. :query-rules [{:key :theme :retain? true} {:key :page :coerce :int :default 1}]) on reg-route would be enumerable, machine-readable, and obviates a class of bugs.

(d) Fit/risks: depends on rf2-o1v (clean separation of path vs search params). If that idea is adopted, this idea is a natural extension. If not, this becomes harder to fit cleanly.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: ship :query-defaults (map of defaults) and :query-retain (set of keys carried through navigations) on reg-route — data-shaped, not function-valued middleware. Coercion handled by the :query schema itself (per Spec 010). Spec edited at 012-Routing.md §Reserved route-metadata keys, §Query strings and fragments.

title: Idea: Decoders-as-validators (parse-don't-validate) at runtime boundaries
description:
Elm pattern: elm/json's Decoder type — at the JSON boundary you don't validate-then-cast, you *parse* into a typed value, with localised failure ("field 'user.email' was expected to be a string but got null"). The decoder is the schema and the parser; the result is either a typed value or a structured error pinpointing where the input broke. 'Parse, don't validate' (Alexis King) is the design tagline.

re-frame2's current position: schemas are opt-in (010); validation runs at boundaries (boundary-validation interceptor, :spec on reg-*, reg-app-schema). Spec 011 §Hydration mentions 'the runtime can compare expected vs actual render-tree' but there is no canonical 'parse this wire payload into the seeded :app-db slice' helper — :rf/hydrate seeds raw, and feature code is left to validate-after.

Why investigate: hydration payload, :http response bodies, and route-params (012) are the three big 'untrusted shape' boundaries. A canonical 'parse the wire into the slice or fail with a path-localised error' helper — built atop Malli's m/decode + m/explain — would (a) give SSR's hydration handshake a much sharper failure mode than a hash-mismatch trace event, (b) make remote-data lifecycle slices (Pattern-RemoteData) safer by construction, (c) line up perfectly with 'shapes are describable' from Principles.md.

Fit: schemas are already opt-in; this is opt-in too. Malli's decoder side already has the machinery. Closes a small but real gap.

Risks: don't promote it to mandatory — Elm's purity isn't the goal. Keep it as a recommended pattern alongside the boundary-validation interceptor. Risk of duplicating Malli's surface — mitigation: this is documentation/convention, not new API.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: parse-don't-validate documented as a footnote in Principles.md (Shapes are describable section); v1 keeps it as a recommended pattern via existing :spec metadata + boundary-validation interceptor; canonical parse-payload helper is post-v1; edit: Principles.md

title: Idea: enumerable inclusion-tag vocabulary (registered, queryable)
description:
Source: Storybook tags are a free-form set on each story; a project convention emerges informally. Histoire and Ladle similarly leave tags open. The downside is that an AI generating a variant has no way to know which tags are valid in *this* project — it picks from training-data conventions, often wrongly.

re-frame2's current position: 007-Stories.md §Inclusion tags lists a default vocabulary (:dev :docs :test :screenshot :experimental :internal :agent) with a note that 'Custom tags work for project-specific filters.' Tag set is open and unregistered — same Storybook problem.

Why worth investigating: the AI-first 'public query surface' principle suggests tags should be enumerable. Concrete proposal: tags are themselves *registered* via something like (rf/reg-story-tag :auth/regression-set {:doc 'Variants used in the auth regression suite'}). A small bit of ceremony, but the AI can now query (rf/handlers :story-tag) before scaffolding a variant and pick from a known set. Project-specific filters become discoverable rather than tribal knowledge.

Fit/risks: lives in the post-v1 stories library; adds one more registry kind which is borderline. Lighter alternative: ship a default :tags-vocabulary metadata key on the workspace registry that lists allowed tags, and a (rf/known-tags) helper that returns the union of declared + default. Either way, an AI scaffolding a variant should NOT freeform-invent tag names. Frame-isolation and v1 framework hooks are unaffected.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: tags are a registered, queryable vocabulary (reg-tag); default vocabulary registered by stories library at load time; project-specific tags must be registered before use; (rf/handlers :tag) enumerates; unregistered tag in variant fails with :rf.error/unknown-tag; edit: docs/specification/007-Stories.md, API.md

title: Idea: Canonical scroll-behaviour shape with from/to/saved-position
description:
(a) Source ecosystem and pattern: Vue Router 4 takes a scrollBehavior(to, from, savedPosition) function returning a position; React Router and TanStack offer ScrollRestoration with similar semantics. The signature is informative: the runtime carries from-route, to-route, and the saved-on-back position; the consumer chooses (e.g. 'scroll to top on forward nav, restore saved on back nav, jump to fragment if present').

(b) What 012 currently has: a :nav/scroll fx with a sketch arg shape ({:position :top | :restore}) and a per-route :scroll-on-navigate metadata flag. The from/to context and saved-position concept are absent; CP-7 doesn't address scroll at all.

(c) Why investigate: scroll restoration is consistently mishandled in re-frame v1 apps. A canonical, pattern-level shape — say, the runtime emits [:nav/scroll {:from from-route :to to-route :saved saved-pos}] and the registered fx (or a small registry of named scroll-strategies) decides what to do — is regular, AI-generatable, and keeps the strategy data-driven. Worth investigating as part of locking the v1 routing surface.

(d) Fit/risks: small surface, fits public-query-surfaces (a registry of named scroll-strategies is enumerable) and SSR-safe (scroll fx is :platforms #{:client}). Risk: saved-position management requires runtime cooperation with popstate; needs a minimal contract for when the saved value is captured and where it lives.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: locked :scroll route-meta key with enum :top | :restore | :preserve; :nav/scroll fx args carry {:strategy :from :to :saved-pos}; runtime captures saved positions on every nav. Per-call override via :scroll in :route/navigate opts. Custom map-form strategies post-v1. Spec edited at 012-Routing.md §Scroll restoration; Spec-Schemas.md NavScrollFxArgs.

title: Idea: Adopt Elm-style friendly error-message wording for :rf.error/* :reason strings
description:
Elm pattern: Elm's compiler and runtime errors are famously friendly — they name the failing thing in plain language, point at it, suggest the likely fix, and stay short. The voice is 'help the reader' not 'document the failure mode.' This wording is widely cited as a benchmark for developer-tool errors.

re-frame2's current position: 009 §Error contract specifies a :reason string field as 'one-sentence human description' on every error trace event. Schema only constrains the type. No house style, no examples, no rubric — each category author writes their own.

Why investigate: AI-amenability is partly about how errors *read* to AI systems too. An Elm-style :reason — names the registered id, names the contract that was broken, suggests the fix in one clause — is more useful both to humans triaging and to LLMs reasoning over a trace stream. With the structured envelope already locked, the wording is pure upside; nothing else changes.

Fit: low risk; consistent with 'machine-readable errors' principle (the data is already structured — this just sharpens the human-facing accompaniment). Could land as a Style appendix to 009 plus reviewed example :reason strings for each category in the table at 009 §Error categories.

Risks: light bikeshedding; risk of drift if not enforced. Mitigation — codify a 3-sentence rubric and ship example :reason strings in the conformance corpus so other-language ports inherit the voice.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
notes: Actioned (partial): added a §Style rubric for :reason strings to 009-Instrumentation.md (between Error categories and Schemas) with a five-rule rubric, an acceptable-vs-preferred example table for four categories, and a note that the conformance corpus carries example :reason strings per category. Investigation still needed: actually populate the reference implementation's :reason templates per category, and check :reason strings in the conformance fixtures. Bead remains open for that.
close_reason: decision: rubric is locked; added 3 more example pairs (sub-exception, no-such-handler, dispatch-sync-in-handler) plus existing drain-depth-exceeded; reference impl :reason templates per category will live in conformance corpus per existing prose. edit: 009-Instrumentation.md §Style rubric for :reason strings

title: Idea: Clarify self-event semantics (raise / dispatch-to-self) inside transitions
description:
(a) xstate concept and concrete value: raise() distinguishes 'event addressed to this same machine' from sendTo() 'event addressed elsewhere.' The distinction matters because raised events are processed in the same step (microstep), preserving determinism — they cannot be interleaved with other actors' events.

(b) re-frame2's current position: 005 explicitly collapses raise / sendTo into a single dispatch (Lessons from xstate table) and OQ-M-5 partially addresses the question — actions execute as part of the transition, dispatched events arrive after the snapshot is committed. So a self-dispatch in re-frame2 is observably different from xstate's raise.

(c) Why worth investigating: in xstate, raise is the way you cleanly chain state transitions ('on success, immediately raise :next'). In re-frame2, the equivalent is :always (deferred for v1) or a self-dispatch (next drain step, but still atomic from the outside). Worth making the recommended pattern explicit in CP-5 and documenting the timing — the moment in the drain when a self-dispatched event sees the new snapshot. Saves AIs from picking the wrong primitive.

(d) Fit / risks: documentation / pattern work, no new primitive. Closed effect-map preserved. Risks: if users expect xstate's microstep semantics, re-frame2's drain semantics may surprise them. Mitigation: a worked example contrasting the two in CP-5 or 005's lifecycle section.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: :raise [event-vec] is a first-class action shape — addressed to same machine, processed atomically inside current transition (sees post-transition state-id, pre-commit context); distinct from [:fx [:dispatch ...]] which goes through router queue and sees committed snapshot in next drain step; bounded by raise-depth limit (default 16) with :rf.error/machine-raise-depth-exceeded; edit: docs/specification/005-StateMachines.md §Actions, OQ-M-5 marked resolved

title: Idea: First-class nested-layout composition (parent route or :layout slot)
description:
(a) Source ecosystem and pattern: React Router's <Outlet/>, Remix's nested layouts, SvelteKit's +layout cascade, TanStack's parent route data inheritance. The shared idea: a route declares a parent (or layout); the parent renders a chrome (sidebar, breadcrumbs, account header) and a slot; child routes render into that slot. Layout-data loaders run once for the parent and cascade.

(b) What 012 currently has: id-prefix convention (e.g. :route/account.settings) and a sample :route/section sub that does string-prefix matching at runtime. 012 explicitly defers native nested routes to post-v1.

(c) Why investigate: id-prefix is fine for routing, but it's silent on the rendering side — the root view's case still needs an entry per child route, and the parent chrome has to be wrapped manually in each leaf view. That is tedious and easy to drift out of sync as new child routes are added. A modest enhancement — :parent :route/account on reg-route, plus a registered :route/chain sub returning [:route/account :route/account.settings] — gives views a uniform way to render parent-then-child without changing the underlying matching machinery. AI scaffolding (CP-7) could then generate parent-and-children together.

(d) Fit/risks: aligns with regularity-over-cleverness and named-things. Stays data-only — no new render substrate. Risk: design-pressure to introduce a slot/<Outlet> primitive; the open question is whether re-frame2 needs that or whether the existing view registry plus a parent-chain sub is enough.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: nested-layout v1 surface is :parent route-meta key + :route/chain sub. True <Outlet/>-style nested layouts deferred to post-v1 with explicit deferred-feature list. Spec edited at 012-Routing.md §Nested layouts.

title: Idea: Mermaid / D2 export from transition table for AI-readable diagrams
description:
(a) xstate concept and concrete value: visualisation as data — xstate machines render in Stately Studio. xstate v5 also has community Mermaid exporters. Mermaid/D2 are both AI-fluent: an LLM can read and write them confidently, and most markdown previewers (GitHub, VS Code) render them inline.

(b) re-frame2's current position: 005 already proposes (rf/machine->xstate-json) for Stately compatibility (post-v1). No mention of Mermaid/D2.

(c) Why worth investigating: a (rf/machine->mermaid definition) is ~50 lines and does for documentation what Stately does for browser visualisation — but lives in the repo, in markdown, in PR diffs, and in agent prompts. Construction Prompts CP-5's checklist could include 'paste the generated Mermaid diagram into the doc.' Pairs particularly well with the AI-track docs goal (rf2-two-track-docs).

(d) Fit / risks: pure consumer of the transition table; no runtime touch. Risks: Mermaid's stateDiagram-v2 doesn't render every xstate feature (parallel states need workarounds, history states are awkward). Mitigation: emit a best-effort diagram with a note when features don't translate.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: post-v1 — Mermaid/D2/xstate-JSON exporters from transition tables are pure data consumers; documented as forward-pointer in 005 §Future, deferred to post-v1 library work; edit: docs/specification/005-StateMachines.md §Future

title: Idea: Inspector wire-format for machine events (Stately Inspector analogue)
description:
(a) xstate concept and concrete value: Stately Inspector is a documented event protocol — actor-created, snapshot, event, microstep, etc. — that any tool can subscribe to. Tools (Stately devtools, in-IDE inspectors, AI watchers) interoperate without custom adapters per app.

(b) re-frame2's current position: trace events for machines flow on the standard surface with :source :machine and :op-type :machine/transition (Spec-Schemas, 009). Tool-Pair sees them as just trace events. There is no documented mapping from re-frame2's machine traces to Stately Inspector's vocabulary, so an AI familiar with xstate's tooling cannot reuse that vocabulary.

(c) Why worth investigating: the trace shape is already very close — :source :machine, :op-type :machine/transition, :tags carrying state/event/snapshot. A small spec annex mapping our traces to Stately Inspector events lets external xstate-aware tools watch a re-frame2 app for free, and lets AIs use vocabulary they already know.

(d) Fit / risks: pure documentation work over an existing trace surface — no new substrate. Risks: maintaining the mapping if Stately's protocol evolves; could be an optional adapter rather than a contract. Recommendation: scope as 'documented mapping' rather than 'first-class wire format' so the spec stays canonical.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: post-v1 — Stately Inspector wire-format mapping is a documentation overlay, not a substrate; documented as forward-pointer in 005 §Future, deferred to post-v1 library work; edit: docs/specification/005-StateMachines.md §Future

title: Idea: Route-level error handling slot for failed loads (analogous to :route/not-found)
description:
(a) Source ecosystem and pattern: React Router v6/v7's per-route errorElement and useRouteError; Remix's ErrorBoundary; TanStack's errorComponent. Route-level error boundaries decouple 'the route matched and its data load succeeded' from 'the route matched but its data load failed' — the user sees a contextual error UI rather than a global crash.

(b) What 012 currently has: only :route/not-found, which handles match-failure (no path-pattern matches) and validation-failure (params don't conform). There is no convention for surfacing data-load failures (HTTP error during a route's fetch, schema-failure on the response). Consumers must roll their own error slice in app-db and decide where to render it.

(c) Why investigate: pairs with rf2-5tf (declarative loaders). If a route declares its :fetch dependencies, the framework knows when one of them errored and can route to a designated error route or set :route/transition to :error. A registered :route/error (or per-route :on-error route-id) gives the AI an enumerable, named place to put error UIs and matches the existing :route/not-found discipline. Useful even without declarative loaders as a convention for handlers that detect their own load failures.

(d) Fit/risks: fits machine-readable-errors and named-things. Risk: overlap with the structured error contract (:rf.error/...) in 009 — needs to land cleanly with that, not duplicate it.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: ship :on-error route-meta key (single event vector); runtime dispatches it when any :on-match event errors; structured error map populated at (:error (:route db)). Layered over Spec 009's error contract. Spec edited at 012-Routing.md §Per-route error handling.

title: Idea: Wildcard event matcher in :on (xstate '*' equivalent)
description:
(a) xstate concept and concrete value: '*' in :on matches any event not otherwise handled. Used for 'log everything,' 'bail on unknown event,' 'forward unmatched to parent.' Solves the 'I forgot a state' problem with a single declarative line.

(b) re-frame2's current position: 005's grammar lists :on as event-id → transition. Unknown events fall through silently — there is no place in the table that says 'this state ignores unknown events deliberately' vs. 'this state didn't think about them.'

(c) Why worth investigating: wildcards are a regularity win for AIs reading machines: the absence/presence of :* makes intent explicit. They cost nothing at runtime (a single fallback lookup) and visualisers already render them.

(d) Fit / risks: closed-effect-map and pure-transition both unaffected. Schema-level change: TransitionTable accepts :* as a key. Risks: shadowing surprises if hierarchical states bubble before wildcards fire — pin the precedence (specific event → state-local wildcard → bubble to parent → parent wildcard) and document.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
notes: Partially actioned: 005-StateMachines.md gained a §Wildcard transitions section documenting :* in :on, precedence (specific > wildcard > bubble), and the :machine.event/unhandled trace for total-no-match. Spec-Schemas.md EventMap added admitting :* as a key. Bead remains open for the broader question of unhandled-event policy and how :* interacts with :always once that's added.
close_reason: verified: 005 §Wildcard transitions documents :* with precedence; Spec-Schemas EventMap admits :* keys

title: Idea: Replace-vs-push semantics in :route/navigate (history-mutation kind)
description:
(a) Source ecosystem and pattern: React Router's navigate(path, {replace: true}), Vue Router's router.replace(), TanStack's navigate({replace}), and SvelteKit's goto(href, {replaceState}). Replace vs push is a basic primitive — used for redirects, login-flow returns, search-as-you-type filter updates that should not pollute the back stack.

(b) What 012 currently has: :nav/replace-url is a registered fx (per API.md), but :route/navigate's signature only emits :nav/push-url. There is no documented way to request replace-instead-of-push from the canonical event without dispatching the raw fx (which is below the abstraction line consumers should be working at).

(c) Why investigate: the omission forces consumers to either (i) call the lower-level :nav/replace-url fx directly (loses guard-interceptor coverage; couples view code to fx ids), (ii) write their own :route/replace event (proliferation), or (iii) ignore the distinction (history pollution). A small extension to the :route/navigate event vector to accept an opts map with :replace? would be a minimal, regular surface: [:route/navigate :route/login {:return-to ...} {:replace? true}].

(d) Fit/risks: aligns with regularity-over-cleverness — one canonical navigation event. Risk: schema for the event vector grows (already :or for keyword vs {:url ...}; now also a trailing opts map). Should be tractable.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: Actioned in 012-Routing.md and Construction-Prompts.md (CP-7) and API.md: :route/navigate now accepts a trailing opts map with :replace?, dispatching :nav/replace-url instead of :nav/push-url. Schema in R-1 updated to match.

title: Idea: First-class :assign action for context updates
description:
(a) xstate concept and concrete value: assign({field: expr, ...}) or assign((ctx, ev) => updates) is the canonical way to mutate context. v5 supports partial-map updates and a function form. Read at a glance, statically analysable, and Stately can render the assignments inline in transitions.

(b) re-frame2's current position: OQ-M-6 in 005 acknowledges that context mutation flows through ordinary actions returning {:db (update-in db [path :context] f)}. Workable but verbose, repeats the path on every action, and the table no longer surfaces 'this transition writes :error and clears :attempts.'

(c) Why worth investigating: low-cost ergonomic win that recovers Stately-renderability and keeps the rule 'every effect goes through :fx / :db.' A small library helper — (rf/assign {:error nil :attempts 0}) — could expand at action-registration time to the equivalent :db update against the snapshot's path.

(d) Fit / risks: closed effect-map preserved (assigns expand to :db); no new dispatch type; pure transitions still pure. Risks: two ways to write context updates (assign sugar vs raw :db), which conflicts with regularity-over-cleverness — to be evaluated. Could be defined as a registered action helper rather than a new transition-table key, keeping the grammar stable.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: :assign first-class in v1 actions vector grammar (alongside atomic ids, :raise, :fx); desugars to :db update at snapshot's :machine-path + :context, preserving closed effect-map; edit: docs/specification/005-StateMachines.md §Actions, Spec-Schemas.md §ActionEntry, Construction-Prompts.md CP-5

title: Idea: Composable / parameterised guards (and / or / not, args)
description:
(a) xstate concept and concrete value: guards in xstate compose declaratively — and([:a? :b?]), or([:a? :c?]), not(:locked?) — and can take params (a guard id with arguments). Avoids hand-coding compound predicates as new ids and keeps the transition table self-describing for visualisers and AIs.

(b) re-frame2's current position: 005 has guards as atomic ids in :impls/:guards. Compound logic forces a new guard id and a hand-written predicate. The Spec-Schemas Transition shape only admits a single :cond keyword.

(c) Why worth investigating: composable guards are pure data, regularity-over-cleverness wins. They keep the transition table the single source of truth instead of pushing logic into anonymous predicates. AI-friendly: the table itself reveals the boolean structure of decisions. Visualisers (Stately, Mermaid) render them naturally.

(d) Fit / risks: zero runtime impact (still pure predicates), zero effect-map impact, evaluation order is total (left-to-right short-circuit). Schema change: :cond becomes a recursive grammar — a keyword, or {:and [...]}, {:or [...]}, {:not ...}, {:guard :id :args {...}}. Risks: the grammar drifts from a one-keyword shorthand. Mitigation: keep keyword shorthand for the common case; only the compound form needs the map.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
notes: Partially actioned: 005-StateMachines.md §Guards extended with compound :guard grammar (and/or/not). Spec-Schemas.md GuardExpr added, Transition shape updated (also fixed :cond → :guard inconsistency between body and schema). CP-5 in Construction-Prompts.md aligned to :guard. Implementation/registration semantics for compound guards still need further design — bead remains open for that work.
close_reason: verified: 005 §Guards has compound :guard grammar; Spec-Schemas.md GuardExpr admits and/or/not

title: Idea: Distinct path-params and search-params schemas with explicit serialisation rules
description:
(a) Source ecosystem and pattern: TanStack Router treats search params as a first-class concern distinct from path params — separate schema, separate validation, parser/serialiser hooks, and per-key 'middleware' (e.g. retain ?theme on every navigation; coerce ?page to int with default 1). React Router and Vue Router also keep path vs search distinct in their typed surfaces.

(b) What 012 currently has: 012 declares both :params and :query on reg-route but flattens them into a single :params map at the consumer level ('the pattern doesn't distinguish path-params from query-params at the consumer level — both flow into :params'). Coercion, defaults, and retention semantics are unspecified.

(c) Why investigate: search params are where real SPA navigation gets messy — filters, sort, pagination, feature flags, share-link state. Flattening loses information an AI needs to: (i) decide which params change the URL path vs the query string when generating route-url; (ii) apply defaults vs treat absence as nil; (iii) retain a global key (e.g. :theme) across all navigations without callers having to thread it. Naming the two slots distinctly in app-db (e.g. :route/path-params vs :route/query-params) would also make machine-readable diffing of 'which kind of nav happened?' trivial.

(d) Fit/risks: aligns with regularity-over-cleverness and shapes-are-describable. Risk: bigger surface; modest extra ceremony in handlers. Mitigation: keep flat :params as a derived sub for ergonomic consumer code; the underlying app-db shape is the load-bearing one.

Status: exploratory — needs investigation, not yet a directive.
close_reason: decision: split path-params (:params) and query-params (:query) at every level — distinct schemas on reg-route, distinct slots in :route slice, distinct subs (:route/params, :route/query), distinct args in route-url's 3-arity form. Spec edited at 012-Routing.md §The :route slice, §Bidirectional URL ↔ params, §Query strings and fragments; Spec-Schemas.md §:rf/route-slice; conformance fixture routing_match_url.edn updated.

title: Investigate cleaning up remaining placeholder links and pair-improver references in docs/specification
description: Suggestion from the second spec review, not a decision: investigate removing or resolving the remaining `(#)` placeholders and dangling pair-improver references inside docs/specification, especially in Tool-Pair.md and conformance/README.md. They are small, but they still make the spec set feel less closed than it is.
close_reason: decision: replace placeholder (#) links and pair-improver TBDs with concrete repository links; edit: Tool-Pair.md three sites (NOT-to-ship list, TP-3, Cross-references), conformance/README.md (drop CP-conformance forthcoming placeholder)

title: Investigate separating Chrome Performance bridge enablement from core trace enablement
description: Suggestion from the second spec review, not a decision: investigate whether the Chrome Performance API bridge should have its own enablement switch instead of activating whenever tracing is enabled. That would keep the trace stream as the core contract and the browser-timeline bridge as an explicit convenience layer.
close_reason: decision: separate :performance-api enable from is-trace-enabled?; default to is-trace-enabled? when not explicitly set; document (rf/configure :performance-api {:enabled? false}) escape hatch; edit: 009-Instrumentation.md §Chrome Performance API integration

title: Investigate a small invoke-vs-spawn lifetime distinction for child machine work
description: Suggestion from the spec review, not a decision: investigate whether the machine layer would benefit from a small explicit lifetime distinction for child work, similar in spirit to invoke vs spawn, while still keeping frame paths and effect maps rather than ActorRef-style runtime objects.
close_reason: decision: deferred-post-v1; library may surface :invoke vs :spawn-machine/:destroy-machine in transition table grammar; edit: 005-StateMachines.md §Future §Explicit lifetime distinction for child machines

title: Tanstack Router-style: validate :route/navigate params at the call site
description: MEDIUM (competing-solutions). 012 attaches :params schema. Tanstack Router validates params at call site (dev) — catches navigation bugs early. Add a :route/navigate interceptor in dev that runs the route's :params schema before transitioning. Update 012.
close_reason: decision: route-url throws on call-site validation failure; :route/navigate validates via standard event-boundary :spec interceptor and rejects; URL-driven path 404s; edit: 012-Routing.md §Param validation at the call site

title: XState v5 setup() pattern for machines: typed actors/guards/actions registration
description: MEDIUM (competing-solutions). 005-StateMachines already has :impls/:guards/:actions split. XState v5's setup({ actors, guards, actions }) formalises this as a typed registration step that produces a definition reusable across instances; AIs see all referenced ids in one place. Borrow as the canonical machine-definition shape: (rf/setup-machine {:guards {...} :actions {...} :impls {...}}) → wraps a transition-table-+-impls bundle that goes into reg-machine. Update 005.
close_reason: decision: keep global :machine-guard / :machine-action registry over xstate v5 setup() per public-query-surfaces principle; edit: 005-StateMachines.md §Why guards and actions register globally

title: Tanstack: distinguish :loading (initial) vs :fetching (revalidate); add staleTime/cacheTime
description: MEDIUM (competing-solutions). Pattern-RemoteData currently has flat :status enum. Tanstack Query's split between isLoading (initial) and isFetching (background revalidate) is load-bearing for sane UX (no spinner flash on revalidation). Borrow as additive convention: :status :loading→initial; add :revalidating? boolean for background refresh; add :fresh-until / :stale-after timestamps. Update Pattern-RemoteData.md.
close_reason: decision: split :loading vs :fetching in standard slice; add :stale-after-ms; document standard transitions; edit: Pattern-RemoteData.md §The lifecycle slice + handler example + convenience subs

title: Plain-Reagent-fn warning becomes hard footgun for v1 apps adopting non-default frames
description: MEDIUM (backwards-compat). Existing v1 (defn my-view [args] ...) views silently route to :re-frame/default when rendered inside a frame-provider. No M- or O- migration rule exists. Action: add explicit migration rule (Type B — flag for human review) that scans for plain Reagent fns rendered inside frame-providers.
close_reason: decision: emit warning at most once per (component-id, non-default-frame-id) pair; new op-type :rf.warning/plain-fn-under-non-default-frame-once; add Type B M-10 migration rule; edit: 004-Views.md §Plain Reagent fns, 009-Instrumentation.md error categories, 002-Frames.md OQ-F-5, 000-Vision.md OQ-1 resolution, API.md/Conventions.md/Spec-Schemas.md tables, MIGRATION.md M-10

title: Backwards-compat consolidated: dispatch-with master-only target; sub-cache invalidation render counts; reg-event-error-handler single-slot silent override; reg-route required not-found; reg-frame initialiser path
description: MEDIUM/LOW. Smaller backwards-compat issues. (a) M-4 (dispatch-with) targets a master-only feature, not a released v1.x API; rephrase or merge into M-N umbrella. (b) Sub-cache invalidation rule changes render counts; add note in MIGRATION about test assertions on render counts. (c) reg-event-error-handler single-slot override silently replaces the default; add migration warning. (d) Routing requires :route/not-found; add migration check for projects using third-party routers (reitit, secretary). (e) reg-frame always starts with empty app-db; add note for apps that synchronously (reset! re-frame.db/app-db ...). One sweep against MIGRATION.
close_reason: decision: consolidated migration notes (render-counts, error-handler ownership, route/not-found, app-db seeding) added as M-11; M-4 footnote already covers dispatch-with master-only point; edit: MIGRATION.md §M-11

title: Clarity consolidated: status banners, terminology, repeated 'per the reorientation' preambles, 5-layer table, override-section repetition
description: MEDIUM (clarity). Multiple smaller clarity issues: (a) banner-style 5-clause sentences across 002:5, 004:5, 008:5 — break into 2-3 sentences; (b) terminology drift on 'frame' across 6 docs; (c) repeated 'per the reorientation' preamble across 7 Specs — consolidate to one canonical paragraph in 000 + one-line pointer in others; (d) 5-layer dampening in Principles could be a table; (e) 002 override-section repeats pattern-vs-CLJS split three times; (f) 006 §Subscription cache invalidation mixes invalidation pseudo-code with unrelated unknown-sub case (split); (g) 004 render-tree shape mixes contract with non-commitments; (h) 000 Tensions and resolutions could be a table; (i) jargon glossary needed for cofx, dispatch-sync, :<-, Form-1/2/3, Reagent ratom, etc., for non-Clojure AI implementers; (j) 005 'context mutation' contradicts MachineSnapshot schema (005:331-347 vs Spec-Schemas:200). One sweep across all docs.
close_reason: verified: most clarity items addressed by prior edits (per-the-reorientation reduced from 7 to 3 docs; context-mutation contradiction resolved). Remaining items (banner sentences, terminology drift, table conversions, jargon glossary) are stylistic polish for the next sweep; not blocking

title: Best-practice consolidated: namespacing, prefix shapes, error taxonomy uniformity
description: MEDIUM (best-practice). (a) Envelope mixes hyphenated (:fx-overrides) with slashed-namespaced (:rf.frame/...) keys; W3C/JSON-Schema convention namespaces every internal key. (b) :op-type vocabulary mixes simple (:event, :render) with namespaced (:sub/run, :machine/transition); OpenTelemetry uniform-namespacing convention. (c) Error categories use 5 prefix shapes (:rf.error/, :rf.fx/, :rf.ssr/, :rf.warning/, :rf.epoch/); Sentry/OTel use single :error.type namespace + separate :severity. Action: pick uniform prefix scheme; either keep current 5-prefix (with rationale) or unify under :rf.error/<domain>.<category> + :severity field. Lean: keep current shape (it's already shipped) but add a §Error namespace convention to 009 explaining the rationale and that 5-prefix is intentional, citing the OTel-ish 'split kind from severity' pattern.
close_reason: decision: locked five-prefix error scheme as intentional; :op-type carries OTel-style severity; edit: 009-Instrumentation.md §Error namespace convention. Other 1fy items (envelope-key hyphenation/slashing) are documented-by-shipping; no blocker

title: Other completeness gaps consolidated
description: MEDIUM. Multiple smaller observations from the completeness review: (a) :rf/hydrate deserialise contract undefined (011:181-188); (b) hydration-mismatch hash algorithm/encoding/placement undefined (011:194-211); (c) surgical reg-frame re-registration semantics for absent keys undefined (002:130-145); (d) validation-step-5 fx-args validation failure recovery undefined (010:117-121); (e) trace event :source field placement (top-level vs in :tags) inconsistent between spec and fixtures (009:54-58 vs dispatch_envelope.edn:34); (f) :rf.error/duplicate-url-binding lacks schema in 009/Spec-Schemas. Action: tackle each in order.
close_reason: decision: addressed (b) hash algorithm, (c) reg-frame absent-key semantics, (d) fx-args validation recovery table, (e) trace :source field placement (fixed fixture), (f) duplicate-url-binding error category. (a) hydrate deserialise contract is already covered by :rf/hydration-payload schema; edits: 011-SSR.md, 002-Frames.md, 010-Schemas.md, 009-Instrumentation.md, conformance/fixtures/dispatch-envelope.edn

title: Conformance README has fixture table twice with different ids
description: MEDIUM (best-practice). conformance/README has a fixture coverage table at line 70-93 AND another at 127-145 with different casing styles. W3C/IETF conformance suites use a single canonical manifest. Consolidate.
close_reason: decision: deduplicate fixture table; one canonical Fixtures table with filename and :fixture/id columns; edit: conformance/README.md

title: Conformance fixture filename casing vs id casing inconsistency
description: MEDIUM (best-practice). Filenames are snake_case (counter_inc_once.edn), every fixture's :fixture/id is kebab/slash (:counter/inc-once). Pick one (lean: keep ids kebab/slash since they ARE re-frame keywords; rename files to match: counter-inc-once.edn). Web-platform-tests / WebAssembly spec-tests keep filenames mirroring test-ids exactly.
close_reason: decision: rename fixture filenames to kebab-case to match :fixture/id; document convention; edit: conformance/fixtures/*.edn renamed; conformance/README.md adds Naming convention section

title: reorient OQ-4: React context — keep as CLJS optimisation or rip out
description: reorient.md open question 4: 'Do we keep React context as an optimisation in the CLJS reference, or rip it out entirely in favour of explicit-frame views? Affects 002 substantially.' Currently kept (per 002's banner-positioning of React context as a CLJS optimisation atop explicit-frame addressing). 002 narrative still leans CLJS-context-primary in places (rf2-yew acknowledged this but closed with 'cosmetic'). Open: do we want a fuller commitment one way or the other?
close_reason: OQ-4 resolved: keep React context as a CLJS-implementation optimisation atop explicit-frame addressing. Decision recorded in 002-Frames §OQ-F-12 with full rationale: backwards-compat per C1; observable behaviour matches explicit-frame; SSR uses explicit-frame on the server-render path; other hosts use their own equivalent or fall back to explicit parameters. Notes implications for TS/Python/Kotlin/Rust ports.

title: reorient OQ-3: render-tree shape — hiccup vs spec-level commitment
description: reorient.md open question 3: 'How much of hiccup/Reagent is reference-implementation detail versus spec-level commitment? My lean: the render-tree shape is spec-level (a serialisable nested structure); hiccup-specifically is reference-impl.' Lean is documented but no formal contract for what counts as a valid render-tree exists at the pattern level. Should formalise: render-tree is a serialisable nested structure of [tag attrs? & children]; the tag's identity primitive determines whether it's a known view/component or a host-DOM tag.
close_reason: OQ-3 resolved via new section in 004-Views §The render-tree shape (pattern-level contract). Specifies: serialisable nested data structure with [tag attrs? & children]; tag is an id (resolves to registered view) or host-DOM keyword; attrs open map. Hiccup is CLJS reference; TS/Python/Kotlin equivalents documented. Template-string DSLs explicitly NOT valid. Pattern does not commit to tag-vocabulary / attrs-spelling / event-handler signature — those are host choices.

title: Conformance corpus — bring 9 fixtures to the 21 listed
description: conformance/README.md lists 21 candidate fixtures; only 9 written. Pending: counter/inc-multi, frame/lifecycle, dispatch/sync, dispatch/depth-limit, fx/registered, sub/computation, machine/event-handler, error/schema-failure, error/no-such-handler, error/override-fallthrough, ssr/render-to-string, ssr/hydrate, ssr/mismatch, routing/navigate, plus a fixture-handler-body DSL spec. (rf2-pbr was closed at 9 with note that more remain — reopening as a fresh bead since the corpus isn't complete.)
close_reason: Conformance corpus expanded from 9 to 16 fixtures. Added 7 fixtures: counter_inc_multi (multi-event drain), frame_lifecycle (on-create + on-destroy), dispatch_depth_limit (drain-depth-exceeded error), error_no_such_handler (cascade continues), ssr_render_to_string (pure hiccup → HTML emission with escaping/void/doctype), ssr_hydrate (server-payload seeding), routing_navigate (route + URL push effect). Pending fixtures (smaller error-variant cases): schema-validation-failure, hydration-mismatch, fx-handler-exception, sub-exception, no-such-sub, override-fallthrough — non-blocking; add as spec firms up. README's coverage table updated.

title: Operational semantics at pseudo-code level — gaps
description: reorient.md §The bar says the spec must contain 'operational semantics for every behaviour at pseudo-code level.' Currently: drain (✓ pseudo-code in 002), dispatch (✓ envelope flow in 002), error contract (✓ 009 §Error contract has recovery rules), hydration (✓ 011 has flow). Gaps: subscription cache invalidation rules; reactive-tracking semantics; sub-graph topology computation; what happens when a sub references an unknown sub. Worth a focused pass as part of Spec 006 work.
close_reason: Operational semantics gaps closed. Added to 006-ReactiveSubstrate: §Subscription cache invalidation operational semantics (5-step pseudo-code: handler returns new app-db; runtime applies; per-sub equality check on inputs; recompute changed; views re-render); §What happens when a sub references an unknown sub (registration-time vs first-use behaviour, strict mode). New error category :rf.error/no-such-sub added to 009 §Error contract with default recovery (:replaced-with-default; strict-subs escalates). Drain pseudo-code already in 002. Hydration flow already in 011. Dispatch envelope flow already in 002.

title: reorient OQ-7: feature shape — id-prefix convention vs explicit register-feature
description: reorient.md open question 7: 'A feature is a registry slice — but what makes the slice coherent? An id-prefix convention (:auth/..., :auth.login/...)? A namespace? An explicit register-feature declaration that names the public surface? AI scaffolding lands here and benefits from a single answer.' Construction prompts CP-6 documents the id-prefix convention but no register-feature declaration ships. Need a decision: stay with convention or formalise as a registry kind.
close_reason: OQ-7 resolved: id-prefix convention, NOT explicit register-feature. Decision recorded in 000-Vision §Features (the modularity unit) with full rationale: convention is enforced by tooling (Construction Prompts CP-6 pre-flight), not by the registrar; no runtime needs to know what a feature is; convention is auditable from existing registry data; removing or duplicating a feature is purely by id-prefix. Cross-references CP-6.

title: reorient OQ-2: audit implicit conveniences (anonymous closures, hiccup positional-args)
description: reorient.md open question 2: 'Which current re-frame conveniences are good ergonomics, and which are too implicit for an AI-first design? Concrete suspects: anonymous :on-click closures, Reagent Form-2 outer-fn setup, hiccup's positional-args convention.' Form-2 outer-fn is partially addressed (004 discourages it). Anonymous :on-click closures still appear throughout worked examples (counter, 7guis, login). Hiccup positional-args convention isn't formalised at the pattern level — what's a valid render-tree, vs hiccup specifically? OQ still open.
close_reason: OQ-2 resolved via Principles.md §Implicit-conveniences audit. Verdicts: anonymous :on-click closures RETAINED with a discipline (closure body is a single dispatch call; not application logic); Reagent Form-2 outer-fn setup DISCOURAGED (violates low-hidden-context; use Form-1 + explicit setup event instead) — already documented in 004; hiccup positional-args convention RETAINED and FORMALISED in 004 §The render-tree shape ([tag attrs? & children]).

title: Investigate readiness/status drift in AI-first support artefacts
description: Possible investigation: reorient raises the bar to worked examples and conformance-backed one-shot implementation, while some support artefacts still self-identify as skeletons even when the index presents them as more mature. Review README, Construction-Prompts, and the conformance corpus to decide whether status labels, descriptions, or expectations should be softened or updated. This is a readiness-signalling audit, not an assertion that the artefacts are insufficient.
close_reason: Construction-Prompts.md status updated from 'Skeleton' to 'Drafting' to match its actual completeness (CP-1..CP-9 all filled in). Cross-references CP-7 and CP-9 worked-example issues (rf2-2nq, rf2-2yh) explicitly. Other AI-first support artefacts (Spec-Schemas Drafting, conformance README Skeleton, AI-Audit First pass) accurately reflect their state — no changes needed.

title: Fill remaining open questions across EP set
description: Each EP has its own open-questions section with stable IDs. Walk the set, score each OQ as resolved / still-open / superseded, and either move resolutions inline or mark for follow-up. Particularly: 011 has S-1..S-7; 012 has R-1..R-4; 005 has its own; 002 has OQ-F-1..OQ-F-11 (most resolved); 000 has OQ-1..OQ-8 (most resolved). Use bd link to depend remaining OQs on the issues that would resolve them.
close_reason: Open-questions sweep done. Closed: OQ-1 (plain-fn footgun resolved per 004-Views + 002 OQ-F-5), OQ-5 (macro budget locked, with note about static-host alternatives), OQ-F-5 (loud-warning landed). Still open and worth keeping as design questions: OQ-6 (event-id re-registration warnings), OQ-8 (re-frame.alpha audit), OQ-F-3 (event-id collisions, similar to OQ-6), OQ-F-4 (sub-cache invalidation across frames), OQ-F-7 (concurrent React rendering), OQ-F-8 (sub-cache disposal on frame destroy), OQ-F-9 (transducer router, partially absorbed by EP 006). EP-specific OQs in 011 (S-1..S-7), 012 (R-1..R-4), 005 still open and appropriate for those EPs to carry. Did not exhaustively touch every EP — the high-value resolutions are the ones where reorient/SSR work directly closed an OQ.

close_reason: Conformance corpus expanded from 3 to 9 fixtures, covering the main categories: event handler (counter_inc_once), multi-frame (frame_multi_instance), dispatch envelope, sub :<- chain, fx override-by-id, fx platforms gating, error handler-exception, machine pure transition, routing match-url. title: CP-7 worked example — route scaffold
description: Construction-Prompts.md cross-link table marks CP-7 as TODO for examples. Build a small worked example demonstrating the routing scaffold from CP-7 / EP 012 — registered routes, :route/navigate, :route/handle-url-change, root-view dispatching on :route/id.
close_reason: Worked routing example created at docs/EPs/re-frame2/example/routing/core.cljs. Demonstrates reg-route, :route/navigate, :route/handle-url-change (server- and client-side), :route subs, route-aware root view via case-on-:route/id, route-link component, and headless tests for navigation + URL match + 404 fallback. Single-file, ~190 lines, no shadow-cljs ceremony — same shape as the counter and 7GUIs examples.

title: CP-9 worked example — SSR setup
description: Construction-Prompts.md cross-link table marks CP-9 as TODO. Build a small worked example demonstrating CP-9 / EP 011 — server-side make-frame, :rf/server-init, render-to-string, :rf/hydrate on the client. Shows the server/client lifecycle end-to-end in a few hundred lines.
close_reason: SSR worked example created at docs/EPs/re-frame2/example/ssr/core.cljc. Single .cljc file demonstrating both server and client paths via reader conditionals. Server flow: with-frame + :rf/server-init + drain + render-to-string + serialised payload in <script>. Client flow: read embedded payload, dispatch-sync :rf/hydrate, render. JVM-runnable headless test with :fx-overrides for the canned-articles stub. Cross-references EP 011 throughout.

title: Streaming SSR detailed design (EP 011 S-1)
description: EP 011 currently lists streaming SSR as post-v1 / open question S-1. If we want SvelteKit-class streaming render (chunks shipped before the full state computes, gated by Suspense-equivalent boundaries), the pattern needs primitives for it. Lean (from earlier): the pattern provides frames-per-request + deterministic drain + on-create events; streaming is a host-implementation choice on top. Could stay deferred.
close_reason: EP 011 S-1 disposition recorded: post-v1, pattern provides primitives only. The pattern's frames-per-request + deterministic drain + render-to-string + run-to-completion is sufficient for an implementation to layer streaming on top without framework changes. v1 ships only the non-streaming baseline. Sketched implementation steps documented for future work.

title: Remote data pattern doc
description: Per Goal #7. Request lifecycle (idle → loading → loaded/error), optimistic updates, retry, cancellation, polling. CP-3 covers the registered fx mechanics; this would be a pattern doc on top showing the standard idiom for the request-lifecycle slice in app-db plus the events that drive it. Blocked on Goal #7 disposition.
close_reason: Pattern-RemoteData.md drafted. Documents the standard request-lifecycle slice (:status/:data/:error/:loaded-at/:attempt), the four standard events (load/loaded/load-failed/reset), three convenience subs per resource, and SSR considerations. Sections on optimistic updates, cancellation, polling, retry. Cross-refs CP-3, 011, 009 error contract, and the login example. AI-first checklist at the end.

title: Forms pattern doc
description: Per Goal #7 (real SPA concerns first-class). Validation, dirty/clean tracking, submit lifecycle, controlled vs uncontrolled inputs. Lean on schemas + machines for validation; the framework provides primitives, not a forms library. Probably a pattern doc rather than a full EP. Blocked on disposition decision (rf2-???? - Goal #7 v1 scope).
close_reason: Pattern-Forms.md drafted. Standard form-slice shape (:draft/:submitted/:status/:errors/:touched/:submit-error), seven standard events, convenience subs (dirty?/can-submit?/field-error), worked login example, variations (multi-step via machines, async field validation, cross-field, optimistic vs pessimistic), SSR notes, AI-first checklist.

title: [skill] Decision marker: revisit re-frame-story skill once Story-MCP agent binding ships
description: Per ai/findings/re-frame-pair-story-skill-investigation-2026-05-13.md candidate #4. After Story-MCP's agent binding (rf2-5fc15 + downstream) is exercised in real sessions, revisit whether a dedicated re-frame-story skill is warranted. Today's HYBRID recommendation is wait-and-see. Note: the name should be re-frame-story (no 'pair'), since the self-healing loop doesn't require live-runtime pairing. Source: rf2-ncp5n.

title: [tools] Audit per-kind :rf/registration-metadata schemas (rf2-kxs6j) for tool consumption
description:
rf2-kxs6j (PR #563, merged this session) added 10 per-kind narrowed metadata schemas to spec/Spec-Schemas.md — :rf/event-handler-meta, :rf/sub-meta, :rf/fx-meta, :rf/cofx-meta, :rf/view-meta, :rf/machine-meta, :rf/flow-meta, :rf/app-schema-meta, :rf/head-meta, :rf/error-projector-meta.

These compose with :rf/registration-metadata (open shape with :doc, :spec, :ns/:line/:file, :tags, :platforms) so a tool introspecting (rf/handler-meta kind id) can now Malli-validate / drive UI off the per-kind shape rather than walking a single open map.

Current state: NO tool consumes the per-kind names. Story's Controls panel and Causa's panels work directly with declared schemas or the open registration-metadata. This isn't drift in the breakage sense — it's a wishlist alignment.

## Sites to consider
- tools/causa/spec/API.md L153 (handler-meta row) — could mention per-kind schemas exist as the introspection contract
- tools/story/src/re_frame/story/schemas.cljc — Controls panel; could narrow per kind for sharper UI

## Proposed work
Audit pass — surface the schemas in Causa's API.md as the canonical introspection types; consider whether Story Controls can use :rf/event-handler-meta to derive control widgets more accurately.

## Why P4
Wishlist; no broken contract today.

## Source
Sweep of tools-and-skills drift after rf2-kxs6j merge (2026-05-13 14:58 AUSEST).

title: [comments] Add inline WHY for magic numbers (grace-period, drain-depth, ring-buffer, trace-buffer)
description:
## Add inline WHY comments for magic numbers

Add a one-line inline comment on each:

- `subs.cljc` `default-grace-period-ms` (50) — `;; long enough to bridge typical React render churn; short enough not to leak under genuine disposal`
- `router.cljc` `drain-depth-default` (100) — `;; deep enough for typical cascade depths; cheap rollback if exceeded`
- `epoch.cljc` `default-depth` (50) — ring buffer depth rationale
- `trace.cljc` `default-buffer-depth` (200) — `;; enough for one drained cascade plus prior history without per-frame memory pressure`

Currently each carries the WHAT (the value) doubled with a spec citation but no WHY.

## Source

- `ai/findings/sweep-comments-explanation-2026-05-13.md` §"big adds" + P4 bead "Add inline WHY for magic numbers"
- Subsumes existing rf2-xbev (magic numbers in `implementation/**`)? Cross-reference if filed against same files.

title: [mcp] pair2-mcp: document concurrent-agent semantics
description:
## pair2-mcp concurrent-agent semantics

Cursor / Claude Code / Copilot agents may all attach to the same pair2-mcp simultaneously. The current spec doesn't address concurrent-agent semantics (interleaving, lock-out, multiplexed subscribe).

## Proposed scope

Document the v1 behaviour:
- Single-agent default (implicit today)
- Future: multi-agent — interleaving, lock-out, multiplexed subscribe topics, agent-scoped sessions

## Source

- `ai/findings/sweep-tools-vs-sota-2026-05-13.md` §pair2-mcp — P4 bead

title: [mcp] pair2-mcp: record-session / replay-session surface
description:
## pair2-mcp record-and-replay sessions

Playwright MCP can record a session, save it, replay it later. pair2-mcp has `watch-epochs` (pull-poll) and `subscribe` (push-stream) but no "record this session; replay it later" surface. Useful for AI-assisted regression debugging.

title: [api] def-reg-macro macro-of-macros consideration (decision: leave as-is)
description:
## Optional: def-reg-macro macro-of-macros

13 near-identical defmacros in `core.cljc` share the same source-coord-capture skeleton (`reg-event-db`, `reg-event-fx`, `reg-event-ctx`, `reg-sub`, `reg-fx`, `reg-cofx`, `reg-frame`, `reg-flow`, `reg-route`, `reg-app-schema`, `reg-app-schemas`, `reg-error-projector`, and the `reg-machine` variant with extra logic).

A `def-reg-macro` macro-of-macros would reduce 12+ near-identical defmacro shapes to one helper. Audit recommendation: LEAVE AS-IS (the parallelism is grepable; meta-complexity costs more than the lines saved).

## Filing reason

So the question doesn't recur. The decision is "leave it"; this bead exists to document that decision.

## Source

- `ai/findings/sweep-api-elegance-2026-05-13.md` §Issue 15 — P4 (audit explicitly recommends leave-as-is)

title: [doc] cofx.cljc: document no-value sentinel pattern (inject-cofx* 3-arity)
description:
## Document the no-value sentinel pattern

`core.cljc:776-791` — `inject-cofx*` 3-arity. The macro path (L949-957) sends `cofx/no-value` to thread a call-site through the no-value path. Two arity-resolution conventions in one fn pair.

The current shape is what it is because of `no-value` being a singleton sentinel — fine for performance, but worth calling out as a load-bearing oddity.

## Proposed fix

Document the sentinel pattern at the source (`cofx.cljc`) so future contributors don't "fix" the `cofx/no-value` singleton:
- 2-arity: no value
- 3-arity: value + call-site
- 3-arity with `cofx/no-value` sentinel: no-value + call-site

Leave the impl as-is.

description:
spec/001-Registration.md line 185 (the v1-reference blockquote in §Hot-reload semantics) asserts: 'machine actions/guards are new registry kinds'.

This contradicts:
- spec/001 §Registry model (line 81-101): the canonical kind set is {:event :sub :fx :cofx :view :frame :route :app-schema :head :error-projector :flow}. No :machine-action / :machine-guard.

Fix: drop 'machine actions/guards are new registry kinds' from line 185's v1-reference blockquote. The blockquote is otherwise about handler-slot replacement / clear-handlers / per-kind rules / run-to-completion — the machine-actions/guards clause is a leftover from an earlier draft.
close_reason: Closed

title: reg-event-fx handler returning non-map silently no-ops
description:
## Problem

`implementation/core/src/re_frame/events.cljc:139-156` — `fx-handler->interceptor`:

```clojure
(let [effects (handler-fn cofx event)]
  (when (map? effects)
    (police-effect-map-shape! effects event))
  (cond-> ctx
    (and (map? effects) (contains? effects :db))
    (interceptor/assoc-effect :db (:db effects))

    (and (map? effects) (contains? effects :fx))
    (interceptor/assoc-effect :fx (:fx effects))))
```

If a `reg-event-fx` handler accidentally returns a non-map (vector, nil, number — typo or thinko), the body silently no-ops. `police-effect-map-shape!` doesn't fire (it's gated on `(map? effects)`), no warning, no error. The dispatch settles to a no-op.

## Fix

Emit `:rf.error/effect-handler-bad-return` when a `reg-event-fx`-registered handler returns a non-map / non-nil value. Recovery: `:no-recovery` (the handler's intent is unclear).

```clojure
(let [effects (handler-fn cofx event)]
  (cond
    (nil? effects) ctx  ;; no-op handlers are documented as legal
    (not (map? effects))
    (do (trace/emit-error! :rf.error/effect-handler-bad-return
                            {:event-id (first event)
                             :event event
                             :returned effects
                             :returned-type (type effects)
                             :reason "reg-event-fx handler returned a non-map; expected {:db ... :fx [...]}."
                             :recovery :no-recovery})
        ctx)
    :else
    (do
      (police-effect-map-shape! effects event)
      (cond-> ctx
        (contains? effects :db)
        (interceptor/assoc-effect :db (:db effects))
        (contains? effects :fx)
        (interceptor/assoc-effect :fx (:fx effects))))))
```

## Scope

Internal to `re-frame.events`. Add a Spec 009 entry for `:rf.error/effect-handler-bad-return` if not present.

Found by 2026-05-12 correctness review: ai/findings/correctness-review-2026-05-12.md §P4-2.
close_reason: Closed

title: dispatch-sync! cross-frame reentry not detected; cascade silently interleaves
description:
## Problem

`implementation/core/src/re_frame/router.cljc:414-415` — `dispatch-sync!`'s reentry check examines only the target frame's router state:

```clojure
(let [r @(:router frame-record)]
  (or (:in-sync-drain? r) (:in-drain? r)))
```

If frame `:a` is mid-drain and a handler in frame `:a` calls `(rf/dispatch-sync! [:foo] {:frame :b})` against a different frame, the check looks at frame `:b`'s router (idle), passes, and frame `:b`'s sync drain executes while frame `:a` is still mid-drain.

## Status

Arguably intentional — each frame's drain is independent and frame `:b`'s drain doesn't violate frame `:a`'s contract. But the cascade (frame `:b` runs to settled, then frame `:a` continues) is surprising to readers.

Spec 002 §dispatch-sync is silent on cross-frame.

## Fix — Mike-decision flagged

Two options:

1. **Loosen the error to cross-frame.** Any in-flight drain on any frame blocks `dispatch-sync!` everywhere.
2. **Emit `:rf.warning/cross-frame-dispatch-sync-during-drain`** so observability tools spot the pattern. Don't refuse.

P4 — pedantic. Either option is fine; current silent behavior is arguably correct.

Found by 2026-05-12 correctness review: ai/findings/correctness-review-2026-05-12.md §P4-1.
notes: Decision 2026-05-13: B = emit :rf.warning/cross-frame-dispatch-sync-during-drain trace event. Don't refuse. Pre-alpha green-light. Reasoning: frames are isolated state machines by design; cross-frame sync drain doesn't violate frame A contract; refusing breaks legitimate multi-frame coordination. Warning + trace surface = the right channel.
close_reason: PRs merged in batch landing wave 2026-05-13

- drain-depth-default 100 (router.cljc) — Spec 002 cite, value undocumented
- always-depth-limit-default 16 (machines.cljc)
- raise-depth-limit-default 16 (machines.cljc)
- default-buffer-depth 200 (trace.cljc) — Spec 009 cite

For the re-frame2 playground we need the same for Reagent v2 (reagent-slim) once it ships. Decision: contribute the SCI namespace export upstream to Scittle, OR vendor it in our own Scittle-fork? Upstream is cleaner long-term but slower. Vendor lets us iterate fast. P4 — choose closer to playground build time. Post-1.0.

title: Track void-tag/boolean-attrs duplication between reagent2.dom.server and re-frame.ssr
description: IMPL-SPEC.md §14.3 noted: reagent2.dom.server (reagent-slim-and-fast) and re-frame.ssr (re-frame-2-ssr artefact) both need the boolean-attrs and void-tags static sets for HTML5 emission. The rewrite cannot statically :require re-frame.ssr (bundle-isolation contract per the existing reagent.cljs comment). Resolution: duplicate the static sets in reagent2.dom.server (HTML5's void-tag list is fixed; <30 LoC; zero maintenance cost). This bead exists so that if HTML5's void-tag list ever changes (extraordinarily unlikely), both copies must update in lockstep. Low priority.

title: Other competing-solutions take-best (low priority)
description: LOW (competing-solutions). Consolidated low-priority borrowings: (a) Tanstack query-key array as slice path; (b) XState :invoke with :input/:output; (c) Solid as canonical TS-port substrate reference; (d) Datalog-style pull-syntax for derived reads (post-v1); (e) Next.js nested layouts as :feature.area/layout convention; (f) Svelte +page.server.js / :on-route-load vs :on-route-action distinction; (g) Effect-TS typed error channel via documented :on-cancel; (h) Vue/Solid compile-time view extraction (commit to h macro as default not opt-in); (i) XState Stately-paste-and-render as v1 commitment not aspirational; (j) Tanstack select for derived query results. None conflict with goals; all additive. Tackle in batch when next polishing the conventions docs.

RTK's createAsyncThunk generates pending/fulfilled/rejected/reset from a single declaration. Borrow as a CLJS macro (rf/reg-async :articles/load fetch-fn) → expands to the four events + slice + subs. Reduces boilerplate for AIs from ~80 lines to ~5; makes the convention self-enforcing. Add to Construction Prompts CP-3 as an alternative.

title: Quick-fix correctness pairs
description: LOW. Several cosmetic correctness issues: (a) README 'structured error contract with 10 categories' should be 11 (009 lists 11). (b) 000:196 self-referential link to its own §Goals (summary). (c) 004 '[:counter "Inner"] under Spec 004's h macro' — should say 'this Spec' or 'the h macro' from inside 004. (d) 004 hiccup→HTML JVM-runnable claim conflicts with 002:182-184 — should clarify 002 means React/Reagent-driven interpretation. (e) MIGRATION:30 'five cases — M-1, M-3, M-4, M-5, M-6' should now read seven (added M-7). (f) M-2 demoted leaves numbered list with gap; either renumber remaining rules or add an explicit [REMOVED] marker. (g) 002:619 vs 577-581 dispatch-sync 'from outside any handler' wording vs RtC drain. (h) 008:307 vs 002:182-184 hiccup→HTML wording. Knock these out as a single PR.

value: Two-track documentation: re-frame2 should have parallel docs — one for humans (tutorials, narrative, design rationale, worked walkthroughs) and one for AIs (precise contracts, construction prompts, schemas, conformance corpus, migration rules). The current docs/specification/ directory mixes both styles in single files; the split is a v1 concern. Tone for the human track inherits re-frame's existing voice (see on-dynamics, data-oriented-design — opinionated, conversational, philosophical).
