All 20 Cross-Spec Interactions list "future fixture X.edn" — known queue.
list

EffectMap shape: ONLY two top-level keys allowed — :db and :fx. The :rf/effect-map schema is closed. Every non-:db effect goes through :fx as [[fx-id args] ...]. Top-level :dispatch, :dispatch-later, :dispatch-n, :http etc. are NOT part of the contract; legacy code is migrated by M-7 in MIGRATION.md. The single-form rule lets the runtime walk one ordered list of effects, fits regularity-over-cleverness.

re-frame2 goal ordering (11 goals total). Goals 1-3 are LENSES every other goal is shaped by: (1) AI-first amenability, (2) Simplest possible computational model that gets the job done, (3) Embody the best of the Clojure ethos. Goals 4-11 are CONTENT the lenses are applied to: mechanical upgrade from re-frame, language-agnostic pattern, SSR-capable, real SPA concerns first-class, feature modularity, strong introspection surface, deterministic testable runtime, preserve re-frame's ergonomic taste. When goals conflict, AI-first is the tiebreaker.

Vocabulary: drop 'EP' (Enhancement Proposal) framing for spec docs. EP carried over from re-frame's docs/EPs/ directory which housed actual proposals against v1; in re-frame2 the docs are a specification of a new pattern, not proposals. New terminology: 'Spec 002' or just '002'. File names stay (002-Frames.md) — cross-references use them. The spec stays high-level (pattern + reference-impl description); the actual CLJS implementation will live in this repo separately (likely src/) and is distinct from the spec.

Frame is redefined (per reorient) as an ISOLATED RUNTIME BOUNDARY — multi-instance widget, per-test fixture, per-request server-side render, per-session — all the same shape. Not specifically 'multiple re-frame instances on a page'. Override seam moves from function-valued to id-valued at the pattern level (functions don't serialise across the wire / SSR). CLJS reference also accepts function values for one-off lambdas, but id-valued is the portable form. The CLJS reference's React-context-driven view injection is positioned as a CLJS implementation OPTIMISATION atop explicit-frame addressing as the pattern contract.
mcp-testing-audit

Two-track doc voice (refines rf2-two-track-docs). HUMAN track: marketing-oriented — explains the philosophy, the reasons behind choices, the benefits, with enough technical detail to be useful. Inherits re-frame's existing opinionated/conversational/philosophical voice (see on-dynamics.md). AI track: dry and to the point — contracts, signatures, shapes, semantics, fixtures, prompts. No narrative, no rationale, no marketing on the AI side.

Worked examples live in docs/EPs/re-frame2/example/. (1) example/counter — 7GUIs #1, the simplest demo. (2) example/7guis — all 7 7GUIs benchmarks in re-frame2 (counter, temperature, flight-booker, timer, CRUD, circle-drawer, cells). Mike directed: use 7GUIs (not RealWorld/Conduit) for in-spec examples; Conduit is too big and would live in a separate showcase repo. (3) example/login — full feature scaffold demonstrating every construction prompt; preserved as a parallel example because it exercises the state-machine pattern more thoroughly than any 7GUIs task.

When skills are created (re-frame2-pair-improver, future skills), they live under /skills at the repo root. Layout: /skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md plus references/. Mirrors the re-frame-pair-improver repo structure. Don't action skills yet — design phase only.

Two key supporting artefacts beyond the EPs: (1) Construction-Prompts.md — per-kind templates for AI-driven scaffolding (CP-1 event, CP-2 sub, CP-3 fx, CP-4 view, CP-5 machine, CP-6 feature, CP-7 route, CP-8 schema, CP-9 SSR setup; sibling to MIGRATION.md but for new code rather than upgrades). (2) Spec-Schemas.md / conformance/ — the conformance contract; canonical shapes plus EDN fixture corpus for any host's harness to validate against. Construction prompts cross-reference the worked examples (counter, full 7GUIs series, login feature).

Schemas are an OPT-IN capability per implementation, not pattern-required. The pattern requires SHAPE DESCRIPTION, not the mechanism. DYNAMICALLY typed hosts (CLJS, Python, JS, Ruby) recover shape-correctness via runtime schema layers — Malli (CLJS reference), Pydantic, Zod, dry-rb. STATICALLY typed hosts (TS, Kotlin, Rust, F#) get the same content from the type system at compile time and may omit a runtime schema library entirely. Both are first-class. EP 010 specifies the CLJS reference's schema integration; non-CLJS implementations choose differently.

The pattern's identity primitive must satisfy 7 properties: stable, namespaceable, value-equal, cheap to compare, serialisable, human-readable, reflective. Clojure keywords (CLJS reference) satisfy all natively. TS port: branded strings with naming convention (NOT Symbol, which doesn't serialise). Python: strings + an Id wrapper class with namespace parsing. Kotlin: sealed-class hierarchies of data objects. Rust: newtype struct Id. UUIDs, opaque integers, and Java ==-identity comparison are NON-conformant; the pattern leans heavily on cheap, namespaceable, human-readable ids.

CLJS reference public namespace stays re-frame.core (NOT re-frame-cljs.core). Locked. Other-language ports use language tags (re-frame-ts, re-frame-py); the '2' suffix is a CLJS-version artefact. Open maps, not closed records, is the load-bearing shape principle — applies in any host (Pydantic with extra='allow', Zod's .passthrough(), TS index signatures). Closed records / exactOptionalPropertyTypes / class hierarchies for runtime data are the wrong port regardless of host.

Backwards compatibility from re-frame v1.x is a property of the CLJS reference implementation only (per the reorient pivot). Other-language implementations are greenfield. Migration tooling (MIGRATION.md) is the price the CLJS reference pays — the new shape is decided by AI-amenability; the migration rule's job is to translate. 'Mechanical upgrade' is a constraint on tooling, not on design.

re-frame2 ships with two AI-paired tools (refines rf2-paired-tooling). (1) re-frame-pair — runtime nREPL-attached AI companion: reads state, dispatches, hot-swaps. EP 009 covers forward-compat. (2) re-frame-pair-improver — a CLAUDE SKILL (not a runtime tool, not pair-extension) that reviews a user's session with the pair tool and surfaces concrete improvement proposals FOR THE PAIR TOOL ITSELF. Diagnosis-first; no issues filed without user approval; multiple layers (SKILL.md / scripts / warnings/results / tests/fixtures / upstream). Reference: https://github.com/mike-thompson-day8/re-frame-pair-improver. The shape closes a feedback loop: the AI critiques the tool that helps AIs work in the codebase.

re-frame2 has been reoriented as a language-agnostic SPA pattern with a Clojure/CLJS reference implementation. Canonical orientation lives in docs/EPs/re-frame2/reorient.md. Rationale lives in re-frame's existing docs (on-dynamics.md, data-oriented-design.md, a-loop.md, application-state.md, etc.). re-frame2's per-EP docs operationalise that rationale; they don't re-litigate it.

The bar (per reorient.md): the spec aims to be sufficiently complete that an AI can ONE-SHOT THE IMPLEMENTATION in any host language. That bar drives every choice. Implies the spec must contain: precise contracts for every primitive, operational semantics (drain, dispatch, hydration), an error model, observable invariants, worked examples, conformance tests. The conformance corpus (docs/EPs/re-frame2/conformance/) is how this claim is verified — fixtures in EDN, expected emissions in EDN, host-language-agnostic.

re-frame2's AI-amenable surface is THREE layers, not two. Layer 1: AI-targeted docs (Construction Prompts, Spec-Schemas, MIGRATION, conformance corpus, AI track of the two-track docs). Layer 2: runtime pair tool — re-frame2-pair (nREPL-attached; reads state, dispatches, hot-swaps). Layer 3: meta / improvement skill — re-frame2-pair-improver (Claude skill that reviews pair sessions and surfaces improvements to the pair tool). Layer 3 closes the feedback loop: the AI critiques the tool that helps AIs. All three are v1 deliverables of re-frame2.

State machines and actors are adopted from xstate. Mike's directive: credit xstate explicitly in EP 005. The reasoning: constrained execution models (FSMs, actors with message-passing, run-to-completion) are easier to reason about than Turing-complete control flow — disproportionately so for AI use. xstate is also a vocabulary AIs already understand, so leaning into it lowers generation/repair/explanation costs.
